31.31: hinei yamim baim neum YHWH vekharati et-beit Yisrael veet-beit Yehudah berit hadasha (hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm nĕʾum Yhwh wĕkārattî ʾet-bêt yiśrāʾēl wĕʾet-bêt yĕhûdāh bĕrît ḥădāšāh): 'Behold the days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah.' Berit hadasha (new covenant): the only occurrence of this exact phrase in the OT. Hadash (new) can mean 'renewed' (as in the new moon, hodesh) or 'qualitatively different.' Jeremiah's contrast makes it the latter: 'not like the covenant I made with their fathers ... which they broke' (v. 32). The new covenant is distinguished by three characteristics: (1) internalized law (v. 33: on the heart, not stone); (2) universal direct knowledge of YHWH (v. 34: no longer 'know the LORD'); (3) permanent forgiveness (v. 34: I will remember their sin no more).
31.1: Bāʿēt hahîʾ (bāʿēt hahîʾ), "at that time," opens ch31 as a direct continuation of the promise begun in ch30. The phrase functions as a temporal bracket connecting the new covenant oracle to the preceding reversal of fortunes. YHWH declares himself lēʾlōhîm (lēʾlōhîm) — God — to kol mišpĕḥôt yiśrāʾēl, all the families/clans of Israel, not just Judah. The breadth is programmatic: the restoration encompasses the whole covenant people, including the northern kingdom carried away by Assyria.
31.2: Māṣāʾ ḥēn bammidbar (māṣāʾ ḥēn bammidbar): the exodus wilderness becomes the paradigm for divine grace in judgment. The survivors of the sword (śĕrîdê ḥāreḇ) find grace (ḥēn) not as earned status but as unexpected gift. Hālôk lĕhargîʿô (hālôk lĕhargîʿô), "going to give him rest," uses the Hiphil of rāgaʿ (to give rest, to quiet). The wilderness, normally a place of death and testing, becomes the space of grace — a Hosea 2 pattern ("I will allure her into the wilderness and speak tenderly").
31.3: The pivotal theological statement: ʾahăḇat ʿôlām ʾăhăḇtîḵ (ʾahăḇat ʿôlām ʾăhăḇtîḵ) — "with an everlasting love I have loved you." The noun ʿôlām (long duration, eternity) modifies the love-action absolutely: this is no conditional or seasonal affection but the settled disposition of YHWH toward Israel from of old (mērāḥôq, "from far away/long ago"). Mĕšaḵtîḵ ḥāsed (mĕšaḵtîḵ ḥāsed): "I have drawn you with steadfast love/ḥesed." The verb māšaḵ (to draw, pull) combined with ḥesed is unusual — YHWH's covenant loyalty is portrayed as the cord by which he draws the exiled people back. John 6:44 ("no one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him") uses helkō in a directly analogous theological move.
31.4: Tîḇnēḵ wĕniḇnêt (tîḇnēḵ wĕniḇnêt): "I will build you up and you will be built" — the Qal imperfect with the Niphal passive creates a divine-human synergy: YHWH's action produces the community's rebuilt state. Bĕtûlat yiśrāʾēl (bĕtûlat yiśrāʾēl), "virgin Israel," is not a critique of moral status but a term of tender address recalling the nation before the violations of exile. The image of women taking up tambourines (tuppîm) to join the dance (mĕḥôl hāmĕśaḥăqîm) echoes Miriam's victory celebration (Exod 15:20), placing restoration in the typological pattern of exodus-deliverance.
31.5: Nāṭĕʿû kĕrāmîm bĕhārê šōmĕrôn (nāṭĕʿû kĕrāmîm bĕhārê šōmĕrôn): vineyards replanted on the hills of Samaria signals the inclusion of the northern kingdom (Ephraim/Israel) in the restoration — not merely Judah. Nāṭĕʿû ḥillĕlû (nāṭĕʿû ḥillĕlû): "those who plant will also enjoy/use" (Piel of ḥālal, to begin as common/unconsecrated after the three-year waiting period, Lev 19:23-25). The allusion to the firstfruits law implies a stable, normalized covenant life has returned — planting carries through to harvest without the interruption of war or exile.
31.6: Nōṣĕrîm bĕhar ʾEprayim (nōṣĕrîm bĕhar ʾEprayim): "watchmen on the hills of Ephraim" — the term nōṣĕr (Qal participle of nāṣar, to watch) normally refers to military sentinels or lookouts over vineyards. Here they call pilgrims to worship rather than warning of danger. Qûmû wĕnaʿăleh ṣiyyôn (qûmû wĕnaʿăleh ṣiyyôn): the pilgrimage call "Rise up! Let us go up to Zion" is the ʿaliyyâ vocabulary of the Psalms of Ascent (Ps 120-134). The convergence of Ephraim and Judah in a single pilgrimage to Zion marks the healing of the north-south schism that began with Rehoboam.
31.7: Ronnû lĕyaʿăqōḇ śimḥâ (ronnû lĕyaʿăqōḇ śimḥâ): "Shout with joy for Jacob" — rānan is the exultant, ringing cry of joy that belongs to the Psalter's celebration vocabulary. Ṣahlû bĕrōʾš haggôyim (ṣahlû bĕrōʾš haggôyim): "shout at the head of the nations" — the restored Israel does not hide its praise; the proclamation is public, international witness. Hôšaʿ YHWH ʾet-ʿammeḵā ʾet šĕʾērît yiśrāʾēl (hôšaʿ YHWH ʾet-ʿammeḵā): the imperative to save echoes the hôšîʿāh nāʾ of Ps 118:25 — the same liturgical cry that greets Jesus at the triumphal entry ("Hosanna," Matt 21:9).
31.8: Hinnĕnî mēbîʾ ôtām mēʾereṣ ṣāpôn (hinnĕnî mēbîʾ ôtām): the emphatic hinnēnî ("behold me") announces YHWH's direct personal action in the return. The gathering (qibbēṣ) is universal: ʾiwwēr wĕpissēaḥ hārâ wĕyōlĕdet yaḥdāw (ʾiwwēr wĕpissēaḥ hārâ wĕyōlĕdet) — blind, lame, pregnant, and laboring women. These are precisely those who by human capacity are least able to make a desert march. Their inclusion reverses the values of the ban on entering the sanctuary (Lev 21:18; 2 Sam 5:8) — in YHWH's restoration, the excluded are specifically gathered. Qāhāl gādôl (qāhāl gādôl), "a great assembly," is the term for the covenant assembly of Israel.
31.9: Biḇkî yāḇōʾû ûḇĕtaḥănûnîm ʾôḇîlēm (biḇkî yāḇōʾû): they come with weeping — not weeping of despair but of repentance and profound relief, as the Hiphil ʾôḇîlēm ("I will lead them") makes clear: YHWH himself leads the procession. ʾEl naḥălê māyim bĕdereḵ yāšār lōʾ yikkāšlû bāh (ʾel naḥălê māyim): streams of water on a level path — the exodus/wilderness imagery inverted; instead of waterlessness and stumbling, YHWH provides both the path and the sustaining water. The climax: kî-hāyîtî lĕyiśrāʾēl lĕʾāḇ ûʾEprayim bĕḵôrî hûʾ (kî-hāyîtî lĕyiśrāʾēl lĕʾāḇ) — "For I have become a father to Israel, and Ephraim is my firstborn." The designation bĕḵôr (firstborn) for Ephraim over Manasseh recalls Jacob's reversal (Gen 48:19); more fundamentally, it echoes Exod 4:22 ("Israel is my firstborn son") and grounds the paternal claim of YHWH as the basis for filial restoration.
31.10: Šimʿû dĕḇar-YHWH gôyim (šimʿû dĕḇar-YHWH gôyim): the proclamation extends to the nations — the restoration of Israel is a matter of international witness. Mĕzārēh yiśrāʾēl yĕqabbĕṣennû ûšĕmārô kĕrōʿeh ʿedrô (mĕzārēh yiśrāʾēl yĕqabbĕṣennû): "He who scattered Israel will gather him, and he will watch over him as a shepherd watches his flock." The same agent who scattered now gathers — the scattering was punitive, the gathering is redemptive, but the shepherd (rōʿeh) image is the same continuous care. Jesus's self-identification as the good shepherd (John 10:11) and his mission to gather the scattered sheep of the house of Israel (Matt 15:24) are direct fulfillments of this restoration-shepherd expectation.
31.11: Kî-pādâ YHWH ʾet-yaʿăqōḇ wĕgĕʾālô miyyad ḥāzāq mimmennû (kî-pādâ YHWH ʾet-yaʿăqōḇ wĕgĕʾālô): two key redemption terms in parallel: pādâ (ransom, to pay the price of liberation — a commercial/legal term) and gāʾal (kinsman-redeemer act, to act as family-protector who retrieves what was lost). YHWH acts as both ransomer and kinsman-redeemer. Miyyad ḥāzāq mimmennû (miyyad ḥāzāq mimmennû): "from the hand of one too strong for him" — Jacob/Israel could not free himself from Babylon's grip; the deliverance is entirely YHWH's initiative and power. Paul uses pādâ/apolytrōsis and gāʾal/exagorazō vocabulary directly for Christ's redemptive work (Rom 3:24; Gal 3:13).
31.12: Wĕbāʾû wĕrinnĕnû biḇmôt ṣiyyôn (wĕbāʾû wĕrinnĕnû biḇmôt ṣiyyôn): the pilgrimage song reaches its destination — Zion's heights. Wĕnāhărû ʾel-ṭûḇ YHWH (wĕnāhărû ʾel-ṭûḇ YHWH): the unusual verb nāhar (to flow, to stream — normally of rivers flowing toward the sea) pictures the people streaming toward YHWH's goodness as rivers flow; a radiant face (nāhar also means to shine/be radiant, Isa 60:5) captures the dual meaning. Wĕhāytâ napšām kĕgan rāweh (kĕgan rāweh): "their being will be like a well-watered garden" — the nephesh as the whole person, fully satisfied; Ps 23:1-3 uses the same imagery of YHWH as the shepherd who leads beside still waters and restores the nephesh.
31.13: Ûḇĕtûlâ tiśmaḥ bĕmāḥôl ûḇaḥûrîm ûzĕqēnîm yaḥdāw (ûḇĕtûlâ tiśmaḥ bĕmāḥôl): the communal celebration encompasses all generations — young women, young men, and elders rejoice together. Wĕhāpaḵtî ʾeḇlām lĕśāśôn (wĕhāpaḵtî ʾeḇlām lĕśāśôn): "I will turn their mourning into joy" — the Qal perfect of hāpaḵ (to overturn, reverse) is the same verb used for Sodom's overthrow (Gen 19:25); here divine reversal works positively. The consolation (niḥamtîm) and rejoicing in place of sorrow (śamaḥtîm miyyĕgônām) becomes the NT pattern of divine comfort throughout (2 Cor 1:3-4; Rev 21:4).
31.14: Wĕribbêtî nefesh hakkōhănîm dāšen (wĕribbêtî nefesh hakkōhănîm dāšen): the priests are filled with fatness (dāšen — rich abundance, the fat of offerings representing fullness of blessing). The priestly abundance is YHWH's own initiative (wĕribbêtî, I will satisfy them abundantly). Wĕʾammî ʾet-ṭûḇî yiśbāʿû (wĕʾammî ʾet-ṭûḇî yiśbāʿû): "my people will be satisfied with my goodness" — ṭôḇ (goodness) is the same word used in v12 ("flowing toward the goodness of YHWH"). The repetition creates an inclusion framing vv10-14 as a single portrait of covenantal abundance.
31.15: The most cited verse of this chapter outside the New Covenant oracle itself: qôl bĕrāmâ nišmaʿ nehi bĕḵî tamrûrîm Rāḥēl mĕḇakkâ ʿal-bānêhā (qôl bĕrāmâ nišmaʿ nehi). Rāmâ was near Bethlehem in Benjamin's territory, also the site where exiles were assembled before deportation (Jer 40:1). Rachel, the mother of Benjamin and Joseph (through whom came Ephraim and Manasseh) — the matriarch of the northern tribes — weeps for children who "are no more" (ʾênennû, a permanent-state negation). Mēʾănâ lĕhinnāḥēm (mēʾănâ lĕhinnāḥēm): she refuses comfort, because comfort would require acknowledging the reality of the loss as final. Matthew 2:18 cites this text for Herod's slaughter of the innocents, reading Rachel's weeping as a typological pattern: the same grief that accompanied Israel's exile accompanies the persecution of the Messiah in his infancy.
31.16: Minĕʿî qôlēḵ mibbekî wĕʿênayiḵ middimʿâ (minĕʿî qôlēḵ mibbekî): YHWH's response to Rachel's weeping is a command to stop — not because the grief is unjustified, but because it is now unnecessary. Yēš śākār lĭpĕʿullātēḵ (yēš śākār lĭpĕʿullātēḵ): "there is a reward/wages for your labor" — the labor (pĕʿullâ) of bearing and raising children has not been in vain; those children will return. Wĕšāḇû mēʾereṣ ʾôyēḇ (wĕšāḇû mēʾereṣ ʾôyēḇ): the return from the land of the enemy is the reversal that transforms the weeping into hope.
31.17: Wĕyēš-tiqwâ lĕʾaḥărîtēḵ (wĕyēš-tiqwâ lĕʾaḥărîtēḵ): "there is hope for your future" — the term ʾaḥărît (latter end, future) in the context of hope is a key restoration term in Jeremiah (29:11: "plans for a future and a hope"). The hope is not abstract optimism but grounded in YHWH's specific promise: wĕšāḇû ḇānîm ligĕḇûlām, "your children will return to their own territory." The land promise is inseparable from the restoration hope — it is a return to the concrete inheritance.
31.18: The lament of Ephraim (the northern kingdom as a whole): šāmôaʿ šāmaʿtî ʾEprayim mitnôdēd (šāmôaʿ šāmaʿtî ʾEprayim mitnôdēd): the doubled infinitive absolute šāmôaʿ šāmaʿtî emphasizes YHWH's attentive hearing. Yissartanî wāʾiwwāsēr kĕʿēgel lōʾ lummad (yissartanî wāʾiwwāsēr kĕʿēgel lōʾ lummad): "You disciplined me, and I received the discipline, like an untrained calf" — the calf metaphor for an undisciplined, stubborn people (Hos 4:16: "Israel is stubborn like a stubborn heifer"); Ephraim confesses that the exile was just discipline, not capricious cruelty. hăšîḇênî wĕʾāšûḇâ (hăšîḇênî wĕʾāšûḇâ): "Restore me and I will be restored" — the classic šûḇ vocabulary, but with a theologically important structure: the repentance/return can only happen if YHWH himself initiates it. Cf. Lam 5:21 ("Return us to yourself, O LORD, that we may return"). This is the OT expression of the Augustinian/Reformed insight that even repentance is a divine gift.
31.19: Kî ʾaḥărê šûḇî niḥamtî (kî ʾaḥărê šûḇî niḥamtî): "After I turned away I repented/was comforted" — niḥam (Niphal of nāḥam) can mean either to repent or to be comforted; here it is genuine remorse. Wĕʾaḥărê higgāḏĕʿî sāpaḵtî ʿal-yārēḵ (wĕʾaḥărê higgāḏĕʿî sāpaḵtî ʿal-yārēḵ): "after I understood, I struck my thigh in shame" — striking the thigh is a gesture of deep grief and horror at one's own behavior (Ezek 21:12). Bōštî wĕgam niklāmtî kî nāśāʾtî ḥerpat nĕʿûrāy (bōštî wĕgam niklāmtî): the shame vocabulary is twofold — bōšet (the internal state of shame) and kĕlimmâ (public disgrace/humiliation). The exile has exposed the failure of the nation's entire early history.
31.20: The most tender verse in Jeremiah — YHWH's internal monologue over wayward Ephraim: hăḇēn yaqîr lî ʾEprayim (hăḇēn yaqîr lî ʾEprayim): "Is Ephraim not my dear/precious son?" — yāqîr (Piel participle of yāqar, to be precious/honored) — the question expects "yes." ʾim yeled šaʿăšûʿîm (ʾim yeled šaʿăšûʿîm): "is he not my darling child?" — šaʿăšûʿ (child of delight, darling) appears in Prov 8:30-31 for Wisdom as YHWH's delight. midday dabberî bô zākōr ʾezkĕrennû ʿôd (midday dabberî bô zākōr ʾezkĕrennû): "Even when I speak against him I still remember him" — the remembering (zākar) is covenant memory, the same zākar that YHWH exercises in Exod 2:24 of his covenant with Abraham. ʿal-kēn hāmû mēʿay lô raḥēm ʾăraḥămennû (ʿal-kēn hāmû mēʿay lô raḥēm ʾăraḥămennû): "therefore my heart is deeply moved for him; I will surely have compassion on him" — hāmâ mēʿayim is lit. "my bowels/intestines roar/churn" — the most visceral expression of emotion in Hebrew, used also in 1 Kings 3:26 for the real mother's compassion for her child. raḥēm ʾăraḥămennû: doubled infinitive absolute for absolute certainty: "I will most certainly have compassion." The root riḥam is cognate with reḥem (womb) — this is the maternal compassion YHWH uses for himself in Isa 49:15 ("Can a mother forget her nursing child... even if she should forget, I will not forget you").
31.21: Haṣṣiḇî lāḵ ṣiyyunîm śîmî lāḵ tamrurîm (haṣṣiḇî lāḵ ṣiyyunîm): "Set up road markers for yourself, establish guideposts" — the feminine singular imperatives address virgin Israel. Ṣiyyûn (marker, signpost) and tamrûr (bitter marker, boundary post) are both terms for landmarks along a road; in the context of exile this means remembering the way home so the return route is known. Šîtî libbēḵ lammĕsillâ (šîtî libbēḵ lammĕsillâ): "set your heart on the highway" — the mĕsillâ is the raised causeway/highway, the main road. Memory of the way home and intentional heart-orientation toward return are the twin responsibilities of the exile.
31.22: The most debated verse in the chapter: kî-bārāʾ YHWH ḥădāšâ bāʾāreṣ nĕqēḇâ tĕsôḇēḇ gāḇer (kî-bārāʾ YHWH ḥădāšâ bāʾāreṣ nĕqēḇâ tĕsôḇēḇ gāḇer): "For YHWH has created a new thing on earth: a female will court a man." Bārāʾ (to create) is the verb of Genesis 1:1 — a genuinely unprecedented act. The interpretive options for nĕqēḇâ tĕsôḇēḇ gāḇer include: (a) the weakened Israel (female) will now seek YHWH/Messiah (male) in a reversal of the prior rejection; (b) the newly restored nation, whose strength has been restored, will no longer need to be pursued; (c) a proverbial statement about the power-reversal of restoration. Most likely the image follows the preceding "faithless daughter" address: instead of the faithless daughter wandering (hitnôdĕdâh, v22a), she turns and pursues — the restoration reverses the direction of wandering. The context of "something unprecedented" supports a reading where YHWH's transformative act means the covenant people will be so thoroughly renewed that the typical pattern (people fleeing God) is reversed.
31.23: ʾOd yōmĕrû ʾet-haddāḇār hazzeh bĕʾereṣ yĕhûdâ ûḇĕʿārāyw (ʾOd yōmĕrû): "they will again say this word in the land of Judah and in its cities" — the prophetic formula of blessing will be spoken again, implying a restored land, a restored community, and restored liturgical/social speech. Yĕḇārĕkĕḵā YHWH nĕwēh-ṣedeq har haqōdeš (nĕwēh-ṣedeq har haqōdeš): "home of righteousness, O holy mountain" — the restored Jerusalem's character is defined by ṣedeq (righteousness/justice), which ch31's new covenant will produce by inward transformation. Har haqōdeš (holy mountain) is the Zion theology vocabulary of Pss 2, 48, 87.
31.24: Wĕyāšĕḇû ḇâ yĕhûdâ wĕḵol-ʿārāyw yaḥdāw ʾikkārîm wĕnāsĕʿû bĕʿēder (ʾikkārîm wĕnāsĕʿû bĕʿēder): farmers (ʾikkārîm) and those who move with their flocks (nāsĕʿû bĕʿēder, lit. "those who journey with the flock") — the two primary livelihoods of ancient Judah, agricultural and pastoral. Their yaḥdāw (together, united) settlement signals the end of the disruptive migrations and forced removals of war. The normalcy of daily agricultural life is itself a form of restoration.
31.25: Kî hirwêtî nefesh ʿăyēpâ wĕḵol-nefesh dāʾăḇâ millēʾtî (kî hirwêtî nefesh ʿăyēpâ): "For I have satisfied the weary nefesh and replenished every faint nefesh." Nefesh is the whole person — the seat of appetite, longing, vitality. ʿĀyēpâ (weary, exhausted) and dāʾăḇâ (faint, languishing) describe the depleted state of exile; hirwêtî (Hiphil of rāwâ, to drink one's fill, to be saturated) and millēʾtî (I have filled, Piel of māléʾ) are the states of complete satisfaction. This is the experiential basis of the covenant renewal — YHWH first satisfies before commanding.
31.26: ʿAl-zōʾt hĕqîṣōtî wāʾerʾeh ûšĕnātî ʿārĕḇâ lî (ʿAl-zōʾt hĕqîṣōtî wāʾerʾeh ûšĕnātî ʿārĕḇâ lî): "At this I awoke and looked, and my sleep had been sweet to me." A remarkably intimate aside — the prophet awakens from the vision of vv1-25 (hayyôm hazeʾ). The sweetness of the sleep suggests the vision itself was a restorative gift. This brief interruption of the oracular material frames chs 30-31 as a written vision-collection (the "Book of Consolation") deliberately set apart from the surrounding material of judgment.
31.27: Hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm (Hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm): the eschatological formula marking a new oracle. Wĕzāraʿtî ʾet-bêt yiśrāʾēl wĕʾet-bêt yĕhûdâ zera ʾādām wĕzera bĕhēmâ (zera ʾādām wĕzera bĕhēmâ): "I will sow the house of Israel and the house of Judah with seed of humans and seed of animals" — the fertility curse of exile (depopulation, livestock death) is reversed with an agricultural image: YHWH as farmer sowing the land with human and animal populations. Both houses together (wĕʾet-bêt yiśrāʾēl wĕʾet-bêt yĕhûdâ) — the unification of north and south continues as a theme.
31.28: Wĕhāyâ kaʾăšer šāqadtî ʿălêhem linĕtôš ûlintôṣ ûlĕhaʾărōš ûlĕhaʾăḇîd ûlĕhārēaʿ kēn ʾešqōd ʿălêhem liḇnôt wĕliṭṭôaʿ (kaʾăšer šāqadtî ʿălêhem linĕtôš): the šāqad wordplay reappears from 1:12 — YHWH "watches over" his word to perform it; there he was watching to destroy, now to build. The six infinitives of judgment from 1:10 (linĕtôš ûlintôṣ ûlĕhaʾărōš ûlĕhaʾăḇîd ûlĕhārēaʿ) are now answered with the two infinitives of restoration (liḇnôt wĕliṭṭôaʿ). The book's structural symmetry: what was announced in the inaugural call (1:10) is now being fulfilled in the reverse direction.
31.29: Bayyāmîm hāhēm lōʾ-yōmĕrû ʿôd ʾāḇôt ʾāḵĕlû ḇōser ûšinnê ḇānîm tiqhênā (ʾāḇôt ʾāḵĕlû ḇōser ûšinnê ḇānîm tiqhênā): the sour-grapes proverb also appears in Ezekiel 18:2, where it is similarly rejected. The proverb expresses the complaint of the generation experiencing exile — they are suffering for their ancestors' sins. The rejection of this proverb does not deny intergenerational consequence but announces a new principle of individual accountability in the coming age.
31.30: Kî ʾim-ʾîš bĕʿăwōnô yāmût (kî ʾim-ʾîš bĕʿăwōnô yāmût): "But each person will die for their own iniquity (ʿāwôn)." This is the principle of direct accountability that replaces the intergenerational blame-shifting. The phrase kol-hāʾādām hāʾōḵēl habbōser tiqhênā šinnāyw ("every person who eats sour grapes — their own teeth will be set on edge") applies the proverb to the individual: the consequence is personal to the one who commits the wrong. This prepares for the new covenant's internalized knowledge (v34) — individual relationship with YHWH means individual accountability.
31.32: The contrast clause: lōʾ kabbĕrît ʾăšer kāratî ʾet-ʾăḇôtām (lōʾ kabbĕrît ʾăšer kāratî): "not like the covenant I made with their fathers" — the new covenant is defined first negatively. Bĕyôm heḥĕzîqî bĕyādām lĕhôṣîʾām mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim (bĕyôm heḥĕzîqî bĕyādām): "on the day I took them by the hand to lead them out of Egypt" — the intimacy of the exodus relationship, YHWH holding their hand, contrasts sharply with what follows. ʾăšer-hēmmâ hēpĕrû ʾet-bĕrîtî (ʾăšer-hēmmâ hēpĕrû): "a covenant they broke" — pārar (to break, to annul) is the technical term for covenant violation (cf. Isa 24:5; Ps 55:21). Wĕʾānōḵî bāʿaltî bām (wĕʾānōḵî bāʿaltî bām): "even though I was their husband" — baʿal as husband/master; the marriage metaphor for the Sinai covenant (cf. Jer 2:2; Hos 2:14-20). The covenant's failure is entirely Israel's — YHWH fulfilled his husband-obligations; they were the unfaithful partner.
31.33: The heart of the new covenant: kî zōʾt habbĕrît ʾăšer ʾeḵrōt ʾet-bêt yiśrāʾēl ʾaḥărê hayyāmîm hāhēm nĕʾum-YHWH nātattî ʾet-tôrātî bĕqirbām wĕʿal-libbām ʾektĕḇennâ: the covenant is defined by two parallel verbs: nātattî (I will give/put, Qal perfect of nātan) and ʾektĕḇennâ (I will write it, Qal imperfect of kātaḇ with 3fs suffix). Nātattî ʾet-tôrātî bĕqirbām (bĕqirbām): "I will put my tôrâ within them" — qereb (inward parts, inner being) is the seat of thought and intention. Wĕʿal-libbām ʾektĕḇennâ (ʿal-libbām): "and on their heart I will write it" — the lēḇ/heart is the organ of will, understanding, and moral disposition in Hebrew anthropology. The contrast with Sinai is absolute: the stone tablets (Exod 24:12; 31:18) are replaced by the heart-tablet. The Torah is not abolished but relocated — from external code to internal disposition. Ezekiel 36:26-27 provides the mechanism: "I will give you a new heart... I will put my Spirit within you." Hebrews 8:8-12 and 10:16 cite Jer 31:33 as the definitive OT anticipation of what Christ accomplishes. Wĕhāyîtî lāhem lēʾlōhîm wĕhēmmâ yihyû-lî lĕʿām (wĕhāyîtî lāhem lēʾlōhîm): the covenant formula in its fullest, unconditional form — no "if" conditions, no conditional verb.
31.34: Wĕlōʾ yĕlammĕdû ʿôd ʾîš ʾet-rēʿēhû wĕʾîš ʾet-ʾāḥîw lēʾmōr dĕʿû ʾet-YHWH (lēʾmōr dĕʿû ʾet-YHWH): the mediated knowledge of YHWH that characterizes the whole prophetic office — prophets calling Israel to "know YHWH" — becomes unnecessary. Kî-ḵullām yēdĕʿû ʾôtî lĕmiqqĕṭannām wĕʿad-gĕdôlām (kî-ḵullām yēdĕʿû ʾôtî): "for they will all know me, from the least to the greatest" — kol (all) and the universal range (lĕmiqqĕṭannām wĕʿad-gĕdôlām, from the smallest to the greatest) eliminate all exceptions. The yādaʿ here is the relational, covenantal knowing of 1:5 — not merely cognitive. Kî ʾeslaḥ laʿăwōnām ûlĕḥaṭṭāʾtām lōʾ ʾezkār-ʿôd (ûlĕḥaṭṭāʾtām lōʾ ʾezkār-ʿôd): "I will forgive their iniquity and remember their sin no more" — the double negation (lōʾ... ʿôd, not... again) is absolute. The zākar (remembering) that YHWH exercises in covenant memory is here applied negatively to sin: YHWH will not bring the record of sin back into covenant relationship. Hebrews 10:17 cites this as the definitive proof that the new covenant's sacrifice (Christ's) achieved what the repeated Levitical sacrifices could not — permanent forgiveness.
31.35: Kōh ʾāmar YHWH nōtēn šemeš lĕʾôr yômām ḥuqqôt yārēaḥ wĕḵôḵāḇîm lĕʾôr lāylâ (nōtēn šemeš lĕʾôr yômām): the two cosmic guarantees — sun for daytime light, fixed order of moon and stars for nighttime light — are the standards by which the continuation of Israel's national existence is measured. Ḥuqqôt yārēaḥ wĕḵôḵāḇîm (the fixed orders/decrees of moon and stars) — ḥuqqôt (statutes, fixed orders) transfers the legal covenant vocabulary to the cosmic realm. Rōgaʿ hayyām wayyehĕmû gallāyw YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt šĕmô (rōgaʿ hayyām wayyehĕmû gallāyw): "who stirs up the sea so that its waves roar" — the cosmic power language establishes YHWH's authority to make such a guarantee.
31.36: ʾIm-yāmûšû haḥuqqîm hāʾēlleh millĕpānay nĕʾum-YHWH gam-zeraʿ yiśrāʾēl yišbĕtû mihyôt-gôy lĕpānay kol-hayyāmîm (ʾIm-yāmûšû haḥuqqîm hāʾēlleh millĕpānay): the conditional is deliberately impossible — yāmûšû (Qal imperfect of mûš, to depart, to be removed) applied to the cosmic fixed orders. The guarantee of Israel's continuation as a covenant people is as secure as the daily sunrise. Gam-zeraʿ yiśrāʾēl yišbĕtû mihyôt-gôy lĕpānay kol-hayyāmîm: they would cease to be a nation before YHWH only if the cosmos ceased to function — an impossibility. Paul's argument in Rom 11:1-2 ("Has God rejected his people? By no means!") stands on exactly this kind of irrevocable promise.
31.37: Kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾim-yimmaddû šāmayim millĕmaʿlâ wĕyēḥāqĕrû môsĕdê-ʾāreṣ lĕmāṭṭâ gam-ʾănî ʾemʾas bĕḵol-zeraʿ yiśrāʾēl (ʾim-yimmaddû šāmayim millĕmaʿlâ wĕyēḥāqĕrû môsĕdê-ʾāreṣ lĕmāṭṭâ): two impossibilities — the heavens above measured, the foundations of the earth below fully explored. Māʾas (to reject, despise) with bĕḵol-zeraʿ yiśrāʾēl ("all the offspring of Israel") — the totality is emphatic: not partial rejection, not conditional rejection, but the total rejection YHWH says cannot happen. ʿAl kol-ʾăšer ʿāśû (ʿal kol-ʾăšer ʿāśû): "for all they have done" — even accounting for the full record of their sin, rejection is impossible. This is grace that transcends performance.
31.38: Hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm nĕʾum-YHWH wĕniḇnĕtâ hāʿîr lĕ-YHWH mimmigdal Ḥananēʾl ʿad-šaʿar happinnâ (mimmigdal Ḥananēʾl ʿad-šaʿar happinnâ): the restored city's boundaries are traced beginning at the Tower of Hananeel (NE corner of the city, Neh 3:1; 12:39; Zech 14:10) to the Corner Gate (NW, 2 Kings 14:13). The prophetic topography is precise and geographically verifiable — the promise is rooted in actual Jerusalem geography, not abstracted symbolism.
31.39: Wĕyāṣāʾ ʿôd qaw hamiddâ negdô ʿal-gibʿat Gārēḇ ûnāsaḇ Gōʿātâ (ʿal-gibʿat Gārēḇ ûnāsaḇ Gōʿātâ): the measuring line extends to Gareb Hill and turns toward Goah — locations not certainly identified, but probably in the west and SW of Jerusalem. The expanding perimeter suggests the restored city will be larger than its pre-exile footprint. The detailed boundary description functions as a title deed of promise, as concrete as the field purchase in ch32.
31.40: Wĕḵol-hāʿēmeq hapĕgārîm wĕhaddešen wĕḵol-haššĕdēmôt ʿad-naḥal qidrôn ʿad-pinnah šaʿar-hassûsîm mizrāḥâ qōdeš lĕ-YHWH (Wĕḵol-hāʿēmeq hapĕgārîm wĕhaddešen): the valley of dead bodies (pĕgārîm) and ashes (dešen) — the Hinnom Valley/Tophet, the site of child sacrifice and judgment (7:31-34; 19:6) — will be holy to YHWH. The most defiled space becomes consecrated space. The word qōdeš (holy) applied to what was the valley of slaughter is the ultimate expression of YHWH's restorative power: he does not avoid the defilement, he transforms it. Lōʾ-yinnātēš wĕlōʾ-yēhārēs ʿôd lĕʿôlām (lōʾ-yinnātēš wĕlōʾ-yēhārēs ʿôd lĕʿôlām): "it will never again be uprooted or demolished" — the six infinitives of judgment from 1:10 are permanently reversed with the two infinitives of building (v28). The city stands forever.

7.1: The superscription marks this as a new oracle unit — the davar YHWH (word of YHWH) formula distinguishes commissioned prophetic speech from the prophet's own words. The MKT renders it plainly as "This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD" to emphasize the divine commissioning rather than Jeremiah's own initiative.
7.2: ʿamad bĕšaʿar bêt YHWH — "stand at the gate of the LORD's house"; the temple gate was the highest-traffic public space in Jerusalem, making this a maximally public proclamation. The MKT follows MT in specifying "all you of Judah who come through these gates to worship" — the sermon is addressed to the worshipping public, not just the priesthood.
7.3: hêtîbû darkêkem — "reform your ways and your actions"; the Hebrew heitev (make good, reform) is an intensive hiphil: genuine transformation, not superficial adjustment. The MKT's "reform your ways and your actions" preserves the dual emphasis — both conduct (derekh, path/lifestyle) and deeds (maʿăśîm, concrete acts). The conditional structure follows: reform → remain in the land.
7.4: divrê hashsheqer — "deceptive words"; sheqer is falsehood, deception, fraud — the same root used for false oaths and false prophecy. The triple repetition "the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD!" mimics what the crowd was saying and ridicules it simultaneously. The MKT preserves the triple repetition because it is structurally essential to understanding what YHWH is calling false.
7.5: The conditional clause of the temple-sermon: kî ʾim heytîb teyṭîbû — an infinitive absolute construction (literally "if in reforming you truly reform") that the MKT renders "if you truly reform." The infinitive absolute with a finite verb is a Hebrew device for emphasis; "truly" captures this better than a literal translation that would seem redundant.
7.6: gēr, yātôm, ʾalmānâ — the triad of the vulnerable: foreigner, fatherless, widow. This triad recurs throughout the OT as the paradigmatic category of the socially unprotected. "Shed innocent blood" (dām nāqî) refers to judicial murder; "follow other gods" is the religious-covenantal violation. The MKT preserves all three categories to show the breadth of the required reform.
7.7: The apodosis of the conditional temple-sermon: wĕšikantî ʾetkem — "I will let you live in this place." Šākan is the verb root of miškan (tabernacle/dwelling); YHWH promises continued dwelling-in-place if covenant obligations are met. The phrase "for ever and ever" (lĕmîn-ʿôlām wĕʿad-ʿôlām) is emphatic — the land-promise is indefinitely open, contingent on faithfulness.
7.8: divrê hashsheqer lĕvilti hoʿîl — "deceptive words that can do you no good"; lĕvilti hoʿîl is a common prophetic phrase for things that are utterly useless. The MKT renders it idiomatically — the point is that these words not only fail to help but actively harm those who trust in them. The temple-security slogan is not merely inert but destructive.
7.9: The covenant-violation catalogue: steal (gānōb), murder (rāṣaḥ), commit adultery (nāʾap), swear falsely (šābaʿ laššeqer), sacrifice to Baal, follow other gods. This is a near-echo of the Decalogue violations — six of the Ten Commandments broken by the worshipping community. The MKT preserves the staccato list to convey the cumulative effect of itemized covenant-breaking.
7.10: nilṣalnû — "we are safe"; from nāṣal, to be rescued/delivered. The crowd enters the temple guilty of Decalogue violations and immediately declares themselves delivered. The MKT's "We are safe!" captures the presumptuous self-assurance better than "we are delivered" — it is not a theological claim but a smug dismissal of the need for repentance.
7.11: mĕʿārat pārîṣîm — "den of robbers"; pārîṣîm are violent men, robbers, but specifically violent ones who use force (pāraṣ, to break through). This verse is directly cited by Jesus in the Temple Cleansing (Matt 21:13; Mark 11:17; Luke 19:46). The MKT follows the traditional rendering because it is the phrase preserved in the NT citation; "den of violent robbers" would be technically more precise but would obscure the biblical cross-reference.
7.12: Šîlōh — Shiloh was the first permanent sanctuary site of the tabernacle (Josh 18:1), destroyed by the Philistines ca. 1050 BCE (1 Sam 4). YHWH cites his own destruction of Shiloh as precedent for what he can and will do to Jerusalem. The MKT renders "my place that was in Shiloh" to preserve the possessive — YHWH acknowledges Shiloh was his; that did not protect it.
7.13: haškēm wĕdabbēr — "I spoke again and again"; the idiomatic Hebrew for speaking persistently and early (hiškim = to rise early, hence to act persistently). The MKT renders it "I spoke to you again and again" rather than the more literal "rising early to speak" because the idiom's force is persistency, not the time of day.
7.14: The threat parallels the Shiloh precedent: as Shiloh was destroyed, so will this temple. The phrase ʾăšer nātattî lākem wĕlaʾăbôtêkem — "that I gave to you and your ancestors" — underlines that the gift of the temple does not make it inviolable; what YHWH gives, YHWH can take. The MKT preserves this emphasis by making "gave" explicit.
7.15: kol-zeraʿ ʾeprāyim — "the entire offspring of Ephraim"; the Northern Kingdom was "driven out" (gārašti) in the Assyrian deportation of 722 BCE. YHWH here says explicitly that he drove them out from his presence — the passive phrasing common in history-writing is corrected to active divine agency. The MKT preserves "I drove out" to maintain the theological claim.
7.16: ʾal-tittpallēl... wĕʾal-tippgaʿ-bî — "do not pray... do not press me with intercession"; this is one of the most startling commands in the prophets: YHWH forbids Jeremiah to intercede. Pāgaʿ means to encounter, meet, intercede; the MKT's "do not press me" captures the picture of Jeremiah's characteristic intercessory intensity. The prohibition signals that judgment has become irrevocable.
7.17: A rhetorical question directed at Jeremiah: "Do you not see?" (hărōʾeh ʾattā). YHWH points Jeremiah to the visible idolatry in public spaces as evidence for the judgment he has just pronounced. The MKT's "towns of Judah and in the streets of Jerusalem" follows MT in specifying both urban and rural scope of the apostate worship.
7.18: meleʾket haššāmayim — "queen of heaven"; the Hebrew phrase (with meleʾket = work/labor/queen, a disputed reading) refers to the Mesopotamian goddess Ishtar/Inanna or the Canaanite Asherah. The MKT renders "queen of heaven" — the majority reading that fits the context of family-organized cultic baking of cakes in the goddess's image.
7.19: YHWH's rhetorical response: is it me they are provoking (hăkʿăsunî)? The answer is no — the idolaters only harm themselves. The reflexive dimension of sin is captured in "bringing shame on themselves" (lĕmāʿan bōšet pĕnêhem). The MKT preserves this by asking "Is it not themselves they are provoking?" — YHWH is not threatened by idol-worship; the worshippers are self-destructing.
7.20: ʾap YHWH wĕḥămātî — "My anger and my wrath"; the pairing of ʾap (nose/anger, the nose flares in anger) and ḥēmâ (burning wrath, heat) intensifies the divine emotion beyond ordinary anger. The MKT renders both: "my anger and my wrath." The judgment is on humans, animals, trees, and ground — a reversal of creation's fruitfulness.
7.21: sĕpû ʿôlôtêkem ʿal-zivḥêkem — "add your burnt offerings to your other sacrifices and eat the meat"; this is ironic permission: since you treat the temple as a guarantor of safety regardless of behavior, go ahead and eat the burnt offerings (normally reserved entirely for God). The MKT preserves the sarcasm by rendering "Go ahead — add your burnt offerings."
7.22: lō-dibbartî... ʿal-ʾōlôt — "I did not speak with them... about burnt offerings and sacrifices"; this difficult verse argues that the Exodus command's prime content was covenantal relationship and obedience, not sacrificial detail. The MKT follows the MT: "I did not speak or give them commands about burnt offerings." The emphasis is not that the sacrificial law was never given, but that it was subordinate to the covenantal relationship. Verse 23 immediately supplies what the prime command actually was.
7.23: šimʿû bĕqôlî — "Obey my voice" or "listen to my voice"; the MKT renders "Obey me" — the prime command given at Sinai is not a list of sacrifices but a relational summons to hearing-obedience. "I will be your God, you will be my people" (lĕʿam / lēʾlōhîm) is the covenant formula; "walk in all the ways I command you" follows from the relational priority.
7.24: bĕsĕrîrût libbām hārāʿ — "the stubbornness of their evil hearts"; šĕrîrût denotes hardness, stubbornness — the intractable resistance of the will. This phrase recurs as one of Jeremiah's signature diagnostic terms for Israel's condition. The MKT translates "the stubbornness of their evil hearts" rather than "the imagination of their evil hearts" (KJV) because šĕrîrût is primarily about hardness/stubbornness, not imagination.
7.25: haškēm wĕšālôaḥ — "persistently sent"; another idiom using the "rising early" root for persistent action. The MKT renders "I have persistently sent all my servants the prophets" — the emphasis is on the relentless, patient divine effort to communicate before judgment. The phrase "again and again" (repeated from v.13) underlines the long record of ignored warnings.
7.26: qaššû ʾet-ʿarpām — "they were stubborn and stiff-necked"; qāšâ ʿōrep (hard of neck) is the classic stiff-necked idiom for resistance to divine correction. The MKT renders both the "stubborn" and "stiff-necked" (rather than collapsing them into one English word) to preserve the rhetorical doubling. "They behaved even worse than their ancestors" — the downward spiral of apostasy through the generations.
7.27: YHWH addresses Jeremiah directly: you will preach and they will not listen — a pre-announcement of failure. This functions as prophetic call-within-call: Jeremiah is told in advance that his ministry will be formally rejected. The MKT preserves the direct address ("You will speak... you will call") to maintain the personal commissioning of the prophet.
7.28: ʾemûnâ ʾābĕdâ — "Truth has vanished"; ʾĕmûnâ is faithfulness, reliability, truth as a practiced commitment (not merely propositional accuracy). The loss of ʾĕmûnâ is the social-ethical collapse of covenant. The MKT renders "Truth has vanished from their lips" — ʾĕmûnâ is more than truth-telling; it is the fabric of communal trustworthiness that covenant loyalty creates.
7.29: gĕzî nizrēk — "Shave off your hair and throw it away"; cutting off one's hair (nēzer = consecrated hair/crown, here in the sense of hair as one's "crown") as a mourning gesture. The MKT renders "Shave off your hair" — the mourning rite signals a lament over the generation YHWH has rejected and abandoned.
7.30: gillûlêhem — "their detestable idols"; gillûlîm is a contemptuous term for idols (possibly related to the word for dung-pellets or logs). The MKT uses "detestable idols" — the term is deliberately derogatory, YHWH refusing to name the idols with their own titles. Installing them babbayit ʾăšer-niqrāʾ šĕmî ʿālāw (in the house on which my name is called) is the ultimate desecration.
7.31: lō-tsiwwîtî — "something I never commanded"; the explicit divine repudiation of child sacrifice. Bāmôt hattōpet bĕgêʾ ben-hinnōm — the shrines of Topheth in the valley of Ben Hinnom (the geographic origin of Gehenna). YHWH states clearly that child sacrifice was never in the covenant: "something I never commanded, nor did it enter my mind." The MKT preserves the first-person emphatic (lō-tsiwwîtî) to make the repudiation direct and unambiguous.
7.32: gêʾ hahărēgâ — "Valley of Slaughter"; the Valley of Ben Hinnom where children were burned will be renamed. The MKT preserves the ominous name "Valley of Slaughter" — Topheth (place of burning) becomes a mass burial site. This verse begins the judgment-description that the temple sermon has been building toward.
7.33: hayyôm lāzāyit — "food for the birds of the sky and the wild animals"; unburied corpses are the ultimate covenant curse (Deut 28:26). The phrase "no one will drive them away" means even the minimal dignity of shooing away scavengers will be absent. The MKT follows MT precisely here because the covenant-curse language needs no softening.
7.34: qôl śāśôn wĕqôl śimḥâ qôl ḥātān wĕqôl kallâ — "the sounds of joy and gladness, the voices of the bridegroom and the bride"; the four-fold joy-formula that will be silenced. This phrase also appears in 16:9; 25:10; 33:11 (where its restoration signals new-covenant renewal). The MKT preserves all four terms: joy, gladness, bridegroom, bride — the complete vocabulary of wedding celebration ended.

8.1: The desecration of the bones of the ruling classes — kings, officials, priests, prophets, and people. The exposure of bones (yôṣîʾû ʾet-ʿaṣmôt) was the ultimate post-mortem humiliation in the ancient world; death offered no security from divine judgment. The MKT lists all five categories to show the comprehensive scope: no class of Judahite leadership is exempt.
8.2: ʾăšer ʾăhēbûm ûʾăšer ʿăbādûm — "which this people loved, served, followed, consulted, and worshipped"; the five-fold description of the sun/moon/stars cult mirrors the five classes of victims in v.1. The MKT preserves all five verbs because together they describe the totality of idolatrous devotion — the same comprehensive energy that should have been directed toward YHWH. "They will not be gathered up or buried" — the non-burial is the ironic punishment for those who worshipped celestial bodies.
8.3: yibḥar māwet mēḥayyîm — "will prefer death to life"; an inversion of the covenant's life/death choice (Deut 30:19). The survivors of the exile will be in conditions so wretched that death is preferable to survival. The MKT renders the future tense strongly: "will prefer death to life" — the covenant inversion is complete when life becomes intolerable.
8.4: hăyippōl wĕlōʾ yāqûm — "When people fall, don't they get back up?"; a wisdom observation used as the ground for the rhetorical indictment. The MKT renders the wisdom saying as a question (hă- interrogative), as MT indicates. Natural behavior and common sense confirm the abnormality of Judah's persistent non-repentance.
8.5: mĕšûbâ nitsaḥat — "perpetual backsliding"; mĕšûbâ is apostasy, turning-away; the nitsaḥat (perpetual, enduring) intensifies it. The MKT renders "turned away in perpetual backsliding" — the repetitiveness of the apostasy is as much the indictment as the apostasy itself. They cling to deception (heḥĕzîqû bĕmirmâ) and refuse to return.
8.6: ʾên-ʾîš niḥam ʿal-rāʿātô — "no one repents of his wickedness"; nāḥam (repent, relent) is YHWH's own word for emotional turning. Here it is used for Israel's required turning — no one engages in the self-examination that would lead to repentance. The MKT's "No one repents of his wickedness and asks, 'What have I done?'" preserves the introspective question that nobody is asking.
8.7: ḥăsîdâ... yādĕʿâ mōwʿădêhā — "Even the stork in the sky knows when to migrate"; ḥăsîdâ (stork; its root ḥesed means loyal love — the bird was considered loyal to its young). The migration analogy: birds instinctively follow their appointed times (môʿēd) — but Israel does not follow YHWH's appointed covenant. The MKT preserves the four birds (stork, turtledove, swallow, crane) as a rhetorical accumulation.
8.8: ʿēt šeqer sōpĕrîm — "the lying pen of the scribes has turned it into a lie"; ʿēt is a scribal pen or stylus; šeqer is falsehood/deception. The scribal class that handles the Torah has itself become the instrument of falsification. The MKT renders "the lying pen of the scribes has turned it into a lie" — the tôrat YHWH that they claim to possess has been adulterated by their scribal handling.
8.9: ḥāpĕrû ḥăkāmîm — "The wise will be disgraced"; the ḥākāmîm (the wise ones, the sages) are specifically shamed because they claimed the Torah and were trusted with it. The MKT preserves the targeting of the wise class: "they have rejected the word of the LORD — so what wisdom is left in them?" Wisdom that rejects the word of YHWH is self-refuting.
8.10: The judgment on the prophets and priests who falsify: their property will be redistributed — wives to other men, fields to new owners. The phrase miqqāṭōn wĕʿad-gādôl (from the least to the greatest) applies the judgment comprehensively. The MKT follows MT in specifying both the prophets (nāvîʾ) and the priests (kōhēn) as greedy practitioners of the false-peace message.
8.11: šālôm šālôm wĕʾên šālôm — "Peace, peace, when there is no peace"; the doubled šālôm is the signature phrase of Jeremiah's critique of false prophecy — also appearing in 6:14. Šālôm denotes comprehensive well-being (health, wholeness, safety, prosperity) not merely absence of conflict. The MKT renders the doubling as "Peace, peace," — the repetition itself performs the falsity of the prophets' assurance.
8.12: hōbîšû kî tôʿēbâ ʿāśû — "Were they ashamed when they committed these detestable acts?" The answer: they could not even blush (wĕlikhˊlōm lōʾ yādĕʿû). Shame and blush (ḥāpar / kālam) are closely related — the capacity for moral shame has been lost. The MKT preserves both: "no shame... did not even know how to blush" — the double negation emphasizes complete moral insensibility.
8.13: ʾāsōp ʾăsîpēm — "I will take away their harvest"; an infinitive absolute construction emphasizing the thoroughness of the removal. The agricultural images (no grapes, no figs, withered leaves) reverse the covenant blessings of the land. The MKT follows MT: "no grapes on the vine, no figs on the fig tree, even the leaves will wither" — the complete reversal of land-prosperity.
8.14: The people's own lament-speech: "Let us flee to the fortified cities" — a despairing acceptance that the judgment announced in v.13 is arriving. The phrase YHWH ʾĕlōhênû hĕdîmānû — "the LORD our God has condemned us" — shows some recognition of YHWH's agency. The MKT renders "condemned us" (rather than "silenced us") following the root dāmam in the sense of bringing to an end.
8.15: qāwwîtā lĕšālôm wĕʾên ṭôb — "We hoped for peace, but no good has come"; the ironic reversal of the false prophets' promises. The people had been told šālôm would come; instead, there is terror (beʿātâ). The MKT preserves the pair "peace/healing" from the false-prophecy vocabulary and "good/terror" as what actually arrived.
8.16: miDdan nišmaʿ pinḥat sûsāyw — "From Dan the snorting of their warhorses is heard"; Dan was the northernmost city; Babylon's armies approach from the north. Pinḥat (snorting, loud neighing) creates an onomatopoetic effect. The MKT renders "snorting... stallions' neighing" to capture the two different horse-sounds in the Hebrew.
8.17: nĕḥāšîm tsipʿōnîm — "poisonous snakes... vipers that cannot be charmed"; a metaphor for the Babylonian army. Ṣipʿōnî is likely the horned viper; the incantation-resistant quality ("cannot be charmed") means no prophetic intervention will deflect this judgment. The MKT preserves "vipers that cannot be charmed" — the snake-charming image underlines that Jeremiah's intercession is futile (cf. 7:16).
8.18: mabligîtî ʿālê yāgôn — "My sorrow is beyond healing"; the prophet's first-person lament begins. Bālag means to comfort, restore strength; the negated form suggests there is no comfort available. The MKT renders "My sorrow is beyond healing; my heart is faint within me" — identifying these as Jeremiah's own words of empathetic suffering before YHWH's speech resumes.
8.19: hăʾên YHWH bĕṣiyyôn — "Is the LORD not in Zion?" The people's cry in exile is interrupted by YHWH's counter-question: "Why have they provoked me with their idols?" The MKT preserves this dramatic interruption — the people ask one question, YHWH answers with a different one. "Her King no longer there?" presupposes YHWH as Zion's true king (malkāh).
8.20: ʿābar qātsîr kālâ qāyits — "The harvest is over, the summer has ended — and we are still not saved"; an agricultural calendar metaphor: the grain harvest (spring) and summer fruit harvest have passed; the year's natural opportunities for survival have closed, yet the salvation the people waited for has not come. The MKT preserves the agricultural imagery because it grounds the lament in the real experience of watching each season pass without relief.
8.21: ʿal-šeber bat-ʿammî — "I am broken over the brokenness of my people"; wordplay on šābar (break, shatter): the prophet is broken (nišbartî) because of the shattering of the people. The MKT renders "I am broken over the brokenness of my people" — the translation must preserve the echo of the same root to show that the prophet's suffering is a participation in the people's suffering.
8.22: hăṣōrî ʾên bĕgilʿād — "Is there no balm in Gilead?" Ṣōrî is the resin of the mastic tree, an ancient medicinal substance; Gilead was famous as its source. The rhetorical question assumes the answer is yes, there is balm in Gilead — but the wound of Israel goes unhealed anyway. The MKT preserves the rhetorical question rather than smoothing it into a statement: "Is there no physician there?" — the problem is not lack of remedy but refusal to receive it.

9.1: mî-yittēn rōʾšî mayim — "Oh that my head were a spring of water"; the optative-wish formula (mî yittēn, "who will give?" = "would that...") expresses Jeremiah's grief-wish. The weeping-prophet motif is central to his identity — his tears are not weakness but covenantal mourning. The MKT preserves "weep day and night for the slain of my dear people" — the word ḥălāl (slain, pierced) emphasizes violent death.
9.2: ûminnāhem ʾēlek — "so that I could leave my people and go away from them"; Jeremiah's second wish: to escape from the people he is condemned to love. Gō mallôn ʾōrḥîm (a lodging place for travelers) in the wilderness — the wish for anonymity and distance. The MKT preserves the tension: Jeremiah does not hate his people, but the weight of their sin is unbearable to the prophet who must live among them.
9.3: qāšĕtû ʾet-lĕšônām qĕšĕtām — "They have bent their tongue like a bow to shoot falsehood"; the tongue-as-weapon metaphor: lāšôn (tongue) bent like a qešet (bow), shooting šeqer (falsehood/lies). The MKT preserves the imagery: "grown powerful in the land but not by truth." Wĕlōʾ leʾĕmûnâ gābarû bāʾārets — they have prevailed in the land but not through faithfulness.
9.4: kol-ʾāḥ ʿāqōb yaʿqōb — "every brother is a schemer"; this is a pun on the name Jacob (yaʿăqōb, from ʿāqēb, heel) — Jacob the heel-grasper/supplanter. The MKT renders "every brother is a schemer" rather than "every brother will act like Jacob" because the wordplay requires a footnote to work in English; the MKT chose the semantic content over preserving the untranslatable pun. Ideally a reader would know the Jacob connection.
9.5: limmĕdû lĕšônām dabber šeqer — "They have trained their tongue to lie"; limmēd (train, teach) implies deliberate practice — lying has become a cultivated skill. The phrase "they exhaust themselves committing iniquity" (nilʾû bĕhašĕkīl) portrays sin as hard work that they devote serious effort to. The MKT preserves this irony: the energy that should go toward YHWH goes toward deliberate deception.
9.6: bĕtôk mirmâ yāšābû — "You live in the midst of deception"; YHWH addresses Jeremiah directly about the people. Mirmâ (deception, treachery) is the ambient environment — they inhabit deception the way one inhabits a house. "Through their deceit they refuse to know me, declares the LORD" — knowledge of YHWH and deception are mutually exclusive; the commitment to falsehood necessarily drives out knowledge of the God of truth.
9.7: tsāraptîm ûbĕḥantîm — "I will refine them and test them"; the metallurgical purification metaphor: tsārap (smelt, refine) and bāḥan (test, assay) are the verbs for removing dross from metal. The rhetorical question "what else can I do with my sinful people?" (ʾêk ʾeʿĕśeh mippĕnê bat-ʿammî) conveys divine constrained purpose: the refining is YHWH's response to apostasy that cannot be ignored.
9.8: ḥēts šāḥûṭ lĕšônām — "Their tongue is a lethal arrow that shoots out deceit"; the tongue-bow metaphor of v.3 is here intensified: the tongue is itself the arrow (ḥēts šāḥûṭ, a polished/sharpened arrow). The MKT renders "with their mouths they speak peace to their neighbors, but in their hearts they set a trap." The gap between spoken peace (šālôm) and planned ambush is the essence of social duplicity.
9.9: The covenant-lawsuit formula: "Should I not punish them for all this? Should I not take vengeance on such a nation?" The rhetorical double question (hăʾal-ʾēlleh lōʾ-ʾepqōd) expects the answer yes. Pāqad (punish, visit, attend to) is YHWH's covenant-enforcement verb — the same root used in promises of blessing. The MKT preserves both questions to maintain the juridical deliberation.
9.10: qînâ ûnĕhî — "weeping and wailing" and "lamentation" (nĕhî); the mourning vocabulary for the desolated mountains. The divine lament ("I will take up weeping" — YHWH is the subject) shows the peculiar Jeremianic characteristic: YHWH himself mourns for the land he is judging. The MKT preserves YHWH as the grieving subject to maintain this theological tension.
9.11: wĕnātattî ʾet-yĕrûšālaim lĕʿiyyîm — "I will make Jerusalem a heap of rubble"; ʿî (rubble-mound, desolate ruin) — the word used for the remnant-mounds of destroyed cities. "A haunt of jackals" (mĕʿôn tannîm): jackals inhabit ruins in OT imagery. The MKT preserves both images — rubble-mound and jackal-haunt — as the two paired symbols of total desolation.
9.12: A wisdom-challenge: "Who is wise enough to understand this? Who has received the word from the LORD and can explain it?" The two questions distinguish human wisdom from prophetic revelation. The MKT preserves the distinction: understanding (bîn) vs. having received the word (ʾăšer dibbartî YHWH ʾēlāyw). The answer (from YHWH in vv.13-16) requires the latter, not the former.
9.13: YHWH's answer to the wisdom-challenge: they abandoned his Torah. ʿāzĕbû ʾet-tôrātî — "they have abandoned my law that I placed before them"; nātan lĕpānêhem (placed before them) — the law was openly displayed/available, not hidden. The MKT follows MT in rendering Torah as "law" — the full Mosaic instruction-set that they comprehensively abandoned.
9.14: ʾaḥărê haShĕbāʿalîm — "went after the Baals"; the plural bĕʿālîm denotes the various local Baal-manifestations worshipped at different shrines. "Just as their ancestors had taught them" (kĕʾăšer limmĕdûm ʾăbôtām) — the apostasy is generationally transmitted; it is not mere individual deviation but a family tradition of idolatry. The MKT preserves the plural Baals and the ancestral transmission.
9.15: laʿănâ — "wormwood"; a bitter plant (Artemisia) used as the OT's metaphor for bitter suffering (Lam 3:15, 19). "Poisoned water" (mê rōʾš) — rōʾš is a poisonous plant (likely hemlock or a similar herb). The combination of bitter food and poisoned drink represents total inversion of covenant blessing (land of milk and honey). The MKT follows standard rendering of these paired covenant-curse images.
9.16: ûbizzîtîm bĕtôk haggôyim — "I will scatter them among nations"; zārâ (scatter, winnow — the agricultural image of grain chaff blown away) is the diaspora verb. The pursuit-by-sword until destroyed is the military covenant curse (Deut 28:41-65). The MKT preserves the diaspora as divine act: "I will scatter... I will pursue."
9.17: hammĕqônĕnôt — "the professional mourning women"; qînâ (lament, dirge) + participle = women skilled in the art of public mourning. Professional mourners were a recognized occupational class in the ancient world. The MKT renders "professional mourning women" to clarify this was not amateur grief but skilled lament-performance. "The most skilled" (hāḥăkāmôt) — even the mourning requires expertise.
9.18: The professional mourners' commission: weep until eyes stream (tērednā dimʿâ ʿênênû) and eyelids overflow (yizzĕlû mayim ʿapʿappênû). The two different Hebrew words for eyes (ʿêyin, outer eye; ʿapʿappe, eyelid) indicate the totality of weeping. The MKT preserves both anatomical terms: "eyes... eyelids" — the total-body weeping for the total judgment.
9.19: The lament-cry from Zion: šuddadnû bōšnû meʾōd kî ʾāzabnû ʾārets — "We are devastated! We are utterly disgraced — because we have had to leave the land." The triple emphasis (devastated, disgraced, exiled) is the MT's way of conveying the magnitude of the loss. The MKT preserves all three expressions rather than collapsing them.
9.20: YHWH addresses the women directly: šimʿā nāšîm dĕbar YHWH — "women, hear the word of the LORD." The commission to teach daughters to mourn suggests the lament will outlast the immediate generation — it is something that must be institutionalized. The MKT preserves the direct female address as significant: the mourning women are given the prophetic commission to transmit the lament.
9.21: māwet ʿālâ bĕḥallônênû — "death has climbed through our windows"; Death is personified (māwet as a figure, possibly an echo of the Canaanite death-god Mot) entering houses through windows. "Cutting down children in the streets and young men in the public squares" — the harvest-metaphor for mass death: likrôt ʿôlāl meḥûts. The MKT preserves the personification of Death as an active agent.
9.22: wĕnāpal niblat hāʾādām kĕdōmen ʿal-pĕnê haśśādeh — "Human corpses will fall like manure on the open field"; the covenant-curse of unburied dead (Deut 28:26) in its starkest form. Dōmen (dung, manure) is a deliberately undignified comparison. The MKT preserves the bluntness — translation that softens "like manure" undermines the prophetic force.
9.23: ʾal-yithalēl ḥākām bĕḥokmātô — "Let not the wise boast in their wisdom, nor the mighty in their strength, nor the rich in their riches"; the three-fold anti-boast formula. Hālal (boast, praise) — the same root as hallel. The wisdom/strength/riches triad represents the three spheres of human self-sufficiency. The MKT preserves all three in parallel because their cumulative effect is essential — no domain of human competence is exempt from this rejection.
9.24: kî ʾim bĕzōʾt yithalēl hammishtallēaḥ haskēl wĕyādōaʿ ʾôtî — "let him who boasts boast in this: that he understands and knows me"; the only legitimate boast is YHWH-knowledge (daʿat YHWH). The three divine attributes: ḥesed (steadfast love/covenant loyalty), mišpāṭ (justice), ṣĕdāqâ (righteousness) — the same triad that defines YHWH's character throughout the OT. Paul cites this verse in 1 Cor 1:31 and 2 Cor 10:17: "let him who boasts, boast in the Lord." The MKT's rendering of the three attributes is deliberate: "steadfast love, justice, and righteousness in the earth."
9.25: kol-mûl bĕʿorlâ — "all who are only outwardly circumcised"; the category of the physically circumcised who are simultaneously uncircumcised (i.e., in heart). The MKT renders "all who are only outwardly circumcised" — paraphrasing the literally ambiguous kol-mûl bĕʿorlâ (all circumcised in/with foreskin). This verse sets up the spiritual-circumcision contrast with Paul's argument in Rom 2:28-29.
9.26: ʿărēl lēb — "uncircumcised in heart"; the phrase used for the nations and equally for Israel. The MKT renders "for all these nations are uncircumcised in the flesh, and all the house of Israel is uncircumcised in heart." The startling equivalence: Israel's heart-circumcision deficit is no better than Gentile physical uncircumcision. This verse is the OT basis for Paul's argument in Rom 2:28-29 (circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit).

1.1: The book opens with divrey Yirmeyahu (diḇrê yirmĕyāhû, words of Jeremiah) — not the more common prophetic formula dĕḇar YHWH (word of YHWH). This is deliberate: Jeremiah is both the human speaker and the divine conduit; the book will contain both first-person prophetic oracles and biographical narrative. The prophet's lineage is given: ben-Hilqiyyahu min-hakohanim — a priestly family from Anathoth (modern Anata, northeast of Jerusalem). Being a priest-prophet (like Ezekiel) shapes Jeremiah's concern for cultic purity and covenant fidelity throughout the book.
1.2: The call-narrative uses a standard historical introduction: asher haya debar-YHWH elav (ʾăšer hāyâ dĕḇar-YHWH ʾēlāw) — the relative clause in the third person, marking the transition from book-title to historical setting. The thirteenth year of Josiah (ca. 627 BCE) places Jeremiah's call just after Josiah's accession but before the great reform of 621 BCE (2 Kings 22-23). Jeremiah's ministry thus preceded and likely informed the reform.
1.3: The superscription spans nearly half a century of ministry: Josiah (640-609), Jehoiakim (609-598), Zedekiah (598-586), ending with the Babylonian exile (galut Yerushalayim, gālût yĕrûšālayim). The phrase bĕḥōdeš haḥămîšî (in the fifth month) marks the destruction of the temple (586 BCE; 2 Kings 25:8-10). No other OT prophet's ministry is documented across so many successive crises.
1.4: The messenger formula wayhi davar-YHWH elay lemor (wayhî dĕḇar-YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr) — literally "and it came to pass, the word of YHWH to me, saying" — is the standard first-person prophetic reception formula. The word (dāḇār) arrives as an event with agency, not merely as an intellectual communication; the repeated use of this formula across the book signals each new oracle unit.
1.5: Beterem etsorcha babeten yadatikha (bĕṭerem ʾeṣōrkĕkā babbéṭen yĕdaʿtîkā): Before I formed you in the womb I knew you. Yādaʿtî is the Qal perfect of yādaʿ — the Bible's word for the most intimate knowing, including sexual union (Gen 4:1, yādaʿ ʾet-ḥawwâ). This is not intellectual foreknowledge but covenantal election. Qiddaštîkā (qiddaštîkā) — Piel perfect of qādaš (to set apart as holy); the Piel intensifies: a comprehensive, decisive consecration. Nāḇîʾ laggôyim — prophet to the nations: the breadth of the commission is global from the start, even though Jeremiah will minister primarily in Jerusalem.
1.6: Jeremiah's protest follows the prophetic convention of inadequacy (hesitation topos): lo yādaʿtî dabbēr — I do not know how to speak; the verb yādaʿ (know) mirrors YHWH's election-knowing in v5 — Jeremiah claims not-knowing in precisely the area YHWH knows him for. Naʿar ʾānōkî — I am only a youth/child; naʿar can mean boy, servant, or young man (used of Joseph at 17, Gen 37:2; of Solomon as king, 1 Kings 3:7). The objection is the same structure as Moses' speech-disability protest (Exod 4:10) and Gideon's clan-weakness protest (Judg 6:15).
1.7: Al-tōmar naʿar ʾānōkî — Do not say, I am a youth. YHWH's refusal is grammatically a prohibition (jussive al + imperfect): the objection is closed. The counter-commission is twofold: el-kol-asher ešlāḥăkā telēk (to everyone I send you, you shall go) and veet kol-asher atzavvĕkā tĕdabbēr (everything I command you, you shall speak). The authority is transferred entirely to the sender: Jeremiah's adequacy is irrelevant because his words are YHWH's commands.
1.8: Al-tîrāʾ mippĕnêhem — do not be afraid of their faces. The construction mippĕnêhem (from their faces) is more visceral than our translation "of them" — it is the overwhelming human presence that produces fear. The antidote is kî-ittĕkā ʾănî lĕhaṣṣîlekā — for I am with you to deliver you; the divine presence (immanuel-pattern) grounds the command not to fear. This phrase forms an inclusio with v19 (same words closing the call-narrative).
1.9: Wayyišlaḥ YHWH ʾet-yādô — YHWH extended his hand. The reaching out of the divine hand is the investiture gesture of prophetic commission. Wayyagga‛ ʿal-pî — and touched my mouth: the mouth that claimed inadequacy (v6) is now supernaturally equipped. The mouth-touching parallels Isaiah 6:7 (the seraph's coal touches Isaiah's lips) and Ezekiel 3:1-3 (Ezekiel eating the scroll). Each commissioning act physically engages the prophetic speech-organ. Hinnēh nātattî dĕḇāray bĕpîkā — I have placed my words in your mouth: the prophet's words are now literally YHWH's words.
1.10: The commission is given as six infinitives: linttôš ûlintôts ûlĕhaʾăḇîd ûlahărôs ûliḇnôt ûlintôaʿ — to uproot, tear down, destroy, overthrow, build, and plant. The first four verbs are negative (deconstruction); the last two positive (reconstruction). This 4:2 ratio characterizes the entire book: the judgment oracles vastly outnumber the restoration promises. Yet the restoration verbs at the end are deliberate — Jeremiah's ministry, despite its overwhelming focus on judgment, has a salvific telos. The verbs bānâ (build) and nāṭaʿ (plant) recur in 31:28 as the marker of the new covenant era.
1.11: Maqēl šāqēd ʾănî rōʾeh — I see a branch of an almond tree. The vision is a visual pun: šāqēd is the almond tree (from the root šāqad, to watch/wake), known in Hebrew as "the waking tree" (hāʿēṣ hammĕšakkēm liphroaḥ) because it is the first tree to blossom after winter dormancy — the "watcher" that wakes in early spring. The visual image of the almond blossom sets up the wordplay in v12.
1.12: YHWH's interpretation: kî-šōqēd ʾănî ʿal-dĕḇārî laʿăśōtô — for I am watching over (šōqēd) my word to perform it. The pun is complete: šāqēd (almond, the watcher-tree) → šōqēd (watching). The almond's aggressive early blooming — its vigilant wakefulness at the first sign of spring — illustrates YHWH's equally vigilant, prompt performance of his prophetic word. The MKT cannot capture this wordplay; only the Hebrew makes the hermeneutic visible.
1.13: Sîr nāpûaḥ ûpānāyw mippĕnê ṣāpôn — a boiling/blown pot with its face (opening/mouth) from the north. The word nāpûaḥ (blown, from nāpaḥ) means the pot is actively being blown into flame from underneath; it is at full boil. Pānāyw (its face/opening) is the direction from which it tilts — the scalding liquid will pour southward toward Jerusalem when the pot tips from the north.
1.14: Mitstsāpôn tippātaḥ hārāʿâ — disaster will break open/crack from the north. The verb pātaḥ (break open) is unusual in this context; most translations say "break out" but the Hebrew more precisely describes a sealed vessel cracking open — the disaster has been sealed/restrained and now erupts. The "north" (ṣāpôn) is a theological rather than purely geographic direction in Jeremiah; Babylon lies due east, but invading armies would approach Jerusalem along the north-south road through Syria.
1.15: The enemies are kol-mišpeḥôt mamlekhôt ṣāpôn — all the families/clans of the kingdoms of the north. The collective noun mišpeḥôt (families, clans) applied to kingdoms is notable — it personalizes the political entities as kinship units. The image of each setting his throne at Jerusalem's gates (natanû ʾîš kissĕʾô petaḥ šaʿărê Yĕrûšālāyim) depicts a siege assembly with administrative occupation — the gates are where kings sit in judgment.
1.16: Triple charges of covenant violation: ʿāzāḇûnî (they abandoned me) — the verb ʿāzaḇ for covenant desertion is common in Deuteronomic texts; wayĕqattĕrû lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm (burned incense to other gods) — the Piel of qāṭar denotes the specific act of incense-burning, a priestly act misdirected to idols; wayyištaḥăwû lĕmaʿăśēh yĕdêhem (bowed down to the work of their hands) — the Hishtaphel of ḥāwâ is the strongest prostration term, used for the most complete act of submission.
1.17: ʾĀzōr ḥalṣêkā wĕqamtā — gird your loins and arise. The idiom "gird the loins" (ʾāzar ḥalāṣāyim) means to tuck up the lower garment for action — running, fighting, working. It is the preparation posture of the Passover night (Exod 12:11) and Elijah's running (1 Kings 18:46). The double command (gird and arise) signals decisive movement from the call to the commission. Pĕn-ʾăḥittĕkā lĕpānêhem — lest I terrify you before them: the threat cuts both ways — fear of the people produces fear from YHWH.
1.18: Three structural metaphors for the prophet's invulnerability: ʿîr miḇṣār (fortified city), lĕʿammûd barzel (iron pillar), lĕḥômôt nĕḥōšet (bronze walls). Each metaphor builds on the previous: a fortified city withstands siege; an iron pillar is structurally indestructible; bronze walls are impenetrable. The opponents listed — kings, officials, priests, and the people — constitute the entire social hierarchy; Jeremiah will face all of them.
1.19: Wĕnilḥămû ʾēlêkā wĕlōʾ-yûklû lāk — they will fight against you but will not overcome you. The verb lākad (overcome/prevail against) in the Qal indicates total defeat; the negation is absolute. The closing promise kî-ittĕkā ʾănî lĕhaṣṣîlekā repeats v8's inclusio verbatim — the call-narrative ends where it began: the divine presence as the sole guarantee of the prophet's mission.

2.1: The transition marker wayĕhî dĕḇar-YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr opens Jeremiah's first major oracle (ch2-3). The oracle is structured as a rîḇ (covenant lawsuit) — a legal genre in which YHWH brings charges against Israel using the form of a legal proceeding: summons (v4), charges (v5-13), evidence (v14-30), verdict (v31-37). The prophet is the prosecuting witness.
2.2: Zākartî lāk ḥesed nĕʿûrāyik ʾahăḇat kĕlûlōtāyik — I remember the ḥesed (covenant-loyalty/devoted-love) of your youth, the love (ʾahăḇat) of your bridal days. Ḥesed is the covenantal term for the steadfast love that binds covenant partners; applying it to Israel rather than YHWH is striking — YHWH remembers Israel's early responsiveness as her own ḥesed toward him. Lektĕk ʾaḥărây bammidbar — your following me through the wilderness: the wilderness period idealized as the honeymoon of the covenant relationship (contra the Golden Calf incident — this is pastoral idealization, not historical).
2.3: Qōdeš Yiśrāʾēl lĕYHWH rēʾšît tĕḇûʾātô — Israel was holy to YHWH, the firstfruits of his harvest. Bikkûrîm (firstfruits) are the first and best portion consecrated to YHWH (Exod 23:19); applying the term to Israel as a nation inverts the harvest metaphor — Israel is what YHWH has "first-fruited" from the nations, hence sacred property; anyone who consumes/threatens the firstfruits incurs guilt.
2.4: The summons formula šimʿû dĕḇar-YHWH bêt Yaʿăqōḇ (Hear the word of YHWH, O house of Jacob) addresses the covenant assembly. Using Yaʿăqōḇ (Jacob) alongside mišpeḥôt bêt Yiśrāʾēl (families of the house of Israel) recalls the patriarchal covenant identity — the people addressed are the heirs of the covenant promises.
2.5: Mah-māṣĕʾû ʾăḇôtêkem bî ʿāwel — what fault did your fathers find in me? The legal rîḇ begins with a challenge-question (rhetorical indictment): the defendant (YHWH) turns the accusation around. Wayyēlkû ʾaḥărê hahheḇel wayyehbāl — they went after the worthless thing and became worthless themselves. Heḇel is the Ecclesiastes word for vapor/breath/vanity; the verb wayyehbāl (they became heḇel) captures the principle that worshippers take on the character of their object of worship — those who worship empty things become empty.
2.6: The indictment exposes selective amnesia: the ancestors did not ask ʾayyēh YHWH hammĕʿăleh ʾōtānû mēʾereṣ Miṣrāyim — where is YHWH who brought us up from Egypt? The Exodus is the foundational saving event that should have generated ongoing remembrance and inquiry. The wilderness descriptions that follow — ʾereṣ ʿărāḇâ (land of the desert), ṣalmāwet (deep darkness, lit. shadow of death), lōʾ-ʿāḇar bāh ʾîš (no one crosses it) — emphasize that YHWH led them through impossible terrain, which makes the forgetting all the more inexcusable.
2.7: Waʾāḇîʾ ʾetkhem ʾel-ʾereṣ hakkarmel — I brought you into the land of the fruitful field (karmel = garden-land, orchard). The contrast is stark: from the uninhabitable wilderness of v6 to the productive garden-land. Watĕṭammĕʾû ʾet-ʾarṣî — you defiled my land: ṭāmēʾ (Piel intensive, make unclean) is the cultic term for ritual defilement; what is designated YHWH's inheritance (naḥălātî) has been made unclean — a sacrilege against covenantal property.
2.8: Four leadership groups charged: the priests who did not ask ʾayyēh YHWH; the tōpĕśê hattôrâ (Torah-handlers/lawyers) who did not know YHWH — the construction is ironic: those whose vocation is to know the Torah did not know the God the Torah reveals; the rulers (rōʿîm, shepherds) who transgressed; and the prophets who prophesied by Baal. The systemic failure runs from priest to prophet — every institutional mediator has failed.
2.9: Lākēn ʾōd ʾāriḇ ʾittĕkem — therefore I will still bring a lawsuit against you. The legal term rîḇ (contend, bring charges) is now explicit; YHWH is both plaintiff and judge. Wĕʾet-bĕnê ḇĕnêkem ʾāriḇ — and against your grandchildren's children I will bring a lawsuit: the covenant breach has multigenerational consequences (cf. Exod 20:5, visiting the iniquity to the third and fourth generation).
2.10: The proof from comparative religion: cross (ʿiḇrû) to Cyprus (western coast of the Mediterranean) and look; send to Kedar (Arabian desert nomads to the east) — the geographic extremes of the known world. Hahĕhēlîpû gôy ʾĕlōhîm — has a nation exchanged its gods? Rhetorically, the answer is no: even pagans are loyal to their (non-existent) gods. Israel's apostasy is uniquely irrational — they have traded a real God for non-gods.
2.11: Hēmîrû kĕḇôdô bĕlōʾ yôʿîl — they exchanged their glory for what profits nothing. Kāḇôd (glory) is what Israel traded away — the word refers to YHWH himself (cf. Ps 106:20, "they exchanged their glory for the image of a bull that eats grass"). The exchange is one of infinite loss: the divine glory for lōʾ yôʿîl (what does not profit/avail) — a practical description of idols: they cannot help.
2.12: Šōmmû šāmayim ʿal-zōʾt — be appalled, O heavens, at this. The heavens are called as witnesses to the covenant lawsuit (cf. Deut 32:1; Isa 1:2), and now as a horrified audience. Šāmam (be appalled/desolate) is the word used for devastated land — the heavens are called to react with the same stunned horror as if they had seen devastation. Śaʿărû ḥorĕḇû meʾōd — shudder and become very dry/desolate: the verbs escalate from shock to structural paralysis.
2.13: The double indictment: ʿāzāḇûnî mĕqôr mayim ḥayyîm — they abandoned me, the spring of living/flowing water. Māqôr is a natural spring (not a cistern), self-replenishing, inexhaustible. Mayim ḥayyîm — living water, meaning flowing/fresh water as opposed to stagnant; the same phrase Jesus uses in John 4:10-11 and 7:37-38, applying it to himself. The second charge: laḥṣōḇ lāhem bōrôt bōrôt nišbārîm — they hewn out for themselves cisterns, broken cisterns. The cistern (bôr) is an artificial pit lined with plaster to catch and hold rain — wholly dependent on human construction; when cracked (nišbārîm), it holds nothing. The contrast captures the theological irony: abandoning the inexhaustible self-replenishing source for a human construction that fails.
2.14: The rhetorical question: hăʿeḇed Yiśrāʾēl ʾim-yĕlîd-bayt hûʾ — is Israel a slave? is he a house-born servant? The yĕlîd-bayt (born in the house) is the lowest-status slave, born into slavery with no prior freedom. The question implies: if Israel is not a slave, why is he being treated as plunder? The political disasters (Assyrian and Babylonian aggression) are traced to the spiritual apostasy of ch2's charges.
2.15: ʿĀlāyw yišʾăgû kĕpîrîm — young lions roar over him. The lions (kĕpîrîm, young adult lions in their prime) are likely a metaphor for foreign powers, though unnamed. Wayyitrû ʾarṣô — they made his land a waste (šămmâ), cities burned, without inhabitants — the past tenses may anticipate the coming judgment or describe the Assyrian devastation of the northern kingdom as a type.
2.16: Gam-bĕnê-Nōp wĕTahpanḥēs — the people of Memphis (Noph) and Tahpanhes (in the Nile delta). These Egyptian cities represent the Egyptian alliance Judah was seeking as protection against Babylon — the very alliance YHWH forbids throughout Jeremiah. Yirʿûk qodqōd — they have shaved the crown of your head: a sign of subjugation and humiliation, stripping the dignity of the vassal. The irony is that Egypt, sought as a protector, treats Judah as conquered territory.
2.17: Hălōʾ-zōʾt taʿăśeh-lāk ʿāzĕḇēk ʾet-YHWH — is this not brought on yourself by abandoning YHWH your God? The reflexive causation is explicit: the disasters are self-inflicted through covenant abandonment. The phrase bĕʿēt môlîḵēk badderek — when he led you on the way — invokes the wilderness leading as the context of YHWH's provision that was then abandoned.
2.18: The oracle continues with a polemic against the foreign-alliance strategy: mah-lāk lĕdereḵ Miṣrayim — what is it to you on the Egyptian road? lištoṯ mê šîḥôr — to drink the waters of the Nile (šîḥôr = the dark/black waters of the Nile delta)? The metaphor of drinking foreign waters stands against the living spring of v13 — seeking political security from Egypt is substituting Nile water for YHWH's spring. Similarly for Assyria's Euphrates (Nahar).
2.19: Tĕyassĕrēk rāʿātēk — your own wickedness will discipline you. The verb yāsar (discipline, instruct through consequence) is the pedagogical term of Proverbs and Deuteronomy; the consequences of apostasy are built into the covenant structure — they are not arbitrary but inherent correctives. Wĕtôkĕḥēk mĕšuḇōtāyik — and your turning-away will rebuke you: mĕšuḇôt (apostasies, lit. turnings-away) is from šûḇ, the same root as repentance — the apostasies that turned Israel away will now turn back on them as rebuke.
2.20: Kî mēʿôlām šāḇarti ʿullēk pittaḥtî môsĕrōtāyik — for long ago I broke your yoke and tore off your chains. The Exodus is again invoked as the act of liberation; the yoke (ʿōl) and bonds (môsērôt) of Egyptian slavery were shattered by YHWH. The response that should have been gratitude and service was defiance: wattōʾmrî lōʾ ʾeʿăḇōd — you said, I will not serve! (ʿāḇad can mean serve God or serve as a slave — the refusal to serve YHWH is ironically the act of a freed slave refusing to serve her liberator). ʿal kol-gib‛â gĕḇōhâ (on every high hill) and taḥat kol-ʿēṣ raʿănān (under every green tree) — the standard Deuteronomic formula for Canaanite high-place worship (Deut 12:2).
2.21: Wĕʾānōkî nĕṭaʿtîkā śōrēq kullōh zeraʿ ʾĕmet — I planted you as a choice vine, wholly pure/true seed. Śōrēq is a specific variety of the finest grape vine (Gen 49:11; Isa 5:2; it denotes quality cultivation). Zeraʿ ʾĕmet — true/genuine seed — stresses the original quality of what was planted. Wĕʾêk nĕhăpaḵtā-lî sûrê haggepen nōkrîyâ — how have you turned against me into the degenerate shoots of a foreign/alien vine? The transformation is complete reversal: the finest cultivated vine has degenerated into a wild foreign stock — not by natural process but by willful apostasy.
2.22: Kî ʾim-tĕkabbĕsî baneṯer — even if you wash with lye (neṯer, a natural sodium carbonate used as soap/bleach). Wĕtarbi-lāk bōrît — and use much scouring-soap (bōrît). Despite the most thorough cleansing possible with available materials, nikṯām ʿăwōnēk lĕpānay — the stain of your iniquity is before me. The metaphor draws from laundry practice: some stains cannot be removed regardless of the cleaner used. Guilt before YHWH is not the superficial kind that soap can treat.
2.23: Êk tōʾmrî lōʾ niṭmēʾtî — how can you say, I am not defiled? The rhetorical challenge invites self-examination. Rĕʾî darkēk bagayʾ — look at your tracks in the valley (the valleys near Jerusalem were sites of Baal worship, including the Valley of Hinnom). The animal metaphors follow: bikriyâ qallâ mĕšarreḵet dĕrāḵêhā — a swift young camel (bikriyâ, a female camel in heat) running in every direction; the animal's frantic, aimless galloping captures Israel's undirected, compulsive pursuit of idols.
2.24: Pereʾ limmûd midbār — a wild donkey accustomed to the wilderness (midbār). The pereʾ is the wild ass — untameable, driven by instinct. Bĕʾawwat napšāh šāʾăpâ rûaḥ — in her craving she sniffs the wind (tracking the scent of a male). The sexual drive of the animal in heat becomes the metaphor for Israel's idolatrous compulsion — the seeking of idols is as instinctual and uncontrollable as animal mating drive.
