10.10: וַיהוָה אֱלֹהִים אֱמֶת (veYHWH Elohim emet, but YHWH is the true/real God): emet is not merely factual correctness but covenantal reliability and deep ontological reality — the opposite of hevel. אֱלֹהִים חַיִּים (Elohim chayyim, living God): the idols are dead artifacts; YHWH is alive and self-existent. מֶלֶךְ עוֹלָם (melekh olam, eternal king): belonging to the age/eternity — the same phrase Jesus applies to the Father in John 17:3 ('the only true God').
10.11: This verse is unique in the Hebrew Bible: written entirely in Aramaic, not Hebrew — the only Aramaic verse in Jeremiah. The Aramaic is likely intentional, addressed to nations who would understand it: in their own language, their gods are condemned. Elahayya di shemayya ve-ar'a la avadu — the gods who did not make heaven and earth will perish (ye'avadu) from the earth and from under the heavens.
10.12: עֹשֵׂה אֶרֶץ בְּכֹחוֹ (oseh eretz bekocho): YHWH made the earth by his power (koach). Three parallel cola: power (koach), wisdom (chokhmah), understanding (tevunah). These are the same divine attributes Prov 3:19-20 names as instruments of creation — the wisdom-creation tradition that becomes Christological in 1 Cor 1:30 and Col 1:16.
10.13: YHWH's voice over the waters produces roaring heavens; clouds rise from the ends of the earth (aqtzei-aretz). Lightning (baraqim) for rain; wind (ruach) from his storehouses — YHWH is not a national deity but the lord of cosmic weather, precisely what the Baal cult claimed for its storm-god.
10.14: נִבְעַר כָּל-אָדָם מִדַּעַת (niv'ar kol-adam mida'at, every person is brutish without knowledge): the idol-maker's problem is epistemic, not technical. The goldsmith is shamed by his idol because it is a פֶּסֶל שֶׁקֶר (pesel sheqer, image of falsehood/deception): sheqer (falsehood) is the moral opposite of emet. No breath (ruach) is in them.
10.15: הֶבֶל הֵמָּה (hevel hemmah, they are vapor/vanity) — the final verdict. מַעֲשֵׂה תַּעְתֻּעִים (ma'aseh ta'tu'im, a work of mockery/delusion): will be destroyed in the time of their punishment (pekudatam).
10.16: לֹא כְאֵלֶּה חֵלֶק יַעֲקֹב (lo ke'elleh chelek Ya'akov): chelek (portion/lot/share) — YHWH is what Jacob possesses, not what Jacob makes. כִּי יוֹצֵר הַכֹּל הוּא (ki yotzer hakol hu, for he is the Maker of everything): the one who makes all things cannot be one of the things made. Israel is his נַחֲלָתוֹ (nachalato, his inheritance) — YHWH Tzeva'ot is his name.
10.17: The scene shifts to impending exile. כַּנְסִי (kansi, gather up): addressed to Jerusalem personified (feminine). Gather your bundle (keli) from the ground — the besieged city is told to prepare for deportation.
10.18: קֹלֵעַ (qola', a slinger): YHWH will fling (qela') the inhabitants as a slinger flings a stone. The violence of the image captures the force of exile — not a gradual departure but an expulsion. הַפַּעַם (hapa'am, at this time) — this time is definitive.
10.19: אוֹי לִי (oy li, woe to me): Jeremiah's lament breaks into the oracle. שִׁבְרִי (shivri) and מַכָּתִי (makati) — my fracture, my wound: Jeremiah identifies with Judah's coming trauma. נְחֵלִי (nechelil): 'This is truly my burden' — I will carry it (essa).
10.20: The lament continues with tent imagery (oholi, my tent; yeriyo'tai, my tent-cords): the covenant community as a tent whose cords are broken. בָּנַי יְצָאֻנִי (banai yetza'uni, my children have gone from me) — exile as family dissolution. There is no one to pitch the tent again.
10.21: הָרֹעִים בָּעֲרוּ (haro'im ba'aru, the shepherds acted brutishly): ba'ar again — the leaders who should guide the flock have become as irrational as idol-worshipers. They have not sought (darash) YHWH — the prophetic demand that leaders consult YHWH (Deut 12:5 uses the same root for seeking YHWH at the appointed place).
10.22: קוֹל שְׁמוּעָה (qol shemu'ah, sound of a report!) — the watchword of judgment from the north. The cities of Judah will become a lair of jackals (tannim) — the standard desolation image in Jeremiah (cf. 51:37).
10.23: יָדַעְתִּי יְהוָה כִּי לֹא לָאָדָם דַּרְכּוֹ (yada'ti YHWH ki lo la'adam darko, I know, O YHWH, that man's way is not his own): one of the most theologically freighted statements in the book. The prophet confesses that human beings cannot direct their own steps — a basis for the new covenant hope of ch 31 (inner transformation by YHWH). Cf. Prov 20:24; Phil 2:13 (God who works in you both to will and to act).
10.24: יַסְּרֵנִי יְהוָה אַךְ בְּמִשְׁפָּט (yassareni YHWH akh bemishpat, correct me, O YHWH, but in justice): Jeremiah asks not to escape chastisement but for it to be measured (bemishpat), not in anger (be'appekha). The fear: being reduced to nothing (lema'at).
10.25: The prayer turns outward: pour out your wrath on the nations who do not know you and on the families who do not call on your name — cf. Ps 79:6. Israel's judgment is not YHWH's final word; the nations who consumed Jacob will face reckoning.

11.1: A new oracle section opens. הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה (hadevarim ha'elleh, these words) refers to the covenant terms — the covenant is accessible as a text, a set of specific terms that can be announced.
11.2: שִׁמְעוּ אֶת-דִּבְרֵי הַבְּרִית (shim'u et-divrei haberit, hear the words of the covenant): the covenant (berit) has specific terms (devarim) that must be heard and proclaimed. This chapter likely connects to Josiah's covenant renewal (2 Kings 23:1-3), though Jeremiah's emphasis falls on covenant failure.
11.3: אָרוּר הָאִישׁ (arur ha'ish, cursed is the man): the covenant curse formula (cf. Deut 27-28). Not hearing the covenant = falling under the curse structure that was always embedded in the covenant. Covenant is not merely a relationship but a legal structure with enforceable consequences.
11.4: כּוּר הַבַּרְזֶל (kur habarzel, furnace of iron): Egypt as crucible — both suffering and refinement. הֱיוּ לִי לְעָם (heyu li le'am, be my people) / אֱלֹהֵיכֶם (Eloheikhem, your God): the mutual covenant formula that runs from Exodus through Jeremiah to Rev 21:3.
11.5: אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ (eretz zavat chalav udevash, land flowing with milk and honey): the promise linked to Sinai-covenant obedience. Its loss (exile) signifies its removal. אָמֵן יְהוָה (Amen YHWH) — Jeremiah's covenant affirmation.
11.6: YHWH commands Jeremiah to proclaim (qara) these words in the cities of Judah and streets of Jerusalem — the covenant is public proclamation, not private instruction. The nationwide command anticipates the nationwide covenant-breaking that follows.
11.7: הָעֵד הַעִדֹתִי (ha'ed ha'idoti): infinitive absolute construction for emphatic repeated action — I earnestly, repeatedly warned your ancestors. The frequency of YHWH's warning heightens the culpability of Israel's refusal.
11.8: לֹא שָׁמְעוּ (lo sham'u, they did not listen): the covenant's perpetual problem. שְׁרִירוּת לִבָּם (sherirul libam, stubbornness of their heart): a key Jeremianic phrase for the structural resistance that makes the old covenant insufficient and the new covenant (ch 31) necessary.
11.9: קֶשֶׁר (qesher, conspiracy/treason): not merely individual disobedience but organized collective rejection — a political-rebellion term. The men of Judah and inhabitants of Jerusalem have returned to the sins of their first ancestors.
11.10: שָׁבוּ עַל עֲוֺנוֹת אֲבוֹתָם (shavu al avonot avotam, returned to the iniquities of their ancestors): covenant-breaking is generational and repetitive — confirming the diagnosis of 10:23 that humans cannot direct their own way. They went after other gods; they broke the covenant.
11.11: הִנְנִי מֵבִיא אֲלֵיהֶם רָעָה (hineni mevi alehem ra'ah): the hinneh particle marks the immediacy of divine action. לֹא יוּכְלוּ לָצֵאת מִמֶּנָּה — from which they cannot escape. The irrevocability is underscored.
11.12: The cities will cry to their gods, but the gods cannot save. The tragic irony: Israel chose gods who cannot answer in the time of need, while abandoning the God who could. כְּמִסְפַּר עָרֶיךָ (kemispar areycha) — as many as your cities are your gods.
11.13: As many altars (mizbechot) as streets in Jerusalem, set for Baal — directly violating the Deuteronomic principle of one worship-place. The proliferation of worship sites signals the complete breakdown of covenant exclusivity.
11.14: אַל-תִּתְפַּלֵּל (al-titpallel, do not pray/intercede): a striking prohibition on prophetic intercession — the covenant-breaking is so complete that even Moses-style intercession is forbidden. This is repeated in 7:16 and 15:1 (not even Moses and Samuel could intercede). The prohibition signals a covenant boundary has been crossed.
11.15: יְדִידִי (yedidi, my beloved): a term of deep covenant affection — cf. Deut 33:12; Song of Songs. YHWH calls Israel 'my beloved' even while announcing judgment. מַה-לִּידִידִי בְּבֵיתִי (mah-liyedidi beveyti): what claim does my beloved have in my house when she acts wickedly? Sacrifice cannot override unfaithfulness.
11.16: זַיִת רַעֲנָן יְפֵה-פְרִי תֹאַר (zayit ra'anan yefeh-peri to'ar, a thriving olive tree, beautiful in fruit and appearance): Israel as YHWH's intended planting — productive and beautiful. The olive tree metaphor recurs in Ps 52:8 and Rom 11:17-24 (Paul's wild-olive grafting). The contrast: with fire and noise, its branches will burn.
11.17: YHWH himself planted (neta') Israel and now pronounces judgment because of the evil done, burning incense to Baal. The planting-pruning imagery anticipates John 15 (the vine; the Father who removes fruitless branches).
11.18: YHWH revealed to Jeremiah the plot of the Anathoth men against him. אָז רָאִיתִי מַעֲלְלֵיהֶם (az ra'iti ma'alelehem): YHWH's revelation is the source of Jeremiah's knowledge — the prophet could not have known by ordinary means.
11.19: כְּכֶבֶשׂ אַלּוּף (kekeves alluf, like a gentle/tame lamb) led to slaughter (tivach) without knowing it. This image is taken up in NT for Christ: Isa 53:7 ('led like a lamb to slaughter'), John 1:29 ('the Lamb of God'), Acts 8:32. נַכְרִיתֶנּוּ מֵאֶרֶץ חַיִּים (nakritenu me'eretz chayyim, let us cut him off from the land of the living): the exact phrase used in Isa 53:8 of the Servant.
11.20: יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת שֹׁפֵט צֶדֶק (YHWH Tzeva'ot shofet tzedek, YHWH of armies, righteous judge): Jeremiah commits his case to the divine judge. בֹּחֵן כְּלָיוֹת וָלֵב (bocheyn kelayot va-lev, tester of kidneys and heart): in Hebrew physiology, kidneys = deep emotion/conscience; heart = thought/will — testing both means examining the complete inner person. Cf. Rev 2:23 (Jesus: 'I search hearts and minds').
11.21: The specific threat: men of Anathoth threaten Jeremiah not to prophesy in YHWH's name. The hometown opposition to the prophet is a type of the broader rejection pattern — anticipating Jesus's rejection at Nazareth (Luke 4:24: 'no prophet is accepted in his hometown').
11.22: YHWH's response: the young men of Anathoth will die by sword; their sons and daughters by famine. The principle that violence plotted against the prophet returns on the plotters — a microcosm of the larger oracle-fulfillment dynamic throughout Jeremiah.
11.23: שְׁאֵרִית לֹא תִהְיֶה לָהֶם (she'erit lo tihyeh lahem, there will be no remnant for them): the reversal of remnant hope applied to Jeremiah's persecutors. The year of their punishment (pekudatam) is coming.

12.1: צַדִּיק אַתָּה יְהוָה (tzaddiq attah YHWH): Jeremiah opens his theodicy complaint by formally acknowledging YHWH's righteousness — the legal premise of the lawsuit (rib). אַךְ מִשְׁפָּטִים אֲדַבֵּר אוֹתָךְ (akh mishpatim adabber otakha, yet I will argue my case with you): mishpatim are legal judgments; the prophetic complaint is a formal legal proceeding. The complaint: why does the wicked prosper (shalehu, be undisturbed)?
12.2: נְטַעְתָּם גַּם-שֹׁרָשׁוּ (neta'tam gam-shorrashu, you planted them and they have taken root): YHWH's own planting is acknowledged. The devastating diagnosis: קָרוֹב בְּפִיהֶם וְרָחוֹק מִכִּלְיוֹתֵיהֶם (qarov befiyhem verachok mikillyotehem, near in their mouths but far from their inner being) — verbal religion without inner reality. Cf. Isa 29:13; Matt 15:8.
12.3: YHWH knows (yada'ta) Jeremiah intimately — the covenantal knowledge that distinguishes genuine relationship from verbal profession. בָּחַנְתָּ לִבִּי (vachanta libbi, you have tested my heart): YHWH's scrutiny confirms genuine covenant loyalty. הַשְׁלִיכֵם כְּצֹאן לְטִבְחָה (hashlikhem ketzon letivchah): the lamb-imagery of ch 11 reversed — the persecutors become the ones marked for slaughter.
12.4: עַד-מָתַי תֶּאֱבַל הָאָרֶץ (ad-matai te'eval ha'aretz, how long will the land mourn): the land participates in covenant consequences — drought and famine as manifestations of broken covenant (Deut 28:23-24). The wicked claim: 'He will not see our latter end' — denying divine judgment.
12.5: YHWH's unexpected response: כִּי אֶת-רַגְלִים רַצְתָּ (ki et-raglim ratztah, if you have raced against foot-soldiers): a challenge-question that escalates stakes. If this level of difficulty wears Jeremiah out, how will he survive the thicket of the Jordan? The point: what is coming will be harder, not easier; the prophet must deepen rather than seek relief.
12.6: Even Jeremiah's own brothers (acheycha) have dealt treacherously (bagadu) with him. אַל-תַּאֲמֵן בָּהֶם (al-ta'amen bahem, do not trust them): the root aman — do not place emunah in them. Micah 7:5-6 captures the same family-betrayal dynamic; Jesus invokes it in Matt 10:35-36.
12.7: עָזַבְתִּי אֶת-בֵּיתִי (azavti et-beiti, I have forsaken my house): YHWH speaks as one who has abandoned his own household — temple and covenantal family. נָתַתִּי אֶת-יְדִידוּת נַפְשִׁי (natati et-yedidut nafshi, I have given the beloved of my soul): yedidut is passionate covenant-love. Giving her over to enemies is an act of grief-driven self-sacrifice.
12.8: My inheritance (nachalati) has become like a lion roaring against me — the covenant relationship that should be the greatest good has become the source of the greatest grief. The lion that should be a lamb has turned predatory.
12.9: Is my inheritance like a speckled bird of prey surrounded by other birds of prey? The image suggests Israel's exposed, vulnerable position — made a target by the surrounding nations because of her unfaithfulness.
12.10: רֹעִים רַבִּים שִׁחֲתוּ כַרְמִי (ro'im rabbim shichatu karmi, many shepherds have ruined my vineyard): kerem (vineyard) is the covenant metaphor for Israel (cf. Isa 5:1-7). The shepherd-rulers who trample the vineyard stand condemned — the sequence continues through Ezek 34 and John 10.
12.11: שְׁמָמָה (shemamah, desolation): the land mourning (avelah) before YHWH. Because no one lays it to heart (sheym al lev, takes it seriously), the desolation persists.
12.12: Destroyers have come upon all the bare heights in the wilderness — YHWH's sword devours from one end to the other. No peace (shalom) for any flesh — the sword is a covenant curse for treaty-breaking (Lev 26:25).
12.13: זָרְעוּ חִטִּים וְקֹצִים קָצָרוּ (zar'u chittim veqotzim qatzaru, they sowed wheat but reap thorns): the agricultural reversal of covenant blessing (Deut 28:18-24) — futile labor as covenant curse. Ashamed (hivoshu) of their produce because of YHWH's fierce anger.
12.14: The nations: evil neighbors who touch Israel's inheritance will be uprooted (attesh) from their land. But after uprooting, YHWH will have compassion — the first hint that judgment on nations serves a larger restorative purpose.
12.15: וְשַׁבְתִּי וְרִחַמְתִּים (veshavti verichhamtim, I will return and have compassion on them): the compassion-after-judgment pattern that drives the new covenant promise. Each person will be brought back to their heritage (nachlatohu) and land.
12.16: Nations who learn Israel's ways — who learn to swear by YHWH's name rather than Baal — will be built up (yibbaneh) among YHWH's people. Covenant membership is extendable to nations who adopt the covenant pattern — anticipating the Gentile inclusion of the NT.
12.17: The nation that does not listen (lo yishma') will be utterly uprooted and destroyed — the same covenant-obedience standard applies to all nations. Covenant relationship brings both the promise of inclusion and the threat of consequence.

13.1: אֵזוֹר פִּשְׁתִּים (ezor pishtim, linen belt/waistcloth): the sign-act (mashal) begins with an intimate garment — worn next to the skin, signifying the closeness of the YHWH-Israel bond. Linen (pishtim) was the material of priestly garments (Ex 28:39), adding priestly overtones to the sign. אַל-תְּבִיאֵהוּ בַמָּיִם (al-tevi'ehu vamayim, do not put it in water): kept unwashed, representing the uncleansed intimacy of the relationship as it was meant to be.
13.2: Jeremiah buys the sash and wears it according to YHWH's word — sign-acts require faithful literal enactment; the prophet's body becomes the medium of the prophetic message.
13.3: YHWH's second word: go to the Euphrates (Perat) and hide it there in a crevice of rock. The Euphrates is Babylon — the hiding place is the location of exile.
13.4: The command is enacted — whether by literal travel or visionary enactment (the distance makes literal two-way travel possible but logistically demanding). The sign-act is narrated to be read, not audited for logistics.
13.5: Jeremiah went and hid the sash at the Euphrates as commanded — the prophet's obedience in sign-acts is as constitutive of the message as the spoken word. The enacted word is YHWH's word.
13.6: Many days later YHWH says: arise, retrieve the sash — the time gap represents the exile period. What was hidden is now to be recovered and examined as evidence.
13.7: וְהִנֵּה נִשְׁחַת הָאֵזוֹר (vehinneh nishchat ha'ezor, and behold the sash was ruined): shachat (ruin/corruption) — the same root used for the moral corruption of the people. לֹא יִצְלָח לַכֹּל (lo yitzlach lakol, good for nothing at all): completely useless. The ruined garment is the sermon before the sermon.
13.8: The word of YHWH came again to interpret the sign — the sign-act requires prophetic interpretation. Ezekiel's sign-acts follow the same pattern; Jesus's parables function similarly.
13.9: כָּכָה אַשְׁחִית (kakha ashchit, in this way I will ruin): the sign is the sermon. גְּאוֹן יְהוּדָה (ge'on Yehudah) and גְּאוֹן יְרוּשָׁלַם (ge'on Yerushalayim, the great pride of Judah and Jerusalem): their pride will be ruined precisely as the sash was — the pride-judgment link runs throughout the prophets.
13.10: הָעָם הָרַע הַזֶּה (ha'am hara' hazeh, this evil people): moral diagnosis. שְׁרִירוּת לִבָּם (sherirul libam, stubbornness of their heart): this phrase recurs throughout Jeremiah (3:17; 7:24; 9:13; 11:8; 23:17) — the structural intractability that makes the new covenant (ch 31) necessary rather than optional. They cling (halakh achar) to other gods.
13.11: כַּאֲשֶׁר יִדְבַּק הָאֵזוֹר אֶל-מָתְנֵי-אִישׁ (ka'asher yiddaq ha'ezor el-motnei-ish): davaq (cling/cleave) — the same word for the marriage bond (Gen 2:24), for Israel's commanded devotion to YHWH (Deut 10:20: uvdo uvo tidbaq, serve him and cling to him), and for Ruth clinging to Naomi (Ruth 1:14). YHWH bound Israel to himself as intimate garment for renown (tiferet), praise (tehillah), and glory (tif'eret) — but they would not listen.
13.12: Every wineskin (nevel) is filled with wine — a proverb the people use dismissively ('yes, of course!'). But YHWH redirects: I will fill every inhabitant with drunkenness (shikkaron). The wine/judgment equation runs through the prophets (Jer 25:15-17 — the cup of wrath).
13.13: The drunkenness will cause them to dash (nifatzti) against one another — father against son. YHWH will not spare (lo achmel), pity (lo arachem), or have mercy (lo achus) — triple negation for the completeness of judgment.
13.14: וְנִפַּצְתִּים אִישׁ אֶל-אָחִיו (venifatztim ish el-achiv, I will dash them man against his brother): the internal violence following covenant-breaking. No pity, no compassion, no mercy — three ways of saying the protective covenant relationship is suspended.
13.15: שִׁמְעוּ וְהַאֲזִינוּ (shim'u veha'azinu): hear and give ear — synonymous verbs for emphatic summons. אַל-תִּגְבְּהוּ (al-tigbehu, do not be arrogant): the pride-warning before the deportation. כִּי יְהוָה דִּבֵּר (ki YHWH dibber) — the prophetic authority-claim.
13.16: תְּנוּ לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם כָּבוֹד (tenu laYHWH Eloheykhem kavod, give glory/honor to YHWH your God): kavod (glory/weight) — the call to acknowledge YHWH's weightiness before the darkness of exile falls. Before the feet stumble on the mountains of twilight, before the hoped-for light becomes deep darkness.
13.17: תִּבְכֶּה נַפְשִׁי בְמִסְתָּרִים (tivkeh nafshi bemistarim, my soul will weep in secret): one of the most intimate prophetic self-revelations — Jeremiah's hidden tears for the flock's captivity. The prophet's inner grief mirrors YHWH's own grief over the covenant people. מִפְּנֵי גַאֲוָה (mipney ga'avah) — because of pride: pride is the reason for exile.
13.18: The command to king (melekh) and queen mother (gevira): sit low (shiflu), for your crown (ateret tif'artechem, crown of your glory) has fallen. This likely addresses Jehoiachin and Nehushta (2 Kings 24:8) — the royal house goes into exile and its dignity is stripped.
13.19: The cities of the Negev are shut up (sug), with no one to open them. All Judah is carried into exile — the geography of deportation expanding from south to north. The totality fulfills the covenant curse of Lev 26:33.
13.20: Jerusalem is addressed directly: שְׂאִי עֵינַיִךְ (se'i einayikh, lift up your eyes): see those coming from the north. Where is your beautiful flock that was given to you? The shepherd-imagery turned accusatory — Jerusalem is responsible for the flock she received and failed to protect.
13.21: What will you say when they appoint your trained allies as head over you? A rhetorical question exposing the bitter irony: the alliances cultivated for security will become Judah's captors. The pain (chavalim) of a woman in childbirth — sudden, unavoidable, consuming.
13.22: לָמָּה קָרָאנִי זֹאת (lamah qara'ani zo't, why has this happened to me?): Jerusalem's anticipated question. The answer: בְּרֹב עֲוֺנֵךְ (berov avonekh, because of the multitude of your iniquities). The skirt-lifting metaphor: the shame of exposed unfaithfulness as both military defeat and judicial consequence.
13.23: הֲיַהֲפֹךְ כּוּשִׁי עוֹרוֹ (hayahafokh Kushi oro, can a Cushite change his skin): the Cushite (Kushi) is used for a visually unmistakable difference. וְנָמֵר חֲבַרְבֻּרֹתָיו (venamed chavarburotav, or a leopard its spots): the leopard's pattern is as fixed as the Cushite's skin. Theological point: Judah has become so habituated to evil (hiskhilu laro'a, accustomed to doing evil) that self-generated moral change is as impossible as changing these natural characteristics. One of the OT's most direct statements of the bondage of the will — grounding the need for the new covenant's heart-replacement in ch 31.
13.24: כְּקַשׁ עֹבֵר (kekash over, like passing chaff): scattered by the desert wind (ruach midbar). The wind-chaff image for judgment appears in Ps 1:4 and continues into NT judgment imagery (Matt 3:12 — chaff burned with unquenchable fire).
13.25: זֶה גוֹרָלֵךְ מְנָת-מִדַּיִךְ (zeh goralekh menat-midayikh): goral (lot/fate) and menah (measured portion) frame exile as YHWH's judicial apportionment. שָׁכַחְתְּ אוֹתִי (shakacht oti, you have forgotten me): the sin is ultimately relational — forgetting YHWH is the root of all the rest.
13.26: YHWH himself will lift her skirts over her face — shame of exposed nakedness as both military metaphor for defeat and legal metaphor for the consequences of unfaithfulness. Cf. Ezek 16:37; Hos 2:3.
13.27: The closing indictment: נִאֻפַיִךְ (ni'ufayikh, your adulteries) — covenant infidelity as sexual unfaithfulness, the Hosea-Jeremiah metaphorical frame; מִצְהֲלוֹתַיִךְ (mitzhalotayikh, your lustful cries/neighings); זִמַּת זְנוּתֵךְ (zimmah znutekh, the obscene acts of your prostitution). The verdict: אוֹי לָךְ יְרוּשָׁלַם (oy lakh Yerushalayim, woe to you, Jerusalem) — covenant lament. Will you not be made clean? How long yet?

14.1: The superscription ʾăšer hāyâ dĕbar YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû ʿal-dibrê habbaṣṣārôt — "the word that came to Jeremiah concerning the drought(s)"; baṣṣārôt (plural of baṣṣōret) denotes droughts or famines — the plural may suggest multiple drought events. The MKT renders "the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD concerning the drought" treating the plural as a collective.
14.2: ʾābĕlâ Yĕhûdâ — "Judah mourns"; ʾābal is the verb for formal mourning, used of communities in distress. "City gates droop in gloom" (šaʿărêhā ʾumĕlālû): ʾāmal means to languish, droop. The personification of the gates as mourners makes the whole city a unified mourning body. The MKT follows the MT's striking personification rather than flattening it.
14.3: The social structure of the drought revealed: officials (ʾaddîrêhem, the great ones) send servants (ṣĕʿîrêhem, the lesser ones) to cisterns that are empty. The shame gesture "cover their heads" (ḥāpû rōʾšām) indicates grief or humiliation. All social levels are equally helpless before the covenant curse of drought.
14.4: kî lōʾ hāyâ gešem — "because there has been no rain"; the explicit causal statement of the drought. The farmers "cover their heads" in the same gesture as the servants in v.3 — the drought levels social distinctions in shared despair. ʿAdāmâ ḥattâ (the ground is cracked/dismayed) — the same verb ḥātat used for human dismay applies to the land.
14.5: The natural world used as evidence: even the nursing doe (ʾayyālâ baśśādeh, doe in the field) abandons her newborn (nāṭĕšâ = abandoned, forsaken) because there is no grass. Animals do not abandon their young except under extreme conditions — the drought is so severe it overrides maternal instinct. The MKT preserves the emotional force of this image.
14.6: pĕrāʾîm ʿāmdû ʿal-šĕpāyim — "the wild donkeys stand on the barren ridges"; wild donkeys were known for their sharp eyes and ability to find water; now they gasp for air like jackals (šāʾăpû rûaḥ kattannîm). "Their eyes grow dim" (kālû ʿênêhem) — the failure of keen eyesight signals total ecological collapse.
14.7: ʿăśēh lĕmaʿan šĕmekā — "act for the sake of your name"; the appeal to YHWH's own reputation grounds Israel's lament. Mĕšûbôtênû rabbû lĕkā ḥāṭāʾnû — "our unfaithfulness is many times over; we have sinned against you." The confession is corporate and unqualified. The MKT preserves the name-appeal as the primary petitionary basis distinct from Israel's merit.
14.8: miqweh Yiśrāʾēl — "O hope of Israel"; miqweh means both hope and a pool/reservoir of water — a deliberate wordplay in the drought context. YHWH is Israel's true water-source while the cisterns are empty. "Like a foreigner in the land, like a traveler who stops only for the night" — the accusation that YHWH has become a transient, not a permanent dweller. The MKT preserves the "hope" rendering but the water-pun enriches it for readers aware of the drought context.
14.9: kĕgibbôr lōʾ-yûkal lĕhôšîaʿ — "like a warrior who is powerless to save"; the bold challenge questioning YHWH's power. The prayer then recovers: "You are still in the midst of us, LORD, and we bear your name." The tension between accusation and reaffirmation is characteristic of biblical lament. The MKT preserves "in the midst of us" (bĕqirbēnû) as the ground for the continued plea.
14.10: YHWH's response refuses the lament: "They have loved to wander" (ʾāhăbû lānûaʿ) — their wandering after idols is a chosen lifestyle, not an involuntary drift. Lōʾ-ḥāśĕkû ragĕlêhem — "they have not restrained their feet." The MKT's "loved to wander" preserves the volitional dimension — this is desire, not weakness.
14.11: YHWH's second prohibition of intercession (cf. 7:16): ʾal-titpallēl bĕʿad hāʿām hazzeh lĕṭôbâ — "Do not pray for the welfare of this people." The phrase lĕṭôbâ (for good/welfare) specifies what is being forbidden — not all prayer, but petitions for blessing. The judgment is now irreversible; intercession would only oppose YHWH's just decree.
14.12: The three channels of approach to YHWH — fasting (ṣûm), burnt offerings (ʿōlâ), and grain offerings (minḥâ) — will all be ignored. The covenant's normal means of approach are suspended. The MKT preserves all three ritual forms to show the comprehensiveness of the suspension: no ritual avenue remains open.
14.13: Jeremiah's objection: the false prophets are promising peace and security — the people are being misled, not simply disobeying. Ḥereb lōʾ-tirʾû ... šālôm ʾĕmet ʾettēn lākem — "You will not face war... I will give you lasting peace." The word ʾĕmet (truth, reliability) placed in the false prophets' mouths is particularly ironic — they claim their peace is the real/true peace.
14.14: šeqer hanněbîʾîm nibbāʾîm — "The prophets are prophesying falsehoods (šeqer) in my name"; three parallel divine disavowals: "I did not send them (lōʾ šĕlaḥtîm), I did not command them (wĕlōʾ ṣiwwîtîm), and I did not speak to them (wĕlōʾ dibbartî ʾălêhem)." The triple negation is emphatic. The MKT preserves all three verbs of sending/commanding/speaking.
14.15: The judgment on the false prophets mirrors what they falsely promised: they promised no sword or famine; they will die by sword and famine. The poetic justice of this reversal is intentional — the prophets who denied the covenant curses will personally experience them. The MKT preserves the verbal echo: same terms (ḥereb and rāʿāb) in judgment that were denied in their oracles.
14.16: The people who trusted the false prophets are thrown into the streets — the reverse of the temple-court security they were promised. Wĕhišpaktî ʿălêhem ʾet-rāʿātām — "I will pour out their wickedness upon them"; the judgment is a pouring-out that mirrors the rain withheld in the drought. The MKT's "pour out" preserves this ironic contrast.
14.17: tērdanâ ʿênî dimʿâ laylâ wĕyômām — "Let my eyes overflow with tears night and day without stopping"; YHWH (or the prophet) commanded to weep. The phrase ʾal-tiddōm (without stopping, without ceasing) intensifies the mourning to uninterrupted grief. "The virgin daughter of my people" (bat-ʿammî) — a personification of the nation as a young woman struck down.
14.18: ûbāʾû ʾel-ʾereṣ — "Both prophet and priest have gone to a land they do not know"; the religious leaders are already going into exile before the final judgment. The verse is difficult: the MKT renders "both the prophet and the priest ply their trade in a land they do not know" — possibly meaning they practice their false trade even in exile. The uncertainty of this verse is acknowledged in the MKT rendering.
14.19: hāmāʾōs māʾasta ʾet-Yĕhûdâ — "Have you completely rejected Judah?" — the infinitive absolute (māʾōs māʾasta, "in rejecting have you rejected") conveys absolute rejection. "Does your very being recoil from Zion?" (bĕṣiyyôn gāʿălâ napšekā) — gāʿal means to loathe/abhor; the question dares to say YHWH's nepeš (inner self/being) is revolted by Zion.
14.20: yādaʿnû YHWH rišʿēnû — "We acknowledge, O LORD, our own wickedness"; the corporate confession moves from questioning (v.19) to acknowledgment. The dual confession: our own wickedness (rišʿēnû) and our ancestors' sins (ʿăwōn ʾăbôtênû). The MKT preserves both the first-person and ancestral dimensions of the confession.
14.21: lĕmaʿan šĕmekā — "for the sake of your name"; the dual appeal of v.21: name (šēm) and covenant (bĕrît). "Do not dishonor the throne of your glory" (kissēʾ kĕbôdekā) — the temple as YHWH's earthly throne is implicated in Israel's fate; its destruction would dishonor YHWH. The MKT preserves this implicit argument: YHWH's honor is at stake in Israel's survival.
14.22: hăyēš bahabrê haggôyim mağšîmîm — "Are there any among the idols of the nations that can make it rain?" The drought context makes the idol-powerlessness argument concrete: can idols control rain? The rhetorical answer is no. Hălōʾ ʾattâ-hûʾ YHWH ʾĕlōhênû — "You are the one, O LORD our God." The monotheistic confession arises from the crisis of the drought.

15.1: Mōšeh ûŠĕmûʾēl — "Even if Moses and Samuel stood before me, my heart would not be moved"; Moses (Exod 32:11-14, Num 14:13-20) and Samuel (1 Sam 7:5-9) were the two supreme intercessors of Israel's history. Both had moved YHWH to relent before. The fact that even they are named as insufficient underlines the finality of the judgment. The MKT preserves the comparison because it is theologically essential — no human intercession can reverse this decree.
15.2: ʾăšer lammāwet lammāwet — "Those destined for death will fall to death"; the fourfold division — death, sword, famine, captivity — is a comprehensive catalogue of the covenant curses. The rhetorical structure (repetition of the noun: "to death to death, to the sword to the sword") is an emphatic Hebrew form. The MKT preserves the fourfold categories but renders them as natural English: "death... sword... famine... captivity."
15.3: ʾarbaʿ mišpāḥôt — "four kinds of destroyers"; the four agents are personified as mišpāḥôt (families/clans) of destruction. The MKT renders "four kinds of destroyers" rather than the more literal "four families" because the English idiom "families of destroyers" obscures the point. The progression from sword to dogs to birds to beasts represents the complete destruction and desecration of the dead.
15.4: Mĕnaššeh ben-Ḥizqiyyāhû — "because of Manasseh son of Hezekiah"; Manasseh's reign (2 Kgs 21) was the paradigm of apostasy that even Josiah's reform could not undo. The phrase "a terrifying sight to all the kingdoms of the earth" (zāʿăwâ lĕkōl mamĕlĕkôt hāʾārets) — the nation becomes a spectacle of divine judgment. The MKT's "terrifying sight" captures the word zaʿăwâ (horror, trembling).
15.5: mî-yāḥmōl ʿālayik — "who will feel sorry for you, O Jerusalem?" The three rhetorical questions (who will have compassion? who will grieve? who will stop?) expect the answer: no one. The compassion vocabulary: yāḥmōl (have compassion), yānûd (show grief/commiserate), yisôl lĕšālôm (ask about your welfare). All three forms of social concern are abandoned because the city had abandoned YHWH.
15.6: nāṭaštî ʾet-ʾaḥôr — "you keep going backward"; nāṭaš (go backward, draw back) in the niphal. The MKT renders "you keep going backward" — the progressive tense captures the ongoing nature of the retreat. Wāʾettî yādî ʿālayik wāʾašḥîtēk — YHWH stretching out his hand against Israel is the reversal of the Exodus (where the hand was stretched out for Israel).
15.7: wāʾezrēm bĕmizreh bĕšaʿărê hāʾārets — "I will winnow them with a fan in the gateways of the land"; the agricultural winnowing image: chaff separated from grain. Bikkaltî šĕkailtî ʾet-ʿammî — "I will strip them of their children (bānim) and destroy my people". The double verb (kibbeltu šikiltu) — bereave and destroy — is an intensive pair. The MKT follows MT's two-verb construction.
15.8: ʿātsĕmû-lî ʾalmĕnôtāyw mēḥôl yammîm — "their widows have become more numerous to me than the sand of the sea"; a striking reversal of the Abrahamic promise (Gen 22:17 — descendants as sand). Here it is widows, not descendants, who are countless. "A destroyer at midday" (šōdēd baṣṣāhŏrāyim) — midday attack emphasizes the openness and completeness of the slaughter.
15.9: ʾummâ šibbĕʿat hāyĕlādîm — "the mother of seven gasps her last breath"; seven sons was the ideal of complete blessing in the ancient world (cf. Ruth 4:15, 1 Sam 1:8). Her sun going down while it is still midday (bāʾ šimšāh bĕʿōd yômmām) inverts the natural order — blessing reversed mid-life.
15.10: ʾîš rîb wĕʾîš mādôn — "a man of contention and strife"; Jeremiah's first personal lament — the prophet curses his own birth (anticipating ch. 20). The MKT preserves "to the whole world" (lĕkol-hāʾārets): Jeremiah's birth was a source of contention nationally, not just locally. "I have neither lent nor borrowed" — his financial innocence makes his social ostracism incomprehensible to him.
15.11: ʾim-lōʾ šĕrîtĕkā lĕṭôb — "Surely I will set what remains of you free for good purposes"; YHWH's assurance to Jeremiah in the midst of his lament. The MKT renders "what remains of you" — šĕʾêrîtĕkā suggests a remnant or what remains of Jeremiah's ministry. The promise that enemies will "appeal to you in the time of disaster" is the reversal of Jeremiah's current humiliation.
15.12: hăyĕrōaʿ barzel barzel mitstsāpôn — "Can iron shatter iron from the north — or even bronze?" A difficult verse; the MKT follows the reading that this refers to Babylon (the iron from the north) being an irresistible force. Some read it as a general proverb; the MKT rendering as a rhetorical question emphasizes the rhetorical force rather than a metaphysical claim.
15.13: ḥêlĕkā ûʾôtsĕrôtêkā lābaz ʾettēn — "Your wealth and your treasures I will hand over to plunder — free of charge"; lōʾ bĕmĕḥîr (not for a price) means without any economic benefit to Israel — the wealth simply flows out. The phrase "because of all your sins, throughout all your territory" (bĕkol-ḥaṭṭōʾtêkā ûbĕkol-gĕbûlêkā) is comprehensive in scope.
15.14: kî ʾēš qĕdaḥat bĕʾappî — "for a fire has been kindled by my anger, and it will burn against you"; the divine anger as consuming fire — a standard OT metaphor. The exile to "a land you have never known" (ʾĕrets ʾăšer lōʾ-yādaʿtā) is the ultimate disorientation: covenantal relationship depended on knowledge of YHWH and of the land; exile dissolves both.
15.15: yādaʿtā YHWH zĕkĕrēnî ûpĕqĕdēnî — "You know, O LORD; remember me and care for me"; the second lament — Jeremiah appeals to YHWH's omniscience as the ground for attention to his own need. "Do not in your forbearance take me away" (bĕʾerek ʾappĕkā ʾal-tiqqāḥēnî) — the prophet fears that YHWH's patience with the persecutors may come at the cost of the prophet's own life.
15.16: nimsĕʾû dĕbārêkā wāʾōklēm — "When I found your words, I devoured them"; ʾākal (eat/devour) applied to the divine word; cf. Ezek 2:8-3:3 (Ezekiel literally eats the scroll). The MKT's "devoured them" preserves the physical urgency — not leisurely reading but consuming the words. "Your word was my joy and the delight of my heart" (śāśôn wĕśimḥat lĕbābî): the word that causes Jeremiah such suffering is simultaneously his deepest joy.
15.17: lōʾ-yāšabtî bĕsôd-mĕśaḥăqîm — "I did not sit in the company of those who celebrate"; Jeremiah's separation from normal social life because of his prophetic call. "I sat alone (bādād) because your hand was on me" — bādād (alone, isolated) is the word used for the isolated leper (Lev 13:46) and for Jerusalem destroyed (Lam 1:1: "How desolate she sits, the city once so full of people"). Prophetic isolation as anticipatory solidarity with the exiled city.
15.18: lāmâ hāyâ kĕʾēbî nĕtsaḥ ûmakkātî ʾănûšâ — "Why is my pain unending and my wound incurable?" The daring accusation that follows: "Are you really going to be to me like a deceptive stream, like waters that fail?" (hĕyôt tihyeh lî kĕʾakzāb). ʾAkzāb is a wadi that appears full in winter but dries up in summer — the most pointed image for unreliable presence. The MKT's "deceptive stream" preserves the shocking accusation that YHWH might be like the failed water-sources of the drought.
15.19: ʾim-tāšûb wĕʾăšîbĕkā — "If you return, I will restore you"; the double-šûb construction: Jeremiah's return (repentance/reorientation) is met by YHWH's returning him to standing. Lĕpānay taʿămōd — "you will stand before me" — the prophetic standing-before-YHWH is restored. "If you speak what is precious rather than what is worthless" — the prophet's speech must be reoriented before his commission is recommissioned.
15.20: lĕḥômâ nĕḥōšet bĕṣûrâ — "a fortified bronze wall" — YHWH's recommissioning of Jeremiah as an impregnable wall of bronze. This parallels the original commissioning of 1:18 where he was made "a fortified city, an iron pillar, a bronze wall." The recommissioning after Jeremiah's complaint restores him to his original calling with the same protective metaphor.
15.21: wĕhiṣṣaltîkā miyyad rāʿîm — "I will deliver you from the power of the wicked"; the final assurance completing the recommissioning. Mikkap ʿārîṣîm ʾepĕddekā — "I will rescue you from the grasp of the ruthless"; ʿārîṣ (ruthless one, violent tyrant) describes Jeremiah's opponents. The divine rescue is YHWH's responsibility, not something Jeremiah must earn.

16.1: A fresh oracle-introduction: wayĕhî dĕbar-YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr — the standard prophetic formula for a new divine word. The MKT follows MT in marking this as a new unit. The commands that follow (celibacy, no mourning, no feasting) are sign-acts, not merely moral instructions.
16.2: lōʾ-tiqqaḥ lĕkā ʾiššâ — "You must not marry or have sons or daughters in this place"; celibacy as prophetic sign-act — one of the most unusual divine commands given to an OT prophet. The MKT renders "in this place" (bammāqôm hazzeh) as specifying Jerusalem — marriage and family in a place destined for destruction would be an act of false hope. The celibacy enacts the end of normal life.
16.3: The reason for the marriage-prohibition stated explicitly: sons and daughters born in this place will die of terrible diseases (bĕmōtê taḥalûʾîm). Their mothers and fathers will also die without the covenant dignities of mourning and burial. The MKT follows MT in listing the deaths of parents alongside children — the sign-act encompasses whole family systems.
16.4: bĕmōtê taḥalûʾîm — "diseases" or "terrible diseases"; taḥalûʾ means a sickness or wasting disease. "Like dung on the surface of the ground" (kĕdōmen ʿal-pĕnê hāʾădāmâ) — the covenant curse of unburied dead (Deut 28:26). The MKT preserves the dignity-stripping directness: no mourning, no burial, just corpses as manure.
16.5: bêt marzēaḥ — "house of mourning"; marzēaḥ was a formal mourning institution or feast associated with death in the ancient Near East. YHWH withdraws his šālôm (well-being), ḥesed (covenant loyalty), and raḥămîm (compassion) from this generation. The MKT renders all three terms because each represents a different covenantal quality being suspended.
16.6: The prohibition on the normal mourning rites: no cutting (lōʾ-yissĕrĕṭû) and no shaving one's head (lōʾ-yiqqārĕḥû) for the dead. These were standard mourning gestures (though the Torah also restricted them, Lev 19:28; 21:5). The MKT follows MT in specifying both rites — the complete social apparatus of mourning is suspended.
16.7: lōʾ-yiprĕsû lāhem leḥem ʿal-ʾēbel — "No one will offer food to the mourner to comfort him for the dead"; the breaking of bread with mourners was the social solidarity ritual. "The cup of consolation" (kôs tanaḥûmîm) was a ceremonial cup given to the bereaved. The suspension of both communal mourning customs signals the breakdown of society's capacity for mutual comfort.
16.8: The second prohibition: bêt mišteh (house of feasting) is also off-limits. Where v.5 forbids mourning and v.8 forbids feasting, Jeremiah's entire social existence — from grief to celebration — is withdrawn. This total social isolation enacts the coming desolation when both mourning and feasting will be silenced (see 7:34).
16.9: mašbît mizzeh-hammāqôm lĕʿênêkem ûbîmêkem — "I am about to silence in this place — within your own sight and in your own days"; YHWH will silence qôl śāśôn wĕqôl śimḥâ qôl ḥātān wĕqôl kallâ — the bridal-joy four-phrase formula (cf. 7:34; 25:10). The temporal specification "within your own sight and in your own days" means those alive now will witness this.
16.10: The people's anticipated question: why has YHWH declared all this disaster? The question uses the covenant-word rāʿâ (evil, disaster) — the people recognize the announcement as disaster but not yet as their fault. The MKT preserves the naive question ("What wrong have we done?") because it sets up the specific answer in vv. 11-13.
16.11: taḥat ʾăšer-ʿāzĕbû ʾôtî ʾăbôtêkem — "Because your ancestors forsook me"; the generational dimension of apostasy — the ancestors' rejection of YHWH. The fourfold description of their idolatry: went after (hālĕkû ʾaḥărê), served (wayyaʿăbdûm), worshipped (wayyištaḥăwû lāhem), forsook (wĕʾōtî ʿāzābû) — four verbs of increasing commitment to idolatry.
16.12: waʾattem hăraʿōtem laʿăśôt — "you have done even worse than your ancestors" — the downward spiral of apostasy. "Every one of you follows the stubborn evil of his own heart" (ʾîš ʾaḥărê šĕrîrût libbô hārāʿ): šĕrîrût lēb (stubbornness of heart) is Jeremiah's diagnostic phrase. The refusal to "listen to me" (lĕbiltî šĕmōaʿ ʾēlay) — the covenant obligation of hearing-obedience abandoned.
16.13: wĕhêtaltî ʾetkem mēʿal hāʾādāmâ — "I will cast you out of this land"; hētaltî (hurl, fling) conveys a violent expulsion. "Into a land that neither you nor your ancestors have ever known" — the reversal of the covenant land-gift. "There you may serve other gods day and night" — the bitterly ironic permission: you wanted idols, you can have them in exile without YHWH.
16.14: ʾim-lōʾ hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm — "But days are coming"; the transition from judgment to restoration oracle. The new exodus will supersede the Exodus formula: "as the LORD lives who brought Israel out of Egypt" will be replaced by a new oath formula. The MKT's "people will no longer say: As the LORD lives who brought Israel out of Egypt" preserves the surprising eclipse of the foundational confession.
16.15: ʾim-kî ḥay-YHWH ʾăšer heʿĕlâ ʾet-bĕnê Yiśrāʾēl mēʾerets ṣāpôn — "As the LORD lives who brought Israel back from the land of the north"; the second exodus will define YHWH more than the Egyptian exodus. "From all the countries where he had driven them" (mikkol hāʾărāṣôt ʾăšer hiddîḥam šāmmâ) — the global scatter reversed by a global gather. The MKT preserves the geographical specificity: north and all countries.
16.16: hinnĕnî šōlēaḥ lĕdayyāgîm rabbîm — "I am sending for many fishermen" — YHWH as the one who sends agents of judgment. The fishermen and hunters are metaphors for the Babylonian armies and their systematic capture of the exiles. The imagery moves from fishing to hunting: both are thorough — no one escapes a comprehensive catch.
16.17: kî ʿênay ʿal-kol-darkêhem — "For my eyes are on all their ways"; the divine omniscience provides the warrant for comprehensive judgment. Lōʾ nistarû minnĕged pānāy — "they are not hidden from my sight" — the absolute transparency of human action before YHWH. Wĕlōʾ niṣpan ʿăwōnām minnĕged ʿênāy — even their guilt (ʿāwōn) is not hidden.
16.18: ûšillāmtî rīšōnâ mišneh ʿăwōnām wĕḥaṭṭāʾtām — "First I will repay their guilt and their sin in double measure"; mišneh (double) — the full covenant-justice repayment. "Because they have defiled my land with the lifeless images of their detestable idols" (nigbĕlātêhem) — the land-pollution language frames idolatry as a pollution crime against the covenantal territory.
16.19: YHWH ʿuzzî ûmāʿuzzî ûmānôsî bĕyôm ṣārâ — "O LORD, my strength and my fortified refuge in the day of distress"; Jeremiah's prayer of confidence framed as a future hope. The nations coming from the ends of the earth (ʾaprĕsê-ʾārets) is a vision of universal recognition of YHWH's supremacy. Their confession: "our ancestors inherited nothing but falsehood" (lōʾ-mōʿîl) — the idols are exposed as useless.
16.20: hăyaʿăśeh-lô ʾādām ʾĕlōhîm — "Can a person make gods for himself?" The rhetorical question exposes the absurdity of idol-making. "But they are no gods at all" (wĕhēmmâ lōʾ ʾĕlōhîm) — the simple declaration. The surrounding nations' confession (vv. 19-20) makes this statement ecumenically significant: even Gentiles will recognize the idols' non-existence.
16.21: hinnĕnî môdîʿām bappaʿam hazzōʾt — "I am going to make them know — this time I will make them know my hand and my strength"; the double yādaʿ (know) — two different qal forms for knowing by experience. Yādĕʿû ʾet-šĕmî YHWH — they will know my name is YHWH. The formula "that I am the LORD" (the recognition formula) is the goal of the entire judgment sequence.

17.1: ḥaṭṭāʾt Yĕhûdâ kĕtûbâ bĕʿēt barzel bĕṣippōren šāmîr — "Judah's sin is written with an iron stylus; with a diamond tip it is engraved on the tablet of their heart"; lûaḥ libbām (the tablet of their heart) directly anticipates the new covenant promise: Jer 31:33 promises the law written on the heart as blessing; here the sin is already written on the heart as curse. The MKT preserves the double inscription: on the heart (lûaḥ libbām) and on the horns of the altars (qarnôt mizbĕḥôtêkem).
17.2: kĕzĕkor bĕnêhem mizbĕḥôtām — "even their children remember their altars and their Asherah poles"; the intergenerational transmission of idolatry — the same cultural-transmission concern as 9:14. Children who remember the pagan cult sites means the practice is being taught as heritage, not abandoned as sin. "Every spreading tree on every high hill" — the stereotyped formula for hilltop-shrine Baal worship.
17.3: harî baśśādeh ḥêlĕkā kol-ʾôtsĕrôtêkā — "O mountain in the open country, I will hand over your wealth and all your treasures as plunder"; the "mountain" may refer to Jerusalem (Zion). The high shrines (bāmôtêkā) become the specific cause of the plunder: the cultic sites are both the site of sin and what triggers the punishment.
17.4: ûšāmaṭtā ûĕʾanāḥaltĕkā ʾăšer nātattî lāk — "through your own actions you will forfeit the inheritance I gave you"; šāmaṭ in the niphal means to release, let drop — the inheritance slips away through the people's own apostasy. "I will make you serve your enemies in a land you do not know" — the reversal from service of YHWH to service of enemies.
17.5: ʾārûr haggeber ʾăšer yibṭaḥ bāʾādām — "Cursed is the one who puts his trust in human beings"; the curse-formula (ʾārûr) matches the blessing-formula of v.7 (bārûk). Bāṭaḥ bāʾādām (trusts in humanity) vs. bāṭaḥ baYHWH (trusts in YHWH) — the same verb exposes the choice. The MKT's "who turns his heart from the LORD" follows MT: wĕšāb libbô mēʾet YHWH.
17.6: kĕʿarôʿêr bāʿărābâ — "like a desert shrub" (ʿarôʿêr = juniper/tamarisk in the desert); the cursed person's condition: dwelling in parched places (ḥărērîm bammidbar), unable to benefit from good when it comes. The contrast with v.8's tree-by-water will be stark. The MKT follows the tree/shrub imagery that connects directly to Psalm 1.
17.7: bārûk haggeber ʾăšer yibṭaḥ baYHWH — "Blessed is the one who trusts in the LORD"; the beatitude formula that mirrors Psalm 1:1-3 almost verbatim. The parallel structure (cursed/v.5-6 :: blessed/v.7-8) is the two-ways teaching at its most concentrated. Ûhāyâ YHWH mibṭaḥô — the LORD is his security (mibṭāḥ = place of trust/security).
17.8: kĕʿēts šātûl ʿal-mayim — "like a tree planted beside a stream"; the parallel with Psalm 1:3 is very close (kĕʿēts šātûl ʿal-palgê mayim). The tree's roots reach the water source (ʿal-yûbal yĕšallaḥ šorāšāyw). The phrase "it does not fear when the heat comes" (wĕlōʾ yirʾeh kî-yābôʾ ḥōm) — security through deep rootedness rather than superficial circumstances.
17.9: ʿāqōb hallēb mikkōl ûʾānûš hûʾ — "The heart is more deceitful than anything else and is desperately sick"; ʿāqōb (deceitful, treacherous) shares the root with Jacob's name (yaʿăqōb) — the heart that supplants and deceives. ʾĀnûš (incurable, desperately sick, weak) — the word used for incurable wounds (cf. 15:18). The rhetorical question mî yēdāʿennû (who can understand it?) is answered in v.10.
17.10: ʾănî YHWH ḥōqēr lēb bōḥēn kĕlāyôt — "I, the LORD, examine the heart and test the inner mind (kĕlāyôt, kidneys/inner being)"; YHWH as the only adequate diagnostician of the human heart. Kĕlāyôt (kidneys/reins) was considered the seat of the deepest inner life, emotions, and will in Hebrew anthropology. The MKT's "inner mind" represents an attempt to convey what "kidneys" meant to ancient Israelites. The purpose: to give everyone what their conduct deserves — the heart-examiner is the perfectly just rewarder.
17.11: kĕqōrēʾ dāgar wĕlōʾ yālād — "like a partridge brooding over eggs she did not lay"; the qōrēʾ partridge was believed to steal and incubate other birds' eggs (the accuracy of this natural history is debated, but the proverb was current). The image: ill-gotten wealth is like stolen eggs — it hatches wealth that abandons its owner. The MKT preserves the proverb rather than explaining it away.
17.12: kissēʾ kābôd mārôm mĕrōšōn — "A glorious throne, exalted from the beginning" — a sudden praise-hymn for YHWH's heavenly throne. The juxtaposition with v.11 (the partridge who loses his wealth) and v.13 (those who forsake YHWH written in the dust) frames the throne-verse as a theological anchor: YHWH's throne is the fixed point against which all human pretension is measured.
17.13: YHWH miqweh Yiśrāʾēl — "O LORD, the hope of Israel"; the miqweh-title from 14:8 appears again — hope and water-reservoir. "Those who turn from you will have their names written in the dust" (yikkātĕbû bāʾārets) — the earth/dust as the anti-book: names written in dust versus names written in the book of life. The contrast: water-reservoir abandoned for broken cisterns leads to names written in dust rather than kept alive.
17.14: rĕpāʾênî YHWH wĕʾērāpēʾ — "Heal me, O LORD, and I will truly be healed"; the double-rāpāʾ (heal) construction — the source of healing and the healed state are both from YHWH. Similarly "save me, and I will truly be saved" (hôšîʿēnî wĕʾiwwāšēʿâ). The MKT's "truly" captures the emphasis that comes from the doubled verbal root: real/final healing depends entirely on YHWH's action.
17.15: ʾayyēh dĕbar-YHWH yābōʾ-nāʾ — "Where is the word of the LORD? Let it come, if it is real!" The taunt against Jeremiah: his prophecy of doom has not yet materialized. This is the standard challenge to the prophet: your announcement seems empty because the disaster has not arrived. The MKT's "if it is real" renders the skeptical nāʾ particle in context.
17.16: wĕʾănî lōʾ-ʾatsti mērōʿeh ʾaḥărêkā — "But I have not run away from the calling of being your shepherd"; rōʿeh (shepherd) here applied to Jeremiah as a pastor/shepherd figure following YHWH. He has not avoided (ʾûts min-) the painful calling. The MKT's "the calling of being your shepherd" is an interpretive rendering of the difficult phrase.
17.17: ʾal-tĕhî lî lĕmĕḥittâ — "Do not be a source of dread to me"; mĕḥittâ is terror, dread, dismay. Jeremiah asks that YHWH himself not become the source of his terror — a prayer that the divine presence remain as shelter (maḥseh) rather than threat. The MKT renders "you are my shelter in the day of disaster" following the positive formulation.
17.18: yēbōšû rōdĕpay wĕʾal-ʾēbōšâ ʾānî — "Let those who persecute me be shamed — but not me"; Jeremiah's imprecation against his persecutors. The threefold structure: shame/dismay/disaster — all the elements he fears for himself he asks YHWH to redirect onto his enemies. The MKT preserves the first-person singular throughout to maintain the intensity of the personal prayer.
17.19: šaʿar bĕnê-ʿām — "the People's Gate"; the location and the audience (malkê Yĕhûdâ, kings of Judah; kol-Yĕhûdâ, all Judah; yōšĕbê Yĕrûšālaim, inhabitants of Jerusalem) are given in full. The public proclamation at a major gate mirrors the temple sermon of ch. 7 in its public setting.
17.20: Jeremiah is commissioned to call kings, Judah, and Jerusalem to hear the word of YHWH — the full public address formula. The inclusivity of the audience (kings to common people) emphasizes that the Sabbath command applies to all social levels equally.
17.21: šimĕrû napsôtêkem wĕʾal-tiśśĕʾû massāʾ bĕyôm haššabbāt — "Take care for your own sakes and do not carry any load on the Sabbath day"; šāmar nepeš (take care of yourself) implies the Sabbath prohibition is a matter of self-preservation, not merely legal compliance. The MKT's "take care for your own sakes" captures the reflexive force.
17.22: wĕlōʾ-tōṣîʾû massāʾ mibattêkem bĕyôm haššabbāt — "Do not carry burdens out of your houses on the Sabbath day"; the prohibition applies to outward commerce as well as inward carrying. "Keep the Sabbath day holy as I commanded your ancestors" (wĕqiddaštĕm ʾet-yôm haššabbāt kaʾăšer ṣiwwîtî ʾet-ʾăbôtêkem) — the ancestral command provides the authority for the current restatement.
17.23: wĕlōʾ šāmĕʿû wĕlōʾ hiṭṭû ʾet-ʾoznām — "But they did not listen or pay attention"; the standard refrain. Wayyaqšû ʾet-ʿarpām (stiffened their neck) — the qāšeh ʿōrep motif again (cf. 7:26). The pattern: instruction given → stiff-necked refusal → judgment. The historical precedent provides the context for the current conditional warnings.
17.24: The positive conditional: wĕhāyâ ʾim-šāmōaʿ tišmĕʿûn — the emphatic conditional ("if you truly listen to me"; infinitive absolute). If Sabbath is kept, the Davidic throne is preserved. The conditional structure mirrors the temple sermon (7:3-7): the covenant's blessing depends on covenant-keeping. MKT preserves the "truly" construction.
17.25: ûbāʾû bĕšaʿărê hāʿîr hazzōʾt mĕlākim wĕśārîm — "then kings and princes sitting on David's throne will enter through the gates" — the restoration of the full Davidic political order is the positive consequence of Sabbath-keeping. The image of royal procession through the city gates (rōkĕbîm bārékeb ûvassûsîm) represents the full covenantal blessing in political form.
17.26: The geographic breadth of those who will come — Benjamin, Shephelah, Negeb, and Jerusalem itself — and the range of offerings — burnt offerings (ʿōlôt), sacrifices (zĕbāḥîm), grain offerings (minḥâ), frankincense (lĕbônâ), thank offerings (tôdâ) — represent the full restored worship of the covenant community. The MKT's list follows MT precisely.
17.27: wĕʾim-lōʾ tišmĕʿû ʾēlay lĕqaddēš ʾet-yôm haššabbāt — "But if you will not listen to me — if you refuse to keep the Sabbath day holy"; the negative conditional completes the two-ways structure. The fire that will burn Jerusalem's fortresses (wĕhiṣṣattî ʾēš bĕšĕʿārêhā) is the covenant curse. The MKT preserves the literal "fire into her gates" as the image of total destruction — fire at the gates is the final judgment on the city.

18.1: The messenger formula haddavar asher haya el-Yirmeyahu meyet YHWH lemor opens the oracle unit. The formula marks a major new oracle section beginning with ch18; the potter's house sign-act (chs 18-19) forms a paired sequence with wet clay (malleable, ch18) and fired flask (irreparable, ch19).
18.2: Qum weyaradta bet hayotser — get up and go down to the potter's house. The command uses two verbs (qûm, arise; yārad, go down) — Anathoth being at higher elevation, a potter's workshop in the lower city would involve an actual descent. Weshamma ashmia et-devaray — and there I will make you hear my words: YHWH will use the visual scene to deliver the oracle — the lived observation precedes the verbal interpretation, a pattern common to Jeremiah's sign-acts.
18.3: Waered beit hayotser wehineh-hu oseh melacha al-ha-obnayim — I went down to the potter's house and there he was, working at the wheel. Hāʾobnayim (the two stones) is the technical term for the potter's wheel — a lower stone turned by the feet driving an upper stone on which the clay rested; the dual form captures the two-disc mechanism. The observation is matter-of-fact reportage, grounding the sign-act in observable physical reality before the oracle provides its interpretation.
18.4: Venishhat hakli asher hu oseh beyad hachomet — the clay vessel he was shaping was ruined in the potter's hand. Šāḥat (Niphal, be ruined/marred) — the clay did not respond properly; something in the clay itself resisted the forming. Wayashov wayaasehu keli acher kaasher yashar beinei hayotser laasot — so he reworked it into another vessel, as seemed right in the potter's eyes. The key phrase is kaʾăšer yāšar bĕʿênê hayyôṣēr — according to what was right in the potter's sight, not the clay's. The potter's sovereign judgment about what the clay should become is the theological point the following oracle will develop.
18.5: The interpretive oracle frame: wayehi devar-YHWH elay lemor — the word of YHWH came to me. The sign-act observation (vv3-4) is complete; the oracle follows as YHWH's authoritative interpretation of what Jeremiah saw.
18.6: Hacheyinner hayotser hazzeh lo uchal laasot lachem beit Yisrael — can I not deal with you as this potter has done with clay, O house of Israel? The rhetorical question (hăkĕ- prefix expecting a yes-answer) establishes the analogy. The key theological move: the potter does not destroy flawed clay, he reshapes it. YHWH as potter is not asserting a right to destroy Israel capriciously but a capacity to reshape them according to his sovereign purpose. Ke-chomer beyad hayotser ken atem beyadi beit Yisrael — like clay in the potter's hand, so you are in my hand. The simile is exact: the relationship of total sovereign control with capacity for remedial reshaping.
18.7: Reˁga adabber ˁal-goy veˁal-mamlakhah liˁtosh velintotz veliˁabed — at one moment I may speak concerning a nation or kingdom, to uproot, tear down, and destroy it. The three verbs (nāṯaš, nātaṣ, ʾāḇad) are three of the six commission-verbs of 1:10 — Jeremiah's call is now being given its operational framework: the announced destruction is not inevitable.
18.8: Veshav hagoy hahu meraˁato asher dibarti ˁalav venichamti ˁal haraˁa asher hashavti laˁasot lo — but if that nation turns (šāb) from its evil about which I spoke, I will relent (wĕniḥamtî) concerning the disaster I intended to bring on it. The verb nāḥam (Niphal, relent/repent/be comforted) is applied to YHWH — YHWH genuinely responds to human response. The Niphal is the reflexive: YHWH turns himself from the announced decree. This is the theological principle that undergirds the entire book's call to repentance.
18.9: Veraˁga adabber ˁal-goy veˁal-mamlakhah livnot velintoˁa — and at another moment I may declare concerning a nation or kingdom that I will build and plant it. The remaining two commission-verbs of 1:10 (bānâ, nāṭaʿ) are now made conditional — they too can be revoked.
18.10: Veˁasa haraˁa beˁeinai leshemoˁa beqoli venichamti ˁal hatova asher amarti lehitiv oto — but if it does evil in my eyes, not listening to my voice, I will relent of the good I said I would do for it. The symmetry is complete: just as announced judgment can be averted by repentance, announced blessing can be withdrawn by apostasy. This is not divine fickleness but divine responsiveness — YHWH's decrees are conditional on human covenantal fidelity. The principle becomes operative immediately in v11 for Judah.
18.11: Veˁatta emor-na el-ish Yehuda veˁal yoshve Yerushalayim lemor — and now speak to the men of Judah and inhabitants of Jerusalem. Hineni yotser aleichem raˁa — I am forming (yōṣēr, the potter-participle) disaster against you. YHWH is the active potter shaping the disaster just as the Jerusalem potter shaped his clay — the present participle (yōṣēr) signals ongoing action, not completed decision. Ushvu na ish midarkam haraˁa veheitivu darkechem umaˁalleichem — turn back each from his evil way and amend your ways and your deeds. The call to šûb (return/repent) is both collective and individual (ʾîš, each man).
18.12: Veamru noash ki-acharei machshevotenu nelech — but they say, It is hopeless (nôʾāš)! We will follow our own plans. Nôʾāš is the same word of resigned despair used in 2:25 — the people have moved beyond the feeling of hopelessness to the deliberate choice of it. Veish sherirut libo haraˁa naˁaseh — and each one will act according to the stubbornness of his evil heart (šĕrîrût libbô hārāˁâ). The phrase šĕrîrût lēḇ (stubbornness of heart) is Jeremiah's recurring term for the incorrigibility that makes repentance impossible (cf. 3:17; 7:24; 9:14; 11:8; 16:12).
18.13: Lachen koh amar YHWH shellu-na bagoyim — therefore thus says YHWH, ask among the nations. The divine appeal to the nations as external witness (cf. 2:10-11) returns: Israel's behavior is so anomalous that even the pagan nations would find it incredible. Naˁarat Yisrael ˁasta shaˁaruriya meod — the virgin Israel has done a truly horrifying thing (šaʿărûrît, a thing that causes the hair to stand on end, from śāˁar, hair/storm).
18.14: Hayaˁazov misheleg Levanon tsur hassade — does the snow of Lebanon leave the mountain crags? The two natural phenomena — Lebanon's permanent snow and the cold mountain streams — are used as standards of constancy to indict Israel's inconstancy. Ayin tsinnurim mayim zarim kefuyim — do the cold flowing waters from afar run dry? The cold mountain streams flowing continuously from distant heights are reliable precisely because they are fed by a source they did not create. Israel had such a source (YHWH, the spring of living water, 2:13) and abandoned it.
18.15: Ki shachechuni ˁammi lashav yaqteru — for my people have forgotten me; they burn offerings to worthless things (lāšāwĕʾ, to vanity/emptiness). Wayikashlu bedarcheyhem shevilot ˁolam — they have stumbled from the ancient paths (šĕḇîlôt ʿôlām, the age-old trails). The ancient paths are the established covenant way; departing from them is not progress but stumbling — the directional metaphor implies not freedom but disorientation.
18.16: Lasim artsam leshamma lishreqat ˁolam — to make their land a desolation, a thing to be hissed at forever. Šerîqâ (hissing) is the onomatopoeic term for the sound passersby make when they see a devastated city — a universal cultural response to shocking destruction (cf. 1 Kings 9:8; Lam 2:15; Zeph 2:15). Kol-ˁover ˁaleyha yisham veyanoˁa berosho — everyone who passes will be appalled and shake his head: the head-shaking (nûaʿ bĕrōʾš) is the visual expression of stunned disbelief at what one sees.
18.17: Keruach qadim apitsem lifney oyev — like the east wind I will scatter them before the enemy. The east wind (rûaḥ qādîm) is the hamsin — the devastating hot wind that parches crops and destroys vegetation, an instrument of agricultural judgment (cf. Gen 41:6; Hos 13:15). ˁoref veloˁ-fanim erehˁem beyom ˁedam — my back and not my face I will show them in the day of their calamity. The contrast with the Aaronic blessing (yāʾēr YHWH pānāyw ʾēlêkā, Num 6:25) is total: the covenant face that shines on the blessed is replaced by the back of the departing God — the ultimate sign of abandoned covenant relationship.
18.18: Veyo-meru lechu venachsheva ˁal Yirmeyahu — then they said, Come, let us devise plans against Jeremiah. The conspiracy invokes institutional authority: ki lo-tovad Torah mikkohen veˁetsa mechakham vedavar minnevi — for instruction will not perish from the priest, counsel from the wise man, and word from the prophet. The three prophetic/wisdom streams (priest's tôrâ, sage's ʿēṣâ, prophet's dāḇār) are cited as proof that Jeremiah's radical message can be ignored — there are other authoritative voices. This is exactly the false-security thinking the book condemns throughout.
18.19: Hashiva YHWH elay ushmаˁ leqol yereivay — give attention to me, YHWH, and listen to the voice of those who contend with me. The fifth Confession begins (vv19-23). Jeremiah's switch from petition to imprecation follows the pattern of the individual lament psalms — from complaint to petition to trust, but in Jeremiah the resolution is darker. Rîḇ (contend/lawsuit) reappears — Jeremiah's adversaries are conducting a legal action against him.
18.20: Hayushalam tachat-tova raˁa ki karu shuchat lenafshi — is evil repaid for good? For they have dug a pit for my life. The rhetorical question inverts the lex talionis principle: Jeremiah interceded for the very people who now plot against him (kî-ˁāmadtî lĕpānêkā lĕdabbēr ʿălêhem ṭôḇ — I stood before you to speak good on their behalf). The intercession Jeremiah performed becomes the basis for his complaint that evil is returned for his good.
18.21: Lachen ten et-benehem laraˁav — therefore hand their children over to famine. Jeremiah's imprecatory prayer against his persecutors escalates through a comprehensive curse: famine for the children, sword for the men, plague for the adults, battle-death for the young men. The imprecation borrows the covenant curse vocabulary of Deuteronomy 28 — the very judgments Jeremiah has been announcing for the nation, he now invokes specifically on those who attacked him.
18.22: Tishmaˁ zeˁaqa mibteyhem behavi ˁaleyhem gedud pitem — let a cry be heard from their houses when you bring marauders against them suddenly. Gedûd (band of raiders, marauders) is the military unit that makes surprise attacks. Ki charu shuchat lelupdeni ulpachim tamnenu lereˁelay — for they have dug a pit to trap me and hidden snares for my feet: the trap-metaphors (pit and snare) are hunting imagery applied to the prophet's enemies.
18.23: Veatta YHWH yadaˁta et-kol-ˁatsatam ˁalay lamavet — but you, YHWH, know all their plots against my life. The imprecation ends with an appeal to YHWH as the omniscient judge (yādaʿtā, you know — the same yādaʿ of relational knowing as in 1:5). Al-tekapper ˁal-ˁavonam vehatatam milfanecha al-timcheh — do not forgive their guilt or blot out their sin from your sight. The reversal of the standard intercessory prayer (normally forgive the sin) is stark — Jeremiah explicitly asks YHWH not to forgive. This is the most morally disturbing of the Confessions and must be read as prophetic identification with the covenant's judgment-logic rather than personal vindictiveness.

19.1: Koh amar YHWH lech veqanita baqbuq yotser cheres — thus says YHWH, Go and buy a potter's clay flask (baqbûq). The baqbûq is a narrow-necked bottle that makes a gurgling sound (baqbaq) when poured — onomatopoeic naming from the liquid-sound. Unlike the wet clay of ch18 (which could be reshaped), the fired ceramic flask is fixed; once broken, it cannot be repaired. This distinction between the two oracle-vessels is the structural key to the sequence. Umiziqney haˁam — some of the elders of the people: witnesses are required for the prophetic sign-act, giving it legal standing.
19.2: Veyatsata el-gey ven-Hinnom asher petach shaˁar hacharsit — go out to the valley of Ben-hinnom at the entrance of the Potsherd Gate. The Potsherd Gate (šaʿar heharsît) is the gate through which pottery shards (broken vessels) were discarded — the potter's dump. The location makes the symbolic action visible: bringing a whole flask to the place where broken pottery accumulates, then smashing it.
19.3: Vequrata sham et-divrey asher adaber eilecha — and proclaim there the words I speak to you. The double audience — kings of Judah (malkey Yehuda) and people of Jerusalem — frames the oracle as a formal legal proclamation before witnesses. Hineni mevi raˁa el-hamakom hazze — I am bringing disaster on this place. Tsalal oznav (his ears will ring) — the resonance of the news will be physically experienced.
19.4: Three charges: ˁazavuni (they abandoned me); wayenakru et-hamakom hazze (made this place foreign — nāḵrî, alien to its covenant identity); and offered incense to gods asher lo yadaˁum (that they did not know — the yādaʿ of covenant relationship: gods with whom there is no covenant bond). Umileu et-hamakom hazze dam neqiim — they have filled this place with the blood of the innocent: the child sacrifice that will be specified in v5.
19.5: Uvanyu et-bamat haBaˁal lisrof et-benehem baesh ˁolot laBaˁal — they have built the high places of Baal to burn their sons as burnt offerings to Baal. The horror of child sacrifice (Molek-tophet cult) is given its most explicit description. The threefold denial that YHWH ever sanctioned this: asher lo-tsiviti velo-dibbarti velo-ˁalta ˁal-libbi — which I never commanded, never spoke of, never entered my mind. The escalating negations move from formal command (ṣāwâ) to spoken word (dibbēr) to even mental conception (ˁālâ ʿal-lēḇ) — the total repudiation goes beyond law to reject the cultic innovation at the deepest level of divine consciousness.
19.6: Lachen hinneh yamim baim neˁum YHWH velo yiqare lamakom hazze ˁod haTofet ugey ven-Hinnom ki im-gey haharega — therefore the days are coming when this place will no longer be called Tophet or the Valley of Ben-hinnom, but the Valley of Slaughter (gêʾ hahereḡâ). The renaming is a prophetic speech act — the new name embodies the new reality. Tophet (possibly from tōp, drum, used to drown out the cries of child sacrifice, or from an Aramaic word for fireplace) is renamed to announce its fate as a mass grave.
19.7: Vebaqoti et-ˁatsat Yehuda viYerushalayim bamakom hazze — I will empty out (ûḇaqqōtî, from bāqaq) the plans of Judah and Jerusalem in this place. The verb bāqaq (to empty, to pour out) is a deliberate pun on baqbûq (the flask, v1) — YHWH will do to Judah's plans what will be done to the flask: pour them out, empty them. The wordplay grounds the sign-act interpretation linguistically. Vehifaltem beherev lifney oyvehem — I will make them fall by the sword before their enemies.
19.8: Venatattiha leshshama ulishriqah — I will make this city a desolation and a thing to be hissed at (šerîqâ, the onomatopoeic hissing sound of v16:16 — the passerby's audible shock). Kol ˁover ˁaleyha yisham vishraq ˁal-kol-makkoteyha — everyone who passes will be appalled and hiss at all its wounds (makkôt, blows/plagues). The wound-metaphor for a city's destruction is vivid: the city has been struck, and the marks remain visible to passersby.
19.9: Vehoˁalti otam basar benehem uvesar benoteihem — I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and daughters. The extreme of siege cannibalism is the most severe covenant curse (cf. Deut 28:53-57; Lev 26:29) — when the siege cuts off food supply, the ultimate boundary is crossed. The phrase uvmatsor uvamatsok asher yetsiru lahem oyvehem — in the desperate siege and distress that their enemies impose on them — grounds the prediction in the historical reality of siege warfare rather than making it supernatural intervention.
19.10: Ushavarta et-habbaqbuq leˁeney haanashim haholchim itach — then break the flask before the men who have come with you. The physical act of smashing the fired clay vessel completes the sign-act. The witnesses see the irreversibility: fired clay shattered into fragments cannot be put back together. This is the decisive difference from ch18 — malleable clay can be reshaped, but the broken flask is an image of judgment completed beyond repair.
19.11: Koh amar YHWH tsvaot kachah eshbor et-haˁam hazze veˁet haˁir hazze — thus says YHWH of hosts: so I will break this people and this city as one breaks a potter's vessel that can never be repaired (asher lo-yukhal lehirafeˁ ˁôd). The word rāpāʾ (repair/heal) is the same verb used of healing wounds and diseases throughout the OT prophets — including Jeremiah's own promise of healing in 30:17. Here it is negated: the break is beyond healing by ordinary means.
19.12: Ken eˁaseh lemakom hazze neˁum YHWH — so I will do to this place. The oracle is grounded in the sign-act: the smashing of the flask enacts the divine decision, making it present and certain before it is historically accomplished. The sign-acts of Jeremiah are not merely illustrative but performative — they participate in the reality they depict.
19.13: Vehayeta batey Yerushalayim uvitey malkey Yehuda ketomeh kemakom haTofet — the houses of Jerusalem and the houses of the kings of Judah will be defiled like Tophet. Lechol habatim asher qitteru ˁal-gaggotehem lechol tsva hashamayim — all the houses on whose rooftops they burned incense to all the host of heaven. Rooftop incense-burning (gag, roof) is the specific cultic practice associated with astral worship — the host of heaven (ṣĕḇāʾ haššāmayim) refers to the sun, moon, and stars as divine beings, a Mesopotamian influence that Josiah's reform had attempted to eradicate (2 Kings 23:5).
19.14: Wayavo Yirmeyahu min-haTofet asher shlaho YHWH sham lehinnave — Jeremiah returned from Tophet where YHWH had sent him to prophesy. The oracle at Tophet is complete; Jeremiah now moves to the temple court (chatsar beit YHWH) for the second public proclamation to a wider audience.
19.15: Hineni mevi el-haˁir hazze veˁal kol-ˁarehah et kol-haraˁa asher dibbarti ˁaleyha — I am going to bring on this city and on all its surrounding towns every disaster I have spoken against it. Ki-hiqqashu et-ˁorfam leilti shmoˁa et-devaray — because they have stiffened their neck (hiqqāšû ʾet-ʿorpām, made their neck hard/stiff) so as not to hear my words. The stiff-neck metaphor (qāšeh ʿōrep) is the Exodus tradition's description of Israel's resistance (Exod 32:9; 33:3,5; Deut 9:6,13) — Jeremiah invokes the foundational pattern of covenant resistance that culminated at Sinai.

20.1: Vayyishmaˁ Pashhur ben-Immer hakohen vehu fagid nagid beyt YHWH et Yirmeyahu mitnabbe et-hadevarim haele — Pashhur son of Immer, the priest who was chief officer in the house of YHWH, heard Jeremiah prophesying these things. Nāgîd (chief officer, leader) — the administrative head of the temple; this is not the same Pashhur as in ch21 (son of Malkijah vs. son of Immer). The institutional authority that will suppress Jeremiah is rooted in the temple hierarchy itself.
20.2: Vayach Pashhur et Yirmeyahu hanavi vayiten oto el-hamahpechet — Pashhur struck (hiḵkâ, Hiphil causative) Jeremiah the prophet and put him in the stocks (mahpeḵet, an instrument of physical confinement and pain). Mahpeḵet comes from hāpaḵ (to turn/twist) — it is a device that holds the body in an unnatural twisted position. The location, shaˁar Binyamin haˁelyon asher beveyt YHWH (the Upper Benjamin Gate of the temple), places the humiliation publicly within the temple precincts.
20.3: Vayehi mimmohorat vayotse Pashhur et Yirmeyahu min-hamahpechet — the next day Pashhur released Jeremiah from the stocks. Jeremiah's response is the prophetic renaming: lo Pashhur qara YHWH shemecha ki im-Magor Missaviv — YHWH has not named you Pashhur but Magor Missaviv, Terror-on-Every-Side. Māgôr missāḇîḇ (terror from all around) — the phrase that recurs in 6:25 and 20:10 as a terror-cry becomes the new identity of the man who thought to terrorize the prophet. Prophetic renaming in the OT constitutes a new reality, not merely a new label.
20.4: Ki koh amar YHWH hineni notench lemahgor lecha ulchol-ohhavecha — for thus says YHWH: I am making you a terror to yourself and to all your friends (ʾōhăḇêkā, those who love/support you). Venaplu beherev oyvehem veˁeinecha roˁot — they will fall by the sword of their enemies while your eyes watch. The specific horror of the prophecy: Pashhur will live to see his friends slaughtered. Venatattiha ˁal kol-Yehuda beyad melech Bavel — I will hand all Judah over to Babylon.
20.5: Venatatti et kol-ochon haˁir hazze veˁet kol yegiˁah veˁet kol yqaroteha — I will give over all the wealth of this city, all its gains, all its valuables (yĕqārôt, precious things). The threefold enumeration — goods (ḥōsen), labor-products (yĕgîˁâ), precious things (yĕqārôt) — comprehensively describes the accumulated wealth of Jerusalem that will be stripped away. Leoyhvehem — to their enemies: the transfer of accumulated covenant-era prosperity to Babylon is itself a judgment on the misuse of wealth.
20.6: Veˁatta Pashhur vekhol yoshhvey vetekha telku bashvi — and you, Pashhur, with all who live in your household, will go into exile (šeḇî). The oracle closes with the personalized fate of Pashhur: Bavel tavo vesham tamut vesham tiqqaver — you will go to Babylon and there you will die and there you will be buried. Vekhol-ohavecha asher nibbeta lahem basheqer — and all your friends to whom you gave false prophecies (šeqer, falsehood). The priestly official who thought the institutional prophetic channels were sufficient (cf. 18:18) is himself identified as one of those false voices.
