26.10: The officials of Judah come up from the king's house to the temple — the civil leadership enters as a higher court. The trial moves from religious to civil authority, reflecting the institutional complexity of the crisis.
26.11: The priests and prophets speak to the officials and all the people: 'This man deserves the death penalty' (mishpat mavet la'ish hazeh) — a formal legal verdict. The accusation: he has prophesied against this city as you have heard with your own ears. The charge conflates the prophet's message with an attack on the institution.
26.12: Jeremiah's defense: יְהוָה שְׁלָחַנִי (YHWH shelachani, YHWH sent me) — the authority claim. The divine commission is the basis of prophetic immunity; killing the prophet is killing YHWH's messenger. The defense is Christological in its structure: the sent one's authority derives entirely from the sender.
26.13: Now amend your ways (hatzivu darkeykhem) and deeds (ma'alelekhem) and obey the voice of YHWH your God — the prophetic appeal is still open even at the moment of threatened execution. Jeremiah preaches while on trial for his life; YHWH may relent.
26.14: הִנְנִי בְיֶדְכֶם (hineni beydekhem, I am in your hands): Jeremiah submits to their jurisdiction — he does not claim prophetic immunity from civil process. Do to me what seems good and right to you. The passive acceptance of possible death mirrors a pattern: the servant who delivers YHWH's word trusts YHWH with the outcome.
26.15: דַּם נָקִי (dam naqi, innocent blood): the legal category — executing Jeremiah would bring innocent blood on the city and its inhabitants, on YHWH himself (al nafshoteykhem ve'al ha'ir hazot). In covenant law, shedding innocent blood defiles the land (Num 35:33-34). The prophets who plotted against Jeremiah in Anathoth (ch 11) were seeking to shed innocent blood.
26.16: The officials and all the people to the priests and prophets: 'This man does not deserve the death penalty — he has spoken to us in the name of YHWH our God.' The civil officials recognize the distinction between institutional threat and genuine prophetic authority; they acquit.
26.17: Some of the elders of the land (ziqnei ha'aretz) rose to speak — the elders invoked as historical witnesses. They provide the precedent that Jeremiah's message is not unprecedented or deserving of death; it follows the pattern of true prophecy.
26.18: מִיכָה הַמֹּרַשְׁתִּי (Mikhah hamMorashthi, Micah of Moresheth): Micah the prophet (Mic 3:12) is cited as a legal precedent — his contemporary was Hezekiah. צִיּוֹן שָׂדֶה תֵחָרֵשׁ (Tzyon sadeh techaresh, Zion will be plowed as a field): Micah's exact words (Mic 3:12) are quoted — the memory of prophetic words was preserved and invoked as legal precedent generations later.
26.19: Did Hezekiah put Micah to death? — the rhetorical historical question. Hezekiah feared YHWH (yare' et-YHWH) and sought (vayechal) his favor — the king's appropriate response to prophetic warning. And YHWH relented (vayyinnachem) of the disaster announced. The precedent proves: repentance is possible, killing prophets is wrong, and YHWH responds to genuine fear of him.
26.20: The case of Uriah son of Shemaiah — a contemporary prophet who also prophesied against Jerusalem and Judah. He fled to Egypt when Jehoiakim sought to kill him — the contrast with Jeremiah who stayed.
26.21: Jehoiakim sent men to Egypt — Elnathan son of Achbor and others — who brought Uriah back from Egypt. The international reach of royal power to silence prophets demonstrates the danger Jeremiah faced.
26.22: They brought Uriah from Egypt and presented him to King Jehoiakim, who killed him with the sword — the executive execution of a prophet. The contrast with Jeremiah is stark: same message, similar threat, different outcomes. Providence rather than merit determines survival.
26.23: His body was thrown into the burial place of the common people (qivrei benei ha'am) — a dishonorable burial that adds shame to death. The contrast: Jeremiah is preserved to fulfill his calling; Uriah's death is a warning of what prophets risked.
26.24: אֲחִיקָם בֶּן-שָׁפָן (Achiqam ben-Shaphan, Ahikam son of Shaphan): the Shaphan family were consistent supporters of both Josiah's reform and Jeremiah. Ahikam's protection (lo natan oto beyad ha'am lehammito, he did not give him into the hand of the people to put him to death) was decisive. Providence working through covenant-faithful individuals preserves the prophet for his mission.

27.1: The beginning of Zedekiah's reign — the textual note in brackets in many translations (the MT has 'Jehoiakim' but the chapter clearly concerns Zedekiah, a scribal error corrected in most manuscripts). The yoke sign-act is placed in a critical early moment of Zedekiah's reign.
27.2: עֲשֵׂה לְךָ מֹוסֵרוֹת וּמֹטוֹת (aseh lekha moserot umottot, make for yourself bonds and yoke-bars): the yoke-components — moserot are the leather straps/thongs that secure the yoke to the animal's neck; mottot are the wooden crossbars of the yoke. The complete physical apparatus of agricultural servitude becomes a walking prophetic symbol.
27.3: Send the yoke to the ambassadors of Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, and Sidon — the nations whose envoys were gathered in Jerusalem, presumably forming a coalition against Babylon. Jeremiah disrupts the diplomatic conference with YHWH's counterword: submit, do not resist.
27.4: The message to their masters: This is what YHWH of armies, the God of Israel, says to you — the universal divine title. The nations are addressed as subjects of YHWH's word even though they are not Israel's covenant partners; YHWH's sovereignty is total.
27.5: אָנֹכִי עָשִׂיתִי אֶת-הָאָרֶץ (anokhi asiti et-ha'aretz, I made the earth): the creation sovereignty claim grounds the political claim. Because YHWH made the earth, the people, and the animals by his great power and outstretched arm (zeroa netuyah), he has the absolute right to assign ownership to whoever seems good to him (lakol asher yashar be'einav). The political theology of sovereignty: Creator = Owner = Sovereign over all political arrangements.
27.6: עַבְדִּי (avdi, my servant): Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is again called YHWH's servant — the title appears twice (here and ch 25:9). Even the wild animals (chayyot hassadeh) are given to him to serve him. The cosmic servitude of nature to YHWH's appointed ruler is a creation-theology claim.
27.7: All nations (kol-hagoyim) will serve him — his son and his grandson — until the time (ad bo et yomo, until his time comes): a bounded sovereignty. The Babylonian empire is not permanent but has a YHWH-appointed duration. Then many nations and great kings will make him serve them — Babylon will be enslaved as it enslaved others.
27.8: The nation or kingdom that does not serve Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and does not put its neck under his yoke (mottoh) — I will punish with sword, famine, and pestilence (cherev, ra'av, vaDever). Resistance to YHWH's appointed instrument is resistance to YHWH himself; the covenant-curse triad returns.
27.9: אַל-תִּשְׁמְעוּ אֶל-נְבִיאֵיכֶם (al-tishme'u el-nevieyekhem, do not listen to your prophets): followed by a list of false guidance-sources — קֹסְמֵיכֶם (qosmeykhem, your diviners), חֹלְמֵיכֶם (cholmeykhem, your dreamers), עֹנְנֵיכֶם (onneykhem, your soothsayers/cloud-readers), כַּשָּׁפֵיכֶם (kashafeykhem, your sorcerers). The full spectrum of divination practices condemned in Deut 18:10-12. These are the sources that say: 'You will not serve the king of Babylon' — but they lie.
27.10: They prophesy a lie (sheqer) to you — the false prophets promise what the people want to hear. The outcome of following false prophecy: removal (harchiqenu) from your land and expulsion in exile and perishing. Ironically, the prophecies meant to give security produce the catastrophe they promised to prevent.
27.11: But the nation that puts its neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon and serves him — I will leave it in its own land (vehinachtihu al-admatoh). The paradox of submission-as-survival: accepting the yoke is the path to staying in the land; resisting it leads to exile. This theological logic is exactly the kind of counter-intuitive message that got prophets killed.
27.12: Jeremiah speaks the same words to Zedekiah king of Judah: bring your necks under the yoke of the king of Babylon, serve him and his people, and you will live. The personal application of the prophetic word to the king — the message is not general but specific to the moment of decision Zedekiah faces.
27.13: Why should you and your people die by sword, famine, and pestilence (cherev vera'av vedaver) as YHWH has said about the nation that will not serve the king of Babylon? The rhetorical question exposes the self-destructiveness of the false-prophecy choice.
27.14: אַל-תִּשְׁמְעוּ אֶל-דִּבְרֵי הַנְּבִיאִים (al-tishme'u el-divrei haneviim, do not listen to the words of the prophets): the false prophets tell you that you will not serve the king of Babylon — שֶׁקֶר הֵם נִבְּאִים לָכֶם (sheqer hem nib'im lakhem, they are prophesying falsehood to you).
27.15: לֹא שְׁלַחְתִּים (lo shelachtim, I have not sent them): the diagnostic of false prophecy — the false prophet cannot demonstrate a divine commission. They prophesy in YHWH's name (bishmi) without YHWH's authorization. The result: I will drive you out and you will perish — both the prophets and those who believe them.
27.16: Jeremiah speaks to the priests and all the people: do not listen to the prophets who say the vessels of YHWH's house (kelei beit-YHWH) will soon be brought back from Babylon. שֶׁקֶר הֵם נִבְּאִים לָכֶם (sheqer hem nib'im lakhem, they are prophesying falsehood to you) — the repeated verdict.
27.17: Do not listen to them — serve the king of Babylon and live (ichyu). Why should this city become a desolation (chorbah)? The logic of prophetic pragmatism: not theological surrender but realistic assessment of what YHWH has determined.
27.18: But if they are prophets and if the word of YHWH is with them, let them intercede (yifge'u na) with YHWH of armies that the vessels remaining in the house of YHWH not go to Babylon — a challenge to the false prophets. If they have genuine prophetic access to YHWH, let them pray effectively. Their inability to prevent the remaining vessels from being taken proves their falsehood.
27.19: The specific vessels remaining — the bronze pillars (ha'amudim), the sea (hayyam), the stands (hamkhaonot), and the other vessels: the great ceremonial objects of the Solomonic temple. Their fate is being contested between true and false prophecy.
27.20: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon did not take them when he took Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim of Judah into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon — a historical note. The first deportation (597 BCE) included people but not all the temple vessels.
27.21: Thus says YHWH of armies, the God of Israel, concerning the vessels remaining in the house of YHWH, in the house of the king of Judah, and in Jerusalem — YHWH addresses specifically the objects of false prophecy's hopeful claims.
27.22: בָּבֶלָה יוּבָאוּ וְשָׁמָּה יִהְיוּ (Vavelah yuba'u veshammah yihyu, they will be brought to Babylon and there they will remain): YHWH's counter-prophecy to the false prophets. The vessels will go to Babylon. But: עַד יוֹם פָּקְדִי אֹתָם (ad yom poqdi otam, until the day I attend to them) — a bounded removal, not permanent loss. YHWH will bring them back — the same pattern as the exile of people: bounded by YHWH's purposes, not open-ended. Cf. Ezra 1:7-11 (Cyrus restores the temple vessels).

28.1: The dating formula is unusually precise: baššānâ hahîʾ bĕrēʾšît mamlekût Ṣidqiyyāhû... bašššānâ hārĕbîʿît baḥōdeš haḥămîšî — "in that same year, at the beginning of Zedekiah's reign, in the fourth year, in the fifth month." The dating aligns with the wider context of ch. 27 where Jeremiah wore the wooden yoke before the envoys of surrounding nations. Hananiah the son of Azzur (ben-ʿAzzûr) is identified as "the prophet from Gibeon" — a named opponent who functions as a case study in false prophecy. The MKT preserves all the chronological markers because they function as verifiable historical grounding.
28.2: šābart ʾet-ʿōl melek Bābel — "I have broken the yoke of the king of Babylon"; Hananiah's oracle uses the same first-person divine voice as Jeremiah's oracles, the same divine titles (YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl), and the same prophetic formula (kōh-ʾāmar YHWH). The counterfeit oracle is structurally identical to genuine oracles — the only difference is content. The MKT renders Hananiah's oracle straightforwardly to show how plausible it sounded: "I have broken the yoke of the king of Babylon."
28.3: bĕʿōd šĕnatayim yāmîm — "within two years"; Hananiah's timeline — two years — is specific and testable. He specifies the return of the temple vessels taken under Jehoiakim (605 BC). The specificity of the timeline makes this prophecy falsifiable in a way that vague promises are not; the same specificity that lends credibility will ultimately expose it as false. The MKT renders "within two years" literally to preserve the testability.
28.4: Hananiah extends his oracle to include Jeconiah (Jehoiachin) himself and all the exiles of Judah who went to Babylon with him. The phrase nĕʾûm-YHWH (declares the LORD) closes the oracle — the same authenticating formula used in genuine prophecy. The comprehensive scope (temple vessels + the king + all exiles) makes this the most optimistic possible oracle at the most politically fraught moment.
28.5: Jeremiah's response is remarkable for its lack of immediate counter-attack: he speaks "in the presence of the priests and all the people who were standing in the temple" — the full public setting. The confrontation is not private; both prophets' reputations are on the line before the community. The MKT preserves the audience specification because the public nature of the confrontation gives the subsequent events their narrative weight.
28.6: ʾāmēn kēn yaʿăśeh YHWH — "Amen! May the LORD do so!" Jeremiah's startling "Amen" — he genuinely wishes the oracle were true. "May the LORD fulfill the words you have prophesied (yāqem YHWH ʾet-dĕbārêkā ʾăšer nitbāʾtā)" — an authentic expression of longing for the exile to end and the vessels to return. The MKT renders this as a sincere blessing rather than irony; Jeremiah's subsequent "but" in v.7 is what introduces the challenge, not sarcasm in v.6.
28.7: ʾak šĕmaʿ-nāʾ haddābār hazzeh — "But hear now this word"; the adversative ʾak (but, however) marks the transition from Jeremiah's sincere blessing to his challenge. The word Jeremiah speaks is addressed both to Hananiah and to "all the people" (bĕʾoznê kol-hāʿām). The public audience is maintained — the counter-challenge must be heard by all who heard Hananiah's oracle.
28.8: hannĕbîʾîm ʾăšer hāyû lĕpānay ûlĕpānêkā mēʿôlām — "The prophets who preceded you and me from of old"; Jeremiah appeals to the prophetic tradition as evidence. The historical pattern: authentic prophets (nābāʾ ʿal-ʾărāṣôt rabbôt wĕʿal-mamlākôt gĕdōlôt, against many lands and great kingdoms) characteristically prophesied war (milhāmâ), disaster (rāʿâ), and plague (deber). The MKT preserves the threefold list — war, disaster, plague — as the standard pattern of classical prophecy, which is the opposite of Hananiah's message.
28.9: hanābîʾ ʾăšer yinnābēʾ lĕšālôm — "As for the prophet who prophesies peace"; the epistemological problem of peace-prophecy. For judgment prophecy: the tradition and moral coherence of YHWH's character can validate it in advance. For peace prophecy: bĕbôʾ dĕbar hannābîʾ yiwwādaʿ hannābîʾ — "only when his word comes true will the prophet be known as one truly sent by the LORD." The future-fulfillment criterion applies uniquely to positive oracles. The MKT's "only when his word comes true" preserves the deferred-validation principle that will be vindicated in v.17.
28.10: wayyiqqaḥ ḥananyāhû hannābîʾ ʾet-hammôṭâ — "Then Hananiah took the yoke bar"; mōṭâ (yoke bar, yoke pole) — the symbol Jeremiah had been wearing publicly. Hananiah's dramatic breaking of the bar (wayĕšabbĕrēhû) is a prophetic counter-sign: the yoke-breaking is his enacted oracle. In prophetic tradition, symbolic actions were not merely illustrative but effective; by breaking the yoke, Hananiah claimed to be enacting YHWH's redemptive act. The MKT renders the action narrative directly: "broke it."
28.11: kāzōʾt ʾešbōr ʾet-ʿōl Nĕbûkadrĕʾṣar melek Bābel — "This is how I will break the yoke of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon"; Hananiah interprets his own sign-act before the whole people. The public proclamation in Hananiah's words parallels YHWH's own proclamation-form. "And the prophet Jeremiah went his way" (wayyēlek yirmĕyāhû lĕdarkô) — Jeremiah's departure is significant: he does not counter immediately but waits for a fresh divine word.
28.12: The structure of divine response: Jeremiah does not speak on his own authority but waits for the word of YHWH to come (wayĕhî dĕbar-YHWH ʾel-yirmĕyāhû ʾaḥărê šĕbōr ḥananyāhû hannābîʾ ʾet-hammôṭâ). The temporal note "after Hananiah had broken the yoke bar" makes clear that this is a responsive word — YHWH addresses what Hananiah has done. The MKT preserves the causal relationship: the divine word responds to the prophetic action.
28.13: môṭôt ʿēts šābartā ûʿāśîtā taḥtêhen môṭôt barzĕl — "You have broken yokes of wood, but you have made yokes of iron to replace them"; the terrible irony: Hananiah's act of breaking the wooden yoke (ʿōl ʿēts) has resulted in an iron yoke (ʿōl barzĕl) being imposed. The symbolic act intended to enact liberation has enacted a heavier bondage. The MKT renders the irony starkly: wood broken, iron made.
28.14: ʿōl barzĕl nātattî ʿal-ṣawwĕʾar kol-haggôyim hāʾēlleh — "I have placed a yoke of iron on the neck of all these nations so that they may serve Nebuchadnezzar"; YHWH's own interpretation of the iron yoke. The phrase "including the wild animals" (wĕgam ʾet-ḥayyat haśśādeh nātattî lô) — the dominion given to Nebuchadnezzar is comprehensive, including the animal kingdom. The MKT preserves "wild animals" as a specific addition that emphasizes the totality of Babylon's divinely-granted sovereignty.
28.15: šĕmaʿ-nāʾ ḥananyāhû lōʾ-šĕlaḥăkā YHWH — "Listen, Hananiah! The LORD has not sent you"; the direct confrontation Jeremiah had withheld in v.6-9 now comes. The triple disavowal of 14:14 (lōʾ šĕlaḥtîm... wĕlōʾ ṣiwwîtîm... wĕlōʾ dibbartî) is here compressed into the single decisive verb: "not sent you." The charge: wĕʾattâ hibṭaḥtā ʾet-hāʿām hazzeh ʿal-šāqer — "you have made this people trust in a lie."
28.16: hinnĕnî mĕšallĕḥăkā mēʿal pĕnê hāʾădāmâ — "I am about to remove you from the face of the earth"; the verb šālaḥ (send) appears ironically: YHWH did not send (šālaḥ) Hananiah (v.15), but now YHWH is sending (mĕšallĕḥăkā) him — from life. "This very year you will die (tāmût), because you have spoken rebellion against the LORD" (sārāh dibbartā ʾel-YHWH). The specific "this year" (bašššānâ hazzōʾt) will be tested by v.17.
28.17: wayyāmat ḥananyāhû hannābîʾ bašššānâ hahîʾ baḥōdeš haššĕbîʿî — "And the prophet Hananiah died that same year, in the seventh month"; the narrative fulfillment completes the prophetic contest. The seventh month (two months after the fifth-month confrontation of v.1) and the same year — precise temporal verification. The narrative mentions Hananiah's title "the prophet" even in his death — the ironic designation of the false prophet who died. The MKT's literal rendering preserves the narrative's deliberate precision.

29.1: wĕʾēlleh dibrê-hassēper ʾăšer šālaḥ yirmĕyāhû hannābîʾ — "These are the words of the letter that the prophet Jeremiah sent"; sēper (letter, document, scroll) — a formal written communication carried by official envoys. The recipients are specified in layers: "surviving elders among the exiles" (yeter ziknê haggôlâ), priests, prophets, and all the people. "Surviving elders" (yeter) implies some had already died — the exile was not gentle. The MKT preserves "surviving" to acknowledge the mortality within the exile community.
29.2: The chronological anchoring: ʾaḥărê ṣēt Yĕkonyāhû hammelek wĕhaggĕbîrâ wĕhassārîsîm śārê Yĕhûdâ wîrûšālaim wĕheḥārāš wĕhammasger — after King Jeconiah, the queen mother, court officials, princes, craftsmen, and metalworkers had been taken to Babylon. The fullness of the leadership vacuum is specified: king, queen mother, officials, princes, skilled workers — all the pillars of a functioning society removed.
29.3: The letter is entrusted to Ĕlāśāh ben-Šāpān ûGĕmaryāhû ben-Ḥilqiyyāhû — named emissaries of Zedekiah on official diplomatic business with Nebuchadnezzar. Jeremiah uses the official diplomatic channel to send his pastoral letter. The names matter: the Shaphan family were supporters of Jeremiah (cf. 26:24), and Hilkiah was the high priest who found the book of the law in Josiah's day (2 Kgs 22:8). The letter travels in trustworthy hands.
29.4: ʾăšer hĕglêtî mîrûšālaim Bābĕlâ — "whom I have sent into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon"; YHWH's own ownership of the exile — not Nebuchadnezzar's conquest but YHWH's deportation. The pastoral letter begins by reframing the exile's cause: the exiles did not lose to Babylon; YHWH sent them. This reframing is essential to what follows — if YHWH sent them, YHWH can bring them back; if they simply lost a political contest, return is uncertain.
29.5: bĕnû bāttîm wĕšēbû... ṭĕʿû gannôt wĕʾiklû ʾet-pĕriyāhen — "Build houses and settle in them; plant gardens and eat what they produce"; two paired imperatives: build + settle, plant + eat. The verbs are positive engagement with life in Babylon — not temporary shelter or survival, but establishment. The instruction to plant and harvest implies a multi-year commitment: trees planted do not bear fruit immediately. The MKT's "settle in them" captures the permanence-intention in šēbû (dwell, settle).
29.6: qĕḥû nāšîm ûhôlîdû bānîm ûbānôt — "Marry and have sons and daughters"; the marriage-and-birth instruction directly reverses what Jeremiah was personally commanded in 16:1-4 (no marriage, because of the coming disaster). Jeremiah personally cannot marry; the exiles should marry and multiply. The instruction "give your daughters to husbands and your sons to wives so that they too may multiply" is a full community-multiplication program in exile. The MKT renders the parallelism of sons/daughters in all its symmetry.
29.7: wĕdirĕšû ʾet-šĕlôm hāʿîr ʾăšer higlêtî ʾetkem šāmmâ — "Seek the welfare of the city where I have sent you into exile"; šālôm of Babylon is YHWH's goal for the exiles. The same word Hananiah promised Israel (28:9 — šālôm) is to be the exiles' gift to their captors. "And pray to the LORD on its behalf" (wĕhitpallĕlû bĕʿādāh ʾel-YHWH) — prayer for Babylon's welfare is a covenant obligation. The final clause: kî bišlômāh yihyeh lākem šālôm — "for in its welfare you will have welfare" — the unusual theological claim that Israelite flourishing is tied to Babylonian flourishing. The MKT preserves all three dimensions: seek, pray, because.
29.8: ʾal-yaššîʾû ʾetkem nĕbîʾêkem ʾăšer-bĕqirbĕkem — "Do not let the prophets and diviners among you deceive you"; the false prophets in Babylon parallel those in Jerusalem (28:1-17). Kōsĕmîm (diviners) alongside nĕbîʾîm (prophets) — the full range of false intermediaries. "Do not listen to the dreams they are causing you to dream" (ʾal-tišmĕʿû ʾel-ḥalōmôtêkem ʾăšer ʾattem maḥlĕmîm) — the unusual causative ("dreams you are causing to dream") implies that the community's wish-fulfillment desires are generating these dreams.
29.9: kî bišĕqer hēm nĕbāʾîm lākem bišmî — "For they are prophesying lies to you in my name"; the triple disavowal: "I have not sent them" (lōʾ šĕlaḥtîm). The brevity of the disavowal contrasts with the formal prophetic oracle-form Hananiah deployed (28:2-4) — YHWH's refutation requires only one phrase.
29.10: kî kōh ʾāmar YHWH kimlōʾt lĕbābel šibʿîm šānâ — "For thus says the LORD: When seventy years for Babylon are complete"; the seventy-year timeframe (cf. 25:11-12) is given a positive reinterpretation: it is not just a period of punishment but a period with an end. ʾEpqōd ʾetkem (I will come to you, attend to you — the verb pāqad meaning to visit/attend) — the divine attention that has seemed absent will return. "I will fulfill my good promise to bring you back to this place" (dĕbārî haṭṭôb) — the restoration is a kept promise, not an afterthought. The MKT's "good promise" translates haddābār haṭṭôb (the good word/promise).
29.11: kî ʾānōkî yādaʿtî ʾet-hammaḥăšābôt ʾăšer ʾānōkî ḥōšēb ʿălêkem — "For I know the plans I have for you, declares the LORD"; the famous verse. Maḥăšābôt (thoughts, plans, purposes) — the same word used for YHWH's purposes in judgment (e.g. 18:11: ḥōšēb ʿălêkem rāʿâ). Here the maḥăšābôt are lĕšālôm wĕlōʾ lĕrāʿâ — for welfare (wholeness, peace) and not for harm. Lātēt lākem ʾaḥărît wĕtiqwâ — to give you a future (ʾaḥărît = end/afterward, the word for "future") and a hope (tiqwâ = hope, also the word for a cord/rope — something that holds you). The MKT renders "a future and a hope" because both nouns carry forward momentum: ʾaḥărît is eschatological orientation; tiqwâ is present confidence about it.
29.12: ûqĕrāʾtem ʾōtî ûhălaktem wĕhitpallaltĕm ʾēlay — "Then you will call on me and come and pray to me, and I will listen to you"; the three verbs: call (qārāʾ), come (hālaḵ), pray (hitpallēl) — all three directing the exiles toward YHWH. The covenant relationship restored through prayer. The verse follows v.11's plan-announcement: the plans for welfare have prayer as their medium of access.
29.13: ûbiqaštĕm ʾōtî ûmĕṣāʾtem kî tidĕrĕšûnnî bĕkol-lĕbabkem — "You will seek me and find me, when you search for me with all your heart"; the deuteronomic condition (Deut 4:29 — "if you search for him with all your heart and with all your soul") now stated as a promise rather than a conditional. The MKT's "when you search for me with all your heart" preserves the shift from "if" to "when" — the conditional has become an assurance. The verb mĕṣāʾtem (you will find) is stated declaratively.
29.14: wĕnimṣēʾtî lākem — "I will let myself be found by you"; nimṣēʾtî is the niphal reflexive/passive of māṣāʾ (find): YHWH makes himself findable. The restoration that follows is comprehensive: šabtî ʾet-šĕbûtkem (I will restore your fortunes — the double-šûb formula); gather from all nations; bring back to the place from which I sent you into exile. The MKT translates šabtî ʾet-šĕbûtkem as "restore your fortunes" — the idiom implies a full reversal of all that the exile has taken.
29.15: The transition: "You may be saying, 'The LORD has appointed prophets for us here in Babylon'" — Jeremiah addresses the community's likely response. The existence of prophets in Babylon is not denied; the question is whether those prophets are authentic. The MKT renders this as a quotation from the community rather than a statement of fact, following the likely sense.
29.16: yēdaʿ kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾel-hammelek hayôšēb ʿal-kissēʾ Dāwîd — "Know that this is what the LORD says concerning the king who sits on David's throne"; the oracle against those who remained in Jerusalem (vv. 16-19) contrasts with the restoration promised to the exiles (vv. 10-14). Those who stayed behind and claim prophets are still under judgment; those who went into exile hold the covenant promise.
29.17: hinnĕnî mĕšallaḥ bāhem ʾet-haḥereb wĕʾet-hārāʿāb wĕʾet-haddāber — "Thus says the LORD: I am going to send sword, famine, and pestilence against them"; the three judgment agents. The image that follows is viscerally unfavorable: "I will make them like rotten figs that are inedible they are so bad" (kattĕʾēnîm hašşōʿārôt ʾăšer lōʾ-tēʾāknâ mērōaʿ). The contrast with the "good figs" of ch. 24 is intentional — those taken to Babylon are good figs; those who stayed are rotten.
29.18: wĕrādaptî ʾaḥărêhem beḥereb bārāʿāb ûbaddāber — "I will pursue them with sword, famine, and pestilence"; the verb rādap (pursue) is relentless — judgment will not stop. "I will make them a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth" — lĕzaʿăwâ (horror/trembling), lĕqĕlālâ (curse), lĕšammâ ûlišrēqâ (desolation and hissing). The fourfold shame vocabulary comprehensively captures the social humiliation of covenant curse.
29.19: yaʿan ʾăšer lōʾ-šāmĕʿû ʾel-dĕbāray — "because they did not obey my words" — the standard covenant-breach formula. "Which I sent to them persistently through my servants the prophets" (šāllōaḥ wĕšāllōaḥ — the infinitive absolute expressing persistent/repeated sending): YHWH's messengers came repeatedly; the refusal was persistent. The MKT renders "persistently" to capture the emphatic repetition of the infinitive absolute.
29.20: wĕʾattem šimĕʿû dĕbar-YHWH kol-haggôlâ — "So hear the word of the LORD, all you exiles"; the contrast re-established: the exiles who receive the restoration promise (vv. 10-14) are now distinguished from those under judgment (vv. 16-19). The address kol-haggôlâ (all the exile) is the audience for the warning about false prophets that follows.
29.21: ʾel-ʾAhāb ben-Qôlāyāhû wĕʾel-Ṣidqiyyāhû ben-Maʿăśēyāhû — specific false prophets in Babylon named and condemned. Unlike Hananiah whose punishment was death within the year, these two are condemned to become a byword: "May the LORD make you like Zedekiah and Ahab, whom the king of Babylon roasted in the fire" (qālāhû... bāʾēš). Execution by fire — they are to be publicly destroyed by the very king they were presumably comforting the exiles about.
29.22: The curse-formula (qĕlālâ) that will be used by Judean exiles: "May the LORD make you like Zedekiah and Ahab, whom the king of Babylon roasted in the fire." The false prophets become proverbial — their fate is made into a curse-saying that will outlive them. The MKT preserves qālāhû (roasted) — the stark word for their execution method.
29.23: yaʿan ʾăšer ʿāśû nĕbālâ bĕYiśrāʾēl — "because they have done a disgraceful thing in Israel"; nĕbālâ (folly, disgraceful act) is the technical term for sexual immorality and its social-covenant violation. The two charges: adultery with neighbors' wives and speaking false words in YHWH's name. The combination of sexual and prophetic unfaithfulness mirrors Hosea's analysis — false prophecy and sexual immorality are paired covenant violations.
29.24: The Shemaiah episode is introduced: ûlĕŠĕmāʿyāhû hanne-ḥĕlāmî tōʾmar — a new oracle directed against Shemaiah the Nehelamite. The unusual word Nehelamite may be a place name or a word-play on ḥālam (dream) — the "dreamer" prophet. Shemaiah is not in Jerusalem but another exile in Babylon who wrote counter-letters to Jerusalem.
29.25: yaʿan ʾăšer šālaḥtā sĕpārîm bĕšimkā — "Because you sent letters in your own name" — Shemaiah's unauthorized counter-letters, sent in his own name to Jerusalem's priest Zephaniah, attempted to subvert Jeremiah's authority. Sending letters bĕšimkā (in your own name) rather than in YHWH's name marks the self-authorization. The MKT preserves this as the formal charge: letters sent in Shemaiah's name, not YHWH's.
29.26: Shemaiah's letter claimed that Zephaniah the priest had been appointed to replace Jehoiada "to be in charge of the house of the LORD" (lihyôt pĕquddôt bêt-YHWH) and to deal with any "madman who plays the prophet" (kol-ʾîš mĕšuggāʿ ûmitnaббēʾ). The word for Jeremiah used by Shemaiah — mĕšuggāʿ (madman, disturbed person) — is an insult. The MKT translates it directly as "madman who plays the prophet" to preserve the contemptuous force.
29.27: lāmmâ lōʾ gāʿartā bĕYirmĕyāhû hāʿănātôtî — "Why then have you not rebuked Jeremiah of Anathoth?" Shemaiah names Jeremiah with his village origin — "of Anathoth" — perhaps a further diminishment (a provincial prophet). The expected response: Zephaniah should have used his priestly authority to silence Jeremiah. That he did not is what Shemaiah's letter is designed to correct.
29.28: kî ʿal-kēn šālaḥ ʾēlênû Bābĕlâ — "For he has sent word to us in Babylon, saying: Your exile will be long (ʾārkâ)"; Shemaiah quotes Jeremiah's letter (29:5-7) — build houses, plant gardens. The instruction to settle in for a long exile is what Shemaiah finds intolerable. The word ʾārkâ (long, extended) is Shemaiah's characterization of Jeremiah's message — and it is accurate. The MKT follows the quoted speech.
29.29: Zephaniah read the letter aloud to Jeremiah — the priest showed Jeremiah the letter rather than acting on it. The implication: Zephaniah is not an enemy of Jeremiah, even if Shemaiah wanted him to be. The MKT preserves the "aloud" reading because it implies that the letter was read in Jeremiah's hearing as an act of informing rather than condemning him.
29.30: The standard prophetic formula introduces the divine response to Shemaiah: wayĕhî dĕbar-YHWH ʾel-yirmĕyāhû lēʾmōr. The sequence: Shemaiah's unauthorized letter → Zephaniah reads it to Jeremiah → YHWH speaks to Jeremiah about Shemaiah. YHWH responds to private opposition to his prophet with public divine judgment, just as with Hananiah.
29.31: šĕlaḥ ʾel-kol-haggôlâ lēʾmōr — "Send word to all the exiles"; Jeremiah is to broadcast the judgment against Shemaiah publicly — to all the exiles, not just to Zephaniah or to Shemaiah privately. The reason for the public condemnation: yaʿan ʾăšer nibbā lākem Šĕmāʿyāhû wĕʾānōkî lōʾ-šĕlaḥtîw — "because Shemaiah has prophesied to you and I did not send him." The core criterion: lōʾ šĕlaḥtîw — unsent prophecy is false prophecy.
29.32: lāken kōh-ʾāmar YHWH hinnĕnî pōqēd ʿal-Šĕmāʿyāhû — "Therefore thus says the LORD: I am going to punish Shemaiah the Nehelamite and his descendants"; three punishments: none of his descendants will survive (lōʾ-yihyeh lô ʾîš yôšēb bĕtôk hāʿām hazzeh), he will not see the good YHWH does for his people, and he will not return from exile. The punishment for opposing the covenant promise: exclusion from the very blessing he attempted to hasten by false prophecy.

30.1: The Book of Consolation begins (chs. 30-33) with the standard formula: haddābār ʾăšer hāyâ ʾel-yirmĕyāhû mēʾēt YHWH lēʾmōr. The shift from judgment oracles to restoration oracles marks a decisive turn. The MKT preserves the formula as the opening of a new section rather than continuing the letter-narrative of ch. 29.
30.2: kŏh ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr kŏtāb-lĕkā ʾēt kol-haddĕbārîm ʾăšer dibbartî ʾēlêkā ʾel-sēper — "Write in a book all the words I have spoken to you"; the command to write (kĕtōb lĕkā ʾel-sēper) creates a document — the consolation oracles are not merely spoken but preserved. The Book of Consolation is thus a self-consciously created literary unit. The MKT renders "write in a book" rather than "write on a scroll" for readability, though sēper most naturally refers to a scroll in this period.
30.3: kî hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm nĕʾum-YHWH wĕšabtî ʾet-šĕbût ʿammî Yiśrāʾēl ûYĕhûdâ — "For the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will restore the fortunes of my people Israel and Judah"; šabtî ʾet-šĕbût — the double-šûb restoration formula. Both northern Israel (Yiśrāʾēl) and southern Judah (Yĕhûdâ) are included in the restoration — the divided kingdom reunified. The phrase wĕhăšibōtîm ʾel-hāʾārets ʾăšer-nātattî laʾăbôtām wîrĕšûhā — "I will bring them back to the land I gave their ancestors, and they will take possession of it."
30.4: The structural marker introducing the poetry that follows: wĕʾēlleh haddĕbārîm ʾăšer dibbēr YHWH ʾel-Yiśrāʾēl wĕʾel-Yĕhûdâ — "These are the words the LORD spoke concerning Israel and Judah." The transition from prose (vv. 1-3) to poetry (vv. 5-7) is introduced formally. The audience is both kingdoms together — the eschatological vision overrides the historical division.
30.5: qôl ḥarādâ šāmāʿnû paḥad wĕʾên šālôm — "We have heard a cry of terror, of dread — not of peace"; the Book of Consolation begins paradoxically with a terror oracle. Before the restoration comes "Jacob's trouble" (v.7). The word ḥarādâ (terror, trembling) and paḥad (dread, fear) establish the severity of what must be endured before the restoration. Wĕʾên šālôm — "and there is no peace" — the peace Hananiah falsely promised is genuinely absent in the time of trouble.
30.6: šaʾălû-nāʾ ûrĕʾû ʾim-yōlēd zākār — "Ask yourselves and look: can a man give birth?" The rhetorical impossibility: a man cannot give birth; yet every man is clutching his sides kĕyôlēdâ (like a woman in labor) and faces have turned pale (nehĕpĕkû kol-pānîm lĕyērāqôn). The unprecedented nature of the coming distress is conveyed by an image of gender reversal — men experiencing birth pangs. The MKT preserves the rhetorical question to maintain the shock of the image.
30.7: ʾôy kî gādôl hayyôm hahûʾ mēʾên kāmōhû — "How terrible that day will be! There has never been another like it"; ʾôy (woe/alas) opens the verse. The phrase wĕʾēt-ṣārâ hîʾ lĕYaʿăqōb — "It is a time of anguish for Jacob" — the phrase "time of Jacob's trouble" used in subsequent eschatological interpretation. The climactic assurance: ûmimmennâ yiwwāšēaʿ — "but he will be saved from it." The salvation comes through the trouble, not around it. The MKT renders "anguish" for ṣārâ (distress, trouble, straits).
30.8: wĕhāyâ bayyôm hahûʾ nĕʾum YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt ʾešbōr ʿullô mēʿal ṣawwāʾrekā — "On that day, declares the LORD of hosts, I will break the yoke from your neck"; the same vocabulary as Hananiah's false promise (28:2) — but now YHWH genuinely promises it for the eschatological day. Hananiah's premature "I have broken the yoke" will be truly fulfilled in the future YHWH envisions. The MKT's translation preserves the verbal echo of ch. 28.
30.9: wĕʿābĕdû ʾēt YHWH ʾĕlōhêhem wĕʾēt Dāwîd malkām ʾăšer ʾāqîm lāhem — "Instead they will serve the LORD their God and David their king, whom I will raise up for them"; the Davidic restoration: Dāwîd malkām ʾăšer ʾāqîm lāhem — David their king whom I will raise up. Since David has been dead for centuries, this must refer to a Davidic heir whom YHWH will "raise up" (qûm in the hiphil). The phrase ʾăšer ʾāqîm lāhem (whom I will raise up for them) makes the Davidic king a divine gift given in the future, not a continuation of the present dynasty. The MKT preserves the future-oriented language of divine raising-up.
30.10: wĕʾattâ ʾal-tîrāʾ ʿabdî Yaʿăqōb nĕʾum YHWH — "So do not be afraid, my servant Jacob, declares the LORD"; the divine reassurance formula "Do not fear" (ʾal-tîrāʾ) addressed to "my servant Jacob" (ʿabdî Yaʿăqōb) — the servant-language for the nation. "For I am going to save you from far away, your descendants from the land of their exile" — the restoration is a rescue from mērāḥôq (far away), from all the lands of exile. The parallel "Jacob will return to safety" (wĕšāb Yaʿăqōb wĕšāqaṭ) — šāqaṭ means to be quiet/at rest, the opposite of the terror described in vv. 5-7.
30.11: kî ʾittĕkā ʾānî nĕʾum YHWH lĕhôšîʿekā — "For I am with you, declares the LORD, to save you"; the 'immanuel'-formula ("I am with you") as the ground of salvation. Three assurances: YHWH with Jacob, full destruction of the nations where scattered, discipline of Jacob in just measure. Wĕyissar tĕkā lammišpāṭ — "I will discipline you in just measure" (lammišpāṭ = according to justice). The guarantee: wĕnaqkeh lōʾ ʾănaqkek — "I will by no means leave you unpunished" — the discipline is real but bounded, not annihilation.
30.12: kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾānûš lĕšibrĕkā naḥlâ makkātēk — "For thus says the LORD: Your injury is incurable, your wound is severe"; ʾānûš (incurable, desperately sick) — the same word used of the heart in 17:9. The wound (makkâ) is described with the word for a blow struck in battle: YHWH has struck Israel as an enemy would strike. The shift from restoration promise (vv. 8-11) to wound-description (vv. 12-15) creates a dialectical movement — the wound must be acknowledged before the healing can be received.
30.13: ʾên-dān dînēk lĕmĕzôr rĕpuʾôt tĕʿālâ ʾên lāk — "There is no one to plead your case; you have no medicines for your wound — no recovery for you"; dān dînēk (pleading your case) — no advocate; mĕzôr (medicines for a wound, a compress) — no physician. The medical vocabulary frames the wound as both legally and physically unaddressable by human means. The hopelessness established here makes YHWH's healing promise in v.17 all the more radical.
30.14: kol-mĕʾahăbayik šĕkēḥûk — "All your allies have abandoned you; they no longer care about you"; mĕʾahăbayik (your lovers/allies) — the political allies who were to provide security have forgotten Israel. The wound described: YHWH struck Israel makkâ ʾôyēb — "the blow of an enemy" — and mûsar ʾakzārî — "the punishment of a cruel one." The terrible irony: the enemy-blow comes from YHWH himself, not from the nations alone.
30.15: mah-tizʿaq ʿal-šibrĕk ʾānûš maʿkōbēk — "Why do you cry out over your wound? Your pain is incurable"; the diagnostic: the crying is understandable, but the cause is the people's own sin: ʿal rōb ʿăwōnēk ʿāṣĕmû ḥaṭṭōʾtayik — "because your guilt is great and your sins are many, I have done these things to you." The covenant-justice explanation for the incurable wound: the severity of the wound is proportional to the severity of the sin.
30.16: lākēn kol-ʾōkelayik yēʾākēlû — "Therefore all who devour you will be devoured"; the reversal oracle begins with a lākēn (therefore) that pivots from diagnosis to restoration. The three reversals: devouring devourers; captive captors; plundering plunderers — each attacker is subjected to the same fate they inflicted. The MKT preserves the wordplay in each pair (devour/devoured, captive/captivity, plunder/plundered).
30.17: kî ʾaʿăleh ʾărûkâ lāk ûmimmakkôtayik ʾerpāʾēk nĕʾum YHWH — "For I will restore your health and heal your wounds, declares the LORD"; ʾārûkâ (new skin, healing, health) — the medical term for recovery from a wound. The reason for healing: kî niddāḥâ qārĕʾû lāk — "because they called you Outcast (niddāḥâ), 'Zion for whom no one cares'" — the social shame of abandonment becomes YHWH's motivation for intervention. YHWH heals Zion precisely because the nations have written her off. The MKT renders niddāḥâ as "Outcast."
30.18: hinnî-šāb šĕbût ʾohŏlê Yaʿăqōb — "Thus says the LORD: I am going to restore the fortunes of Jacob's tents"; šĕbût is here used again — the restoration formula (cf. v.3). The specific images: the city (ʿîr) will be rebuilt on its mound (tel); the palace (ʾarmôn) will stand in its rightful place. Tūrăm al-mišpāṭām — "on its own site, in its rightful place" — the restoration is to the same location, not relocation to somewhere else.
30.19: wĕyāṣāʾ mēhem tôdâ wĕqôl mĕśaḥăqîm — "From them will come songs of thanksgiving and the sound of rejoicing"; the acoustic image of restoration: tôdâ (thanksgiving, praise) and qôl mĕśaḥăqîm (sound of those who make merry). "I will multiply them so that they will not be few; I will honor them so that they will not be small" — the reversed direction: from decrease to increase, from humiliation to honor. The MKT renders both pairs to preserve the doublet structure.
30.20: wĕhāyû bānāyw kĕqedem — "Their children will be as they were in former times" (kĕqedem, as in the former days); the restoration of the ancestral community pattern. Wĕʿēdātô lĕpānay tikkôn — "their congregation will be established before me" — the covenantal assembly restored. "And I will punish all who oppress them" — the covenant protection against oppressors is reimposed.
30.21: wĕhāyâ ʾaddîrô mimmennû ûmōšĕlô miqirbô yēṣēʾ — "Their leader will come from among them, and their governor will emerge from their own midst"; ʾaddîr (mighty one, noble leader) and mōšēl (ruler, governor) — both from among the people, not from a foreign power. The phrase wĕhiqqarbtîw wĕniggaš ʾēlay — "I will bring him near, and he will approach me" — priestly language: niggaš ʾēlay means drawing near to YHWH in the way a priest draws near. The leader who is both ruler and priestly-access figure anticipates the Christological theme of Melchizedek-priest-king. The MKT renders "approach me" to preserve the theologically loaded nāgaš (draw near) vocabulary.
30.22: wîhîtem lî lĕʿām wĕʾānōkî ʾehyeh lākem lēʾlōhîm — "You will be my people, and I will be your God"; the covenant formula in its shortest and most compressed form. The bilateral structure: your people (lĕʿām) / your God (lēʾlōhîm). This formula appears at the heart of the covenant (Exod 6:7; Lev 26:12; Deut 26:17-18) and marks its climactic restoration in both Jeremiah (31:33) and Ezekiel (36:28). Its placement here, mid-consolation, anchors the entire restoration to the covenant relationship.
30.23: hinnēh saʿărat YHWH ḥēmâ yāṣĕʾāh saʿar mitgôrēr — "See, the storm of the LORD! His wrath has gone forth, a sweeping whirlwind"; an intrusion of the judgment oracle before the final climax. Saʿar (storm, whirlwind) and mitgôrēr (sweeping, swirling) — the storm imagery for YHWH's judgment (cf. 23:19). "It will break upon the head of the wicked" (ʿal rōʾš rĕšāʿîm yāḥûl). The storm oracle is not against Israel but against the nations and the wicked — the same wrath that judged Israel will fall on oppressors. The MKT renders the storm vocabulary consistently with its appearance in ch. 23.
30.24: lōʾ yāšûb ḥărôn ʾap-YHWH ʿad-ʿăśōtô wĕʿad-hăqîmô mĕzimmôt libbô — "The fierce anger of the LORD will not turn back until he has done and performed the purposes of his heart"; mĕzimmôt libbô — the purposes/devices of YHWH's heart — what YHWH plans will be executed. The closing phrase: bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm tĕtbônănû bāh — "In the latter days you will understand this" — the consolation and the storm alike will become clear at the end. The MKT renders ʾaḥărît hayyāmîm as "the latter days" (the standard eschatological phrase) and tĕtbônănû (you will understand/perceive it) to preserve the future comprehension promised.

32.1: Bišnat hāʿăśirît lĕṣidqiyyāhû melek-yĕhûdâ hî haššānâ šĕmoneh ʿeśrēh šānâ lĕNĕḇuḵadrĕʾṣṣar (bišnat hāʿăśirît lĕṣidqiyyāhû): the dual dating — 10th year of Zedekiah = 18th year of Nebuchadnezzar — is 588 BCE, a year before Jerusalem falls. The precise dating is significant: this is an acted prophecy of hope performed at the darkest moment. The specificity of dating grounds the sign-act in verifiable historical reality.
32.2: Wĕḥêl melek-bāḇel ṣārîm ʿal-yĕrûšālāyim (wĕḥêl melek-bāḇel ṣārîm ʿal-yĕrûšālāyim): the Babylonian siege (ṣārîm, Qal participle — ongoing action) is the immediate context. WîrmĕyāhÛ hānnāḇîʾ hāyâ kālûʾ baḥăṣar hammaTTārâ (kālûʾ baḥăṣar hammaṭṭārâ): Jeremiah is confined (kālûʾ, Qal passive — locked up) in the court of the guard (ḥăṣar hammaṭṭārâ), a semi-open area within the palace complex (Neh 3:25). The imprisonment details sharpen the paradox: a man physically confined performs a sign-act of land-purchase that proclaims freedom and restoration.
32.3: ʾĂšer kĕlāʾô ṣidqiyyāhû melek-yĕhûdâ lēʾmōr (ʾĂšer kĕlāʾô ṣidqiyyāhû): Zedekiah's accusation is reproduced: maddûaʿ ʾattâ nibbāʾ lēʾmōr ("why do you keep prophesying?") — the Hitpael of nābāʾ is iterative/habitual action. The content of the prophetic word that earned imprisonment — "YHWH is handing this city to the king of Babylon" — is precisely the word being enacted in reverse through the field purchase. Zedekiah opposes the word; Jeremiah enacts hope through the same word.
32.4: Wĕṣidqiyyāhû melek-yĕhûdâ lōʾ-yimmāLēṭ miyyad hakkaśdîm (lōʾ-yimmāLēṭ miyyad hakkaśdîm): the oracle against Zedekiah is stated plainly — no escape. Kî hinnātēn yinnātēn bĕyad melek bāḇel (kî hinnātēn yinnātēn): the doubled infinitive absolute makes the surrender absolutely certain: "he will surely be given." Ûpîw ʾim-pîw yĕdabbēr wĕʿênāyw ʾet-ʿênāyw tirʾênâ (ûpîw ʾim-pîw yĕdabbēr wĕʿênāyw ʾet-ʿênāyw tirʾênâ): "his mouth will speak with his mouth and his eyes will see his eyes" — a personal, face-to-face encounter with Nebuchadnezzar, fulfilling the public humiliation of exile (2 Kings 25:6-7).
32.5: Wĕḇāḇel yôliḵ ʾet-ṣidqiyyāhû wĕšām yihyeh ʿad-pŏqĕdî ʾōtô (ʿad-pŏqĕdî ʾōtô): "until I attend to him" — pāqad (to attend to, to visit) is YHWH's sovereign act of intervention, either punitive or restorative. The ambiguity is intentional: Zedekiah will remain in Babylon until YHWH's visitation, whose nature is left open. Kî tiṣṣĕḇû ʾet-hakkaśdîm lōʾ taṣlîḥû (kî tiṣṣĕḇû ʾet-hakkaśdîm lōʾ taṣlîḥû): "if you fight the Chaldeans, you will not succeed" — military resistance is futile because this is YHWH's judgment, not merely Babylonian aggression.
32.6: Wayyōʾmer Yirmĕyāhû hāyâ dĕḇar-YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr (hāyâ dĕḇar-YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr): the classic prophetic reception formula introduces the private divine communication that preceded the public sign-act. Jeremiah's narration in the first person (vv6-15) distinguishes this passage from the surrounding third-person biographical prose, lending the account the weight of personal eyewitness testimony.
32.7: Hānāmēʾl ben-Šallûm dōdĕḵā bāʾ ʾēlêḵā lēʾmōr qnēh-lĕḵā ʾet-śādĕhî ʾăšer bāʿănātôt kî lĕḵā mišpaṭ haggĕʾullâ liqnôt (kî lĕḵā mišpaṭ haggĕʾullâ liqnôt): the key legal term: gĕʾullâ (gĕʾullâ) — the right of the kinsman-redeemer. Under Leviticus 25:25, when a family member was forced to sell land due to poverty, the nearest kinsman (gōʾēl) had both the right and obligation to purchase it and keep it within the family. Jeremiah as Hanamel's nearest relative has the mišpaṭ haggĕʾullâ (legal right of redemption). The theological weight: YHWH enacts his own gōʾēl role in the restoration (Isa 41:14; 43:1; 44:6), and Jeremiah's act of purchase is the prophetic enactment of that divine redemptive purpose.
32.8: Wayyābōʾ ʾēlay Ḥănāmĕʾl ben-dōdî kiddĕḇar YHWH ʾel-ḥăṣar hammaṭṭārâ (kiddĕḇar YHWH): "as the word of YHWH had said" — the prior divine word's exact fulfillment in the cousin's arrival is the confirmation of the divine word's reliability. Wāʾēdaʿ kî dĕḇar-YHWH hûʾ (Wāʾēdaʿ kî dĕḇar-YHWH hûʾ): "I knew that this was the word of YHWH" — the yādaʿ (to know) is not mere recognition but the relational, experiential certitude that this purchase is YHWH's commanded act. Faith is enacted through a legal transaction in the face of imminent catastrophe.
32.9: Wāʾeqneh ʾet-haśśādeh mēʾēt Ḥănāmĕʾl ben-dōdî ʾăšer bāʿănātôt (Wāʾeqneh ʾet-haśśādeh): the actual purchase. Wāʾeškōl-lô ʾet-hakkesep šiḇʿâ šeqālîm waʿăśārâ hakkesep (šiḇʿâ šeqālîm waʿăśārâ hakkesep): seventeen shekels of silver. The specific price (wāʾeškōl-lô, I weighed out to him) is verifiable detail — this is not symbolic; it is a legal transaction conducted with precision. Anathoth is already captured by the Babylonians; the purchase has no immediate commercial value. Its entire value is eschatological: it enacts trust in YHWH's future.
32.10: Wāʾeḵtōḇ bassēper wāʾeḥtōm wāʾāʿēd ʿēdîm wāʾeškōl hakkesep bĕmōznāyim (Wāʾeḵtōḇ bassēper wāʾeḥtōm wāʾāʿēd ʿēdîm): four verbs of legal procedure — I wrote (kātaḇ), I sealed (ḥātam), I brought in witnesses (ʿûd Hiphil), I weighed (šāqal). Each verb represents a standard step in ancient Near Eastern property law. The Babylonian and Assyrian parallel purchase documents confirm that this description is technically accurate to the period. The prophetic act is simultaneously a genuine legal transaction and a theological statement.
32.11: Wāʾeqqaḥ ʾet-sēper hammiqqnâ ʾet-hehātûm hammîṣwâ wĕhaḥuqqîm wĕʾet-haggālûy (ʾet-hehātûm hammîṣwâ wĕhaḥuqqîm wĕʾet-haggālûy): the purchase document came in two copies — a sealed copy containing the full terms and conditions (hammiṣwâ wĕhaḥuqqîm) and an open copy. This matches the standard ANE practice of a duplicate deed with one sealed for long-term preservation and one open for immediate reference. The sealed copy was the legally binding original; the open copy the working document.
32.12: Wāʾettēn ʾet-sēper hammiqqnâ ʾel-Bārûḵ ben-Nēriyyâ ben-Maḥsēyâ (ʾel-Bārûḵ ben-Nēriyyâ ben-Maḥsēyâ): Baruch the scribe — Jeremiah's closest associate, the one who wrote the scroll in ch36 — receives the deed in the presence of Hanamel, the witnesses, and all the Judeans in the court of the guard. The public nature of the transaction before multiple witnesses invests it with maximum legal force. Baruch's role as custodian of the documents connects the sign-act to the prophetic literary legacy.
32.13: Wāʾăṣawweh ʾet-Bārûḵ lĕʿênêhem lēʾmōr (Wāʾăṣawweh ʾet-Bārûḵ lĕʿênêhem): the formal instruction (ṣāwâ) given to Baruch publicly ("before their eyes") — this is not a private arrangement but a public prophetic declaration enacted through legal form. The oral instructions give the legal transaction its interpretive framework.
32.14: Kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl qāḥ ʾet-hassĕpārîm hāʾēlleh ʾēt sēper hammiqqnâ hazzeh wĕʾēt hehātûm wĕʾēt sēper haggālûy hazzeh wĕnĕtattām biḵlî-ḥāreś lĕmaʿan yaʿamĕdû yāmîm rabbîm (biḵlî-ḥāreś lĕmaʿan yaʿamĕdû yāmîm rabbîm): the clay jar (kĕlî ḥāreś) for long-term preservation — the same technology that preserved the Dead Sea Scrolls for two millennia. The purpose: lĕmaʿan yaʿamĕdû yāmîm rabbîm ("that they may stand for many days"). The documents are not just legal evidence for an immediate transaction; they are a long-term prophetic charter meant to outlast the current catastrophe.
32.15: Kî ḵōh ʾāmar YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl ʿôd yiqqānû ḇāttîm ûśādôt ûḵĕrāmîm bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt (ʿôd yiqqānû ḇāttîm ûśādôt ûḵĕrāmîm bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt): "houses and fields and vineyards will yet be bought in this land." The word that interprets the sign-act: the purchase is not an individual eccentricity but a divinely-ordered prophetic enactment of the future. The three categories — houses (bāttîm), fields (śādôt), vineyards (ḵĕrāmîm) — represent the full range of productive property. The prophecy anticipates not just land ownership but a functioning agricultural economy.
32.16: Wāʾetpallēl ʾel-YHWH ʾaḥărê titî ʾet-sēper hammiqqnâ ʾel-Bārûḵ ben-Nēriyyâ lēʾmōr (Wāʾetpallēl ʾel-YHWH ʾaḥărê): the prayer follows the sign-act. The sequence is significant: Jeremiah first obeys the command to purchase (then prays), which is the pattern of faith-obedience preceding understanding. The Hitpael of pālal (to pray, intercede) is the same form used in 7:16 when YHWH forbade intercession — the prohibition has been lifted by the new reality of the sign-act of restoration.
32.17: ʾĂhāh ʾădōnāy YHWH (ʾĂhāh ʾădōnāy YHWH): the unique prayer opening — ʾăhāh (Ah!/Alas!) combined with ʾădōnāy YHWH (Lord GOD) expresses a kind of reverent astonishment. It appears also in 1:6 (Jeremiah's initial objection to his call), 4:10, and 14:13 — always as a cry from the depth of bewilderment before the paradox of divine action. Hinnēh ʾattâ ʿāśîtā ʾet-haššāmayim wĕʾet-hāʾāreṣ bĕḵōḥăḵā haggādôl ûbiḏrōʿăḵā haNNĕṭûyâ lōʾ-yippālēʾ mimmĕḵā kol-dāḇār (lōʾ-yippālēʾ mimmĕḵā kol-dāḇār): the creation formula grounds YHWH's power — heaven and earth made by the great power (kōaḥ gādôl) and outstretched arm (zĕrôaʿ nĕṭûyâ). The lōʾ-yippālēʾ mimmĕḵā kol-dāḇār ("nothing is too difficult/wonderful for you") is the Niphal of pālāʾ (to be wonderful, beyond normal human capacity) — what is pālāʾ for humans is not so for YHWH. The prayer's answer in v27 echoes this exactly.
32.18: ʿŌśeh ḥesed lāʾălāpîm ûmĕšallēm ʿăwōn ʾāḇôt ʾel-ḥêq bĕnêhem ʾaḥărêhem (ûmĕšallēm ʿăwōn ʾāḇôt ʾel-ḥêq bĕnêhem): the tension of ḥesed (steadfast love) extended to thousands (lāʾălāpîm, cf. Exod 20:6) alongside punishment (šillēm, Piel of šālam, to repay, make whole) of ancestral iniquity into the laps of descendants. This is the Sinai tension of Exod 34:6-7, cited in Jeremiah's prayer as the theological framework of the crisis — how can the God of ḥesed punish at all, and how can the God of justice extend mercy? The new covenant of ch31 resolves this tension: the forgiveness is complete and the ancestral debt is cancelled.
32.19: Gĕdōl hāʿēṣâ wĕrab hāʿălîliyyâ ʾăšer-ʿênêḵā pĕqūḥôt ʿal-kol-darkê bĕnê-ʾādām lātēt lĕʾîš kiddĕrāḵāyw ûḵipĕrî maʿălālay (ʾăšer-ʿênêḵā pĕqūḥôt ʿal-kol-darkê bĕnê-ʾādām): YHWH's eyes are open to all the ways of human beings — omniscience as moral oversight. The divine council theology of Job 1-2, Ps 139, Zech 4:10 (seven eyes that range through the whole earth) underlies this. Lātēt lĕʾîš kiddĕrāḵāyw ûḵipĕrî maʿălālay (lātēt lĕʾîš kiddĕrāḵāyw): retributive justice is not mechanical fate but YHWH's personal, morally attentive response to each person's deeds.
32.20: ʾĂšer-śamtā ʾōtôt ûmôpĕtîm bĕʾereṣ miṣrāyim wĕʿad-hayyôm hazzeh ûḇĕyiśrāʾēl ûḇāʾādām (ʾōtôt ûmôpĕtîm bĕʾereṣ miṣrāyim): the exodus signs and wonders (ʾōtôt ûmôpĕtîm) — the Deuteronomic language of the ten plagues and the sea-crossing — are cited as evidence of YHWH's historical fidelity. Wattaʿăśeh-lĕḵā šēm kĕhayyôm hazzeh (Wattaʿăśeh-lĕḵā šēm kĕhayyôm hazzeh): the exodus created YHWH's šēm (name, reputation) — a demonstration of divine character that endures to Jeremiah's present. The prayer positions the current crisis as requiring an equally definitive act of YHWH-revelation.
32.21: Wattôṣēʾ ʾet-ʿammĕḵā ʾet-yiśrāʾēl mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim bĕʾōtôt ûḇĕmôpĕtîm ûḇĕyād ḥăzāqâ ûḇiḏrôaʿ nĕṭûyâ ûḇĕmôrāʾ gādôl (bĕyād ḥăzāqâ ûḇiḏrôaʿ nĕṭûyâ): the tricolon of exodus power — mighty hand, outstretched arm, great terror — is the Deuteronomic formula (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 7:19; 11:2; 26:8) that defines YHWH's saving intervention. The prayer's historical rehearsal is not mere recitation but the activation of covenant memory: YHWH who acted thus for Israel in Egypt is the same YHWH being addressed now.
32.22: Wattittēn lāhem ʾet-hāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt ʾăšer nišbaʿtā lăʾăḇôtām lātēt lāhem ʾereṣ zāḇat ḥālāḇ ûdĕḇāš (ʾereṣ zāḇat ḥālāḇ ûdĕḇāš): the land flowing with milk and honey — the covenant promise to the patriarchs (Exod 3:8, 17; 13:5; 33:3; Lev 20:24; Num 13:27; Deut 6:3 etc.) — was given; Israel entered and took possession. The prayer establishes the full arc of covenant history: promise → exodus → settlement → failure → judgment. This arc creates the context within which the restoration promise of ch31 is the climactic reversal.
32.23: Wayyāḇōʾû wayyirĕšû ʾōtāh wĕlōʾ šāmĕʿû bĕqôlĕḵā ûḇĕtôrātĕḵā lōʾ-hālāḵû ʾēt kol-ʾăšer-ṣiwwîtā ʾôtām laʿăśôt lōʾ ʿāśû (wĕlōʾ šāmĕʿû bĕqôlĕḵā ûḇĕtôrātĕḵā lōʾ-hālāḵû): the three-fold negation — did not listen to your voice, did not walk in your Torah, did not do all you commanded — summarizes the entire pre-exile history. Wattaḇiʾ ʾălêhem ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ hazzōʾt (Wattaḇiʾ ʾălêhem ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ hazzōʾt): "Therefore you brought all this disaster on them" — the prayer's theology of history is unambiguous: the exile is YHWH's just response to covenant infidelity. Jeremiah does not pray for YHWH to change his assessment; he prays in the context of complete agreement with YHWH's judgment.
32.24: Hinnēh hasōlĕlôt bāʾû hāʿîr lĕlŏkdāh (Hinnēh hasōlĕlôt bāʾû hāʿîr): the siege ramps (sōlĕlôt) are a visible, physical confirmation of the prophetic word — the disaster YHWH announced has arrived. Wĕhāʿîr nittĕnâ bĕyad hakkaśdîm hammiLlāḥămîm ʿālêhā (Wĕhāʿîr nittĕnâ bĕyad hakkaśdîm): the city is being given (nittĕnâ, Niphal perfect — prophetic perfect of certainty) into the hand of the Chaldeans. ʾĂšer dibbartā hāyâ wĕhinnĕḵā rōʾeh (ʾĂšer dibbartā hāyâ wĕhinnĕḵā rōʾeh): "What you said has come true, and you yourself see it" — the prayer acknowledges the full evidence of judgment before making its final appeal.
32.25: Wĕʾattâ ʾădōnāy YHWH ʾāmartā ʾēlay qnēh-lĕḵā haśśādeh bakkesep wĕhāʿēd ʿēdîm wĕhāʿîr nittĕnâ bĕyad hakkaśdîm (Wĕʾattâ ʾădōnāy YHWH ʾāmartā ʾēlay qnēh-lĕḵā haśśādeh bakkesep): the prayer's climactic paradox: "You have told me: Buy the field with money and get witnesses — even though the city is being handed to the Chaldeans." This is the prayer of a man caught between two divine words — the word of judgment being enacted outside his window and the word of restoration being enacted through his legal transaction. The prayer does not resolve the paradox; it simply presents it to YHWH, confident that YHWH's answer will be the reconciliation of both words. The faith of vv7-15 was enacted in action; now it is expressed in prayer.
32.26: Wayhî dĕḇar-YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû lēʾmōr (Wayhî dĕḇar-YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû lēʾmōr): the divine response comes immediately after the prayer, introduced by the standard prophetic reception formula. The answer occupies the rest of the chapter (vv27-44). The structure — human obedience → prayer → divine word — models prophetic intercession as the movement through which YHWH chooses to explain his redemptive purposes.
32.27: Hinnēh ʾănî YHWH ʾĕlōhê kol-bāśār hăyippālēʾ mimmennî kol-dāḇār (ʾănî YHWH ʾĕlōhê kol-bāśār hăyippālēʾ mimmennî kol-dāḇār): YHWH's self-identification: "I am the LORD, the God of all flesh" — kol-bāśār (all flesh) transcends Israel; this is universal sovereignty. The rhetorical question echoes Jeremiah's prayer (v17: lōʾ-yippālēʾ mimmĕḵā kol-dāḇār) and returns it as divine declaration: "Is anything too difficult (yippālēʾ) for me?" The answer to the paradox of v25 is the transcendent power of YHWH over all flesh and all circumstances. Luke 1:37 ("For nothing will be impossible with God") applies the same adynaton formula to the annunciation.
32.28: Lāḵēn kōh ʾāmar YHWH hinnĕnî nōtēn ʾet-hāʿîr hazzōʾt bĕyad hakkaśdîm (hinnĕnî nōtēn ʾet-hāʿîr hazzōʾt bĕyad hakkaśdîm): the divine word confirms the judgment unambiguously — the hinnēnî ("behold me") emphasizes YHWH's personal agency in the handing-over. The restoration promise does not cancel or mitigate the judgment; it follows it. Both words stand: the city will fall (vv28-35) and houses and fields will yet be bought (v15; vv36-44).
32.29: Ûḇāʾû hakkaśdîm hammiLlāḥămîm ʿal-hāʿîr hazzōʾt wĕhiṣṣîtû ʾet-hāʿîr hazzōʾt bāʾēš ûśĕrāpûhā (wĕhiṣṣîtû ʾet-hāʿîr hazzōʾt bāʾēš): the burning of the city is stated plainly. Wĕʾet-habbāttîm ʾăšer qiṭṭĕrû ʿal-gaggôtêhem laBBaʿal wĕhissĕkû nĕsāḵîm lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm lĕmaʿan hakʿisēnî (lĕmaʿan hakʿisēnî): "to provoke me to anger" — the rooftop incense and drink offerings to Baal and other gods are explicitly cited as the cause of judgment. The covenant lawsuit (7:31; 19:5 on Molech; 44:17 on the queen of heaven) is the theological basis of the military catastrophe.
32.30: Kî-hāyû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ûḇĕnê yĕhûdâ raq ʿōśîm hārāʿ bĕʿênay minnĕʿurêhem (raq ʿōśîm hārāʿ bĕʿênay minnĕʿurêhem): "only doing evil in my eyes from their youth" — the indictment is temporal (minnĕʿurêhem, "from their youth") and total (raq ʿōśîm, "doing nothing but evil"). This parallels the earlier accusation against Jerusalem in 2:20-22 ("from of old I broke your yoke") and recalls YHWH's repeated sending of prophets (7:25; 11:7; 25:3-4; 26:5; 29:19; 35:15; 44:4). The long history of repeated sending and repeated rejection is the weight behind the judgment.
32.31: Kî ʿal-ʾapî wĕʿal-ḥămātî hāytâ lî hāʿîr hazzōʾt lĕminnê hayyôm ʾăšer bānû ʾôtāh wĕʿad hayyôm hazzeh (lĕminnê hayyôm ʾăšer bānû ʾôtāh wĕʿad hayyôm hazzeh): "from the day it was built to this day" — the scope of provocation is the entire history of Jerusalem from its establishment as David's capital. The city's founding did not change the fundamental disposition of its inhabitants. The oracle anticipates the critique in Acts 7:51 (Stephen's speech): the stiff-necked people "always resist the Holy Spirit," from Moses to Jesus.
32.32: ʿAl kol-rāʿat bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ûḇĕnê yĕhûdâ ʾăšer ʿāśû lĕhaḵʿisēnî hēmmâ malkêhem śārêhem kōhănêhem ûnĕḇiʾêhem wĕʾîš yĕhûdâ wĕyōšĕḇê yĕrûšālāyim: the comprehensive list of offenders — kings, officials, priests, prophets, the men of Judah, and the residents of Jerusalem. No class, no office, no tier of society is exempted. The total coverage is significant for the new covenant promise of ch31:34 ("they will all know me, from the least to the greatest") — the new covenant must be as comprehensive as the indictment.
32.33: Wayyipnû ʾēlay ʿōrep wĕlōʾ pānîm (Wayyipnû ʾēlay ʿōrep wĕlōʾ pānîm): "they turned their backs (ʿōrep) to me, not their faces (pānîm)" — the spatial metaphor for apostasy: to turn the neck/back is to reject relationship; to turn the face is to enter it. Ezekiel uses the same imagery (Ezek 8:16: worshipers "turning their backs to the temple of the LORD and facing east"). Wĕlammēd ʾôtām haškēm wĕlammēd wĕʾênām šōmĕʿîm lāqaḥat mûsār (haškēm wĕlammēd): "rising early to teach" — the haškēm (rising early) formula used throughout Jeremiah for urgent, persistent prophetic address (7:13; 7:25; 11:7; 25:3-4) is now applied to divine teaching: YHWH himself persistently taught, and they persistently refused correction (mûsār).
32.34: Wayyāśîmû šiqqûṣêhem babbayit ʾăšer niqrāʾ-šĕmî ʿālāyw lĕṭammĕʾô (šiqqûṣêhem babbayit ʾăšer niqrāʾ-šĕmî ʿālāyw): detestable things (šiqqûṣîm — the technical term for idols, Deut 7:26; 29:17; Ezek 5:11) in the temple that bears YHWH's name. The profanation of the name (niqrāʾ-šĕmî ʿālāyw) is the precise violation of the Decalogue's third commandment (Exod 20:7: "you shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain") applied institutionally. The temple, intended to be the house of YHWH's name, became the house of his rivals.
32.35: Wayyiḇnû ʾet-bāmôt habbaʿal ʾăšer bĕgêʾ ḇen-Hinnōm lĕhaʿăḇîr ʾet-bĕnêhem wĕʾet-bĕnôtêhem lammōleḵ ʾăšer lōʾ-ṣiwwîtîm wĕlōʾ ʿālĕtâ ʿal-libbî: the triple negation for Molech child sacrifice — lōʾ-ṣiwwîtîm (I did not command them), wĕlōʾ ʿālĕtâ ʿal-libbî (it did not come up on my heart/mind) — matches 7:31 and 19:5 exactly. The repetition in three separate contexts (7, 19, 32) functions as legal triple attestation: YHWH's total disavowal of the practice is absolute and documented. The phrase lōʾ ʿālĕtâ ʿal-libbî (it never entered my mind) is an extreme statement — the very thought was alien to YHWH's nature.
32.36: Wĕʿattâ lāḵēn kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-hāʿîr hazzōʾt ʾăšer ʾattem ʾōmĕrîm nittĕnâ bĕyad melek bāḇel bĕḥereḇ ûḇārāʿāḇ ûḇaddāḇer (Wĕʿattâ lāḵēn): the transitional wĕʿattâ lāḵēn ("now therefore") pivots from indictment (vv28-35) to restoration promise (vv36-44). The city being delivered by sword, famine, and plague is the acknowledged reality; what follows is not a denial of it but YHWH's word about what comes after.
32.37: Hinnĕnî mĕqabbĕṣām mikkol-hāʾărāṣôt ʾăšer hiddaḥtîm šām bĕʾappî ûḇăḥămātî ûḇĕqeṣep gādôl (mikkol-hāʾărāṣôt ʾăšer hiddaḥtîm šām): the gathering from all the lands is YHWH's direct reversal of the scattering. bĕʾappî ûḇăḥămātî ûḇĕqeṣep gādôl (in my anger, fury, and great wrath) — the same three terms used in ch30's opening oracle — acknowledges that the scattering was genuine divine anger, not merely Babylonian military superiority. Wahăšîḇōtîm ʾel-hammāqôm hazzeh wĕhôšaḇtîm lāḇeṭaḥ (wĕhôšaḇtîm lāḇeṭaḥ): the security (beṭaḥ) of the restored community is YHWH's guarantee, not their own military capacity.
32.38: Wĕhāyû-lî lĕʿām wăʾănî ʾehyeh lāhem lēʾlōhîm (Wĕhāyû-lî lĕʿām wăʾănî ʾehyeh lāhem lēʾlōhîm): the covenant formula in its restored form. The formula appears in both the Sinai covenant (Lev 26:12; Exod 6:7) and the new covenant (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:28; 37:27; Rev 21:3). The simplicity of the declaration — "they will be my people, and I will be their God" — contains all covenant theology. In 32:38 it is restoration of what was broken; in 31:33 it is the new covenant's fulfillment; in Rev 21:3 it is the eschatological completion of the entire biblical trajectory.
32.39: Wĕnātattî lāhem lēḇ ʾeḥād wĕdereḵ ʾeḥād lĕyirʾâ ʾôtî kol-hayyāmîm lĕṭôḇ lāhem wĕliḇnêhem ʾaḥărêhem (lēḇ ʾeḥād wĕdereḵ ʾeḥād): "one heart and one way" — the lēḇ ʾeḥād (one heart) is the community-level version of the new covenant's internal Torah (31:33). Ezekiel 11:19-20 and 36:26 give the mechanism: removal of the heart of stone, implantation of a heart of flesh with the Spirit. lĕyirʾâ ʾôtî kol-hayyāmîm (to fear me all the days) — the yirʾat YHWH (fear of YHWH) is not an emotion to be cultivated but a divine gift to be given: YHWH will put his fear in their hearts (v40).
32.40: Wĕkāratî lāhem bĕrît ʿôlām ʾăšer lōʾ-ʾāšûḇ mēʾaḥărêhem lĕhêṭîḇ ʾôtām wĕʾet-yirʾātî ʾettēn bĕlibbām lĕḇiltî sûr mēʿālay: the everlasting covenant (bĕrît ʿôlām) contains the resolution of the covenant problem — YHWH guarantees his own fidelity (lōʾ-ʾāšûḇ mēʾaḥărêhem, "I will never turn away from doing good to them") and the people's fidelity (wĕʾet-yirʾātî ʾettēn bĕlibbām lĕḇiltî sûr mēʿālay, "I will put my fear in their hearts so they will not turn away from me"). The failure of the Sinai covenant was Israel's unfaithfulness; the new everlasting covenant removes the structural cause of that failure by making human faithfulness a divine provision, not a human achievement. Hebrews 13:20-21 cites this "eternal covenant" as the ground of the divine work in believers.
32.41: Wĕśaśtî ʿălêhem lĕhêṭîḇ ʾôtām wĕnāṭaʿtîm bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt bĕʾemet bĕḵol-libbî ûḇĕḵol-napšî (Wĕśaśtî ʿălêhem lĕhêṭîḇ ʾôtām): "I will rejoice in doing good to them" — śûś (to rejoice, exult) is divine delight in the act of blessing. bĕḵol-libbî ûḇĕḵol-napšî (bĕḵol-libbî ûḇĕḵol-napšî): "with all my heart and all my soul" — YHWH applies the Shema language to himself (Deut 6:5: "love YHWH your God with all your heart and all your soul"). The command Israel failed to obey, YHWH fulfills toward them: his covenant commitment is a whole-hearted, whole-being investment in their good. Zephaniah 3:17 echoes this: "He will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing."
32.42: Kî ḵōh ʾāmar YHWH kaʾăšer hēḇēʾtî ʾel-hāʿām hazzeh ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ haggĕdōlâ hazzōʾt kēn ʾānōḵî mēḇîʾ ʾălêhem ʾet kol-haṭṭôḇâ ʾăšer ʾānōḵî dōḇēr ʾălêhem (kaʾăšer hēḇēʾtî... kēn ʾānōḵî mēḇîʾ): "Just as I have brought all this great disaster... so I will bring on them all the good." The kāʾăšer... kēn (as... so) structure uses the certainty of the judgment-fulfillment as the guarantee of the restoration-fulfillment. If the word of judgment was reliable (and v24 confirms it is being enacted), the word of restoration is equally reliable. The same sovereignty that brought the disaster brings the good.
32.43: Wĕniknû haśśādôt bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt ʾăšer ʾattem ʾōmĕrîm šĕmāmâ hî mēʾên ʾādām ûḇĕhēmâ nittĕnâ bĕyad hakkaśdîm (ʾăšer ʾattem ʾōmĕrîm šĕmāmâ hî mēʾên ʾādām ûḇĕhēmâ): "the land about which you say it is desolate, without man or beast." The human perception of total desolation (siege, fall, deportation) is acknowledged as the present reality. Wĕniknû haśśādôt bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt (Wĕniknû haśśādôt bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt): "Fields will be bought in this land" — the prophecy of vv9-15 is now declared as YHWH's own word: the field purchase is the enacted sign of what YHWH has determined to do.
32.44: Śādôt bakkesep yiqnû wĕkātôḇ bassēper wĕḥātôm wĕhāʿēd ʿēdîm bĕʾereṣ Binyāmîn ûḇisĕḇîḇôt yĕrûšālāyim ûḇĕʿārê yĕhûdâ ûḇĕʿārê hāhār ûḇĕʿārê haššĕpēlâ ûḇĕʿārê hannegeḇ kî ʾāšîḇ ʾet-šĕḇûtām nĕʾum-YHWH: the final verse encompasses the full geographical range of the land of Israel — Benjamin (Jeremiah's own tribe, where Anathoth is), the environs of Jerusalem, the towns of Judah, the hill country (hāhār), the Shephelah (haššĕpēlâ), and the Negev. The comprehensive geography is the scope of the promise: the entire land will be restored. The chapter closes with the covenantal formula kî ʾāšîḇ ʾet-šĕḇûtām ("for I will restore their fortunes") — a technical term (šûḇ šĕḇût/šĕḇît) used repeatedly in restoration oracles (30:3, 18; 31:23; 33:7, 11, 26; Job 42:10; Ezek 16:53; Amos 9:14; Zeph 3:20) for the comprehensive reversal of exile. The field purchase of vv7-15 is thus the sign of what v44 declares as YHWH's absolute promise.

37.1: A brief regnal formula for Zedekiah — מַלַּךְ (mallakh, was made king): the passive/causative points to Nebuchadnezzar as the appointing power (v. 1b makes this explicit). תַּחַת (tachath, in place of): Coniah/Jeconiah was deposed and exiled (597 BCE); Zedekiah rules as a vassal king, not a free monarch. The opening frames everything that follows: a king installed by Babylon is now secretly consulting the prophet to resist Babylon.
37.2: לֹא שָׁמְעוּ (lo shame'u, they did not listen): the Jeremianic indictment verb — shama' (listen, obey) or its negation is the hinge between covenant obedience and covenant disaster throughout the book. All three parties are indicted: the king, his servants (avadav), and the people of the land (am ha'aretz) — no class is exempt. The words were spoken through (beyad, by the hand of) Jeremiah — the prophetic instrumentality formula.
37.3: דְּרַשׁ (drash, inquire/seek): Zedekiah's sending of envoys to Jeremiah is an act of cultic consultation — drash is the technical verb for seeking a divine oracle. The irony is acute: the king who refuses to heed Jeremiah's sustained preaching still treats him as an oracular resource to be consulted in a crisis. הִתְפַּלֵּל (hitpallel, pray): the request is for intercessory prayer — YHWH is approached through the prophet's mediation.
37.4: בָּא וְיֹצֵא (ba veyotze', coming and going): Jeremiah's freedom of movement is noted because it is about to end. The circumstantial clause explains the state of affairs before the main action of the chapter. The author's literary technique: establish the prophet's relative freedom, then show how it is taken away by those he is trying to warn.
37.5: Pharaoh's army (cheil Par'oh) advances and the Chaldeans lift the siege temporarily. שָׁמְעוּ (same'u, they heard): the same root used in v2 for Israel's failure to hear — the Chaldeans hear military intelligence and respond; Israel hears the word of YHWH and does not respond. The contrast is built into the vocabulary.
37.6: The characteristic prophetic messenger formula: the word of YHWH came (vayehi devar YHWH el) — the initiative is entirely divine; the prophet is a receiver and transmitter, not an originator. The oracle in vv7-10 is YHWH's direct reply to Zedekiah's request for a word.
37.7: שֹׁב יָשׁוּב (shov yashuv, he will return): the infinitive absolute for emphasis — Pharaoh's army will certainly return to Egypt; its departure is not permanent rescue. The same shuv root that means 'repent/return' throughout Jeremiah is used here in its literal sense, but the irony is present: what Israel fails to do (turn/repent to YHWH), Pharaoh's army does naturally (return to its own land).
37.8: וְלָכְדוּהָ וּשְׂרָפוּהָ בָאֵשׁ (velakheduha userafuha va'esh, they will capture it and burn it with fire): the dual fate of the city is stated with unambiguous finality. The burning with fire (esh) is a recurring prophetic image for Jerusalem's destruction (cf. 21:10, 32:29, 38:18) and the fire of YHWH's covenant judgment (Deut 32:22).
37.9: אַל-תַּשִּׁאוּ נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם (al-tashi'u nafshoteykhem, do not deceive yourselves): the reflexive idiom — literally 'do not lift up your own lives [in vain hope].' The warning against self-deception is psychologically precise: under siege pressure, people generate false hope from any temporary relief. Halokh yelekhu (they will certainly go away) uses emphatic infinitive absolute — the Chaldeans are definitely not leaving permanently.
37.10: The argument reaches extreme hypothetical: even if only wounded men (anashim medukkaim) were left from the Chaldean army, they would still rise and burn the city. The point is theological: the judgment is YHWH's own determination and cannot be deflected by military events. Human calculations are irrelevant when divine decree is operative.
37.11: A narrative transition verse — הֵעָלוֹת חֵיל הַכַּשְׂדִּים (he'alot cheil hakhashdim, when the Chaldean army had withdrawn/gone up): the siege is lifted. The vocabulary of military withdrawal sets the scene for Jeremiah's attempt to leave the city on private business.
37.12: חָלַק מִשָּׁם (chalaq misham, to receive his portion there): the unusual phrase may mean receiving a hereditary property division or share. Anathoth was Jeremiah's hometown (1:1). The mundane detail — the prophet trying to conduct personal property business during a crisis — is characteristic of Jeremiah's narrative: his life and the city's fate are constantly intertwined. בְּנִיָמִן (Binyamin, Benjamin): Anathoth was in the tribal territory of Benjamin.
37.13: נֹפֵל אַתָּה אֶל-הַכַּשְׂדִּים (nofel attah el-hakhashdim, you are defecting/falling to the Chaldeans): nafal el (fall/go over to) is the idiomatic expression for defection, used throughout chs 37-39. The arrest is based on political suspicion, not legal evidence — Irijah does not listen (lo shama', again the indictment verb) to Jeremiah's denial.
37.14: שֶׁקֶר (sheqer, lie/falsehood): Jeremiah's denial uses the same word that characterizes the false prophets throughout the book. The irony: the only true prophet accuses his accusers of falsehood, while the false prophets are never arrested. Irijah refuses to listen — the echo of v2 (the people refused to listen) is intentional.
37.15: הִכּוּ (hikku, they beat him): the officials' violence against the prophet is unambiguous. בֵּית הָאֵסוּר (beit ha'esur, house of detention/imprisonment): literally 'house of the bond/chain.' The scribal house of Jonathan is converted into a prison — the institution of literacy (the scribe) becomes the instrument of prophetic suppression.
37.16: חֲנֻיֹּות הַבּוֹר (chanuyot habor, the vaulted cells of the pit): bor (pit/cistern) is Jeremiah's prison vocabulary — the same word appears in ch38's cistern narrative. The pit (bor) is also the underworld, the place of the dead in Psalms and elsewhere. The prison conditions approximate death — a motif that will be made literal in ch38.
37.17: הֲיֵשׁ דָּבָר מֵאֵת יְהוָה (hayesh davar me'et YHWH, is there a word from YHWH?): Zedekiah's private inquiry is urgent and genuine — he wants to know YHWH's word. Jeremiah's reply is direct: יֵשׁ (yesh, there is). The answer: 'You will be handed over to the king of Babylon.' The word from YHWH has not changed since ch21. No imprisonment changes the prophetic word.
37.18: Jeremiah's legal complaint (ma chatatati lekha, what have I sinned against you): the language is formal legal protestation of innocence. He inverts the question — if his prophecy was false, what specific wrong did he do? The implicit argument: prophecy that accurately describes what is happening cannot be criminalized.
37.19: הַנֹּבְאִים לָכֶם (hannov'im lakhem, the prophets who prophesied to you): the false prophets who promised 'the king of Babylon will not come against you' are rhetorically invoked. Their non-appearance before the enemy is proof of their falsehood — where are they now? The prophetic vindication comes through historical fulfillment, not institutional endorsement.
37.20: תִּפֹּל תַּחֲנוּנָתִי (tipol tachanunatiy, let my plea fall before you): tachanunim are earnest supplications/entreaties — the word used in prayers seeking mercy. The request not to be returned to Jonathan's prison is the only personal request Jeremiah makes in the entire chapter. The prophet who has given YHWH's word faithfully asks only for basic survival.
37.21: חֲצַר הַמַּטָּרָה (chatzer hamattarah, court of the guard): the less severe confinement — an open courtyard detention rather than a dungeon cell. כִּכַּר לֶחֶם (kikkar lechem, loaf of bread): the daily provision from the bakers' street (chutz ha'ofim) until the bread ran out. The narrator preserves the physical detail — the prophet of divine judgment is kept alive by a daily bread allotment, a small mercy in the catastrophe.

38.1: Four officials are named who heard Jeremiah's words: Shephatiah son of Mattan, Gedaliah son of Pashhur, Jucal (Jehucal) son of Shelemiah, and Pashhur son of Malchiah. Two of these (Jucal/Jehucal and Pashhur) were previously mentioned as envoys of Zedekiah (37:3; 21:1). They now turn from messengers to accusers — the officials who once sought Jeremiah's help are now seeking his death.
