38.2: הַיֹּשֵׁב בָּעִיר הַזֹּאת יָמוּת (hayoshev ba'ir hazot yamut, whoever stays in this city will die): Jeremiah's message is stark — the city is under covenant-curse. הַנֹּפֵל אֶל-הַכַּשְׂדִּים יִחְיֶה (hannofeil el-hakhashdim yichyeh, whoever goes over to the Chaldeans will live): the same nafal el (fall to/defect) vocabulary of ch37 — what got Jeremiah arrested is now presented as the survival strategy. The triple death-by-triad: sword, famine, pestilence (cherev, ra'av, dever) for those who stay; life for those who go over.
38.3: הִנָּתֵן תִּנָּתֵן (hinaten tinaten, she will certainly be handed over): emphatic infinitive absolute construction expressing absolute certainty. The grammatical stress on inevitability is characteristic of Jeremiah's judgment speeches — the time for conditional clauses has passed; YHWH's word is now simply declarative.
38.4: מְרַפֶּא הוּא אֶת-יְדֵי אַנְשֵׁי הַמִּלְחָמָה (merappe hu et-yedei anshei hamilchama, he is weakening the hands of the soldiers): the charge uses military/political language — Jeremiah's preaching is not heresy but sedition, undermining military morale (yedei, literally 'the hands' = military capacity). דֹּרֵשׁ לְשָׁלוֹם (doresh leshalom, seeking/desiring the welfare): the accusation implies Jeremiah is a collaborator; the officials have inverted the prophetic vocation — the prophet who seeks the true welfare of the people is accused of seeking their harm.
38.5: הִנֵּה הוּא בְיֶדְכֶם (hineh hu beyedkhem, he is in your hands): Zedekiah's capitulation — the royal phrase of abdication. אֵין הַמֶּלֶךְ יוּכַל אֶתְכֶם דָּבָר (ein hammelekh yukhal etkhem davar, the king cannot do anything against you): a remarkable admission of the king's powerlessness against his officials. This is the theological problem of Zedekiah's reign: a man who secretly fears YHWH (vv16-17) but is politically paralyzed by his officials.
38.6: בּוֹר (bor, cistern/pit): the same word as ch37's prison (beit ha'asur = house of detention → bor = actual pit). The cistern was used for water storage; when empty (ein bo mayim, no water in it) it became a prison. וַיִּשְׁקַע יִרְמְיָהוּ בַּטִּיט (vayishqa Yirmeyahu batit, Jeremiah sank into the mud): the imagery of Ps 40:2 — 'He drew me up from the pit of destruction, out of the miry bog' — is realized literally. The prophet of judgment experiences covenant-curse conditions first-hand.
38.7: עֶבֶד-מֶלֶךְ הַכּוּשִׁי (Eved-Melekh haKushi, Ebed-melech the Ethiopian/Cushite): the name means literally 'servant of the king.' The irony is layered: a foreigner (Cushite, from sub-Saharan Africa), whose name means 'servant of the king,' serves the king of Judah while the Judean officials are murdering the king's — and ultimately YHWH's — prophet. סָרִיס (saris, court official/eunuch): the same term for Joseph's Egyptian master (Gen 39:1). A marginalized foreigner whose service to YHWH's prophet will be honored with a specific personal oracle (39:15-18).
38.8: Ebed-melech goes directly from the cistern to the king — the narrative pace accelerates. The bureaucratic geography (cistern → king's house) maps the power asymmetry: Ebed-melech, though low in status, has access to the king that he uses on behalf of the imprisoned prophet.
38.9: הָרֵעוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים הָאֵלֶּה (hare'u ha'anashim ha'elleh, these men have done evil): Ebed-melech's accusation before the king uses the covenant-ethics vocabulary — ra' (evil). The practical argument: Jeremiah will die of hunger (ra'av, famine — the word from the covenant-curse triad) before any military action kills him. Even in prison, the covenant curses pursue.
38.10: Zedekiah's command: take thirty men (shloshim anashim). The number is strikingly large — it suggests either great concern for Jeremiah's safety against opposition, or the physical difficulty of the rescue from the deep cistern.
38.11: סְחָבוֹת בָּלוֹת וּמְלָחִים (sechabot balot umelachim, worn-out rags and scraps): the mundane materials of Jeremiah's rescue — old rags and worn garments to cushion the rope. The physical detail is preserved because it humanizes the rescue: a powerful court official using worn cloth to save a prophet from a muddy pit. The covenantal imagination sees grace in small provisions.
38.12: שִׂים נָא הַסְּחָבוֹת וְהַמְּלָחִים תַּחַת אַצִּלוֹת יָדֶיךָ (sim na hassechabot vehammelachim tachat atzilot yadekha, put the rags and cloths under your armpits beneath the ropes): physical tenderness in a life-or-death rescue. Ebed-melech's care for the prophet's physical comfort (preventing rope-burn) is noted — the detail reveals a character who does good thoroughly, not minimally.
38.13: יַעֲלוּ אֶת-יִרְמְיָהוּ בַּחֲבָלִים (ya'alu et-Yirmeyahu bachavalim, they pulled Jeremiah up with ropes): the reversal of v6 — thrown in by officials who use their power for death; pulled out by a foreign servant who uses his access for life. Jeremiah returns to the court of the guard (chatzer hamattarah), the intermediate confinement.
38.14: בַּמָּבוֹא הַשְּׁלִישִׁי (bammavo hashlishi, the third entrance): the private, oblique approach — Zedekiah summons Jeremiah to an out-of-the-way entrance for a secret conversation. The secrecy is itself significant: a king who cannot publicly acknowledge the prophet whose words he privately desires.
38.15: הֲמוֹת תְּמִיתֵנִי (hamot temiteni, you will certainly put me to death): emphatic infinitive absolute. Jeremiah pre-empts the risk — his counsel cannot be honest unless he has immunity. The conditional structure (if I tell you ... if I counsel you ...) reveals the trap: the king needs the prophet's word but cannot protect the prophet from his own officials.
38.16: חַי-יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה-לָנוּ אֶת-הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַזֹּאת (chai-YHWH asher asah-lanu et-hannefesh hazot, as YHWH lives who made this life for us): an oath formula invoking YHWH as both witness and life-giver. Nefesh (life/breath/being) in YHWH's creative provision — the same root as nishmat chayyim (breath of life) in Gen 2:7. Zedekiah's oath is the most theologically weighted statement in the chapter: he knows YHWH, swears by him, but cannot serve him.
38.17: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי צְבָאוֹת אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (YHWH Elohei Tzvaot Elohei Yisrael): the full throne-room title — YHWH of Hosts, God of Israel. The gravitas of the divine name proportionate to the gravity of the choice. יָצֹא תֵצֵא (yatzo tetze, if you go out): the choice Jeremiah presents is shuv (turn/surrender) and live, or refuse and be burned (vv8,18).
38.18: The covenant consequences of refusal: the city burned (nitzteta ba'esh), personal capture (lo timalet miyad). The warnings of ch21 and 34 are recapitulated here in direct address to the king. The personal dimension is sharpened — Zedekiah is not just warned about the city but told exactly what will happen to him personally.
38.19: יָרֵאתִי (yare'ti, I am afraid): the royal confession of fear. The king who cannot submit to Babylon fears a third party — the Judean defectors (hanefashim asher nafelu el-hakhashdim). The same nafal el idiom. Political paralysis layered on fear: afraid of Nebuchadnezzar, afraid of the defectors, afraid of his own officials — and therefore unable to act on the word of YHWH.
38.20: שְׁמַע בְּקוֹל יְהוָה (shema beqol YHWH, obey the voice of YHWH): the Deuteronomic obedience formula — shama' beqol is the covenantal call to hear-and-obey. Jeremiah's promise: וְיִיטַב לְךָ (veyitav lekha, and it will go well with you) — the blessing side of the covenant. The offer of life is still available, even at this late hour.
38.21: The adversative condition: וְאִם-מָאֵן אַתָּה (ve'im-maen attah, but if you refuse): the hinge between blessing and curse. YHWH has revealed (hirani YHWH) what will happen if Zedekiah refuses. Prophetic revelation gives exact knowledge of covenant consequences.
38.22: הֻצְאוּ כָּל-הַנָּשִׁים (hutzu kol-hannashim, all the women will be led out): the women of the royal court, Zedekiah's dependents, will be taken to the Babylonian officials. הֶחֱלִיאֻךָ וְיָכְלוּ לְךָ אַנְשֵׁי שְׁלֹמֶךָ (hecheli'ukha veyakhlu lekha anshei shlomekha, your trusted men have prevailed against you and defeated you): the taunt-song the women will sing exposes the social reality — the king was destroyed by the very men who should have supported him.
38.23: מַלֵּט לֹא תִמָּלֵט (mallet lo timaleit, you will not escape at all): emphatic double infinitive absolute — absolute certainty of capture. תִּתָּפֵשׂ (titafes, you will be seized): the fulfillment comes in 39:5 — word becomes event. The prophecy is not threat but description of inevitable consequence.
38.24: Zedekiah's response to prophetic counsel: not acceptance or rejection but a demand for secrecy. אַל-יֵדַע אִישׁ (al-yeda ish, let no man know): the king's primary concern is political exposure, not personal survival through obedience.
38.25: The scenario Zedekiah anticipates: officials questioning Jeremiah about the content of the private conversation. The king provides a cover story — a plausible official reason for the meeting (Jeremiah was petitioning about his imprisonment).
38.26: The cover story: אֲנִי מַפִּיל תְּחִנָּתִי לִפְנֵי הַמֶּלֶךְ (ani mappil techinnati lifnei hammelekh, I was laying my plea before the king): techinnah (entreaty/petition) is the word Jeremiah used in 37:20 for his actual request about imprisonment. The official story is a partial truth that conceals the whole truth. The episode raises the ethical complexity of prophetic survival in an authoritarian environment.
38.27: וַיִּשְׁאֲלוּ אֹתוֹ (vayyish'alu oto, they questioned him): the officials do exactly what Zedekiah predicted. Jeremiah's answer follows the king's script. וַיֶּחֱשׁוּ מִמֶּנּוּ (vayechashu mimmenu, they were silent/stopped questioning him): the cover holds. The political survival of the prophet is secured — but the theological problem remains: the word that would have saved the city is suppressed.
38.28: עַד-הַיּוֹם אֲשֶׁר-נִלְכְּדָה יְרוּשָׁלָ͏ִם (ad-hayyom asher-nilkedeha Yerushalayim, until the day Jerusalem was captured): the understated narrative close. Jeremiah's confinement continues until the city falls. The final clause of the verse in some manuscripts links directly to ch39 — the prophet in the court of the guard when Jerusalem is captured.

39.1: Precise date: the ninth year of Zedekiah, tenth month (January 588 BCE). The Babylonian siege is documented in 2 Kings 25:1 and the Babylonian Chronicles. The specificity of the dating serves a theological function: YHWH's word spoken through Jeremiah is grounded in verifiable historical events, not general predictions. The beginning of the siege is the beginning of the end.
39.2: בֻּקְעָה הָעִיר (buq'ah ha'ir, the city was breached): baqa' (breach/split/cleave) is a violent word — a wall is torn open as a body is torn. The date: eleventh year of Zedekiah, fourth month, ninth day (July 586 BCE). The 18-month siege is over. Tisha B'Av (the ninth of Av) commemorates this event; the later temple destructions are dated to the same annual date in Jewish tradition, making it the day of national catastrophe.
39.3: The Babylonian officials take up their positions at the Middle Gate (sha'ar hatavekh). Their names and titles are given: Nergal-sharezer, Samgar-nebo, Sarsekim the Rab-saris, Nergal-sharezer the Rab-mag. The Rab-mag (rav mag) and Rab-saris (rav saris) are Babylonian administrative titles. The presence of named officials in a Hebrew text from this period provides historical corroboration of Jeremiah's setting.
39.4: וַיֵּצְאוּ לַיְלָה (vayyetze'u layelah, they went out by night): Zedekiah's midnight flight — the king who would not surrender by day flees by night. דֶּרֶךְ גַּן הַמֶּלֶךְ (derekh gan hammelekh, by way of the king's garden): the king who would not enter the way of covenant submission takes an escape route through his own garden. The irony of 'the king's garden' — all that worldly property he fled to preserve is left behind.
39.5: עֲרָבוֹת יְרִיחוֹ (aravot Yerikho, the plains of Jericho): the geography of the flight and capture is precise. מִשְׁפָּטִים (mishpatim, judgments): at Riblah before Nebuchadnezzar, Zedekiah faces formal legal judgments (mishpatim) — the covenant vocabulary for the legal verdict. Riblah in Syria is the administrative center where Nebuchadnezzar conducted his court.
39.6: אֶת-בְּנֵי צִדְקִיָּהוּ (et-benei Tzidkiyahu, the sons of Zedekiah): killed before their father's eyes. The fulfillment of 32:4 and 34:3 — Jeremiah had told Zedekiah he would see the king of Babylon face to face, and so he does, but as a prisoner watching his sons die. Also all the nobles of Judah (kol-chorei Yehudah) are executed — the leadership class that resisted YHWH's word is eliminated.
39.7: אֶת-עֵינֵי צִדְקִיָּהוּ עִוֵּר (et-einei Tzidkiyahu iver, he blinded the eyes of Zedekiah): the last thing Zedekiah sees is his sons' deaths; then his eyes are put out. The fulfillment of Ezek 12:13: 'I will bring him to Babylon ... yet he shall not see it' — a prophecy that seemed contradictory until now: he would be brought to Babylon but not see it (blinded before arrival). נְחֻשְׁתַּיִם (nechushtatayim, bronze chains — dual): the dual form = two fetters.
39.8: אֶת-בֵּית הַמֶּלֶךְ וְאֶת-בָּתֵּי הָעָם שָׂרַף (et-beit hammelekh ve'et-battei ha'am saraf, burned the king's palace and the people's houses): the covenant curse of Deut 28:52 realized — your cities will be besieged until your high and fortified walls come down. The burning of the physical city is the physical completion of the spiritual desolation that Jeremiah had been announcing for 40 years.
39.9: אֵת יֶתֶר הָעָם (et yeter ha'am, the rest of the people): Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard (rav tabachim, chief of the butchers/executioners — military title) exiles the survivors. The word for exile, golah, is from galah (to uncover, to go into exile, to strip bare) — the deportation as exposure and vulnerability.
39.10: דַּלַּת הָעָם (dallat ha'am, the poorest of the people — from dal, thin/weak/poor): the ironic remnant. Those who had nothing are given land and vineyards — the covenant promise of the land is partially fulfilled for the poor, the ones the ruling class always marginalized. The covenant's land-promise passes over the king and nobles to the dallim.
39.11: בְּיַד נְבוּזַרְאֲדָן (beyad Nevuzaradan, through Nebuzaradan): the divine arrangement is mediated through the foreign commander. YHWH's protection of Jeremiah reaches through the Babylonian chain of command — Nebuchadnezzar himself gives the orders.
39.12: קַח אֹתוֹ וְשִׂים עֵינֶיךָ עָלָיו (qach oto vesim einekha alav, take him and look after him): the 'set your eyes on him' idiom means attentive protection. כַּאֲשֶׁר יְדַבֵּר אֵלֶיךָ כֵּן עֲשֵׂה (ka'asher yedabber elekha ken aseh, do to him as he says to you): complete deference to the prophet's expressed wishes. The foreign king gives the prophet of YHWH more honor than the king of Judah did.
39.13: The named officials who carry out the orders — Nebuzaradan, Nebushazban the Rab-saris, Nergal-sharezer the Rab-mag — are the same officials from v3. The administrative machinery that conquered Jerusalem is now the instrument of Jeremiah's protection. Providence works through institutions, including hostile ones.
39.14: אֶל-גְּדַלְיָהוּ בֶּן-אֲחִיקָם בֶּן-שָׁפָן (el-Gedalyahu ben-Achiqam ben-Shaphan): Gedaliah son of Ahikam, the Shaphan-family member who had protected Jeremiah earlier (26:24). The family of Shaphan appears at every critical juncture of Jeremiah's protected survival. Jeremiah is handed over to the one family who consistently honored the prophetic word. לְהֵשֶׁב בְּתוֹךְ הָעָם (leheshev betokh ha'am, to live among the people): Jeremiah's preferred place — among the remaining people in the land.
39.15: A temporal insertion: the oracle to Ebed-melech was delivered while Jeremiah was still in the court of the guard — before the city fell. The placement here, after the fall narrative, signals the author's thematic intention: the faithfulness of the foreign servant is rewarded with a personal salvation oracle, placed in contrast to Zedekiah's fate.
39.16: מֵבִיא אֶת-דְּבָרַי אֶל-הָעִיר הַזֹּאת לְרָעָה וְלֹא לְטוֹבָה (mevi et-devarai el-ha'ir hazot lera'ah velo letovah, bringing my words against this city for harm and not for good): the words-coming-to-fulfillment formula. YHWH's words are the active agent — they 'come' (bo) to the city as he himself 'comes' in judgment. לְעֵינֶיךָ בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא (le'einekha bayyom hahu, before your eyes on that day): Ebed-melech will witness the fulfillment.
39.17: אֲבָל אַמַּלְטְךָ בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא (aval amaltetkha bayyom hahu, but I will deliver you on that day): the adversative aval (but/however) is the pivot of grace within judgment. While the city falls, the foreign servant is exempted. לֹא תִנָּתֵן בְּיַד הָאֲנָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה יָגוֹר מִפְּנֵיהֶם (lo tinaten beyad ha'anashim asher-attah yagor mipneihem, you will not be given into the hands of the men you fear): the covenant protection language.
39.18: כִּי-בָטַחְתָּ בִּי (ki-vatachta bi, because you trusted in me): the theological ground of Ebed-melech's rescue — not his ethnicity, not his court status, but his batach (trust/reliance) in YHWH. The same verb (batach) used in the Psalms for confident trust in YHWH as refuge. A foreign servant who trusted YHWH receives the covenant promise of protection that Judah's own king refused through disobedience.

40.1: Released at Ramah (Ramah): a holding area for deportees north of Jerusalem. The opening temporal clause situates this after the fall but before Jeremiah's definitive movement — a second calling/release narrative. בַּחֲנִקִּים אֲסֻר בְּתוֹךְ כָּל-גָּלוּת יְרוּשָׁלַ͏ִם (bachanickim asur betokh kol-galut Yerushalayim, bound with chains among all the exiles of Jerusalem): the prophet of exile is himself counted among the exiles, chained with them — he shares the suffering of the people whose judgment he announced.
40.2: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ דִּבֶּר אֶת-הָרָעָה הַזֹּאת (YHWH Elohekha dibber et-hara'ah hazot, YHWH your God spoke this disaster): a pagan commander confessing YHWH's prophetic sovereignty — 'YHWH your God pronounced this disaster.' The Babylonian acknowledges what Zedekiah's court refused to acknowledge: the disaster was YHWH's word made event. The irony is total: the foreign conqueror understands the theology; the covenant people did not.
40.3: כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה (ka'asher dibber vayyaas YHWH, as he said, so YHWH has done): Nebuzaradan's creedal summary — YHWH spoke and acted exactly as he said. This is the core of prophetic theology: the word and its fulfillment are one movement. לֹא-שְׁמַעְתֶּם בְּקוֹל יְהוָה (lo-shame'tem beqol YHWH, you did not obey the voice of YHWH): the pagan officer uses the covenant-indictment formula — the shama' beqol that Deuteronomy required and Jeremiah demanded. The foreigner pronounces the verdict the people refused to hear from their own prophet.
40.4: הַיּוֹם פָּטַחְתִּי אֶת-הָאַזִּקִּים (hayyom patachti et-ha'aziqim, today I have released your fetters): aziqim = shackles/chains. The day of Jeremiah's release from chains is a small personal exodus — physically freed from bondage in the moment of national exile. אֶל כָּל-הַטּוֹב בְּעֵינֶיךָ לָלֶכֶת שָׁם (el kol-hattov be'einekha lalechet sham, to wherever seems good in your eyes to go): genuine freedom of choice offered by the Babylonian commander — a freedom the king of Judah never gave the prophet.
40.5: שׁוּב אֶל-גְּדַלְיָּהוּ (shuv el-Gedalyahu, return to Gedaliah): the use of shuv (return) here is ironic — the prophet who spent 40 years calling Israel to shuv to YHWH is now told by a Babylonian to shuv to the land. וּשְׁבֵה אִתּוֹ בְּתוֹךְ הָעָם (ushvei itto betokh ha'am, and live with him among the people): yashav (dwell/sit/remain) — Jeremiah's choice is to dwell with the remnant rather than receive Babylonian support. The prophet identifies with the people in their land even in their humiliation.
40.6: מִצְפָּה (Mitzpah, Mizpah): literally 'watchtower/lookout point' — the administrative capital of the post-conquest remnant community. The covenant symbol: even after the destruction of Jerusalem and the temple, YHWH has a people in the land, and Jeremiah is among them. The land promised to Abraham, which the exile has not permanently revoked, still has a remnant.
40.7: The captains of the forces in the open country (sarei hachayalot asher bassadeh) and their men hear the news of Gedaliah's appointment. They had avoided capture by being outside the city. The post-conquest social geography: the poorest remained (v10), the military forces hiding in the countryside return.
40.8: Named captains come to Gedaliah at Mizpah: Ishmael son of Nethaniah (whose name and pedigree — son of Nethaniah, Elishama — will become significant in ch41), Johanan and Jonathan sons of Kareah, Seraiah, the sons of Ephai the Netophathite, and Jezaniah son of the Maacathite. The inventory of names preserves the historical reality of the remnant community's military leadership.
40.9: אַל-תִּירְאוּ מֵעֲבוֹד אֶת-הַכַּשְׂדִּים (al-tire'u me'avod et-hakhashdim, do not be afraid to serve the Chaldeans): Gedaliah's foundational speech uses yare' (fear) and aved (serve) — the Deuteronomic vocabulary of covenantal service (serve YHWH; fear YHWH). He redirects this language to the political reality: the survival strategy is to serve Babylon, which is precisely what Jeremiah had been saying for decades. שְׁבוּ בָאָרֶץ (shevu va'aretz, settle/dwell in the land): the covenant promise — living in the land — is possible even under Babylonian governance if the community submits.
40.10: לִפְנֵי הַכַּשְׂדִּים (lifnei hakhashdim, before the Chaldeans): Gedaliah will represent the community to the Babylonian administrators. אִסְפוּ יַיִן וְקַיִץ וְשֶׁמֶן (isfu yayin veqayitz veshemen, gather wine and summer fruit and oil): the covenant land's agricultural abundance is still available — the harvest is not canceled. The return to normal agricultural life is both practical instruction and a covenant sign: the land still produces for those who remain in it.
40.11: Jews in Moab, Ammon, Edom, and other nations — the diaspora before the formal exile — hear that the king of Babylon has left a remnant in the land. פְּלֵיטָה (peleitah, remnant/surviving fugitives): the remnant theology — YHWH always preserves a peleitah, a surviving band (cf. Isa 37:32; Joel 2:32). Even in judgment, the covenant promise of continuity is not extinguished.
40.12: וַיָּשֻׁבוּ כָּל-הַיְּהוּדִים מִכָּל-הַמְּקֹמוֹת (vayashuvu kol-haYehudim mikol-hamqomot, all the Jews returned from all the places): the double kol (all) emphasizes the scope — a mini-return from exile. This small return is a foretaste of the larger return prophesied in chs 30-33. The gathering at Gedaliah's Mizpah is a type of the eschatological ingathering.
40.13: Johanan son of Kareah and the military captains come to Gedaliah — the scene transitions from hopeful gathering to ominous warning. The intelligence they bring (Ishmael is plotting assassination) will be refused by Gedaliah, with catastrophic consequences.
40.14: הֲיָדֹעַ תֵּדַע (hayado' teda', do you really know?): the interrogative with emphatic infinitive absolute — surely you know! Baalis king of Ammon (merekh Bene-Ammon) has sent Ishmael to assassinate Gedaliah. The Ammonite king's political interest: preventing a stable Judean remnant that might eventually resist Ammonite expansion into formerly Judean territory. Political instability in the region is geopolitically useful to Ammon.
40.15: בַּסֵּתֶר (basseter, secretly): Johanan offers to kill Ishmael privately — assassination to prevent assassination. לָמָּה יַכֶּכָּה וְנָפֹצוּ כָּל-יְהוּדָה הַנִּקְבָּצִים אֵלֶיךָ (lammah yakkekha venafotzou kol-Yehudah haniqbatzim elekha, why should he strike you down and scatter all the Jews gathered around you?): the practical argument — the remnant community is fragile; killing Ishmael would preserve it.
40.16: לֹא-תַעֲשֶׂה הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה (lo-ta'aseh haddavar hazzeh, you must not do this): Gedaliah's refusal is absolute. שֶׁקֶר אַתָּה דֹּבֵר אֶל-יִשְׁמָעֵאל (sheqer attah dover el-Yishma'el, you are speaking falsely about Ishmael): the tragic final word of ch40. Gedaliah's sheqer — calling Johanan's warning a lie — echoes the entire book's pattern: the truth-tellers are not believed; the covenant community repeatedly fails to hear the word that would save it. The chapter ends with Gedaliah's fatal error stated plainly, awaiting the catastrophe of ch41.

41.1: The dating formula (baḥōdeš haššĕbîʿî, the seventh month) places the assassination just after the destruction of Jerusalem in the fifth month (ch. 39). Ishmael son of Nethaniah is identified as mizzereʿ hammelûkâ — "of royal descent" — which provides his motive: a rival claimant to Davidic authority eliminates the Babylonian-appointed governor. The ten men who accompany him suggest a planned political action, not a spontaneous crime. The MKT preserves "of royal descent" to retain the dynastic dimension of the assassination.
41.2: wayyāqom Yišmāʿēl ben-Nĕtanyāhû wĕhāʿăśārâ ʾănāšîm — the assassination of Gedaliah ends Israel's last chance for a viable remnant in the land. Gedaliah was of the Shaphan family (Ahikam son of Shaphan) — the family that had protected Jeremiah throughout his ministry (26:24). His death eliminates both the protective political leadership and the administrative infrastructure for covenantal life in the land. The MKT renders the action directly: "struck down Gedaliah... with the sword and killed him."
41.3: The wider massacre includes not only Gedaliah but "all the Jews who were with Gedaliah at Mizpah" and "the Chaldean soldiers (ʾanšê milḥāmâ mēhaKaśdîm) who were there" — the Babylonian military presence too. The elimination of the Babylonian garrison removes any protection the remnant had from Chaldean reprisal. Ishmael's action guarantees that the remnant cannot remain in the land safely.
41.4: The secrecy of the initial period — "before anyone knew of it" (ûbayyôm haššēnî lĕhahrōg ʾet-Gĕdalyāhû wĕʾîš lōʾ yādāʿ) — implies that Ishmael contained the news for a day. This detail prepares for the narrative trap he springs on the arriving pilgrims in v.6. The one-day concealment was the tactical window for the deception.
41.5: ûšĕmōnîm ʾîš bāʾû miŠĕkem miŠîlô ûmiŠōmrôn — eighty men from Shechem, Shiloh, and Samaria — all cities of the former northern kingdom. That northerners come with "shaved beards, torn clothing, and self-lacerations" (mĕgūlĕḥê zāqān ûqĕrûʿê bĕgādîm ûmitgōdĕdîm) bearing grain and incense for the temple suggests they were still oriented toward Jerusalem as their cultic center despite the northern kingdom's fall in 722 BC. The MKT preserves all three cities because they demonstrate that Israelite mourning spanned both kingdoms.
41.6: Ishmael's strategic deception: he goes out to meet the pilgrims bākôh bēlēk (weeping as he went), performing mourning to draw them in. The invitation — "Come to Gedaliah son of Ahikam" — is a lie that uses Gedaliah's name as bait. The MKT renders the dramatic irony of a man weeping his way to another murder by following the MT narrative exactly.
41.7: wayyiheî kĕbôʾām ʾel-tôk hāʿîr wayyišḥāṭēm Yišmāʿēl — the slaughter of seventy pilgrims. Wayyišlīkēm ʾel-tôk habbôr — he threw their bodies into the cistern. The scale of the atrocity (seventy killed, ten spared) and the method (thrown into a cistern) is the narrative nadir of post-fall Judah. The MKT follows the MT without softening: "slaughtered them and cast them into a pit."
41.8: ʾal-tĕmîtēnû kî-yeš-lānû matmōnîm baśśādeh — ten men survive by revealing hidden stores of food. Ḥiṭṭîm ûśĕʿōrîm wĕšemen ûdĕbaš (wheat, barley, oil, and honey) — agricultural survival provisions. Ishmael's economic pragmatism (he spares those with resources) shows his murders were not ideologically comprehensive. The MKT preserves the four food items as a specific detail that grounds the survival in practical terms.
41.9: The cistern of King Asa — habbbôr ʾăšer ʿāśâ hammelek ʾĀsāʾ — connects to the narrative of 1 Kgs 15:22 where Asa conscripted all Judah to fortify Mizpah against Baasha of Israel. A defensive cistern built centuries earlier to protect Judah now holds the bodies of murdered Judeans. The MKT preserves the historical note on Asa because it is the narrator's way of marking the horror: the city Asa built for Israel's protection now contains Israel's dead.
41.10: wayyišb Yišmāʿēl ʾet kol-šĕʾērît hāʿām — Ishmael takes captive "all the rest of the people in Mizpah, including the king's daughters" (ʾet-bĕnôt hammelek). The king's daughters are the last Davidic royalty in the land. Ishmael intends to take them to the Ammonites (Baalis king of Ammon was behind the conspiracy, 40:14), possibly as political pawns. The MKT renders "king's daughters" to preserve the Davidic dynastic significance.
41.11: Johanan son of Kareah and the other military commanders had been warned by Ishmael's intent (40:13-14) and now respond. ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ ʾăšer ʿāśâ Yišmāʿēl ben-Nĕtanyāhû — hearing of all the evil Ishmael did. The rescue mission is framed as a response to evil (rāʿâ), using the covenant-violation vocabulary.
41.12: The pursuit ends at hammayyim habbĕrēkâ haggedōlâ ʾăšer-bĕGibʿôn — the great pool at Gibeon. The Gibeon pool had earlier been the site of the civil conflict between Joab and Abner's forces (2 Sam 2:13). The geography carries the weight of intra-Israelite conflict. Johanan's forces "caught up with him" — the rescue is just in time.
41.13: The captives' relief on seeing Johanan: wayyiśmāḥû (they were overjoyed). The single word of emotional response encapsulates the shift from captivity to rescue. The MKT's "they were overjoyed" renders wayyiśmāḥû (the same root as śimḥâ, joy) — the covenantal joy-vocabulary reappearing in the darkest narrative section.
41.14: The captives turn back (wayyāšubû wayyēlĕkû ʾel-Yôḥānān) — using the šûb (return) vocabulary. In the wider context of Jeremiah the verb šûb carries heavy theological weight (return/repentance). Here it is a physical return, but the word choice may be the narrator's signal that this turning back is the right direction — toward Johanan rather than toward Ammon.
41.15: Ishmael escapes with eight men to the Ammonites (wayŷillāṭ Yišmāʿēl... weyēlek ʾel-bĕnê ʿAmmôn). His escape means he faces no earthly justice. The unresolved escape of the assassin is a narrative detail the text does not tidy up — Ishmael vanishes from the narrative as abruptly as he entered it, his crimes unpunished within the story.
41.16: The survivors Johanan recovered: "men of war, women, children, and court officials (sārîsîm), whom he brought back from Gibeon." The word sārîsîm (court officials, eunuchs) indicates this includes the palace infrastructure of the post-fall government. The MKT renders "court officials" as the best English equivalent for sārîsîm in this administrative context.
41.17: wayyēlĕkû wayyēšĕbû bĕGerût Kimhām ʾăšer ʾeṣel Bêt-Laḥem — "They set out and stayed at Geruth Chimham near Bethlehem"; Kimhām was the son of Barzillai the Gileadite (2 Sam 19:37-40) who received land near Bethlehem from David. The remnant rests on Davidic land (Bethlehem, the city of David) on their way to Egypt — the anti-Exodus direction. The MKT preserves the Bethlehem detail because it is geographically and theologically precise.
41.18: The stated motive for going to Egypt: fear of Chaldean reprisal (yārĕʾû mippĕnê haKaśdîm). The fear is rational — Ishmael killed the Babylonian-appointed governor. But the fear drives the decision that YHWH will explicitly forbid in ch. 42. The MKT preserves the fear motive without judgment in this verse; the judgment comes in the oracle that follows.

42.1: The full leadership of the post-fall remnant petitions Jeremiah — all the military commanders, Johanan son of Kareah, Jezaniah son of Hoshaiah, and "all the people from the least to the greatest" (miqāṭōn wĕʿad-gādôl). The inclusivity of the assembly (military leaders + all people) parallels the formal national assemblies of the covenant-making tradition. The decision being sought has community-wide implications.
42.2: tippal-nāʾ taḥinnātênû lĕpānêkā wĕhitpallēl baʿădēnû — "Please hear our plea for mercy and pray to the LORD your God for us." The phrase ʾĕlōhêkā (your God) rather than "our God" is notable — the remnant recognizes that Jeremiah has privileged access to YHWH. They identify YHWH as Jeremiah's God while asking for intercession. The MKT preserves "your God" because the pronoun reveals the community's spiritual distance.
42.3: The request: wĕyaggêd-lānû YHWH ʾĕlōhêkā ʾet-hadderek ʾăšer nēlek bāh — "Ask the LORD your God to tell us the way we should go and what we should do." The framing is obedience to divine guidance — they claim to want to know the way and then do it. This stated openness will be revealed as bad faith in 43:2-4, but in v.3 it sounds like genuine submission.
42.4: šāmaʿtî hinnĕnî mitpallēl ʾel-YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem — Jeremiah accepts the prophetic commission but establishes his role: he will report whatever YHWH says without holding anything back (lōʾ-ʾĕkhaśśēd mikkĕm dābār). The phrase "I will not withhold a word from you" is the prophet's commitment to full truthfulness even if the oracle is unwelcome. The MKT renders "I will not keep back a single word from you."
42.5: YHWH yihyeh bānû lĕʿēd ʾĕmet wĕneʾĕmān — "May the LORD be a true and faithful witness against us if we do not act in accordance with all that the LORD your God sends you to tell us." The oath-formula invokes YHWH as witness-judge. ʾĔmet wĕneʾĕmān (true and faithful) — the covenant-reliability doublet. The oath against themselves (ʾim-lōʾ nĕʿăśeh) is solemn and self-binding. The MKT renders the full conditional oath: "may the LORD be a true and faithful witness against us."
42.6: ʾim-ṭôb wĕʾim-rāʿ bĕqôl YHWH ʾĕlōhênû — "Whether it is good or bad, we will obey the voice of the LORD our God." The absolute commitment — good or bad outcome — appears to be the gold standard of submitted obedience (contrast Abraham's willingness to offer Isaac). The phrase lĕmaʿan yîṭab-lānû (so that it may go well with us) reveals the underlying motive: compliance for benefit rather than obedience as covenant relationship. The benefit-orientation will reappear in 44:17-18 (the queen of heaven argument).
42.7: wayhî miqṣêh ʿăśeret yāmîm — "At the end of ten days the word of the LORD came." The ten-day delay is a narrative feature: YHWH does not respond immediately. This implies genuine divine deliberation (not that YHWH needed time to decide, but that the form of the oracle required waiting). The waiting period also tests the community's patience and willingness to remain oriented toward YHWH rather than making a decision independently.
42.8: Jeremiah summons all the principals — military commanders, Johanan, and all the people — for the oracle delivery. The formal summoning mirrors the formal petition of vv. 1-3. The oracle is given to the whole assembly, not whispered to one leader. The MKT preserves "all the people from the least to the greatest" because the universal assembly formula matches the solemnity of what follows.
42.9: kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer šĕlaḥtem ʾōtî ʾēlāyw lĕhapîl tĕḥinnatkĕkem lĕpānāyw — the oracle formula grounds the divine word in the community's own petition. YHWH responds to the prayer they brought — the oracle is responsive to human seeking. The MKT renders "to whom you sent me to bring your petition" to preserve the responsive character of the oracle.
42.10: ʾim-šôb tēšĕbû bāʾārets hazzōʾt ûbānîtî ʾetkem wĕlōʾ ʾehĕrōs — "If you will remain in this land, I will build you up and not tear you down, and I will plant you and not uproot you"; the build/plant promise echoes Jeremiah's commissioning (1:10) and the positive side of the fig oracle (24:6). Kî niḥamtî ʾel-hārāʿâ ʾăšer ʿāśîtî lākem — "for I relent concerning the disaster I have brought on you" (niḥam, the verb of divine relenting). YHWH's relenting is contingent on Israel staying in the land.
42.11: ʾal-tîrĕʾû mippĕnê melek Bābel ʾăšer ʾattem yĕrēʾîm mippānāyw — "Do not be afraid of the king of Babylon, whom you now fear. Do not be afraid of him, declares the LORD, for I am with you to save you and deliver you from his power." The divine "I am with you" (kî ʾittĕkem ʾānî) formula — the Immanuel-presence promise — is the antidote to the fear of Babylon. The fear named in 41:18 as the reason for fleeing to Egypt is directly addressed and countered.
42.12: wĕnātattî lākem raḥămîm — "I will show you compassion (raḥămîm)"; the covenant-loyalty vocabulary — raḥămîm (womb-compassion, tender mercy) — invoked as the ground for Nebuchadnezzar's own compassion. YHWH will move the Babylonian king to have mercy on them and allow their return to the land. The MKT renders "I will show you compassion so that he will have compassion on you" to preserve the cause-effect chain: divine compassion → human compassion.
42.13: The negative conditional begins: wĕʾim ʾōmĕrîm ʾattem lōʾ-nēšēb bāʾārets hazzōʾt — "But if you say: We will not stay in this land." The either/or structure of the oracle mirrors the covenant's two-ways pattern: blessings for obedience, curses for disobedience. The command to stay is YHWH's word through the prophet; departure is disobedience to covenant instruction.
42.14: lēʾmōr lōʾ kî ʾerets Miṣrayim nēbôʾ — the specific reasons the remnant gives for preferring Egypt: no war, no trumpet alarm, no hunger. All three — war, trumpet, hunger — are the covenant curses they have already experienced. Egypt is conceived as covenant-curse-free space. The tragic irony is that Jeremiah's oracle promises those exact curses will follow them to Egypt (v.16).
42.15: šimĕʿû dĕbar-YHWH šĕʾērît Yĕhûdâ — "Hear the word of the LORD, O remnant of Judah"; the address šĕʾērît Yĕhûdâ (remnant of Judah) occurs throughout the post-fall chapters. The remnant is addressed as a recognized theological entity — the survivors who have the possibility of becoming the continuation of the covenant community. The oracle's weight is: what you do with this word determines whether there will be a remnant.
42.16: hahhereb ʾăšer ʾattem yĕrēʾîm mimmennâ šām tassîg ʾetkem — "Then the sword you fear will overtake you there in Egypt." The irony: what they flee from will find them where they flee to. Wĕhārāʿāb ʾăšer ʾattem dōʾăgîm mimmennû šām yidbaqlākem bĕMiṣrayim — "and the famine you dread will pursue you right into Egypt." The verb dābaqlākem (pursue/cling to you) conveys relentless following.
42.17: wĕhāyû kol-hāʾănāšîm ʾăšer-śāmû pĕnêhem lāboʾ Miṣrayim lāgûr šām — the comprehensive statement: "All who set their faces to go to Egypt to live there shall die by the sword, famine, and pestilence." Lāśûm pānîm lĕ- (to set one's face toward) is the idiom of determined direction. The MKT's "set their faces" preserves the idiom's decisiveness.
42.18: kăʾăšer nittāk ʾappî ûḥămātî ʾel-yōšĕbê Yĕrûšālaim — "Just as my anger and wrath were poured out on the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so my wrath will be poured out on you when you go to Egypt." The comparison to Jerusalem's fate is the most frightening possible warning: what they saw happen to Jerusalem will happen to them in Egypt. Wĕhîytem lĕʾālâ wĕlĕšammâ ûlĕqĕlālâ ûlĕḥerpâ — the fourfold shame vocabulary from 24:9 now applied to the Egypt-bound remnant.
42.19: dabbēr YHWH ʾălêkem šĕʾērît Yĕhûdâ lōʾ-tābōʾû Miṣrayim — "The LORD has spoken to you, O remnant of Judah: Do not go to Egypt." The simplest possible restatement of the prohibition. Yādōaʿ tēdĕʿû kî hăʿîdōtî bākem hayyôm — "Know for certain this day that I have warned you." The infinitive absolute (yādōaʿ tēdĕʿû) and the prophetic testimony formula make this the clearest possible divine warning.
42.20: kî hitʿîtem bĕnapsĕšôtêkem — "For you have been dishonest with yourselves"; the accusation is reflexive — the deceit is self-directed, not just outward dishonesty. Kî ʾattem šĕlaḥtem ʾōtî ʾel-YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem lēʾmōr hitpallēl baʿădēnû — the bad faith: they petitioned YHWH to find out what to do, then the oracle comes and they disobey it. The MKT's "dishonest with yourselves" renders hitʿîtem bĕnapsĕšôtêkem (you have made your souls err/sin against themselves) — the reflexive form signals self-deception.
42.21: The indictment stated: "I have declared it to you today, but you have not obeyed the voice of the LORD your God in anything he sent me to tell you." The final verdict before they act on their intention. The word bĕkōl ʾăšer šĕlaḥānî ʾălêkem (in all/everything he sent me to tell you) is comprehensive — not partial disobedience but total rejection of the oracle.
42.22: wĕʿattâ yādōaʿ tēdĕʿû kî baḥereb bārāʿāb ûbaddāber tāmûtû — "Now therefore know for certain that you will die by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence in the place where you want to go to settle." The oracle closes with the same triple judgment (sword/famine/plague) that has appeared throughout the book as the covenant-curse triad. The MKT renders yādōaʿ tēdĕʿû as "know for certain" (infinitive absolute for emphasis).

43.1: The narrative transition: after Jeremiah finishes speaking "all the words of the LORD their God (kol dibrê YHWH ʾĕlōhêhem) — every word the LORD their God had sent him to tell them" — the community's response is rejection. The redundancy ("all the words... every word") emphasizes the completeness of the message given; there is no remaining word that could have changed their minds. Rejection of comprehensive prophecy is total rejection.
43.2: wayyōʾmer ʿAzaryāhû ben-Hôšaʿyāhû... lōʾ šāqer ʾattâ mĕdabbēr — "Azariah son of Hoshaiah... said to Jeremiah: You are speaking falsely!" Šeqer (falsehood) — the same word Jeremiah used against Hananiah and the false prophets. Now the accusation of false prophecy is turned against the authentic prophet. The MKT renders the blunt accusation: "You are speaking falsely!" The exclamation is appropriate to the direct verbal attack.
43.3: kî Bārûk ben-Nĕriyyāhû massît ʾōtĕkā bānû — "But Baruch son of Neriah is inciting you against us, to deliver us into the hands of the Chaldeans." The scapegoating of Baruch: the accusation that Jeremiah's secretary is the real source of the anti-Egypt advice. Massît (inciting, stirring up) is the vocabulary used of the adversary who incites disobedience (cf. Deut 13:6). Making Baruch a Chaldean agent redirects blame from YHWH's word onto a human conspirator.
43.4: wayyišmaʿ Yôḥānān ben-Qārēaḥ wĕkol-śārê haḥăyālîm wĕkol-hāʿām bĕqôl YHWH — "So Johanan son of Kareah and all the commanders of the forces and all the people disobeyed the voice of the LORD (lōʾ šāmĕʿû bĕqôl YHWH), refusing to stay in the land." The word šāmaʿ bĕqôl (obey, listen to the voice) — the covenantal hearing-obedience term — is negated: they did not listen. The MKT renders the refusal directly: "disobeyed the voice of the LORD."
43.5: The irony of 43:5: Johanan and the commanders took "the entire remnant of Judah who had returned (šābû) from all the nations where they had been scattered" — those who had returned from exile-like scattering are now led away from the land. The double šûb narrative: people who returned are now being taken away again by those who should have led them home.
43.6: The list of those taken to Egypt: men, women, children, royal daughters (ʾet-bĕnôt hammelek), and those the Babylonian captain had placed under Gedaliah. The "royal daughters" reappear — the Davidic remnant. Also Jeremiah and Baruch are taken (wĕʾet-Yirmĕyāhû hannābîʾ wĕʾet-Bārûk ben-Nĕriyyāhû). The prophet who warned against Egypt is taken to Egypt — the covenant witness goes with the covenant-breakers.
43.7: wayyābōʾû ʾerets Miṣrayim kî lōʾ šāmĕʿû bĕqôl YHWH — "They entered the land of Egypt, because they would not obey the voice of the LORD." The motive clause is explicit: the refusal to obey is the narrator's final word on why they came to Tahpanhes. The anti-Exodus is complete: the nation that was brought out of Egypt by YHWH's mighty hand has returned to Egypt by its own disobedience.
43.8: The divine word comes to Jeremiah even in Tahpanhes (in Egypt) — YHWH's presence and speech are not geographically limited to the land of Israel. The oracle that follows is a sign-act (vv. 9-13), one of Jeremiah's most unusual. The oracle in Egypt parallels the oracles against Egypt in the OAN section (chs. 46), completing the frame.
43.9: qaḥ bĕyādĕkā ʾăbānîm gĕdōlôt ûṭĕmantām bammeleṭ bammalben — "Take some large stones in your hands and bury them in the mortar in the brick pavement at the entrance to Pharaoh's palace in Tahpanhes." The sign-act: stones hidden in the pavement of Pharaoh's palace entrance will become the foundation for Nebuchadnezzar's throne. Jeremiah performs this sign publicly, in front of the Jewish community. The MKT renders "mortar in the brick pavement" following MT's bammeleṭ bammalben.
43.10: hinnĕnî šōlēaḥ ûlāqaḥtî ʾet-Nĕbûkadrĕʾṣar melek Bābel ʿabdî — "I am going to send for Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, my servant (ʿabdî)." For the third time (25:9; 27:6) Nebuchadnezzar is designated YHWH's servant — the most theologically shocking claim in Jeremiah's international theology. The pagan conqueror performs YHWH's will. "He will place his throne above these stones I buried" — the Babylonian king will unknowingly sit on the stones Jeremiah buried. The sign-act creates a covenant connection between the prophet's action and the future historical event.
43.11: The comprehensive judgment on Egypt: ʾăšer lammāwet lammāwet wĕʾăšer laššĕbî laššĕbî wĕʾăšer laḥereb laḥereb — "those destined for death, to death; those destined for captivity, to captivity; those destined for the sword, to the sword" — the fourfold formula from 15:2 applied now to Egypt. The Jews who fled to Egypt for safety will encounter there the same covenant judgment they fled.
43.12: wĕhiṣṣattî ʾēš bĕbāttê ʾĕlōhê Miṣrayim — "I will set fire to the temples of the gods of Egypt." The Egyptian gods in their temples will be burned. The image of Nebuchadnezzar picking Egypt clean "as a shepherd picks his garment clean of lice" (kĕhitʿaṭṭôp hārōʿeh ʾet-bigdô) — a colloquial image for thoroughness. The MKT preserves the homely simile as a marker of the oracle's confident tone.
43.13: wĕšibbar ʾet-maṣṣĕbôt Bêt-Šemeš ʾăšer bĕʾerets Miṣrayim — "He will demolish the sacred pillars of Beth-shemesh (Bêt-Šemeš = 'house of the sun') in the land of Egypt." Beth-shemesh in Egypt is Heliopolis — the great temple of the sun-god Ra. The destruction of Ra's sacred pillars (obelisks) by Babylon is the theological climax: even the greatest sun-worship center of the ancient world will fall before YHWH's instrument. The MKT's "Beth-shemesh" preserves the Hebrew name that means "House of the Sun."

44.1: The oracle addresses all Jews throughout Egypt — four geographic areas: Migdol (northeast delta), Tahpanhes (northern delta, where the remnant fled), Noph (Memphis, central Egypt), and Pathros (paṭrôs, Upper Egypt — the south). The comprehensive geographic sweep indicates a substantial Jewish diaspora already in Egypt before the post-fall remnant arrived. The oracle is pan-Egyptian, not limited to the Tahpanhes colony.
44.2: ʾattem rĕʾîtem ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ ʾăšer hēbēʾtî ʿal-Yĕrûšālaim — "You have seen all the disaster I brought on Jerusalem and all the cities of Judah." The oracle begins with the witnessed evidence: they saw Jerusalem destroyed. Wĕhinnām ḥorbâ hayyôm hazzeh wĕʾên yôšēb bāhem — "and they lie desolate this day, with no one living in them." The desolation is presented as visible, present, and irrefutable.
44.3: mippĕnê rāʿātām ʾăšer ʿāśû lĕhaʿăsîsēnî lāleket lĕqaṭṭēr laʿăbōd lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — "because of the wickedness they committed, provoking me to anger by going to burn offerings to other gods — gods that neither they nor you nor your ancestors had known." The repetition "neither they nor you nor your ancestors" (hēmmâ wĕʾattem wĕʾăbôtêkem) is comprehensive across time. The gods are defined by their unknownness: they are not the God of the covenant, not the God of Israel's history.
44.4: wĕʾešlaḥ ʾălêkem ʾet-kol-ʿăbādây hannĕbîʾîm haškēm wĕšālôaḥ — "Yet I sent you all my servants the prophets, again and again, urgently (haškēm wĕšālôaḥ)." The infinitive absolute construction (haškēm wĕšālôaḥ, rising early and sending) conveys persistent, urgent repetition — the standard Jeremianic formula for YHWH's comprehensive prophetic effort. ʾAl-tāʿăśû ʾet-haddebet hammĕtôrāʿâ hazzōʾt ʾăšer śānēʾtî — "Do not do this detestable thing that I hate" — YHWH's stated emotional response to the idolatry: hatred of the detestable.
44.5: wĕlōʾ šāmĕʿû wĕlōʾ hiṭṭû ʾet-ʾoznām lāšûb mērāʿātām — "But they did not listen or pay attention, to turn from their wickedness and stop making offerings to other gods." The refrain of non-listening (lōʾ šāmĕʿû wĕlōʾ hiṭṭû ʾet-ʾoznām) runs throughout Jeremiah (7:24, 11:8, 17:23, 25:4). The covenant hearing-obedience formula negated: they had ears but did not incline them. The MKT preserves "did not listen or pay attention" as two distinct acts of covenant failure.
44.6: wayyittāk ʾappî ûḥămātî wattibʿar bĕʿārê Yĕhûdâ — "Therefore my wrath and my anger were poured out and ignited in the cities of Judah." The spatial progression — cities of Judah → streets of Jerusalem — traces the covenant judgment from the periphery to the center. The result: "they became the desolation and waste that they are today" (lĕḥorbâ ûlišmāmâ kayyôm hazzeh). The present-tense desolation is the evidence for the past judgment and the warning about the future.
44.7: wĕʿattâ kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl — the transition to direct address. Lāmâ ʾattem ʿōśîm rāʿâ gĕdôlâ ʾel-napšôtêkem — "Why are you doing this great harm to yourselves?" The harm of idolatry is self-harm (ʾel-napšôtêkem, to your own souls). The vocabulary of covenant suicide: cutting off man and woman, child and infant from Judah — leaving no remnant (lĕbiltî hišʾîr lākem šĕʾērît).
44.8: lĕhaʿăsîsēnî bĕmaʿăśê yĕdêkem lĕqaṭṭēr lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm bĕʾerets Miṣrayim — "You provoke me to anger with your own handiwork by burning offerings to other gods in the land of Egypt." Lĕmaʿan hikrît lākem ûlĕmaʿan hĕyôtkem lĕqĕlālâ ûlĕḥerpâ bĕkōl gôyê hāʾārets — the terrible irony: the intent of their idol-worship was security and blessing; the result is to make themselves a curse and reproach among all nations.
44.9: hăšĕkaḥtem ʾet-rāʿôt ʾăbôtêkem — "Have you forgotten the wickedness of your ancestors?" The memorial function of covenant history: the past wickedness and its consequences should be remembered as warning. The list: ancestors, kings of Judah, their wives, your own wickedness, the wickedness of your wives — comprehensive across time and gender. The MKT preserves the parallel structure of the comprehensive list.
44.10: lōʾ dukkaʾû ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — "They have not been humbled even to this day." Dākaʾ (be crushed, humbled, broken) — not broken by the disasters they have witnessed. Wĕlōʾ yārĕʾû wĕlōʾ hālĕkû bĕtôrātî ûbĕḥuqqôtay ʾăšer nātattî lĕpânêkem ûlĕpĕnê ʾăbôtêkem — the three covenant-failure verbs: did not fear, did not walk in the Torah, did not keep the statutes. The MKT renders the full triad.
44.11: hinnĕnî śōmēm ʾet-pānay bākem lĕrāʿâ — "I am going to set my face against you for disaster" — YHWH's face turned in judgment rather than blessing. Ûlĕhakrît ʾet-kol-Yĕhûdâ (to cut off all Judah) — the remnant-cutting-off oracle. The remnant in Egypt is not the seed of restoration; it is the object of the final judgment on the covenant community that chose Egypt.
44.12: wĕlāqaḥtî ʾet-šĕʾērît Yĕhûdâ ʾăšer śāmû pĕnêhem lāboʾ ʾerets Miṣrayim — "I will take the remnant of Judah who resolved to go to Egypt to live there, and they shall all be consumed." The twofold fate: baḥereb ûbārāʿāb yittammû (by sword and famine they shall be finished). The series of shame-vocabulary: lĕqĕlālâ lĕʾālâ lĕḥerpâ lĕḥerpâ — curse, waste, horror, reproach. Each noun represents a social dimension of the covenantal humiliation.
44.13: The parallel with Jerusalem is explicit: kĕʾăšer pāqadtî ʿal-Yĕrûšālaim (as I punished Jerusalem). The Egypt exiles will experience exactly what they watched happen to Jerusalem. The three agents — sword, famine, plague — recur for the last time in Jeremiah's Egyptian ministry as the covenant-curse triad.
44.14: wĕlōʾ yihyeh pālîṭ ûśārîd lišʾērît Yĕhûdâ habbāʾîm lāgûr šām bĕʾerets Miṣrayim — "None of the remnant of Judah who have come to Egypt to live there will escape or survive to return to the land of Judah"; the word pālîṭ (escapee) and śārîd (survivor) — the vocabulary of those who narrowly survived disaster. "Which they long to return to and live in" — the tragic irony: they long for the land but will die without reaching it, because they fled the land.
44.15: The community's counter-response: the men who knew their wives had been making offerings to the queen of heaven, "and all the women standing there — a large assembly" (qāhāl gādôl). The formal assembly that petitioned YHWH in 42:1 is now the assembly that formally rejects the oracle. The MKT preserves "large assembly" to maintain the deliberate contrast with the assembly that seemed to commit to obedience.
44.16: haddābār ʾăšer dibbartā ʾēlênû bĕšēm YHWH ʾênennû šōmĕʿîm ʾēlêkā — "As for the word you have spoken to us in the name of the LORD, we will not listen to you." The formal rejection of the prophetic word using the standard oracle-reception formula but negated: "in the name of the LORD" (bĕšēm YHWH) is acknowledged, then rejected. The rejection is not of Jeremiah's personal opinion but of YHWH's named word.
44.17: kî ʿāśôh naʿăśeh ʾet-kol-hadddābār ʾăšer-yāṣāʾ mippînû lĕqaṭṭēr limlekhḗt haššāmayim — "We will do everything we vowed — making offerings to the queen of heaven (melekhḗt haššāmayim) and pouring out drink offerings to her"; the queen of heaven — the astral goddess Astarte/Ishtar/Inanna — whose cult was deeply embedded in popular Judean religion (cf. 7:18). The community's argument: when we worshipped her, we had plenty and were not in danger; when we stopped, disaster followed.
44.18: The theological logic of the community's argument: ûmiddê ḥādalnû lĕqaṭṭēr limlekhḗt haššāmayim... ḥāsarnû kōl wabbahhereb ûbārāʿāb tāmnû — "since we stopped making offerings to the queen of heaven, we have had nothing and have perished by sword and famine." The empirical argument: the correlation between stopping the cult and experiencing disaster. This is a genuine and serious theological objection — the community is offering a post-hoc explanation of their historical suffering that inverts Jeremiah's explanation.
44.19: The women clarify: we offered to the queen of heaven "without our husbands' knowledge (hamibbalʿadê ʾanšênû)?" — implying the husbands were participants. The women's defense of the cult and their claim to have made the offerings with household sanction shows this was communal, not clandestine. The MKT renders the women's speech as direct address to establish their public accountability for the argument.
44.20: wayyōʾmer Yirmĕyāhû ʾel-kol-hāʿām ʿal-hāʾănāšîm wĕʿal-hanāšîm — Jeremiah addresses the whole assembly — men and women both. The comprehensive address mirrors the comprehensive rejection (vv. 15-19). Jeremiah does not address one gender more than the other; the oracle applies to all who made or approved the offerings.
44.21: hălōʾ ʾet-haqqĕṭer ʾăšer qiṭṭartem bĕʿārê Yĕhûdâ — "The offerings you made in the cities of Judah and the streets of Jerusalem — you and your ancestors, your kings and your officials, and the people of the land — did not the LORD remember them?" YHWH's counter-argument: yes, YHWH remembered those offerings — and that is exactly why judgment fell. The community's argument (we sacrificed and prospered) is inverted: you sacrificed and YHWH was angry; the prosperity ended because of the offerings, not despite the lack of them.
44.22: wĕlōʾ-yūkal YHWH ʿôd śĕʾēt mippĕnê rōaʿ maʿălĕlêkem — "The LORD could no longer endure your evil deeds and the abominations you committed." The language of divine exhaustion: YHWH could bear it no longer (lōʾ-yūkal śĕʾēt). This is not a limitation of YHWH's power but of YHWH's patience — the divine long-suffering reaching its limit (cf. 2 Chr 36:16: "until the wrath of the LORD arose against his people until there was no remedy"). The MKT renders "could no longer endure" to convey the exhausted-patience meaning.
44.23: The rebuttal completed: yaʿan ʾăšer qiṭṭartem... ʿal-kēn qārʾat ʾetkem hārāʿâ hazzōʾt — "Because you burned offerings and sinned against the LORD... therefore this disaster has befallen you." The causal chain runs opposite to the community's version: not "we stopped offerings and disaster came" but "you burned offerings and disaster came."
44.24: Jeremiah's formal re-address: "Hear the word of the LORD, all you of Judah who are in the land of Egypt." The shift to the feminine plural (kol-Yĕhûdâ wĕkol-hanāšîm) in the following oracle (v.25) shows the women are specifically addressed for their leading role in the queen of heaven cult (vv. 17-19). The MKT preserves the shift in address as theologically significant.
44.25: kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr ʾattem wĕnĕšêkem — the ironic "permission" oracle: "You and your wives have stated with your mouths and fulfilled with your hands: 'We will surely keep our vows...' Go ahead, fulfill your vows!" YHWH releases them to fulfill their idolatrous vows — but the release is not a blessing; it is the withdrawal of covenant protection that makes the vow-keeping its own punishment. The MKT renders the ironic imperative: "Go ahead, then, fulfill your vows!"
44.26: nišbaʿtî bišmî haggādôl ʾāmar YHWH — "I have sworn by my great name, says the LORD"; the highest oath form available — YHWH swears by himself (cf. Gen 22:16; Isa 45:23; Heb 6:13). "My name will no longer be invoked by any person of Judah anywhere in Egypt" — the covenant-name relationship is severed. The community that rejected YHWH's word will lose access to YHWH's name. The MKT renders the divine self-oath solemnly: "sworn by my great name."
44.27: hinnĕnî šōqēd ʿălêhem lĕrāʿâ wĕlōʾ lĕṭôbâ — "I am watching over them for harm, not for good." The verb šōqēd (watching, alert) here reverses the positive watching of 1:12 (YHWH watches over his word to perform it for good). The same watchful alertness that maintained the covenant promise now maintains the covenant curse. Kōl ʾîš Yĕhûdâ... yittammû — all the men of Judah in Egypt will be consumed.
44.28: The remnant-within-a-remnant: a few (mĕtê mispar, men of number = a countable few) will return from Egypt to Judah. This exception maintains the possibility of a return and future; even in the judgment oracle, a remnant-principle survives. They will know whose word stands — YHWH's or the community's counter-claim. The MKT renders mĕtê mispar as "only a few" — countable rather than countless.
44.29: wĕzōʾt lākem hāʾôt nĕʾum-YHWH kî-pōqēd ʾănî ʿălêkem — "This will be your sign, declares the LORD, that I will punish you in this place." A sign oracle that points to a verifiable future event. The purpose of the sign: lĕmaʿan tēdĕʿû kî qôm yāqûmû dĕbāray ʿălêkem lĕrāʿâ — "so you may know that my words of judgment against you will surely stand." The qôm yāqûm (surely stand) mirrors Jeremiah's credibility test for true prophecy in 28:9.
44.30: hinnĕnî nōtēn ʾet-Parʿōh Ḥopraʿ melek Miṣrayim bĕyad ʾōyĕbāyw — "I am going to hand over Pharaoh Hophra king of Egypt to his enemies and to those who seek his life, just as I handed over Zedekiah king of Judah." The explicit parallel: Hophra/Egypt = Zedekiah/Judah. The fate of the Egyptian king will mirror the fate of the Judean king — historical verification of the oracle. Hophra (Apries) was indeed killed in a revolt c. 570 BC, within Jeremiah's likely lifetime. The MKT preserves "just as I handed over Zedekiah" as the precise comparative.

45.1: Chapter 45 is temporally displaced: bašššānâ hārĕbîʿît lîhôyāqîm ben-Yōšiyyāhû — the fourth year of Jehoiakim (605 BC), when Baruch wrote Jeremiah's dictated scroll. This is the same year as the scroll incident in ch. 36. Placed after the Egypt narrative (chs. 43-44), this oracle functions as a retrospective word to Baruch — a word that applies to his entire ministerial career alongside Jeremiah. The temporal displacement is intentional: the oracle to Baruch closes the prose biographical section before the oracles against the nations begin in ch. 46.
45.2: kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾēlêkā Bārûk — the divine oracle addressed personally to Baruch. Throughout Jeremiah, Baruch appears as the indispensable but unnamed collaborator — secretary, reader, witness. Here YHWH addresses him directly, acknowledging his individual existence before the covenant God. The address is intimate: ʾēlêkā (to you) — to you specifically, Baruch.
45.3: ʾāmartā ʾôy-nāʾ lî kî-yāsap YHWH yāgôn ʿal-maḵʾōbî — "You said: Woe is me! For the LORD has added sorrow to my pain. I am weary with my groaning, and I find no rest." Baruch's lament is quoted back to him by YHWH — YHWH has been listening to Baruch's private complaint. Yāgôn ʿal-maḵʾōbî (sorrow added to my pain) — the accumulation: pain already exists and YHWH adds more. The MKT renders the lament in first person to preserve the emotional directness of Baruch's complaint.
45.4: kōh ʾāmar YHWH hinnēh ʾăšer-bānîtî ʾănî hōrēs — "Thus says the LORD: What I have built I am tearing down, and what I have planted I am uprooting — this entire land." The vocabulary of Jeremiah's commissioning (1:10: linṯôš wĕlinthōs ûlĕhaʾăbîd ûlĕharōs ûlibnet ûlintôaʿ) is recycled in a cosmic scope: what YHWH himself built, YHWH himself tears down. Baruch's personal suffering is contextualized within the universal divine action: the whole land is being unmade. Personal sorrow is real, but it is proportionate to a cosmically significant moment.
45.5: wĕʾattâ tĕbaqqēš-lĕkā gĕdōlôt ʾal-tĕbaqqēš — "And you — are you seeking great things for yourself? Stop seeking them." The rebuke is gentle but clear: Baruch's lament conceals an expectation of personal greatness or comfort that the current moment cannot provide. Kî hinnĕnî mēbîʾ rāʿâ ʿal-kol-bāśār — "For I am going to bring disaster on all flesh" — the scope of the judgment is universal; Baruch's personal ambitions must be surrendered to this reality. The final promise: ûnātattî lĕkā ʾet-napšĕkā lĕšālāl ʾal-kol-hamĕqōmôt ʾăšer tēlēk šām — "but I will give you your life as a prize of war in all the places where you go." Nepeš lĕšālāl (life as plunder/prize) — life itself, not achievement or recognition, is the promised gift. The MKT renders "your life as a prize of war" preserving the military metaphor: survival when others fall is itself the victory.

33.1: The oracle is explicitly dated to Jeremiah's imprisonment (asur bachatzer hamatara, confined in the court of the guard — cf. 32:2-3). The temporal marker 'a second time' (shenit) links this oracle to the previous (ch. 32's land-purchase) as a second confirming word of restoration. Hope spoken from prison is more credible than hope spoken from freedom; the circumstances contradict the message, which forces the question of its source.
33.2: koh-amar YHWH osah zaot YHWH yotzer otah lehachinah YHWH shemo (kōh-ʾāmar Yhwh ʿōśeh zōʾt Yhwh yōṣēr ʾōtāh lĕhăḵînāh Yhwh šĕmô): 'Thus says the LORD who made the earth, the LORD who formed it and established it — the LORD is his name.' Three creation verbs — asah (made), yatzar (formed, the potter's verb of Gen 2:7 and Isa 45:18), hachin (established/fixed) — ground the restoration promise in creative authority. The same God who formed the earth can reform the nation. YHWH shemo (the LORD is his name) is the doxological formula asserting exclusive divine identity (cf. Amos 5:8; 9:6; Isa 42:8).
33.3: qra elai ve'e'enecha ve'agidah lecha gedolot vebetzurot lo yada'ta (qrāʾ-ʾēlay wĕʾeʿĕnekā wĕʾaggîdāh lĕkā gĕdōlôt ûbĕṣūrôt lōʾ yādāʿtā): 'Call to me and I will answer you, and will tell you great and hidden things that you have not known.' This is the only verse in Jeremiah where YHWH directly invites prayer. Gedolot (great things) and betzurot (hidden/fortified/inaccessible things) are revelation beyond ordinary knowledge. Batzur shares a root with the word for fortress/cliff (batzor): these are things 'fortified away' from human access, revealed only by divine disclosure (cf. Deut 29:29: 'the hidden things belong to the LORD our God, but the revealed things belong to us and to our children forever'). Jesus's invitation 'Ask and it will be given you, seek and you will find, knock and the door will be opened' (Matt 7:7) extends this promise to everyone in the new covenant.
33.4: The houses torn down (hetusim) and the siege ramps (solelot) built against them — the very material of the city's destruction is being used as raw material for the city's defense. The oracle speaks of judgment (v. 5) and restoration (v. 6) in tight sequence — the same God who hides his face (v. 5) will reveal health and healing (v. 6).
33.5: ba'im lhilachem et-haKasdim umale otam et-pigrye ha-adam asher hiketi be-api uvachamati: 'The Chaldeans are coming to fight and to fill those houses with the corpses of men whom I struck down in my anger and wrath.' YHWH's 'hiding his face' (histarti panai) from the city is the covenant-withdrawal formula: the withdrawal of divine presence, more catastrophic than any military defeat. The phrase appears in the 'hidden face' texts of Deut 31:17-18 and Isa 54:8 — a covenantal concept rather than a mere absence.
33.6: hineni ma'aleh la arukha umarpeh verapatim vegalitilahem atzumat shalom ve'emet (hinnĕnî maʿăleh lāh ʾărûkāh ûmarpēʾ ûrĕpāʾtîm wĕgillêtî lāhem ʿaṣûmat šālôm wĕʾĕmet): 'Behold, I will bring health and healing to it; I will heal them and reveal to them an abundance of prosperity and security.' Three restoration terms: arukah (new skin/healing, specifically wound-closure — cf. 8:22 where there is no healing), marpeh (cure/medicine), and shalom ve-emet (peace and faithfulness). The medical vocabulary reverses the wound imagery of the book: the incurable wound (30:12-13) will be healed. The divine healer of 30:17 ('I will restore your health and heal your wounds') is here specified with the language of abundant prosperity.
33.7: veshavti et-shevut Yehudah ve'et shevut Yisrael uvenetim otam ke-varishonah: 'I will restore the fortunes of Judah and Israel and rebuild them as they were at the beginning.' Shavti shevut (restore the fortunes) — the idiom, literally 'I will turn the turning,' appears at the center of restoration promises throughout Jeremiah (29:14; 30:3, 18; 31:23; 32:44; 33:11, 26). The Hebrew construction uses the infinitive absolute of shuv (return) to express the completeness of the reversal. Both Judah and Israel — the divided kingdoms — will be restored together, a promise that encompasses all twelve tribes.
33.8: vetihartim mikol avonam asher chate'u li vesalachti lekol avonoteihem asher chate'u li: 'I will cleanse them from all the iniquity by which they sinned against me, and I will forgive all the iniquity and rebellion by which they sinned against me.' The double-forgiveness formula: tihar (cleanse/purify — the priestly term for ritual purification, Lev 13-14) and salach (forgive/pardon — the judicial/covenantal term; only YHWH is ever the subject of salach in the OT). Two different dimensions of sin: avon (iniquity, the guilt and liability of sin) and pisha (rebellion, the active willful defiance). Cleansing addresses the defilement; forgiveness addresses the guilt. Both are promised. This anticipates the new covenant's permanent forgiveness of 31:34 ('I will remember their sin no more') and the NT's 'forgiveness of sins and cleansing from all unrighteousness' (1 John 1:9).
33.9: vehayetah li leshem lesason ulitehillah uletiferet: 'This city will be to me a name of joy, a praise, and a glory before all the nations.' The three terms — shem (name/reputation), sason (joy), tehillah (praise), tiferet (glory/beauty) — are the covenant blessings inverted from the curses. Deut 28:37 pronounced Jerusalem would become a mashal (proverb) and shenina (byword) of shame. Here it becomes a name of joy and praise — the exact opposite. The nations shuddering (pachadu) at YHWH's goodness inverts their watching Jerusalem's destruction with schadenfreude (cf. Lam 1:7; 2:15-16).
33.10: The double designation of desolation — 'without man or beast' (ein adam ve-ein behema) — repeats the judgment formula of 32:43 and anticipates its reversal. The 'cities of Judah and streets of Jerusalem' are the spaces where the covenant community's life was lived — commercial, domestic, and liturgical. The oracle promises that those same spaces will be refilled with joy.
33.11: qol sasson veqol simchah qol chatan veqol kallah (qôl śāśôn wĕqôl śimḥāh qôl ḥātān wĕqôl kallāh): 'The voice of joy and the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride.' This four-fold 'voice of' formula appears in both judgment (7:34; 16:9; 25:10: 'I will silence the voice of joy ... the voice of the bridegroom and bride') and restoration (33:11). The liturgical fragment embedded in the verse — hodu et-YHWH Tzvaot ki tov YHWH ki le-olam chasdo (Give thanks to the LORD of hosts, for the LORD is good, for his steadfast love endures forever) — is the refrain of Ps 136 and 1 Chronicles 16:34. The Psalmic doxology of the temple liturgy will be heard again: the silenced song of 7:34 is restored.
33.12: The desolate land reoccupied by shepherds and flocks represents the return of ordinary pastoral life — a sign of covenant normalcy. The shepherd-and-flock image has been used throughout Jeremiah for covenantal relationships (ch. 23; cf. Ezek 34). Its return signals that the covenant community is functioning again under proper care.
33.13: ba-arei hahar ba-arei hashfela uve-arei hanegev uve-eretz Binyamin uvsvivot Yerushalaim: The geographic catalog — hill country, Shephelah, Negev, Benjamin, Jerusalem environs — represents all of Judah's territory, from north to south, from the highlands to the lowlands. The sheep passing under the shepherd's hands to be counted (ya'avru al-yadei moneh) echoes Ezek 20:37 ('I will make you pass under the rod' — a shepherd's counting method) and implies a covenantal census: every sheep accounted for, none lost. This imagery directly informs Jesus's parable of the shepherd who counts and seeks the one lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7).
33.14: hineh yamim baim neum YHWH vahaqimoti et-hadavar hatov asher dibarti el-beit Yisrael ve'al-beit Yehudah: 'The days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will fulfill the good promise I made to the house of Israel and the house of Judah.' Hadavar hatov (the good word/promise) — the specific content follows in vv. 15-18. This is a meta-promise: YHWH promises to keep his previous promises, establishing a second level of covenantal assurance. The phrase yamim baim (days are coming) is Jeremiah's standard eschatological marker, pointing beyond the immediate historical horizon.
33.15: bayamim hahem uveet hahi atzmich leDavid tzemach tzaddiq (bYyāmîm hāhēm ûbāʿēt hahîʾ ʾaṣmîaḥ lĕdāwid ṣemaḥ ṣaddîq): 'In those days and at that time I will cause a righteous Branch to spring up for David.' The Branch-oracle of 23:5 is repeated almost verbatim — the second occurrence establishes this as Jeremiah's central messianic promise, worthy of repetition. Tzemach (branch/shoot) appears as a messianic title again at Zech 3:8 ('my servant the Branch') and 6:12 ('the man whose name is the Branch, and he will branch out from his place and build the temple of the LORD'). The branching-out image carries growth and organic life: the dead stump of the Davidic line will sprout again, as in Isa 11:1 ('a shoot from the stump of Jesse').
33.16: beyamim hahem tivasha Yehudah viYerushalaim tishkon lavetch veze asher yiqra lah YHWH Tzidqenu (bYyāmîm hāhēm tiwwāšēaʿ yĕhûdāh wîrûšālaim tiškon lābeṭaḥ wĕzeh ʾăšer yiqrāʾ-lāh Yhwh ṣidqênû): 'In those days Judah will be saved and Jerusalem will dwell in safety. And this is the name by which it will be called: 'The LORD is our righteousness.'' Note the crucial shift from 23:6: there, the King is named YHWH Tsidqenu; here, the city Jerusalem receives that name. The community takes on the king's name and character — the theological principle that the people are defined by who rules them. When the Righteous Branch reigns, his righteousness becomes the city's defining characteristic. Rev 21:2-3 captures this: the new Jerusalem coming down from God, 'the dwelling of God is with man' — the city and its king are inseparable.
33.17: ki koh-amar YHWH lo yikaret leDavid ish yoshev al-kise beit Yisrael (kî kōh-ʾāmar Yhwh lōʾ-yikkārēt lĕdāwid ʾîš yōšēb ʿal-kissēʾ bêt yiśrāʾēl): 'For thus says the LORD: There will never fail to be a man from David's line to sit on the throne of the house of Israel.' Lo yikaret leDavid ish (there will not be cut off from David a man) echoes the unconditional form of the Davidic covenant in 2 Sam 7:16 ('your house and your kingdom will endure forever before me'). After the Jehoiachin curse of 22:30 ('record this man as childless'), this reaffirmation of the Davidic line is remarkable: the curse on the throne-succession through natural descendants does not annul the covenant promise fulfilled in a different way.
33.18: velakkohanim halevi'im lo-yikaret ish milfanai ma'aleh olah umaqtir minchah: 'And the Levitical priests shall never lack a man in my presence to offer burnt offerings, to burn grain offerings, and to make sacrifices continually.' The pairing of Davidic kingship (v. 17) and Levitical priesthood (v. 18) in the eternal covenant points toward the NT's union of the two offices in Christ. Hebrews' central argument is precisely the fulfillment: Jesus is both the Davidic king (Heb 1:3-5) and the Melchizedekian high priest (Heb 4:14-5:10; 7:1-28) who supersedes the Levitical priesthood. The inseparability of the two offices promised here is achieved in Christ's single person.
33.19: The messenger formula introducing the covenant-guarantee oracle. The shift from restoration promises (vv. 1-18) to the cosmic-covenant argument (vv. 20-26) is indicated by a fresh divine word — YHWH is not merely promising but arguing from creation order for the reliability of what has been promised.
33.20: koh amar YHWH im-tafiru et-beriti hayom ve-et beriti halaylah (kōh ʾāmar Yhwh ʾim-tāpērû ʾet-bĕrîtî hayyôm wĕʾet-bĕrîtî hallaylāh): 'Thus says the LORD: If you can break my covenant with the day and my covenant with the night, so that day and night no longer come at their appointed time.' YHWH uses creation order as collateral for covenant promise: the regularity of day and night (moadam, their appointed times) is YHWH's covenant with creation (cf. Gen 8:22: 'as long as the earth endures, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night will never cease'). The argument is a fortiori: only if creation's most fundamental rhythm can be disrupted will the Davidic promise fail. Paul uses similar cosmological argument for the security of divine election in Rom 8:38-39 (nothing in all creation can separate from God's love in Christ Jesus).
33.21: gam-beriti tufer et-David avdi: 'Then my covenant with my servant David could also be broken.' The logical chain: cosmic covenant (day/night) → Davidic covenant → Levitical covenant. All three are bound in a single covenantal fabric. The phrase avdi David (my servant David) is the royal honorific — the same title given to Moses (Deut 34:5), Joshua (Josh 24:29), Isaiah (Isa 20:3), and supremely to the messianic servant of Isa 52:13. It is never given to a reigning king in Jeremiah — it is the title of the idealized king of promise.
33.22: kasher lo-yisafer tzeva hashamayim velo yimad chol hayam ken arbeh et-zera David avdi ve'et-haLevi'im meshartai oti: 'As the host of heaven cannot be numbered and the sand of the sea cannot be measured, so I will multiply the offspring of David my servant and the Levites who minister to me.' The innumerable-offspring promise echoes the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 15:5: stars; 22:17: stars and sand; 32:12: sand). Its application to David's line and the Levites connects the messianic promise to the founding covenantal promise: the Davidic fulfillment is the Abrahamic fulfillment.
33.23: The messenger formula signals a new divine communication — a response to a specific objection being raised in the community (v. 24). The oracle addresses those who are drawing theological conclusions from the exile that undermine the covenant promises.
33.24: halo ra'ita mah ha-am hazeh dibberu lemor shnei hamishpachot asher bachar YHWH bahem vayim'asem: 'Have you not noticed what these people are saying? 'The LORD has rejected the two families he chose.'' The 'two families' are the Davidic dynasty and the Levitical priesthood — the two institutions just promised in vv. 17-18. The exile has generated a theology of divine rejection: if YHWH has abandoned Jerusalem, perhaps the covenants are void. The prophetic response does not deny the judgment but disputes the conclusion: discipline ≠ rejection.
33.25: koh amar YHWH im-lo beriti yomam valaylah chuqot shamayim va-aretz lo samti: 'Thus says the LORD: If I have not established my covenant with day and night and the fixed orders of heaven and earth.' Chuqot shamayim va-aretz (the fixed orders of heaven and earth) — the same language as 'the fixed laws of heaven and earth' in v. 25, referring to the natural laws of creation. These are the visible, daily, undeniable evidence of covenant-keeping: every sunrise is YHWH keeping his covenant with day; every night that ends is YHWH keeping his covenant with night. Creation-faithfulness is the argument for covenant-faithfulness.
33.26: ki-eshuv et-shevutam verichhamtim: 'I will restore their fortunes and have mercy on them.' The chapter closes with the restoration formula, now grounded in the cosmic-covenant argument. The descendants of Jacob and David cannot be ultimately rejected because the rejection of the covenants would require the dismantling of creation itself — and the daily evidence of day and night argues against that. Racham (mercy/compassion — from rechem, womb) is the covenant-loyalty term that expresses YHWH's most intimate commitment to his people: parental, physiological, irreversible.

34.1: The oracle is temporally precise: Nebuchadnezzar and his entire coalition (kol-mamlachot eretz memshelet yado, all the kingdoms and peoples under his dominion) — a comprehensive reference to the multi-national Babylonian army described in 2 Kgs 24:2-3 and historically confirmed by the Babylonian Chronicles. The siege of Jerusalem is the backdrop against which Zedekiah receives both a word of judgment and a qualified mercy.
34.2: hineni noten et-ha'ir hazot beyad melech Bavel visrafah ba-esh: 'I am going to hand this city over to the king of Babylon, and he will burn it with fire.' The hand-over formula (noten beyad) is the standard divine speech act for delivering a city to an enemy — the same formula used for Israel's victories in the wilderness (Josh 6:2: 'I have given Jericho into your hand'). Here it is inverted: YHWH delivers his own city. The burning of the city (sarafah ba-esh) is the covenant curse of Deut 13:16 applied to an apostate city — Jerusalem is treated as an ir hanidachat, a city devoted to destruction.
34.3: velo timalet miyado ki tafas titapes uveyadoh titten: 'You will not escape his grasp but will certainly be captured and handed over to him.' The double infinitive absolute (tafas titapes, captured you will be captured) intensifies the certainty.
