The personal encounter — 'your eyes will look into the eyes of the king of Babylon, and he will speak with you in person' (peh el peh yedaber imach) — is the intimate horror of defeat: face-to-face with your conqueror. The prophecy was fulfilled precisely: Zedekiah was captured, brought before Nebuchadnezzar, and had his sons killed before his eyes before being blinded (39:5-7; 52:9-11; 2 Kgs 25:6-7).
34.4: ach shema dvar YHWH Tzidqiyahu melech Yehudah: 'Yet hear the word of the LORD, O Zedekiah king of Judah.' The adversative ach (yet/however) pivots from judgment to the qualified mercy: even for a king whose dynasty is ending, YHWH has a specific word. The content following is not rescue but a mercy within the judgment — a mitigated death rather than a brutal execution.
34.5: beshalom tamut uvemisrefot avoteicha hamelachim harishonim: 'You will die in peace. And as spices were burned in honor of your ancestors, the kings who preceded you, so they will burn spices for you and mourn for you, crying, 'Alas, lord!'' Beshalom tamut (you will die in peace) means death by natural causes rather than by sword — a qualified mercy within judgment. The burning of spices for a king's funeral was a royal honor (2 Chron 16:14 for Asa; 21:19 for Jehoram's failure to receive this honor). Zedekiah receives the minimum of royal dignity in death. The oracle was literally fulfilled: Zedekiah was blinded and imprisoned in Babylon until his death (39:7; 52:11), dying there rather than by execution.
34.6: The note of Jeremiah's public proclamation of this oracle to Zedekiah — while Babylonian armies besieged Jerusalem — underscores the prophetic courage required. The oracle contains both bad news (capture, exile) and qualified good news (survival), making it a message no political or military authority would want proclaimed publicly.
34.7: beihilachem chel melech Bavel al-Yerushalaim veal-kol-arei Yehudah hanissarot al-Lachish ve'al-Azekah: The historical precision: only Lachish and Azekah remained as unconquered fortified cities at this point in the siege. The Lachish Letters (clay ostraca found at Lachish, dated to this period) contain a poignant note from a military commander: 'We are watching for the signals of Lachish according to all the indications my lord has given, because we cannot see the signals of Azekah.' Azekah had already fallen when that letter was written — providing remarkable extra-biblical confirmation of Jeremiah's historical accuracy.
34.8: hadavar asher hayah el-Yirmiyahu me'et YHWH achar karat hamelech Tzidqiyahu berit et-kol-ha'am asher biYerushalaim liqro lahem deror: 'The word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD after King Zedekiah had made a covenant with all the people in Jerusalem to proclaim liberty.' Deror (liberty/release) is the technical term of the Jubilee year (Lev 25:10: 'proclaim liberty throughout the land'). The covenant to release Hebrew slaves invokes this Jubilee framework — a remarkable moment of covenant renewal under siege pressure that was immediately reversed.
34.9: leshallach ish et-avdo ha-ivri ve'et-amatoh ha-ivriyah chofshi: 'Everyone was to release his Hebrew male slave and his Hebrew female slave.' The law behind this action: Exod 21:2 (Hebrew slaves serve six years then go free) and Deut 15:12-18 (the seventh-year shemittah release). The law had apparently been ignored for an extended period — the act of liberation under siege pressure suggests accumulated unpaid obligations, perhaps spanning many sabbatical years. The specificity of 'Hebrew' (fellow-covenant-member) slavery makes the violation doubly problematic: enslaving one's own covenant kin.
34.10: The brief moment of obedience: the officials and people entered the covenant (kol-hasarim vekhal-ha-am asher ba'u vaberit) and released their slaves. The sequence — covenant-making, liberation, release — is the pattern of covenant renewal (cf. Neh 10 where similar obligations are undertaken). The response was real and public.
34.11: vayashuvu acharei-chen vayashivu et-ha'avadim ve'et-ha'amahos asher shilechu chofshim vayichabshum la'avadim vela'amahos: 'But afterward they turned back and took back the male and female slaves they had freed, forcing them back into slavery.' The reversal is almost immediately: the Babylonian army temporarily lifted the siege (probably to deal with an Egyptian relief force, cf. 37:5-11), and the slave-owners interpreted the lifting of pressure as permission to rescind their covenant vows. The economic calculation overrode the covenant obligation. The irony is profound: they freed the slaves when the enemy was at the gates; they re-enslaved them when the enemy temporarily withdrew.
34.12: The messenger formula signals YHWH's direct response to the breach — the divine word comes specifically because of what happened in vv. 10-11. The response takes the form of ironically mirroring the slave-release with a 'release' of judgment against the slave-owners (v. 17).
34.13: koh amar YHWH elohe Yisrael anochi karati berit et-avoteichem beyom hotzi oti otam me-eretz Mitzraim mibet avadim: 'Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel: I made a covenant with your ancestors when I brought them out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.' The appeal to the Exodus covenant grounds the slave-release law in the foundational liberation narrative. Israel's entire existence as a people begins with being freed from slavery by YHWH — this is why they cannot enslave each other indefinitely. Beit avadim (house of slavery) is the standard Exodus identification phrase (Exod 13:3, 14; 20:2; Deut 5:6). Those who were freed must not make their fellows the slaves YHWH freed them from being.
34.14: miqetz sheva shanim teshalechu ish et-achiv ha-ivri asher yimacher lecha: 'At the end of seven years, every one of you must release a fellow Hebrew who has been sold to you.' The Sabbatical-year law (Deut 15:12-18; Exod 21:2): six years of service, release in the seventh. The social logic: the number seven embeds the release in the Sabbath structure — just as the land rests every seventh year (Lev 25), so the enslaved person is released. The violation is not merely economic but cosmological: it disrupts the Sabbatical rhythm built into creation. The indictment 'your fathers did not listen to me or give me their attention' (velo shemu avoteichem elai velo hitu et-aznam) establishes a centuries-long pattern of ignoring this law.
34.15: vatashvu atem hayom vata'asu et-hayashar be-einai liqro deror ish le-re'ehu: 'And you — just recently you turned back and did what was right in my eyes, each of you proclaiming liberty to your neighbor.' The acknowledgment of the momentary act of covenant obedience: 'you did what was right in my eyes' (ha-yashar be-einai) — the highest covenant commendation (used of David in 1 Kgs 14:8; 15:5). The covenant was made in the house bearing YHWH's name (la-shem alav), the temple — the public, sacred context that maximizes the gravity of the subsequent breach.
34.16: vatashuvah vatechalelu et-shemi vatashivu ish et-avdo ve'ish et-amatoh: 'But then you turned back and profaned my name: each of you took back the male and female slaves you had set free.' Chalal shem YHWH (profane/desecrate the name of the LORD) — the same term for violating what is holy (Lev 18:21; 19:12; 20:3; Ezek 36:20-23). Making a covenant oath before YHWH and then breaking it is not merely economic recidivism but blasphemy: it treats YHWH's name as surety for a promise and then repudiates both the promise and the surety.
34.17: lachen koh-amar YHWH atem lo-shema'tem elai liqro deror ish el-achiv ve-ish el-re'ehu hineni qore lachem deror neum YHWH el-hacherev el-hadever ve'el-hara'av: 'Therefore thus says the LORD: You have not obeyed me by proclaiming liberty ... So now I am proclaiming liberty for you — liberty to the sword, to pestilence, and to famine.' The bitter irony: the Hebrew word deror (liberty) used of the slave-release is now applied to the sword, famine, and pestilence — they are 'liberated' to range freely among the people. The covenant logic of Lev 26:25 is enacted: the covenant curses 'avenge the covenant' when it is broken. Refusing to proclaim deror to people results in YHWH proclaiming deror to the judgments against them.
34.18: venatati et-ha-anashim haovrim et-beriti asher lo-qamu et-divrei haberit asher karu lifnai levein batrei ha-egel asher karu lishtayim: 'And I will hand over the men who violated my covenant ... when they cut the calf in two and passed between its parts.' The covenant-cutting ceremony (karat berit, literally 'to cut a covenant') with its divided animal is the Gen 15 pattern: YHWH himself passes between the animal halves in the form of the smoking fire-pot and flaming torch, making himself the implied sacrifice if the covenant is violated. Here the humans passed between the parts — making themselves the implied sacrifice. YHWH is calling in the covenant sanction: those who passed between the calf-halves will become like the calf — cut in pieces, exposed as carrion (v. 20).
34.19: The comprehensive list of those who passed between the calf halves — officials of Judah, officials of Jerusalem, court officials, priests, and all the people — establishes that the covenant-oath violation is community-wide, not confined to a few individuals. The entire leadership structure of Judah participated in the broken oath, making the judgment comprehensive.
34.20: venatati otam beyad oyveihem uveyad mevaqashei nafisham vehayetah nivlatam lema-achal le-of hashamayim ulivehmat ha-aretz: 'I will give them into the hands of their enemies ... Their dead bodies will become food for the birds of the sky and the beasts of the earth.' The death without burial is the covenant curse of Deut 28:26: 'your carcasses will be food for all the birds of the air and the beasts of the earth.' It also mirrors the fate the Babylonian armies threatened Jerusalem with, turned back on those who made covenant-oaths and broke them. The birds and beasts consume what was supposed to be buried — the final degradation of covenant violation.
34.21: ve-et Tzidqiyahu melech Yehudah ve-et-sarav ettein beyad oyveihem: The king and his officials receive the same fate as the slave-owners — inclusion in the judgment is comprehensive across social strata. The army that 'pulled back from you' (ha-olim me-aleichem) is the temporary Babylonian withdrawal to deal with the Egyptian relief force — the very event that prompted the slave-owners to rescind their liberation. The army's return is announced in v. 22.
34.22: hineni metzaveh neum YHWH vahashivotim el-ha-ir hazot venilchamu aleiha: 'I am about to give the command, declares the LORD, and I will bring them back to this city. They will fight against it, take it, and burn it with fire.' The divine command that turns the Babylonian army around: YHWH directs the movements of foreign armies as instruments of covenant judgment (cf. Isa 10:5-6: 'Woe to Assyria, the rod of my anger'; Isa 45:1: Cyrus as YHWH's anointed). The certainty of the return is grounded not in military intelligence but in divine decree.

35.1: The chapter is dated to Jehoiakim's reign — historically earlier than the surrounding chapters (605-597 BCE vs. 587 BCE for ch. 34). The retrospective placement serves a theological purpose: the Rechabites' faithfulness is the immediate contrast to Jerusalem's unfaithfulness in ch. 34. Jeremiah is constructing a case by contrast.
35.2: lek el-beit haRechabim vedibarta otam vahave'otam beit YHWH: 'Go to the house of the Rechabites, speak with them, and bring them to the house of the LORD, into one of the inner rooms, and offer them wine to drink.' The Rechabites were a nomadic clan descended from Jonadab son of Rechab (2 Kgs 10:15-23) who refused wine, houses, and agriculture as a covenant commitment to a nomadic, austere form of life. Jeremiah is instructed to test their obedience publicly by offering them the very thing their ancestor prohibited — in the temple itself, before witnesses.
35.3: The full genealogy of Jaazaniah — four generations named — establishes the lineage and historicity of the Rechabite household. The detail signals that this is a real family with a documented ancestral tradition, not a hypothetical case study.
35.4: The specific chamber (lishkat benei Hanan ben-Igdalyahu ish ha-elohim, the chamber of Hanan son of Igdaliah, the man of God) is noteworthy: a chamber associated with a prophetic figure ('the man of God') was the location of the test. Adjacent to the officials' chambers and above a priestly chamber — the setting is at the intersection of prophetic, official, and priestly spaces, maximizing the public and institutional nature of the test.
35.5: va-eten lifnei benei beit haRechabim gevi'im melaim yayin veqosot va-omar aleichem shetu yayin: 'Then I placed before the members of the Rechabite household pitchers full of wine and cups, and I said to them: Drink wine.' The test is entirely formal: Jeremiah personally offers the wine in an authoritative setting. The Rechabites' refusal therefore cannot be attributed to social awkwardness or lack of opportunity — it is a deliberate, articulate response to a direct invitation from a prophet in the temple.
35.6: vayomru lo nishteh yayin ki Yonadav ben-Rechav avinu tzivanu lemor lo tishteu yayin atem uvneichem ad-olam (wayyōmĕrû lōʾ nište yayin kî yônādāb ben-rēḵāb ʾābînû ṣiwwānû lēmōr lōʾ tišĕtû-yayin ʾattem ûbĕnekem ʿad-ʿôlām): 'But they replied: We will not drink wine, for Jonadab son of Rechab, our father, commanded us: You shall not drink wine, you or your sons, ever.' The response is immediate and communal — the entire household speaks with one voice. The reference to their ancestor's command (tzivanu) as authoritative is the key: they are not deciding in the moment but citing an inherited, binding obligation. Ad-olam (forever/in perpetuity) — the command had no sunset clause; it was meant to define Rechabite identity across generations.
35.7: uvayit lo tivnu vezera lo tizre'u vekharem lo titaa velo yihyeh lachem: 'You shall not build a house, sow seed, or plant a vineyard or own one. You shall live in tents all your days.' The Rechabite lifestyle was a deliberate rejection of the settled agricultural economy that Israel had adopted in Canaan and that was associated with Baal worship (the fertility cult). By refusing houses, vineyards, and cultivation, the Rechabites embodied a counter-cultural testimony to the nomadic covenant-relationship with YHWH, deliberately maintained against the dominant culture. The motivation (lema'an tichyu yamim rabbim al-pnei ha-adamah asher atem garim sham, so that you may live long in the land where you are strangers) makes longevity in the land contingent on faithfulness — the same structure as the covenant's land-tenure conditions.
35.8: The collective voice of the Rechabites: vanishma beqol Yonadav ben-Rechav avinu lechol asher tzivanu (we have obeyed the voice of Jonadab son of Rechab, our father, in everything he commanded us). The contrast with Israel's 'we did not listen' (lo shama'nu, v. 14-17) is the entire point: a human ancestor commanded, and his descendants obeyed for generations; YHWH the Creator commanded, and his covenant people persistently disobeyed.
35.9: The specific enumeration of what they have NOT done — not built houses, not owned vineyards or fields or seeds — follows the ancestral commands of v. 7 point by point. The listing is deliberate: each negative corresponds to a cultural norm they have refused. The Rechabite testimony is structured as a covenant affirmation: here is the command, and here is what we have done and not done in response.
35.10: vaneshev ba-ohalim venishma vanase'ah lechol asher tzivanu Yonadab avinu: 'We have lived in tents and have obeyed and done all that our father Jonadab commanded us.' The summary claim: full and comprehensive obedience. The tents are the physical sign of their covenant identity — portable, impermanent, dependent on YHWH rather than on landed security. The wilderness ideal maintained into the era of urban civilization.
35.11: vayhi bia'lot Nevukhadreatzar melech Bavel al-ha-aretz vanomer bo vanavo Yerushalaim: 'But when Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon came up against the land, we said: Come, let us go to Jerusalem for fear of the Chaldean army and the Syrian army. That is why we are living in Jerusalem now.' The exception proves the rule: even the Rechabites, bound by their ancestor's nomadic ideal, had to take refuge in a city under existential military threat. The concession is practical and explicitly temporary — they are 'living in Jerusalem now,' not permanently settling. The crisis has forced a tactical retreat from their lifestyle, but not from their values.
35.12: The messenger formula introducing the theological application. YHWH now draws the lesson from the Rechabite test — the observation of their obedience becomes the indictment of Israel's disobedience.
35.13: koh amar YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael lech ve-amarta el-anshei Yehudah ve-el yoshevei Yerushalaim halo tiqchu musar lishmo'a el-devarai neum YHWH: 'Go and say to the people of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem: Will you not receive instruction and listen to my words?' Musar (instruction/discipline/correction) is the wisdom term for the teachable reception of correction. The Rechabites received and implemented their father's musar; Jerusalem refuses YHWH's. The rhetorical question (halo tiqchu musar) expects the answer 'yes, you should' but acknowledges by implication the answer has been 'no.'
35.14: huqam mitzvat Yonadav ben-Rechav asher tzivah et-banav levilti shtot yayin ve'azoh lo shatu ad-hayom hazeh ki shamu et-mitzvat avihem: 'The command that Jonadab son of Rechab gave to his sons — to drink no wine — has been kept. To this day they drink none, because they have obeyed their ancestor's command. But I have spoken to you persistently, and you have not listened to me.' The pivotal contrast: Jonadab commanded; they obeyed. YHWH spoke persistently (hashkem vediber, rising early and speaking — the idiom for urgent, repeated communication); they did not listen. The theological force: a human ancestor commands more effectively than the divine Creator. The question implicit in the contrast: why? The answer Jeremiah's book returns to repeatedly: the stubborn, rebellious heart (lev sorer u-moreh, 5:23).
35.15: va-eshlach aleichem et-kol-avadai haneviim hashkem ve-shloch lemor shuvu na ish midarcho hara-ah: 'I have sent you all my servants the prophets, again and again, saying: Turn now, each of you, from your evil way.' The phrase hashkem veshalloach (rising early and sending) — the urgency-idiom for repeated, insistent communication (cf. 7:13, 25; 11:7; 25:3-4; 26:5; 29:19; 32:33; 44:4). YHWH has been 'early and persistent' in sending prophets; Israel has been persistent in not listening. The contrast between YHWH's urgency and Israel's indifference is one of Jeremiah's central theological themes.
35.16: vayaqimu benei Yonadav ben-Rechav et-mitzvat avihem asher tzivam veha-am hazeh lo shamu elai: 'The descendants of Jonadab son of Rechab have kept their father's command, but this people has not obeyed me.' The direct comparison. The indictment is stark in its brevity: eight words in Hebrew. The Rechabites' multi-generational obedience to a human ancestor versus Israel's persistent disobedience to the divine Father who gave them the covenant from Sinai.
35.17: lachen koh-amar YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael hineni mevi el-Yehudah ve-el-kol yoshevei Yerushalaim et kol-hara-ah asher dibarti aleichem: 'Therefore thus says the LORD, the God of hosts, the God of Israel: I am going to bring on Judah and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem all the disaster I have pronounced against them.' The logical consequence is stated without elaboration — the argument of vv. 13-16 has made it self-evident. The comprehensive titles (YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael) frame the judgment as both universal sovereign and covenant-specific God: the disaster comes not from an impersonal force but from the God who is personally invested in the covenant.
35.18: uveit haRechabim amar Yirmiyahu koh-amar YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael ya-an asher shema'tem et-mitzvat Yonadav avichem: 'Because you have obeyed the command of Jonadab your father and kept all his instructions and done all that he commanded you.' The threefold statement of obedience — shematem (listened to), tishmerou (kept), ta-asu (did) — hearing, retention, and action: the complete circuit of covenantal obedience. Each element represents a dimension of faithfulness: hearing the command, retaining it across generations, and enacting it in practice.
35.19: lachen koh-amar YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael lo-yikaret ish liYonadav ben-Rechav omed lefanai kol-hayamim (lākēn kōh-ʾāmar Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl lōʾ-yikkārēt ʾîš lĕyônādāb ben-rēḵāb ʿōmēd lĕpānay kol-hayyāmîm): 'Therefore thus says the LORD of hosts, the God of Israel: Jonadab son of Rechab shall never fail to have a man standing before me.' Omed lefanai (standing before me) is the covenant phrase for priestly and prophetic service (Deut 10:8; 1 Kgs 17:1; Jer 15:19). The Rechabites are promised perpetual covenantal standing — the same guarantee given to the Davidic line (33:17) and the Levitical priesthood (33:18). Faithfulness to covenant obligations is rewarded with covenantal continuity.

36.1: The fourth year of Jehoiakim (605 BCE) is the year of the battle of Carchemish, when Nebuchadnezzar defeated Egypt and became the dominant power in the ancient Near East — the decisive geopolitical turning point that validates Jeremiah's northern-foe oracles. The command to write comes at this moment of historical confirmation.
36.2: qach lecha megillat sefer vechatavta eleiha et kol-hadvarim asher dibarti eleicha al-Yisrael ve-al-Yehudah ve-al-kol-hagoyim miyom dibbarti eleicha miyemei Yoshiyahu ve-ad hayom hazeh: 'Take a scroll and write in it all the words I have spoken to you against Israel, against Judah, and against all the nations, from the day I first spoke to you in the days of Josiah until this day.' Megillat sefer (scroll of a book) — the Hebrew writing medium of the period. The command to write is unprecedented in Jeremiah's ministry up to this point: twenty-plus years of prophetic activity are now to be committed to permanent record. The scroll will outlast its oral delivery and reach those who were not present to hear. This chapter is self-referentially about the nature and indestructibility of the written word.
36.3: ulai yishme'u beit Yehudah et kol-hara-ah asher anochi choshev la-asot lahem lema-an yashuvu ish midarcho hara-ah: 'Perhaps the house of Judah will hear of all the disaster I intend to bring on them, so that each person will turn from his evil way, and I will forgive their iniquity and their sin.' The divine motive for writing: restoration through repentance remains the goal. Even at this late stage — the battle of Carchemish has confirmed the worst — YHWH leaves open the possibility of repentance and forgiveness. Ulai (perhaps) is not divine uncertainty but prophetic openness: the door is not closed until it is demonstrably closed.
36.4: vayiqra Yirmiyahu et-Baruch ben-Neriyah vayichtov Baruch mipi Yirmiyahu et kol-divrei YHWH asher diber elav al-megillat sefer: 'So Jeremiah summoned Baruch son of Neriah, and Baruch wrote on a scroll everything Jeremiah dictated to him — all the words of the LORD that had been spoken to Jeremiah.' Baruch son of Neriah is Jeremiah's scribe and the likely primary editor of the book of Jeremiah. A seal (bulla) bearing the inscription 'Belonging to Berekhyahu son of Neriyahu the scribe' was found among the Moussaieff collection, dated to the sixth century BCE — an extraordinary extra-biblical confirmation of Baruch's historical existence and professional title.
36.5: vaytzav Yirmiyahu et-Baruch lemor ani atzur lo uchal lavo beit YHWH: 'Jeremiah gave Baruch these instructions: I am banned; I cannot go to the house of the LORD.' Atzur (banned/confined/prevented) — the cause of Jeremiah's ban is not specified here, but may be related to his temple sermon (ch. 7, 26) or earlier controversies. His inability to deliver the scroll personally necessitates Baruch's role as his agent — a role Baruch takes on with apparent courage, since reading the scroll publicly would carry risk.
36.6: uvata viqrata bamegillah asher katavta mipi et-divrei YHWH be-oznei ha-am beit YHWH beyom tzom: 'So you go and read from the scroll ... to all the people in the LORD's house on a day of fasting.' The fast-day setting maximizes the audience: pilgrims from throughout Judah (kol-anshei Yehudah haba-im me-areihem) would be present for a communal fast. The public reading on a solemn occasion — when people are most psychologically prepared to hear weighty words — is the optimal context for the scroll's reception.
36.7: ulai tipol techintam lifnei YHWH veyashuvu ish midarcho hara-ah: The same ulai (perhaps) of hope from v. 3 — the possibility of repentance remains. The petition (techinnatam) Jeremiah hopes the people will bring before YHWH echoes the temple dedication prayer structure (1 Kgs 8:46-53). The two-fold motivation — the 'great anger and wrath' pronounced and the open door of forgiveness — frame the reading as both warning and invitation.
36.8: Baruch's obedience to Jeremiah's command: vayya'as Baruch ben-Neriyahu kekol asher tzivahu Yirmiyahu hanavi (Baruch son of Neriah did exactly what the prophet Jeremiah commanded him). The precision mirrors the Rechabites' obedience to their ancestor — but the command comes from a prophet, not merely a human ancestor.
36.9: The fifth year of Jehoiakim, ninth month (approximately December 604 BCE) — a specific calendar date. A communal fast was proclaimed, possibly in response to news of the Babylonian threat after Carchemish. The fast is an act of communal penitence or petition before YHWH — precisely the context for the scroll's message to be heard. The crowds from the towns of Judah (kol-ha-am mi-arei Yehudah) expand the reach beyond Jerusalem's population.
36.10: vayiqra Baruch bamegilah et-divrei Yirmiyahu beit YHWH belishkat Gemariyahu ben-Shaphan hasopher bechatzer ha-elyon: 'Then Baruch read the words of Jeremiah from the scroll in the house of the LORD, in the chamber of Gemariah son of Shaphan the secretary.' Gemariah son of Shaphan connects to the powerful Shaphan family that supported Jeremiah throughout his ministry (2 Kgs 22:3-14: Shaphan the secretary read the law-scroll to Josiah; Ahikam son of Shaphan protected Jeremiah in ch. 26). The chamber's location at the upper court, at the New Gate, gave maximum public access — the scroll was read in a major civic and religious space.
36.11: Micaiah son of Gemariah — Shaphan's grandson — hears the scroll and immediately takes the content to the court officials. The chain of transmission (scroll → Baruch → upper court → Micaiah → officials' chamber) traces the word's movement from prophetic origin to political center. Each link is named, establishing accountability and historical memory.
36.12: The officials listed — Elishama the secretary, Delaiah son of Shemaiah, Elnathan son of Achbor (who had helped retrieve the prophet Uriah from Egypt, 26:22), Gemariah son of Shaphan, Zedekiah son of Hananiah — represent the upper tier of Jehoiakim's court. Their response (v. 16: they were afraid) differs markedly from the king's (v. 23-24: he showed no fear).
36.13: The reporting of Micaiah to the officials: the scroll's content is relayed through successive audiences — each hearing generating a response that shapes the next audience's encounter with the word. The word's movement through institutions is itself part of its narrative: it finds its way despite the obstacles.
36.14: The officials send for Baruch directly: qach et-hamegillah asher qarata bah be-oznei ha-am ule-ch (take the scroll from which you read to the people and come). Their summons uses the scroll as its subject — the scroll has its own identity as the locus of the word. Baruch comes with the scroll to the officials' chamber.
36.15: The officials' first response — 'Sit down and read it to us' (shev na veqra-ennu be-ozeinu) — is one of respectful attention. They are alarmed but not hostile (v. 16). The contrast with Jehoiakim's response (vv. 23-24) is the pivot of the chapter: the officials respond with fear; the king responds with contempt.
36.16: vayehi kishomam et kol-hadvarim pahedu ish el-re-ehu vayomru el-Baruch haged nagid lamelech et kol-hadvarim ha-elleh: 'When they heard all the words, they turned to one another in alarm and said to Baruch: We must report all these words to the king.' Pachadu (alarm/fear) — the appropriate prophetic response to the divine word (cf. Isa 66:2, 5: 'this is the one I esteem: he who is humble and contrite in spirit, and trembles at my word'). The officials hear the word and are afraid — the fear is right; the failure is that they report to the king rather than leading repentance from their position.
36.17: The officials' investigative question — how did Baruch write all these words? (at Jeremiah's dictation?) — establishes the chain of authority for legal purposes: is this Baruch's composition or Jeremiah's, and is it the prophet's own word or YHWH's? The question also protects Baruch legally: it creates a record that he was acting as a scribe, not as a prophet making his own pronouncements.
36.18: vayomer lahem Baruch mipi yiqra elai et kol-hadvarim ha-elleh va-ani kotev al-hasefer bedyo: 'Baruch answered them: He dictated all these words to me, and I wrote them in ink on the scroll.' Bedyo (in ink) — a technical scribal note. The specificity of 'ink on a scroll' underscores the permanence of the written word compared to the spoken oracle: this is now fixed in a physical medium that can be preserved, copied, and transmitted beyond the immediate audience.
36.19: vayomru hasarim el-Baruch lek histater atah viYirmiyahu ve-ish al-yeda ei-zeh atem: 'Then the officials said to Baruch: Go and hide — you and Jeremiah — and let no one know where you are.' The officials are protecting Jeremiah and Baruch from the king's anticipated rage. Their warning implies knowledge of Jehoiakim's character — they know what he will do when he hears the scroll. The intervention parallels earlier protection: Ahikam son of Shaphan protected Jeremiah in 26:24.
36.20: The scroll is stored in Elishama the secretary's chamber while the officials brief the king. The act of storage is significant: even before the king hears its contents, the scroll is being treated as a document requiring custody — both for preservation and as evidence. The officials apparently try to maintain the scroll's existence even while reporting its contents.
36.21: vayishlach hamelech et-Yehudi laqachat et-hamegillah: 'The king sent Jehudi to get the scroll.' The king sends a servant to retrieve the physical object — Jehoiakim wants the scroll itself, not just a report of its contents. The retrieval of the scroll from Elishama's chamber establishes that what follows is a deliberate, calculated act against the word itself, not an impulsive moment.
36.22: vehamelech yoshev beit hachore bachodesh hateshii ve-et haach burneth lefanav: 'The king was sitting in the winter apartment in the ninth month, and there was a fire burning before him in the fire pot.' The domestic detail — the king in his winter quarters, warming himself by the fire — creates the ironic tableau: the fire that warms the king will consume the word. The same fire that Jehoiakim uses for comfort will become the instrument of his act of defiance.
36.23: vayehi ki qara Yehudi shalosh daltot ve-arba-ah yiqra-ennu vayikra-enhu hamelech bitar lapid hasopher vehishlikh el-ha-esh asher el-haach ad tom kol-hamegillah al-ha-esh (wayhî kî-qārāʾ yĕhûdî šāloš dĕlātôt wĕʾarbaʿāh yiqrāʾennû wĕyiqrāʿehu hammelek bĕtaʿar hassōpēr wĕhišlîk ʾel-hāʾēš ʾăšer ʾel-hāʾaḥ ʿad tōm kol-hamĕgillāh ʿal-hāʾēš): 'Each time Jehudi had read three or four columns, the king would cut the section off with a scribe's knife and throw it into the fire, until the entire scroll had been consumed.' Taar hasopher (scribe's knife, penknife) — the tool used for cutting reed pens and trimming scrolls, now used for defacement. The deliberate, methodical destruction — column by column, not in a single outburst — makes the act calculated rather than impulsive: Jehoiakim is consciously and continuously destroying the word of God. The contrast with Josiah's response when he heard the law read (qara et-bigadav, he tore his garments — 2 Kgs 22:11) is complete: one king tears himself; the other tears the scroll.
36.24: velo pakhadu velo qar-u et-bigdeihem hamelech vekhol-avadav hashom-im et kol-hadvarim ha-elleh: 'Yet neither the king nor any of his servants who heard all these words was afraid, nor did they tear their garments.' Lo pachadu velo qaru et-bigdeihem (neither afraid nor did they tear their garments) — the double absence of appropriate response. Fear and garment-tearing were the two marks of authentic covenant response to YHWH's word (2 Kgs 22:11, 19: Josiah tore his garments; Hezekiah sent messengers with torn garments to Isaiah in Isa 37:1). The king and his servants have hardened hearts that cannot receive the word — the Pharaoh-analogy is implicit: Exod 7:13, 22 ('Pharaoh's heart was hardened') uses the same vocabulary of stubborn non-response.
36.25: vegam Elnathan veDel-ayahu veGamarayahu pichalelu ba-melech levilti srifal et-hamegillah velo shama aleichem: 'Even though Elnathan and Delaiah and Gemariah pleaded with the king not to burn the scroll, he would not listen to them.' Three officials (including Gemariah son of Shaphan, who had provided the chamber for the public reading) interceded on behalf of the scroll. Their pleading (hiphgilu) is the same word used for prophetic intercession — they are using prophetic-style advocacy for the preservation of the prophetic word. The king's refusal to listen to them mirrors his refusal to listen to the scroll.
36.26: vaytzav hamelech et-Yerachmeel ben-hamelech ve-et-Seraya ben-Azriel ve-et-Shelemyahu ben-Avdeel laqachat et-Baruch hasopher ve-et-Yirmiyahu hanavi vayastirem YHWH: 'The king commanded Jerahmeel a royal prince, Seraiah son of Azriel, and Shelemiah son of Abdeel to seize Baruch the scribe and Jeremiah the prophet. But the LORD hid them.' Vayastirem YHWH (but the LORD hid them) — the divine protection intervenes at the moment of royal persecution. The brevity of the clause — four Hebrew words — is its strength: no explanation of how, simply the fact that YHWH protected what Jehoiakim sought to destroy. The same word sitr (hide) appears in 33:3's 'hidden things' (betzurot) — but now it is not revelation that is hidden but the prophet himself, preserved by divine concealment.
36.27: After the scroll's destruction, the word of YHWH comes to Jeremiah — the divine response to the burning. The indestructibility of the word is the oracle's premise: burning the scroll does not destroy the word; it only generates a second, fuller scroll.
36.28: shuv qach lecha megillah acheret vechtovchetah aleha et kol-hadvarim harishonim asher hayu al-hamegillah harishonah asher saraf Yehoyaqim melech Yehudah ba-esh: 'Take another scroll and write on it all the same words that were in the first scroll, the one Jehoiakim king of Judah burned.' The command to replace the destroyed scroll is the theological statement: the word of YHWH cannot be annihilated by human action. This is the OT enactment of what Jesus will claim: 'heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away' (Matt 24:35; Mark 13:31; Luke 21:33). The word outlasts the political power that seeks to silence it.
36.29: ve-al Yehoyaqim melech Yehudah tomar koh-amar YHWH atah sarafta et-hamegillah hazot lemor madu katavtah aleiha lemor bo yavo melech Bavel veshichet et-ha-aretz hazot: 'And concerning Jehoiakim king of Judah you shall say: Thus says the LORD — You burned this scroll, asking: Why have you written in it that the king of Babylon will certainly come and destroy this land?' The oracle is addressed directly to Jehoiakim's act of burning — YHWH names the specific question behind the destruction: Jehoiakim burned the scroll because he rejected the Babylonian-threat oracle it contained. The burning is an act of wishful thinking — destroy the word and the future it announces will not come. The divine response makes the burning ineffective and the burning-king the new subject of prophecy.
36.30: lachen koh-amar YHWH al-Yehoyaqim melech Yehudah lo-yihyeh lo yoshev al-kise David venivlatoh tihyeh mushleket lechoreh yomam velaqarachah laylah: 'Therefore thus says the LORD concerning Jehoiakim king of Judah: He will have no one to sit on the throne of David, and his dead body will be cast out to the heat of the day and the frost of the night.' The throne-denial echoes the Coniah curse of 22:30 ('none of his descendants will succeed in sitting on the throne of David') — consistent: Jehoiakim's son Jehoiachin reigned only three months before being deported; neither he nor any descendant ever ruled Judah as an independent king. The exposed body (mushleket) mirrors the 'buried like a donkey' oracle of 22:19.
36.31: ufaqadeti alav ve-al-zaro ve-al-avadav et-avonam: 'I will punish him and his offspring and his servants for their iniquity.' The paqad avon (punish iniquity / visit iniquity) formula — the covenant curse-enactment. The household solidarity of punishment (king + offspring + servants) is the corporate consequence of covenantal violation. The repeated phrase 'because they would not listen' (ya-an asher lo shamu) makes refusal to hear the single sufficient explanation for everything that follows.
36.32: viYirmiyahu laqach megillah acheret vayitenah el-Baruch ben-Neriyahu hasopher vayichtov aleiha mipi Yirmiyahu et kol-divrei hasefer asher saraf Yehoyaqim melech Yehudah ba-esh vaiyosef aleichem od devarim rabbim kehamah: 'Then Jeremiah took another scroll and gave it to Baruch son of Neriah the scribe, who wrote on it at Jeremiah's dictation all the words of the scroll that Jehoiakim king of Judah had burned. And many similar words were added to them.' The burned scroll is replaced — and exceeded. Vayosef aleichem od devarim rabbim kehamah (many similar words were added to them): the attempt to silence the word results in more words than before. The pattern of attempted suppression leading to amplification of the word is the dynamic of the entire canon: persecution does not diminish the word but occasions its expansion. The book of Jeremiah itself — 52 chapters — is the final ironic monument to Jehoiakim's burning of the first scroll.

46.1: ʾĂšer hāyâ dĕḇar-YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû hannāḇîʾ ʿal-haggôyim (ʾal-haggôyim): the superscription to the entire Oracles Against the Nations (OAN) collection (chs 46-51). The phrase ʿal-haggôyim (concerning the nations) positions these oracles as YHWH's sovereign word over powers beyond Israel — a universal claim of divine governance. Comparable OAN collections appear in Isaiah (13-23), Ezekiel (25-32), and Amos (1-2). The OAN genre is not mere political commentary but theo-political declaration: YHWH's mišpāṭ (judgment) extends to all nations, not only to Israel.
46.2: Lĕmiṣrāyim ʿal-ḥêl parʿōh Nĕḵô (Lĕmiṣrāyim ʿal-ḥêl parʿōh Nĕḵô): the historical anchor is precise — Pharaoh Neco II of Egypt met Nebuchadrezzar at Carchemish on the Euphrates in 605 BCE (the 4th year of Jehoiakim). Carchemish was the decisive battle that ended Egyptian hegemony over Canaan and established Babylonian supremacy. Jeremiah and Baruch were in Jerusalem during this battle (cf. ch36, where Jeremiah dictated the scroll in that same 4th year). The oracle thus interprets a contemporary geopolitical event through the lens of YHWH's sovereignty: Egypt's defeat at Carchemish was not merely Babylonian military superiority but YHWH's sentence on Egypt.
46.3: ʿIrĕkû māgēn ûṣinnâ ûgišû lammilḥāmâ (ʿIrĕkû māgēn ûṣinnâ): the imperatives are sardonic — the prophet issues the war-marshal's call to Egypt's army. māgēn (small round shield, for hand-to-hand combat) and ṣinnâ (large full-body shield, for archers and spear-bearers) represent the two main infantry shield types. The mock-military exhortation sets up the reversal of vv5-6: the very troops being rallied will flee in terror. This dramatic irony is a characteristic feature of OAN rhetoric.
46.4: ʾIsrû hassûsîm wĕʿălû happārāšîm wĕhityaṣṣĕḇû bĕḵôḇāʿîm (ʾIsrû hassûsîm): four imperatives continue the mock-rallying: harness the horses, mount the cavalry, stand in the helmets (kôḇaʿ), polish the spears (mĕraqqu hammĕrāmāḥîm, Piel of rāqaʿ — to beat thin, polish), and put on the coats of armor (liḇšû hassirĕyōnōt). The attention to military detail (shields, helmets, spears, armor) mimics the war-preparation genre found in Egyptian and Assyrian royal inscriptions. The full battle-array described in vv3-4 will be utterly routed in v5.
46.5: Maddûaʿ rāʾîtî hēmmâ ḥattîm nĕsôgîm ʾāḥôr (Maddûaʿ rāʾîtî hēmmâ ḥattîm nĕsôgîm ʾāḥôr): the rhetorical question captures the prophet's mock-astonishment at the reversal — just summoned to battle, Egypt's warriors are already (ḥattîm, struck down, from ḥātat, to be shattered/dismayed) and fleeing backward (nĕsôgîm ʾāḥôr). Mĕḡubbĕrêhem mukkaʾtîm ûmānôs nāsû wĕlōʾ hifnû (mĕḡubbĕrêhem mukkaʾtîm): their warriors are beaten down; they flee without looking back. Māgôr missāḇîḇ (Māgôr missāḇîḇ): "terror on every side" — Jeremiah's signature phrase (6:25; 20:3-4, 10; 49:29), which he applied to Judah and which his enemy Pashhur received as a personal name (20:3). Here YHWH extends the same terror-oracle to Egypt.
46.6: ʾAl-yānûs haqal wĕʾal-yimmāleṭ haggibbôr (ʾAl-yānûs haqal wĕʾal-yimmāleṭ haggibbôr): the swift (qal) cannot flee and the mighty (gibbôr) cannot escape — the two human strategies of survival (speed and strength) are both nullified. Ṣāpônâ ʿal-yad nĕhar-pĕrāt kāšĕlû wĕnāpālû (ṣāpônâ ʿal-yad nĕhar-pĕrāt kāšĕlû wĕnāpālû): they stumble and fall beside the Euphrates — the very river that defined Egypt's northern ambition. Carchemish was intended to be Egypt's eastward expansion; it became her burial ground.
46.7: Mî-zeh kayyĕʾōr yaʿăleh kayyĕʾōrîm yitgāʾăšû mêmāyw (Mî-zeh kayyĕʾōr yaʿăleh): the rhetorical question opens the second movement (vv7-12) with a description of Egypt as the Nile in flood. yĕʾōr is the Egyptian loanword for the Nile (used throughout Exod 7-8 for the Nile's waters); yitgāʾăšû (Hitpael of gāʾaš, to surge, rush) pictures the inundation waters in turbulent surge. The Nile's annual flood was the source of Egypt's agricultural life — Jeremiah uses it ironically: Egypt's rise against the nations is like the Nile flood, impressive but ultimately receding.
46.8: Miṣrāyim kayyĕʾōr yaʿăleh ûkayyĕʾōrîm yitgāʾăšû māyim (Miṣrāyim kayyĕʾōr yaʿăleh): Egypt's self-declaration is quoted: ʾaʿăleh ʾăkassel ʾāreṣ ʾôḇîdâ ʿîr wĕyōšĕḇê ḇāh — "I will rise, I will cover the earth; I will destroy city and inhabitants." The triple first-person imperfect (I will rise, I will cover, I will destroy) mimics the divine speech pattern of conquest (cf. Isa 14:13-14, where the king of Babylon uses ʾeʿĕleh / ʾāśîm / ʾĕdammeh in hubristic self-declaration). Egypt's pride matches Babylon's and receives the same judgment.
46.9: ʿĂlû hassûsîm wĕhitĕhôlĕlû hāreḵeḇ wĕyēṣĕʾû haggibbôrîm (ʿĂlû hassûsîm): the mock-rally continues with the international composition of Egypt's forces. Kûš ûFût tōpĕśê māgēn ûlûdîm tōpĕśê dōrĕḵê qāšet (Kûš ûFût ûlûdîm): Cush (Upper Nubia/Ethiopia), Put (Libya), and Lud (Lydia or North Africa — debated; cf. Ezek 27:10; 30:5). All three were mercenary forces in Egypt's army. The enumeration of Egypt's multinational mercenary forces is both impressive and ironic — their very diversity signals Egypt's dependence on hired power rather than the divine patron who alone can save.
46.10: Ûhayyôm hahûʾ laʾădōnāy YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt yôm nāqām lĕhinnaqqēm mîṣṣārāyw (Ûhayyôm hahûʾ laʾădōnāy YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt): the theological summit of the oracle — Carchemish is redefined as yôm nāqām (day of vengeance/retribution). YHWH as the avenger (nāqam) exercises his justice on his enemies through the Babylonian instrument. Wĕʾāḵĕlâ ḥereḇ wĕśāḇĕʿâ wĕrāwĕtâ middāmām (wĕʾāḵĕlâ ḥereḇ wĕśāḇĕʿâ wĕrāwĕtâ middāmām): the sword personified — eating (ʾāḵal), satisfied (śāḇēaʿ), and drinking its fill of blood (rāwâ) — is the combat-violence imagery also used in Isa 34:5-7 and Ezek 21. Kî zeḇaḥ laʾădōnāy YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt bĕʾereṣ ṣāpôn ʿal-nĕhar-pĕrāt (kî zeḇaḥ laʾădōnāy YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt): the battle is a zeḇaḥ (sacrificial feast) — YHWH as divine warrior hosting a sacrifice whose victims are Egypt's army. The same image appears in Isa 34:6 (YHWH's sword has a sacrifice in Bozrah) and Ezek 39:17-20 (the feast of YHWH's sacrifice).
46.11: ʿĂlî gilʿād ûqĕḥî ṣŏrî bĕtûlat bat-miṣrāyim (ʾĂlî gilʿād ûqĕḥî ṣŏrî): the command to "go up to Gilead and take balm" is mocking — Gilead's famous healing balsam (ṣŏrî, resin/balm; cf. 8:22; 51:8; Gen 37:25) cannot help Egypt. Lāšāwĕʾ hirbbêt rĕpūʾôt tĕʿālâ ʾên lāḵ (Lāšāwĕʾ hirbbêt rĕpūʾôt tĕʿālâ ʾên lāḵ): "in vain you heap up medicines; there is no healing for you." The phrase tĕʿālâ ʾên lāḵ (no healing/restoration for you) was used for Jerusalem in 8:22 and for Babylon in 51:8 — the same incurability that characterized unfaithful Zion now belongs to Egypt. The wound of military defeat is beyond pharmaceutical remedy because it is YHWH's judgment.
46.12: Šāmĕʿû gôyim qĕlônēḵ ûṣiwwāḥătēḵ māĺĕʾâ hāʾāreṣ (Šāmĕʿû gôyim qĕlônēḵ): the nations have heard Egypt's shame (qālôn). Kî-gibbôr bĕgibbôr kāšāl gam-šĕnêhem yaḥdāw nāpālû (Kî-gibbôr bĕgibbôr kāšāl): the collapse is total — warrior stumbled over warrior, and together they fell. The image of crowded, chaotic retreat captures the scene at Carchemish: the disciplined formation of an advancing army becomes a tumbling mass of fleeing men. International shame — the nations heard the noise of the collapse — is the polar opposite of the military glory Egypt projected in vv7-9.
46.13: Haddāḇār ʾăšer-dibber YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû hannāḇîʾ lāḇôʾ Nĕḇûḵadrĕʾṣṣar melek-bāḇel lĕhakkôt ʾet-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim (lāḇôʾ Nĕḇûḵadrĕʾṣṣar... lĕhakkôt ʾet-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim): a second oracle heading (vv13-26) introduces the announcement of Nebuchadnezzar's future invasion of Egypt itself (this likely refers to 568/567 BCE, when Nebuchadnezzar did campaign in Egypt). The oracle extends beyond Carchemish to the comprehensive humiliation of Egypt on its own soil.
46.14: Haggîdû bĕmiṣrāyim wĕhašmîʿû bĕmigdōl ûhašmîʿû bĕnōp ûḇĕtaḥpanḥēs (bĕmigdōl ûḇĕnōp ûḇĕtaḥpanḥēs): the three locations — Migdol (NE border fortress), Noph/Memphis (capital of Lower Egypt), Tahpanhes (NE Delta fortress where Jeremiah was taken, ch43) — represent Egypt's northern defense system. ʾEmōr hiṯyaṣṣēḇ wahāḵēn lāḵ kî ʾāḵĕlâ ḥereḇ sĕḇîḇāyiḵ (kî ʾāḵĕlâ ḥereḇ sĕḇîḇāyiḵ): "the sword is eating all around you" — the devouring sword of v10 now reaches Egypt's own garrisons.
46.15: Maddûaʿ nusḥap ʾabbîrêḵ lōʾ ʿāmad kî YHWH hǝdāpô (YHWH hǝdāpô): "Why were your warriors swept away? They could not stand, because YHWH thrust them down." The hāḏap (to thrust, push down) attributes Egypt's military collapse directly to YHWH's action. Egypt's ʾabbîrîm (mighty ones, strong ones — used of horses in 8:16; Ps 22:13) are not felled by superior human arms but by the divine dāhap. The passive helplessness of Egypt before the divine push echoes the exodus reversal: Egypt's horses and riders were once the greatest military power; YHWH dispersed them then, and he does so again now.
46.16: Hirbâ kôšēl gam-nāpal ʾîš ʾel-rēʿēhû wayyōmĕrû qûmâ wĕnāšûḇâ ʾel-ʿammēnû wĕʾel-ʾereṣ môlādtēnû (ʾel-ʿammēnû wĕʾel-ʾereṣ môlādtēnû): the mercenaries — Cush, Put, and Lud of v9 — abandon Egypt, calling to one another to return to their homelands. Mippĕnê ḥereḇ hayyônâ (mippĕnê ḥereḇ hayyônâ): "away from the oppressing/crushing sword" — yônâ (Qal participle of yānâ, to oppress, crush) describes the sword as an oppressive force. The irony: Egypt who oppressed others is now the one causing flight by her own oppressive judgment-sword.
46.17: Qārĕʾû šām parʿōh melek-miṣrāyim šāʾôn heʿĕḇîr hammôʿēd (šāʾôn heʿĕḇîr hammôʿēd): the mocking epithet for Pharaoh — šāʾôn (noise, clamor) and heʿĕḇîr hammôʿēd (he let the appointed time/opportunity pass). The phrase creates a new throne-name for Pharaoh: "King of Egypt: Nothing but Noise who Missed his Moment." The mōʿēd (appointed time, opportunity) refers to the moment at which Egypt could have acted decisively — instead his vaunted military power was only noise. Isaiah 36:6 uses a similar demotion-image for Egypt as a broken reed.
46.18: Ḥay-ʾānî nĕʾum-hammelek YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt šĕmô kî kĕṯāḇôr bāhārîm yāḇôʾ ûḵḵarmel bayyām yāḇôʾ (kî kĕṯāḇôr bāhārîm... ûḵḵarmel bayyām): the divine oath formula (ḥay-ʾānî, "as I live") introduces the certainty of the invasion. Tabor (the conspicuous mountain of the Jezreel valley) and Carmel (the promontory jutting into the Mediterranean) — two of the most visually prominent landmarks in Canaan — serve as similes for Nebuchadnezzar's inevitable, unmistakable coming against Egypt. YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt šĕmô ("the LORD of hosts is his name") is the divine-warrior name formula that authenticates the oath.
46.19: Kĕlê gōlâ ʿăśî lāḵ yōšeḇet bat-miṣrāyim (Kĕlê gōlâ ʿăśî lāḵ): "Pack your bags for exile" — kĕlê gōlâ (exile-vessels, the gear of a deportee) is the same image used in Ezek 12:3-7 for Ezekiel's enacted prophecy of exile. Egypt's inhabitants must prepare for the same deportation she inflicted on others. Kî-nōp lĕšammâ tihyeh wĕnisrĕpâ mēʾên yôšēḇ (Kî-nōp lĕšammâ tihyeh): Memphis (nōp) will become desolation (šammâ) without inhabitant — the very vocabulary of judgment pronounced on Jerusalem (4:7; 9:11; 10:22; 25:11) is applied to Egypt's capital. The empire is no safer than the covenant city when YHWH judges.
46.20: ʿEglâ yĕpēh-piyyâ miṣrāyim (ʿEglâ yĕpēh-piyyâ miṣrāyim): "Egypt is a beautiful heifer" — the calf/heifer metaphor plays on multiple registers: Egypt's abundance (like a well-fed heifer), her fertility (the Nile-blessed land), and her vulnerability (an unsuspecting animal about to be slaughtered). Qereṣ mîṣṣāpôn bāʾ bāʾ (Qereṣ mîṣṣāpôn bāʾ bāʾ): "a horsefly/gadfly from the north has come, it has come" — the doubled bāʾ emphasizes the arrival's certainty. Qereṣ (horsefly, gadfly; only here in the OT) uses a startling bathos: the greatest empire of the age is undone not by a superior empire but by an insect (Nebuchadnezzar as horsefly). The same reductive irony appears in Isa 7:18 (YHWH whistling for flies from Egypt and bees from Assyria).
46.21: Gam-śĕḵîrêhā bĕqirbāh kĕʿeglê marbeq (Gam-śĕḵîrêhā bĕqirbāh kĕʿeglê marbeq): the mercenaries within Egypt are "like fattened calves" (ʿeglê marbeq — stall-fed cattle, Amos 6:4; Mal 4:2) — well-fed, comfortable, and useless for real defense. Kî-gam-hēmmâ pānû nāsû yaḥdāw lōʾ ʿāmādû kî yôm ʾêdām bāʾ ʿălêhem ʿēt pĕquddātām (kî yôm ʾêdām bāʾ ʿălêhem ʿēt pĕquddātām): "for the day of their calamity has come upon them, the time of their reckoning" — ʿēt pĕquddâ (time of visitation/reckoning) is YHWH's appointed moment of judgment, the same vocabulary used for Israel's exile (10:15; 11:23; 23:12; 50:27).
46.22: Qôlāh kaṇṇāḥāš yēlēḵ kî-ḇaḥayil yēlēḵû (Qôlāh kaṇṇāḥāš yēlēḵ): "her sound is like a hissing serpent as they advance" — a double image: Egypt retreating sounds like a hissing snake (the serpent symbolism is rich, as Egypt's royal symbol was the uraeus serpent nāḥāš); simultaneously the invaders advance like woodcutters with axes (bĕqarddummôt). The serpent-sound in retreat is a devastating reversal of the royal uraeus symbol of power. Wayyāḇōʾû lāh bĕqarddummôt kĕḥōṭĕḇê ʿēṣîm (kĕḥōṭĕḇê ʿēṣîm): the attackers are woodcutters — Egypt's army is treated as a mere forest to be logged.
46.23: Kārĕtû yĕʿārāh nĕʾum-YHWH kî lōʾ yiḥqēr kî rābû mēʾarbeh wĕʾên lāhem mispar (kî rābû mēʾarbeh wĕʾên lāhem mispar): they cut down her forest — "though it is dense" (kî lōʾ yiḥqēr, for it cannot be explored/fathomed) — because Babylon's army is more numerous than locusts (mēʾarbeh) and without number (wĕʾên lāhem mispar). The locust simile for a conquering army is a common ANE combat image (Joel 1-2; Judg 6:5; 7:12). Egypt's impenetrable forests offer no shelter because the attacking force is numerically overwhelming.
46.24: Hōḇîšâ bat-miṣrāyim nittĕnâ bĕyad ʿam ṣāpôn (Hōḇîšâ bat-miṣrāyim nittĕnâ bĕyad ʿam ṣāpôn): "the daughter of Egypt is shamed; she is handed over to a people from the north." The feminine personification (bat-miṣrāyim, daughter of Egypt) is the standard OAN form for the nation as a woman suffering defeat and shame (bōšet). The passive nittĕnâ (she is handed over, Niphal perfect) credits YHWH as the agent: Egypt is not merely conquered; she is given by YHWH into Babylon's hands.
46.25: ʾĀmar YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl hinnĕnî pōqēd ʾel-ʾāmôn minnōʾ wĕʿal-parʿōh wĕʿal-miṣrāyim wĕʿal-ʾĕlōhêhā wĕʿal-mĕlāḵêhā (hinnĕnî pōqēd ʾel-ʾāmôn minnōʾ): Amon of No — the chief deity of Thebes (Upper Egypt; nōʾ = Thebes, Ezek 30:14-15; Nah 3:8), whose full name was Amon-Ra. YHWH's judgment explicitly targets the divine patron of Egypt: wĕʿal-ʾĕlōhêhā (and her gods). The oracle is not merely political but theological: YHWH asserts his sovereignty over Egypt's divine pantheon. Cf. Exod 12:12: "against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments" — the exodus pattern is being recapitulated.
46.26: Ûnĕtattîm bĕyad mĕḇaqšê napšām ûḇĕyad Nĕḇûḵadrĕʾṣṣar melek-bāḇel ûḇĕyad-ʿăḇādāyw (ûḇĕyad mĕḇaqšê napšām): YHWH delivers them into the hands of "those who seek their lives" (mĕḇaqšê napšām) — the same phrase used for those who sought Jeremiah's life (11:21; 44:30). Wĕʾaḥărê-ḵēn tiššāḵēn kîmê-qedem nĕʾum-YHWH (Wĕʾaḥărê-ḵēn tiššāḵēn kîmê-qedem): "But afterward Egypt will be inhabited again as in the days of old." This restoration footnote (like Moab's in 48:47 and Ammon's in 49:6) prevents the OAN from reading as pure annihilation — YHWH's judgment is not the last word even for the nations. Ezekiel 29:13-14 provides a parallel: Egypt will be restored, though to a lower status.
46.27: Wĕʾattâ ʾal-tîrāʾ ʿaḇdî yaʿăqōḇ wĕʾal-tēḥat yiśrāʾēl (Wĕʾattâ ʾal-tîrāʾ ʿaḇdî yaʿăqōḇ): the oracle concludes with a remarkable turn to Jacob/Israel — vv27-28 are nearly identical to 30:10-11. After the oracle of doom against Egypt, the word to Israel is ʾal-tîrāʾ (do not fear) — YHWH's universal sovereignty over Egypt is precisely the ground of Israel's security. Kî hinnĕnî môšîʿăḵā mērāḥôq wĕʾet-zarʿăḵā mēʾereṣ šiḇyām (hinnĕnî môšîʿăḵā mērāḥôq): "I am going to save you from far away and your descendants from the land of their captivity" — the same YHWH who controls Nebuchadnezzar over Egypt is simultaneously the one who saves Jacob from exile. Wĕšāḇ yaʿăqōḇ wĕšāqaṭ wĕšaʿănan wĕʾên maḥărîd (wĕšāḇ... wĕšāqaṭ wĕšaʿănan): Jacob will return and be quiet (šāqaṭ), secure (šaʿănan), with no one causing trembling (maḥărîd, Hiphil participle of ḥārad) — a sequence of three šin-rooted words that pile up the completeness of restoration rest.
46.28: ʾAttâ ʾal-tîrāʾ ʿaḇdî yaʿăqōḇ nĕʾum-YHWH kî ʾittĕḵā ʾānî (kî ʾittĕḵā ʾānî): "for I am with you" — the divine presence formula (ʿimmānû / ʾittĕḵā) that recurs throughout the call narrative (1:8, 19) and the letters to the exiles (29:14) is the ultimate assurance. Kî ʾeʿĕśeh kālâ bĕḵol-haggôyim ʾăšer hiddaḥtîḵā šāmmâ wĕʾōtĕḵā lōʾ-ʾeʿĕśeh ḵālâ ûyissartîḵā lammišpāṭ wĕnaqqēh lōʾ ʾănaqkeḵā (kî ʾeʿĕśeh kālâ bĕḵol-haggôyim): "for I will make a complete end of all the nations to which I have driven you, but of you I will not make a complete end" — the word kālâ (complete end, annihilation) is applied to the nations but denied for Jacob. ûyissartîḵā lammišpāṭ wĕnaqqēh lōʾ ʾănaqkeḵā: "I will discipline you justly but will not leave you entirely unpunished" — the tension of grace and justice: YHWH will not pretend the sin did not happen (naqqēh lōʾ, will not leave entirely unpunished) but will not destroy Jacob either. The difference between Israel and the nations is not Israel's merit but YHWH's covenant commitment to Jacob.

47.1: ʾĂšer hāyâ dĕḇar-YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû hannāḇîʾ ʾel-Pĕlištîm bĕṭerem yakkeh parʿōh ʾet-ʿazzâ (bĕṭerem yakkeh parʿōh ʾet-ʿazzâ): the oracle heading dates the Philistine oracle "before Pharaoh attacked Gaza." This refers to Neco II's northward campaign in 609 BCE (when he also killed Josiah at Megiddo, 2 Kings 23:29) or a subsequent Egyptian incursion. The dating places the oracle before the event it partially describes — prophetic foreknowledge of Egyptian military action in Philistia. The Philistines occupied the SW coastal plain of Canaan (the five cities: Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron, Gath).
47.2: Kōh ʾāmar YHWH hinnēh mayim ʿōlîm mîṣṣāpôn wĕhāyû lĕnaḥal šôṭēp (hinnēh mayim ʿōlîm mîṣṣāpôn wĕhāyû lĕnaḥal šôṭēp): the flood imagery — waters rising from the north becoming an overflowing torrent (naḥal šôṭēp, lit. overflowing wadi) — appears also in Isa 8:7-8 for the Assyrian flood-invasion and in Jer 46:7-8 for the Egyptian Nile-flood. Here it applies to Babylon ("from the north") overwhelming Philistia. Wĕšāṭĕpû ʾereṣ ûmĕlōʾāh ʿîr wĕyōšĕḇê ḇāh (Wĕšāṭĕpû ʾereṣ ûmĕlōʾāh): they will swamp the land and its fullness — wĕšāṭap (to overflow, flood) applied to military invasion creates the same metaphorical totality: nothing remains dry, nothing escapes the flood of judgment.
47.3: Miqqôl šaʿăṭat pārsôt ʾabbîrāyw mirrāʿaš lĕmerkĕḇōtāyw hāmôn galgillāyw (miqqôl šaʿăṭat pārsôt ʾabbîrāyw): the soundscape of approaching cavalry — the thunder of hoofs (šaʿăṭat pārsôt, the stamping/clattering of hooves), the rumbling of chariots (hāmôn galgillāyw, the roar of their wheels). The acoustic terror of Babylonian chariot-forces is described with intense attention to sound, matching the visual intensity of v2's flood image. Lōʾ-pānû ʾāḇôt ʾel-bānîm mĕrippayôn yādāyim (Lōʾ-pānû ʾāḇôt ʾel-bānîm mĕrippayôn yādāyim): "fathers will not turn back for their children — their hands go limp" — the rippayôn yādāyim (limpness/slackness of hands — Jer 6:24; Isa 13:7; Ezek 7:17) is the physical manifestation of complete demoralization: soldiers so overcome with fear they cannot even reach for their children.
47.4: ʿAl-hayyôm haBBāʾ lĕšadōd ʾet-kol-Pĕlištîm lĕhaḵrît lĕṣōr ûlĕṣîdôn kol śārid ʿōzēr (lĕhaḵrît lĕṣōr ûlĕṣîdôn kol śārid ʿōzēr): Tyre and Sidon (Phoenicia) are included as Philistia's commercial and military allies — their remaining support (kol śārid ʿōzēr, every remaining helper) will be cut off. Kî-šōdēd YHWH ʾet-Pĕlištîm šĕʾērît ʾî Kaptôr (šĕʾērît ʾî Kaptôr): "for YHWH is destroying the Philistines, the remnant of the coastlands of Caphtor." Kaptôr (Crete or the Aegean region) is the Philistines' place of origin, cited also in Amos 9:7 ("Did I not bring up the Philistines from Caphtor?"). YHWH's sovereignty over the Philistines extends back to their own origin-migration.
47.5: Bāʾâ qorḥâ ʾel-ʿazzâ nidmĕtâ ʾašqĕlôn šĕʾērît ʿimqām (Bāʾâ qorḥâ ʾel-ʿazzâ nidmĕtâ ʾašqĕlôn): Gaza is shaved bald (qorḥâ, the mourning rite of head-shaving, Amos 8:10; Mic 1:16; Isa 15:2); Ashkelon is silenced/destroyed (nidmĕtâ, Niphal of dāmam). ʿAd-mātay titgōdĕdî (ʿAd-mātay titgōdĕdî): "how long will you slash yourself?" — gādad (to slash, cut) refers to the mourning practice of self-laceration forbidden to Israel (Lev 19:28; Deut 14:1) but practiced by neighboring peoples (cf. 1 Kings 18:28; Zech 13:6). The question implies the mourning is endless because the devastation is total.
47.6: Hôy ḥereḇ laYHWH ʿad-ʾānâ lōʾ tišqôṭî (Hôy ḥereḇ laYHWH ʿad-ʾānâ lōʾ tišqôṭî): the sword-lament — a unique personification in the OAN collection. The prophet addresses YHWH's sword: "O sword of YHWH, how long before you rest?" Hēʾāsĕpî ʾel-taʿrēḵ hĕrāgĕʿî wĕdōmî (Hēʾāsĕpî ʾel-taʿrēḵ hĕrāgĕʿî wĕdōmî): "Return to your sheath; be still and stop." The pathos of this plea — the prophet lamenting the extent of divine judgment — is comparable to the Confessions (4:10; 14:13; 20:7). The sword is felt as a burden even when its action is just.
47.7: Êḵ tišqôṭî wĕYHWH ṣiwwāh-lāh ʾel-ʾašqĕlôn wĕʾel-ḥôp hayyām šāmmâ yĕʾādāh (wĕYHWH ṣiwwāh-lāh ʾel-ʾašqĕlôn): the rhetorical answer ends the oracle — "How can it be still when YHWH has given it its charge? Against Ashkelon and the seacoast — there he has set it." The sword cannot rest because it is executing a divinely-ordered commission (YHWH ṣiwwāh-lāh, YHWH commanded it). The lament of v6 is answered not with false comfort but with honest acknowledgment: the judgment is real, authorized, and specific. This frank dialogue with the divine sword's necessity reflects Jeremiah's mature prophetic voice — he does not deny the horror of judgment but locates it within YHWH's sovereign purpose.

48.1: The Moab oracle (chs 48) is the longest OAN in Jeremiah and one of the most extensively developed in all prophetic literature. It draws heavily from Isaiah 15-16 and Numbers 21. Lĕmôʾāḇ kōh ʾāmar YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl hôy ʿal-Nĕḇô kî šuddādâ (hôy ʿal-Nĕḇô kî šuddādâ): the oracle opens with hôy (woe, funeral cry) over Nebo, a Moabite city on or near Mount Nebo (from which Moses viewed Canaan, Deut 34:1). Qiryātāyim hōḇîšâ nileḵĕdâ hammiśgāḇ hōḇîšâ ûḥāttâ (hōḇîšâ wĕḥāttâ): Kiriathaim (twin cities, a Reubenite then Moabite town, Num 32:37; Ezek 25:9) and the stronghold are shamed and shattered — three hōḇîšâ words in this verse and the next create a drumbeat of shame.
48.2: ʾên ʿôd tehillat Môʾāḇ bĕḤešbôn ḥāšĕḇû ʿālêhā rāʿâ lĕkû wĕnaḵrîtennāh miggôy (ḥāšĕḇû ʿālêhā rāʿâ lĕkû wĕnaḵrîtennāh miggôy): "in Heshbon they have plotted its ruin: Come, let us cut it off as a nation." The wordplay on Ḥešbôn/ḥāšĕḇû (Heshbon/they have devised) — the city's name echoes the verb for scheming — is a classic prophetic omen-name technique (cf. Jer 1:12: šāqēd/šōqēd). Gam-madmēn tiddōmmî ʾaḥărêḵ tēlēḵ ḥāreḇ (Gam-madmēn tiddōmmî): Madmen will also be silenced — the wordplay continues: Madmēn/tiddōmmî (Madmen/will be silenced; dāmam).
48.3: Qôl ṣĕʿāqâ mîḤōrônāyim šōd ûšeḇer gādôl (Qôl ṣĕʿāqâ mîḤōrônāyim šōd ûšeḇer gādôl): Horonaim ("two caverns," a Moabite town, Isa 15:5) — the cry of ruin (šōd ûšeḇer gādôl, devastation and great breaking) heard from there signals the cascade of destruction moving through the Moabite city network. The OAN moves town by town through Moab's geography — a literary technique that creates the effect of a reportage from a moving front line.
48.4: Nišbĕrâ Môʾāḇ hišmîʿû zĕʿāqâ ṣĕʿirêhā (Nišbĕrâ Môʾāḇ hišmîʿû zĕʿāqâ ṣĕʿirêhā): "Moab is shattered; her young ones raise a cry." Nišbĕrâ (Niphal perfect of šāḇar, to shatter) signals total structural collapse; ṣĕʿirêhā (her little ones) are either children literally weeping or the small towns of Moab crying out. The LXX reads "even to Zoar" which connects to v34, but the MT reading of "young ones/little ones" produces a poignant image of total vulnerability — even children bear the war's destruction.
48.5: Kî maʿălēh-halûḥît bĕḇekî yaʿăleh-bô kî bĕmôrad ḥōrônāyim ṣārê-yĕlalelû šĕḇer (Kî maʿălēh-halûḥît bĕḇekî yaʿăleh-bô): the ascent of Luhith (also cited in Isa 15:5) and the road down to Horonaim are the two main routes of Moab — one going up, one going down — and on both the refugees flee in continuous weeping (bĕḇekî tamrûrîm, with bitter weeping) and cries of anguish (ṣārê-yĕlalelû šĕḇer). The geography of Moab's road system becomes the geography of its mourning.
48.6: Nûsû mallĕṭû napšĕḵem ûḵĕʿarôʿēr bammidbar tihyû (ûḵĕʿarôʿēr bammidbar tihyû): "Flee! Save yourselves! Be like a desert shrub (ʿarôʿēr) in the wilderness." The word ʿarôʿēr (juniper/tamarisk, a solitary shrub) is the same used in 17:6 for the man who trusts in human beings instead of YHWH: "he is like a shrub in the desert and shall not see any good come." By calling Moab to become what the cursed man is, the oracle identifies Moab's condition as the direct consequence of misplaced trust (v7: "because you trusted in your works").
48.7: Kî yaʿan biṭḥēḵ bĕmaʿăśayiḵ ûḇĕʾôṣĕrôtêḵ gam-ʾat tillaḵēdî (Kî yaʿan biṭḥēḵ bĕmaʿăśayiḵ ûḇĕʾôṣĕrôtêḵ): the theological root of Moab's judgment — trust (bāṭaḥ) in her own works (maʿăśāyim, both deeds and manufactured goods/idols) and in her storehouses (ʾôṣĕrôt, treasure-houses, reserves). This is the antithesis of the trust YHWH requires (cf. 17:7-8: blessed is the person who trusts in YHWH). Wĕyāṣāʾ Kĕmôš baggōlâ hûʾ wĕkōhănāyw wĕśārāyw yaḥdāw (Wĕyāṣāʾ Kĕmôš baggōlâ): Chemosh, Moab's national deity (Num 21:29; Judg 11:24; 1 Kings 11:7, 33) goes into exile along with his priests and officials. The defeat of a people's patron deity is the ultimate OAN statement: Chemosh cannot protect Moab because he himself is carried off as conquered territory. Cf. Isa 46:1-2 (Bel and Nebo borne away on beasts of burden).
48.8: Wĕyāḇôʾ šōdēd ʾel-kol-ʿîr wĕʿîr lōʾ timmāleṭ wĕʾāḇad haʿēmeq wĕnišmad hammîšôr kaʾăšer dibber YHWH (kaʾăšer dibber YHWH): "the destroyer will come to every city; not one city will escape... exactly as YHWH has decreed." The oracle's completion formula (kaʾăšer dibber YHWH) anchors the prediction's authority: what has happened/will happen fulfills the divine word. The completeness — every city (kol-ʿîr wĕʿîr), not one escaping (lōʾ timmāleṭ) — gives the valley and plateau no shelter.
48.9: Tĕnû ṣîṣ lĕMôʾāḇ kî nāṣōʾ tēṣēʾ (Tĕnû ṣîṣ lĕMôʾāḇ kî nāṣōʾ tēṣēʾ): the command "give wings to Moab so she may fly away" is sardonic — Moab will need wings because flight is the only remaining option. nāṣōʾ tēṣēʾ (the doubled infinitive absolute: she will surely fly away) uses the doubled form to express certainty; the irony is that the only "flight" available is the hopeless flight of refugee flight, not the soaring of freedom. Wĕʿārêhā lĕšammâ tihyênâ mēʾên yôšēḇ bāhen (lĕšammâ tihyênâ mēʾên yôšēḇ): the desolation formula — cities becoming šammâ (desolation) without inhabitants — is the same formula applied to Jerusalem (4:7) and Egypt (46:19). No people is exempt from this consequence of covenant/divine-law violation.
48.10: ʾĀrûr ʿōśeh mĕleʾḵet YHWH rĕmiyyâ wĕʾārûr mōnēaʿ ḥarbô middām (ʾĀrûr ʿōśeh mĕleʾḵet YHWH rĕmiyyâ wĕʾārûr mōnēaʿ ḥarbô middām): a striking verse — "cursed is the one who carries out YHWH's work negligently, and cursed is the one who holds his sword back from bloodshed." The oracle uses the covenant-curse formula (ʾārûr, Deut 27-28) to bind the Babylonian executors of judgment to full implementation. rĕmiyyâ (slackness, negligence, deceit) — the ʿōśeh mĕleʾḵet YHWH rĕmiyyâ (one who does YHWH's work with slackness) is cursed along with the one who withholds violence. This verse creates significant theological tension — YHWH binding human soldiers to thoroughness in execution — but it reflects the consistent OAN theology that the conquering army is YHWH's instrument, not an autonomous agent.
48.11: Šaqaṭ Môʾāḇ minĕʿûrāyw wĕšōqēṭ hûʾ ʿal-šĕmārāyw wĕlōʾ-hûraq mikkĕlî ʾel-kēlî ûḇaggōlâ lōʾ-hālāḵ (wĕlōʾ-hûraq mikkĕlî ʾel-kēlî): the wine-on-its-dregs metaphor is the most distinctive image in the Moab oracle. Moab is like undisturbed wine that has never been poured from vessel to vessel — unfiltered wine left on its sediment develops a corrupted, stale flavor. The refinement process of pouring wine from jar to jar (to separate it from the lees/dregs, šĕmārîm) is what creates clarity and quality. ʿAl-kēn ʿāmad ṭaʿmô bô wĕrêḥô lōʾ nāmār (ʿāmad ṭaʿmô bô wĕrêḥô lōʾ nāmār): "his flavor has stayed in him and his aroma has not changed" — the metaphor: Moab's undisturbed prosperity (no exile, no external pressure) has produced cultural/moral stagnation. Peace without discipline produces corruption rather than character. Cf. Ps 55:19 ("those who have no changes dread not God").
48.12: Lāḵēn hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm nĕʾum-YHWH wĕšillaḥtî-lô ṣōʿîm wĕṣĕrûhû ûkĕlêhû yāreqqû wĕnibĕlêhem yĕnappēṣû (wĕšillaḥtî-lô ṣōʿîm wĕṣĕrûhû): "I will send wine-tilters (ṣōʿîm, those who tilt/pour) who will tip him over and empty his vessels and smash his jars." The YHWH-as-vintner sends agents to do what should have been done through natural historical process — the exile (being poured from vessel to vessel) that Moab escaped becomes a violent compulsory process. wĕnibĕlêhem yĕnappēṣû (and his containers — nēḇel, wine-jug — will be smashed): the smashed wine-jug image echoes Jeremiah's own sign-act with the pottery flask (baqbûq) in ch19 — the broken jar as irreversible judgment.
48.13: Wĕḇôš Môʾāḇ miKKĕmôš kaʾăšer-bôšû bêt-yiśrāʾēl mibBêt ʾēl miḇṭaḥām (kaʾăšer-bôšû bêt-yiśrāʾēl mibBêt ʾēl miḇṭaḥām): the comparison to Israel's shame over Bethel is historically precise — the golden calves established by Jeroboam I at Bethel and Dan (1 Kings 12:28-29) were the defining apostasy of the northern kingdom. Israel trusted (bāṭaḥ) in Bethel's bulls; they were proved worthless. Moab trusted in Chemosh; Chemosh too will be proved worthless. The comparison democratizes the experience of idolatrous shame: Israel's history of false trust is the template for the nations' judgment.
48.14: Êḵ tōmĕrû ʾănaḥnû gibbōrîm wĕʾanšê-ḥayil lammilḥāmâ (ʾănaḥnû gibbōrîm wĕʾanšê-ḥayil lammilḥāmâ): the rhetorical question challenges Moab's self-description as "heroes, battle-hardened men" (gibbōrîm... ʾanšê-ḥayil). The vocabulary of military prowess — gibbôr (mighty warrior), ʾîš ḥayil (man of valor) — is the language of Moab's own boasting. This sets up the inversion: the claim of might makes the collapse more devastating. Êḵ (How?) — the same funeral-lament interrogative used in the book of Lamentations (1:1; 2:1; 4:1) — frames the self-congratulatory claim as already past, a subject of lamentation.
48.15: Šuddad Môʾāḇ wĕʿārêhā ʿālâ ûmibḥar baḥûrāyw yārĕdû laṭṭāḇaḥ (wĕʿārêhā ʿālâ ûmibḥar baḥûrāyw yārĕdû laṭṭāḇaḥ): the devastation is comprehensive — the cities are overrun and the choicest (mibḥar) young men go down to slaughter (laṭṭāḇaḥ, the slaughterhouse term; cf. Isa 53:7; Jer 11:19 — both uses of the slaughter-lamb). Nĕʾum-hammelek YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt šĕmô (Nĕʾum-hammelek YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt šĕmô): "declares the King, the LORD of hosts is his name" — the divine warrior title (YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt) with hammelek (the King) combines to present YHWH as the sovereign who commands both the cosmic army and the judgment of nations. Cf. Mal 1:14 ("I am a great King, says the LORD of hosts").
48.16: Qārôḇ ʾêd Môʾāḇ lāḇôʾ wĕrāʿātô māhĕrâ mĕʾōd (Qārôḇ ʾêd Môʾāḇ lāḇôʾ wĕrāʿātô māhĕrâ mĕʾōd): the catastrophe is described as imminent — "Moab's disaster is close at hand and his ruin coming very quickly." ʾêd (disaster, doom, calamity) appears in the Wisdom literature (Prov 1:26-27; 6:15; 24:22) for sudden catastrophe; in Jer 18:17 YHWH shows the back not the face on the day of their ʾêd. The speed (māhĕrâ mĕʾōd) and proximity (qārôḇ) together convey the urgency of irreversibility — there is no more time for repentance.
48.17: Nûdû lô kol-sĕḇîḇāyw wĕḵol-yōdĕʿê šĕmô ʾimrû ʾêḵ nišbar maṭṭēh-ʿōz maqqēl tipʾāret (ʾêḵ nišbar maṭṭēh-ʿōz maqqēl tipʾāret): the funeral lament formula — ʾêḵ (how!) + passive verb of destruction. The "powerful scepter" (maṭṭēh-ʿōz) and "glorious staff" (maqqēl tipʾāret) are regalia of royal power — Moab's imperial sovereignty is the scepter/staff that is broken. Cf. Ezek 19:10-14 (the lament over Judah's scepter/vine); Isa 9:4; 10:24 (breaking the rod of oppression). The lament is addressed to those who surround Moab and know its name — a call to communal mourning for what was, rather than what Moab became.
48.18: Rĕdî miqqāḇôd wĕšĕḇî baṣṣāmāʾ yōšeḇet bat-Dîḇôn (Rĕdî miqqāḇôd wĕšĕḇî baṣṣāmāʾ): "Come down from your glory and sit in thirst, O inhabitants of Dibon." Kāḇôd (glory, weight, honor) down to ṣāmāʾ (thirst, drought) — the polarity is absolute: from the height of national glory to the parched ground of refugee thirst. Dibon was Mesha's royal city (the Mesha Stele describes his building activities there, ~850 BCE). Kî šōdēd Môʾāḇ ʿālâ ḇāḵ šiḥat miḇṣārāyiḵ (kî šōdēd Môʾāḇ ʿālâ ḇāḵ šiḥat miḇṣārāyiḵ): the destroyer has come up against Dibon's fortresses (miḇṣārîm, fortified places) and ruined them.
48.19: ʿimĕdî hadd-dereḵ ûṣappî yōšeḇet ʿarôʿēr šaʾălî nās ûmāleṭ ʾimrî mah-nîhyâ (ʿimĕdî hadd-dereḵ ûṣappî... šaʾălî nās ûmāleṭ): the command to Aroer (on the Arnon River, the southern boundary of Moab's territory, Deut 2:36; 3:12) to stand by the road and question the fleeing and escaping: "What has happened?" (mah-nîhyâ). The question is rhetorical — the questioner will receive a report of total collapse. The posting of a town at the crossroads to gather news from refugees mirrors the way ancient Near Eastern cities functioned as information nodes during military campaigns.
48.20: Hōḇîš Môʾāḇ kî ḥattâ hêlîlî ûzʿāqî haggîdû ʾaḇ-ʾARNÔN kî šuddad Môʾāḇ (hêlîlî ûzʿāqî haggîdû ʾaḇ-ʾARNÔN kî šuddad Môʾāḇ): the lamentation intensifies — Moab is shamed, shattered; the command is to wail, cry, and announce it at the Arnon. hêlîl (wail, howl — the funeral cry used in Isa 13:6; Ezek 21:12; Amos 8:3; Joel 1:5) is the distinctive sound of prophetic judgment literature, the vocalization of šammâ (desolation). The announcement at the Arnon (Moab's northern boundary with Israel) signals the collapse is complete from border to border.
48.21: Ûmišpāṭ bāʾ ʾel-ʾereṣ hammîšôr ʾel-Ḥôlôn wĕʾel-Yahṣâ wĕʿal-Môpāʿat (Ûmišpāṭ bāʾ ʾel-ʾereṣ hammîšôr): the long list of Moabite cities (vv21-24) to which judgment comes exemplifies the prophetic list-curse. Each city-name carries the rhetorical weight of a funeral toll. Hammîšôr (the Plateau/Tableland) is the high Transjordanian plateau east of the Dead Sea — the heartland of Moab's agriculture and settlement. Holon, Jahzah (site of Israel's battle with Sihon, Num 21:23), and Mephaath are named in the Reubenite city lists (Josh 13:17-21).
48.22: Wĕʿal-Dîḇôn wĕʿal-Nĕḇô wĕʿal-Bêt-Diḇlātāyim: Dibon (Mesha's royal city), Nebo (where the inscription found in 1868 naming Moab was excavated near Mount Nebo), and Beth-diblathaim continue the city-by-city roll call of judgment. Each name is a place where real people lived, farmed, and prayed — the oracle's specificity prevents it from being abstracted into generic judgment rhetoric. This is the same technique Jeremiah uses for Jerusalem's own doom (30:4-7; 51:54).
48.23: Wĕʿal-Qiryātāyim wĕʿal-Bêt-Gāmûl wĕʿal-Bêt-Mĕʿôn: Kiriathaim (mentioned in v1), Beth-gamul (unidentified but Moabite), and Beth-meon (also called Baal-meon, Num 32:38; Ezek 25:9 — a city whose very name embeds the Baal cult). The Baal-meon connection anticipates v35's oracle against Moabite high-place religion.
48.24: Wĕʿal-Qĕriyyôt wĕʿal-Boṣrâ wĕʿal kol-ʿārê ʾereṣ-Môʾāḇ hārĕḥōqôt wĕhaqqĕrōḇôt (hārĕḥōqôt wĕhaqqĕrōḇôt): the list concludes with Kerioth and Bozrah (the latter appears also in the Edom oracle, 49:13, 22; Amos 1:12) and the comprehensive formula "all the cities of the land of Moab, far and near." hārĕḥōqôt wĕhaqqĕrōḇôt (the distant and the near) — geographic totality, the formula of complete coverage (cf. Deut 13:7; 29:22; 1 Kings 8:46).
48.25: Nîgdĕʿâ qeren Môʾāḇ ûzĕrōʿô nišbārâ nĕʾum-YHWH (Nîgdĕʿâ qeren Môʾāḇ ûzĕrōʿô nišbārâ): the oracle-within-a-list: qeren (horn, symbol of power and pride — Ps 75:5-10; Lam 2:3; Amos 6:13) and zĕrôaʿ (arm, symbol of military strength) are hewn off and broken. The two images together cover both the external display of power (horn as crown-symbol) and the instrument of power (arm). YHWH as the one who breaks the nations' power is the theological thread running through the entire OAN collection.
48.26: Hašḵîrûhû kî ʿal-YHWH higdîl wĕsāpaq Môʾāḇ bĕqîʾô wĕhāyâ lĭśĕḥōq gam-hûʾ (kî ʿal-YHWH higdîl): the sin specified — Moab "exalted himself against YHWH" (higdîl ʿal-YHWH). The Hiphil of gādal (to make oneself great/lifted above) applied to ʿal-YHWH (against/above YHWH) is the defining sin of hubris — positioning oneself above or in opposition to YHWH's sovereignty. Wĕsāpaq Môʾāḇ bĕqîʾô wĕhāyâ lĭśĕḥōq gam-hûʾ (wĕsāpaq Môʾāḇ bĕqîʾô): "Moab will wallow in his own vomit" — the drunkenness image (hašḵîrûhû, make him drunk) leads to the degradation of vomiting in his own filth, becoming an object of ridicule (śĕḥōq). Cf. Hab 2:16: shame covers glory when the cup of YHWH's wrath is drunk.
48.27: Wĕʾim-lōʾ lĭśĕḥōq hāyâ lĕḵā yiśrāʾēl ʾim-biggannāḇîm nimṣāʾ (Wĕʾim-lōʾ lĭśĕḥōq hāyâ lĕḵā yiśrāʾēl ʾim-biggannāḇîm nimṣāʾ): the oracle turns the mockery back on Moab — "Was not Israel a laughingstock to you? Was he found among thieves?" The question carries the logic of judgment's reversal: Moab mocked Israel in her humiliation; now Moab will be mocked. Kî-midday dibbārĕḵā bô tinnôdēd (kî-midday dibbārĕḵā bô tinnôdēd): "For whenever you spoke of him you wagged your head (tinnôdēd, the head-shaking gesture of contempt, Ps 22:7; Lam 2:15)" — Moab's contemptuous commentary on Israel's fall is what makes Moab's own fall a matter of divine justice-in-kind.
48.28: ʿIzĕḇû ʿārîm wĕšiḵnû bassela yōšĕḇê Môʾāḇ wĕhiyyû kĕyônâ tĕqannēn bĕʿeḇrê pî-paḥat (wĕhiyyû kĕyônâ tĕqannēn bĕʿeḇrê pî-paḥat): "Be like the dove that builds its nest deep in the walls of a ravine." The dove (yônâ) nesting deep in the cliff-walls (bĕʿeḇrê pî-paḥat, on the far side of the mouth of the pit/ravine) is an image of the most hidden, inaccessible nesting — Moab must seek the deepest hiding possible, because the cities offer no protection. Cf. Song 2:14 ("my dove in the clefts of the rock") — the same image of cliff-refuge, there used lovingly; here it is counsel of desperate concealment.
48.29: Šāmaʿnû gĕʾôn-Môʾāḇ gēʾeh mĕʾōd gabbĕhûtô wĕgaʾăwātô wĕgĕʾônô wĕrûm libbô (Šāmaʿnû gĕʾôn-Môʾāḇ gēʾeh mĕʾōd): five words for Moab's pride in a single verse — gĕʾôn (majesty/pride), gēʾeh (haughty), gabbĕhût (loftiness), gaʾăwâ (arrogance), rûm lēḇ (exaltation of heart). The concentration creates a portrait of a nation whose self-assessment was its central feature — and its fatal flaw. Cf. Isa 16:6 (the parallel passage): "We have heard of the pride of Moab — how proud he is! — of his arrogance, his pride, and his insolence." Moab's pride is the standing OT example of national hubris against YHWH (cf. also Ezek 25:8-11).
48.30: ʾĂnî yādaʿtî nĕʾum-YHWH ʿebrātô wĕlōʾ-ḵēn baddāyw lōʾ-ḵēn ʿāśû (ʾĂnî yādaʿtî nĕʾum-YHWH ʿebrātô): "I know his arrogance, declares YHWH — but it comes to nothing; his empty boasts accomplish nothing." YHWH's declaration of knowledge (yādaʿtî, I know) is the divine omniscient assessment. ʿebrâ (arrogance, overflow — Prov 11:4; 21:24) is what YHWH knows; the double lōʾ-ḵēn ("not so, not so") is a dismissal: Moab's bluster is assessed, found hollow, and dismissed. YHWH's calm knowledge of Moab's pride contrasts with the dramatic military collapse described elsewhere in the chapter.
48.31: Lāḵēn ʿal-Môʾāḇ ʾeylelāh wĕlĕḵol-Môʾāḇ ʾizʿāq lĕʾanšê Qîr-ḥereś ʾehgeh (Lāḵēn ʿal-Môʾāḇ ʾeylelāh): the divine lament — YHWH himself wails (ʾeylelāh, Qal imperfect of yālal, to wail) over Moab. This is theologically remarkable: the God who pronounces judgment over Moab also mourns over it. Cf. Jer 48:31 paralleling Isa 16:7 and the divine weeping in Isa 16:9-11. The same tension appears in Lam 3 (YHWH is both the agent of destruction and the one who does not abandon). The divine lament is not contradiction but the full expression of YHWH's character: judgment and compassion coexist.
48.32: ʾEt-bĕḵî yĕʿaZĒR ʾeḇkeh-lāḵ gepen Śiḇmâ nĕṭîšōtayiḵ ʿāḇrû yam ʿad-yam yĕʿaZĒR higgîʿû (gepen Śiḇmâ nĕṭîšōtayiḵ ʿāḇrû yam): the lament over Sibmah's vines — the famous vineyards that spread their tendrils across the sea (the Dead Sea) to Jazer — is a regional elegy for agricultural abundance destroyed. ʿal-qayiṣēḵ wĕʿal-bĕṣîrĕḵā šōdēd nāpāl (šōdēd nāpāl): the destroyer has fallen on the summer fruit and vintage — harvest imagery for the destruction of Moab's agricultural identity.
