Cf. Isa 16:8-10 (the lament over Sibmah's vines in the parallel OAN).
48.33: Wĕneʾespâ śimḥâ wāgîl mikkarmēl ûmēʾereṣ Môʾāḇ ûyayim miyyĕqāḇîm hibbattî yôdēd lōʾ-yôdēd lōʾ hēdād yēdād (yayim miyyĕqāḇîm hibbattî yôdēd lōʾ-yôdēd): joy and gladness stripped from the fertile fields. Yôdēd (the harvest shout, the treaders' cry) — "the only shout is not a harvest shout." The harvest treading song (hēdād, shouting) is silenced; what replaces it is the silence of lōʾ hēdād (no shout). The agricultural calendar of joy — harvest festivals, communal celebration — is extinguished. Cf. Joel 1:11-12 (similar lament over agricultural devastation). Joy and gladness (śimḥâ wāgîl) are YHWH's gifts that can be withdrawn.
48.34: Mizzĕʿaqat Ḥešbôn ʿad-ʾĔlāʿēlēh ʿad-Yahaz nātĕnû qôlām miṣṣōʿar ʿad-Ḥōrônāyim ʿeglat šĕlîšiyyâ kî gam-mê Nimrîm lĭmšammôt yihyû (kî gam-mê Nimrîm lĭmšammôt yihyû): the sweep of the cry from Heshbon to Elealeh (NE of Heshbon), to Jahaz, Zoar (near the S end of the Dead Sea), to Horonaim — this geographic traversal names the major northsouth axis of Moab. ʿeglat šĕlîšiyyâ ("like a three-year-old heifer's bellow") — the bellowing of a fully grown, unsacrificed heifer is the image for the loudness of the cry. Even the waters of Nimrim (probably the Wadi Nimrin, north of the Dead Sea) will be dried up — the fundamental resources of water and agriculture fail together.
48.35: Wĕhišbattî lĕMôʾāḇ nĕʾum-YHWH maʿăleh bāmâ wĕmaqṭîr lēʾlōhāyw (Wĕhišbattî lĕMôʾāḇ... maʿăleh bāmâ wĕmaqṭîr lēʾlōhāyw): YHWH will put an end in Moab to anyone who brings offerings to the high places (bāmôt) and burns incense to his gods (lēʾlōhāyw). The Moabite high places and their cult are the specific target of divine abolition — the same judgment language used for Israel's syncretistic worship (1 Kings 11:7; 2 Kings 23:13-14). Chemosh was worshiped at high places throughout Moab; their elimination marks the end of Moab's national-religious identity.
48.36: Lāḵēn libbî lĕMôʾāḇ kaḥălîlîm yĕhemeh ûlibbî ʾel-ʾanšê Qîr-ḥereś kaḥălîlîm yĕhemeh (libbî lĕMôʾāḇ kaḥălîlîm yĕhemeh): "my heart wails for Moab like flutes" — the divine heart (lēḇ) wailing like funeral flutes (ḥălîlîm, pipe-flutes used at funerals; Matt 9:23 refers to flute-players at death) is the OT expression of divine compassion within judgment. The double repetition ("my heart wails for Moab like flutes... my heart wails like flutes for the men of Kir-hareseth") creates a liturgical lament. ʿal-kēn yitrat ʿāśâ ʾāḇādû (yitrat ʿāśâ ʾāḇādû): "for the riches they gathered have perished" — the accumulated wealth that Moab trusted (v7: biṭḥēḵ bĕmaʿăśayiḵ ûḇĕʾôṣĕrôtêḵ) is gone.
48.37: Kî ḵol-rōʾš qorḥâ wĕḵol-zāqān gĕrûʿâ ʿal-kol-yādāyim gĕdûdōt ûʿal-mŏtnāyim śāq (kî ḵol-rōʾš qorḥâ wĕḵol-zāqān gĕrûʿâ): the comprehensive mourning rites — every head shaved (qorḥâ), every beard cut (gĕrûʿâ), gashes on every hand (gĕdûdōt), sackcloth around every waist — enumerate all four of the standard ancient Near Eastern mourning gestures (cf. Isa 15:2-3; Ezek 27:30-31; Amos 8:10). The universal coverage ("every" repeated four times) signals that Moab's entire population is in mourning — no exceptions, no exemptions.
48.38: ʿAl-kol-gaggôt Môʾāḇ ûbirḥōḇōtêhā kulloh misped kî šāḇartî ʾet-Môʾāḇ kikĕlî ʾên-ḥēpeṣ bô nĕʾum-YHWH (šāḇartî ʾet-Môʾāḇ kikĕlî ʾên-ḥēpeṣ bô): YHWH's first-person claim: "I have shattered Moab like a pot no one wants" — kĕlî ʾên-ḥēpeṣ (vessel in which there is no pleasure/desire) is a broken, worthless piece of pottery. The same image of the worthless broken vessel appears in Hos 8:8 ("Israel is swallowed up; among the nations he is like a vessel no one wants") and Ps 31:12. The pottery imagery throughout Jeremiah (1:10; 18-19; 32; 45) reaches its final OAN expression here: Moab, like the nations, like Jerusalem, is clay in the potter YHWH's hands — broken when he chooses.
48.39: Êḵ ḥattâ hêlîlû êḵ-hipnâ ʿōrep Môʾāḇ bôš wĕhāyâ Môʾāḇ lĭśĕḥōq ûlĕmaḥittâ lĕḵol-sĕḇîḇāyw (êḵ-hipnâ ʿōrep Môʾāḇ bôš): the lament (êḵ ḥattâ hêlîlû, "how it is shattered — wail!") and the specific humiliation — Moab turned its back (ʿōrep) in shame. The same vocabulary used for Israel's apostasy (2:27; 32:33: "they turned their back to me, not their face") applies to Moab's military flight. The turning of the back in battle is both physical retreat and symbolic rejection — shame embodied in posture. lĭśĕḥōq ûlĕmaḥittâ (a laughingstock and a terror) — the reversal of Moab's own contemptuous mockery of Israel (v27).
48.40: Kî ḵōh ʾāmar YHWH hinnēh kaṇṇešer yidʾeh ûpāraś kĕnāpāyw ʾel-Môʾāḇ (hinnēh kaṇṇešer yidʾeh ûpāraś kĕnāpāyw ʾel-Môʾāḇ): the eagle simile for the conqueror — YHWH (or Nebuchadnezzar as YHWH's agent) swoops like a nešer (eagle/vulture, the largest and most powerful raptor of the ANE) spreading wings over Moab. The same image appears for Babylon's invasion of Judah (Ezek 17:3: "a great eagle came to Lebanon"; Hab 1:8: "their horses fly like eagles") and for Edom (Jer 49:22; Lam 4:19). The eagle/vulture embodies the irreversible, airborne speed of military conquest.
48.41: Nilkĕdâ haqqĕriyyôt wĕhammĕṣādôt niltāḥû wĕhāyâ lēḇ gibborê Môʾāḇ bayyôm hahûʾ kĕlēḇ ʾiššâ mĕṣērâ (wĕhāyâ lēḇ gibborê Môʾāḇ bayyôm hahûʾ kĕlēḇ ʾiššâ mĕṣērâ): Kerioth and the fortresses taken; the hearts of Moab's warriors are like the heart of a woman in labor (mĕṣērâ, the one in labor-pain). The labor-pain simile for terror and helplessness recurs throughout the OAN (46:24; 49:22-24; 50:43) and Israel's own judgment (4:31; 13:21; 22:23; 30:6) — it crosses gender boundaries to describe absolute vulnerability and loss of control.
48.42: Wĕniśmad Môʾāḇ mēʿam kî ʿal-YHWH higdîl (kî ʿal-YHWH higdîl): the sin summarized — "Moab will be destroyed as a nation because he exalted himself against YHWH." The causal kî ʿal-YHWH higdîl (because he magnified himself against YHWH) is the oracle's theological thesis. This is the same sin identified in Babylon's judgment (50:24, 29, 31-32), in the king of Babylon's taunt (Isa 14:13-14), and in Nebuchadnezzar's narrative (Dan 4:30). National hubris against YHWH's sovereignty is the universal grounds for judgment in the prophetic literature.
48.43: Paḥad wāpaḥat wāpāḥ ʿālêḵā yôšēḇ Môʾāḇ nĕʾum-YHWH (Paḥad wāpaḥat wāpāḥ ʿālêḵā yôšēḇ Môʾāḇ): the alliterative triple of terror — paḥad (terror, dread), paḥat (pit), and pāḥ (trap/snare) — three p/ph sounds creating the acoustic sensation of inescapable disaster. Isa 24:17 uses the identical formula (paḥad wāpaḥat wāpāḥ) for the universal judgment of the earth in the apocalyptic oracle. Jeremiah's use here intensifies the scope: what Isaiah applied to the whole earth, Jeremiah applies to Moab as a national representative of the principle.
48.44: Hannās mippĕnê happāḥad yippōl ʾel-happāḥat wĕhaʿōleh min-happāḥat yillāḵēd bappāḥ kî ʾāḇîʾ ʾēlêhā ʾel-Môʾāḇ šĕnat pĕquddātām nĕʾum-YHWH (Hannās mippĕnê happāḥad yippōl ʾel-happāḥat): the three-word sequence from v43 becomes a three-stage scenario — the one who flees terror (pāḥad) falls into the pit (paḥat); the one who climbs out of the pit is caught in the trap (pāḥ). No escape route exists: flight leads to the pit, climbing out leads to the snare. This is the logic of inescapable divine judgment (cf. Amos 5:19: "as if a man fled from a lion and a bear met him, or went into the house and a serpent bit him"). šĕnat pĕquddātām (the year of their reckoning) — YHWH's appointed time of visitation, the same phrase used for Egypt's judgment (46:21).
48.45: Bĕṣēl Ḥešbôn ʿāmĕdû mēḵōaḥ nāsîm kî-ʾēš yāṣāʾ mēḤešbôn wĕlehāḇâ mibbên Sîḥôn wattōʾḵal peʾat Môʾāḇ ûqodqod bĕnê-šāʾôn (kî-ʾēš yāṣāʾ mēḤešbôn wĕlehāḇâ mibbên Sîḥôn): an ancient poem about the Amorite king Sihon from Numbers 21:27-29 is cited — fire from Heshbon and flame from Sihon's midst devouring Moab. The citation of the old war-poem integrates the ancient tradition of Moab's vulnerability: the prophetic judgment is not new but the culmination of a long history of conflict. Sihon (bên Sîḥôn, within/from Sihon) was the Amorite king who defeated Moab before being himself defeated by Israel (Num 21:21-30). History's wheel turns through YHWH's sovereign direction.
48.46: Ôy-lĕḵā Môʾāḇ ʾāḇad ʿam Kĕmôš kî luqqĕḥû bānêḵā baššĕḇî ûḇĕnōtêḵā baššiḇyâ (ʾāḇad ʿam Kĕmôš): "the people of Chemosh are destroyed" — the identification of Moab's national identity with Chemosh (ʿam Kĕmôš, people of Chemosh) echoes Numbers 21:29: "Woe to you, Moab! You are ruined, O people of Chemosh." The oracle cites the ancient lament verbatim, completing the literary inclusio: what ancient poets saw in Moab's defeat by Sihon, Jeremiah now announces as YHWH's final judgment. kî luqqĕḥû bānêḵā baššĕḇî ûḇĕnōtêḵā baššiḇyâ: sons captured, daughters led captive — the social destruction of the family, the dismantling of future generations.
48.47: Wĕšaḇtî šĕḇût-Môʾāḇ bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm nĕʾum-YHWH ʿad-hēnnâ mišpaṭ Môʾāḇ (Wĕšaḇtî šĕḇût-Môʾāḇ bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm): the chapter ends with an eschatological restoration footnote — "Yet in the latter days I will restore the fortunes of Moab, declares YHWH." šāḇ šĕḇût (restore the fortunes) is the restoration formula used for Israel (30:3; 31:23; 32:44; 33:7, 11, 26) and here extended to Moab, Ammon (49:6), and Egypt (46:26). The inclusion of Moab's future restoration within the judgment oracle is consistent with YHWH's universal redemptive horizon — judgment is not the last word even for Moab. Paul's universalism in Romans 11:32 ("God has consigned all to disobedience so that he may have mercy on all") resonates with this trajectory: the same divine sovereignty that judges is the sovereignty that restores. ʿad-hēnnâ mišpaṭ Môʾāḇ: "This is the end of the judgment of Moab" — a formal closure formula marking the oracle's completion.

51.1: hinnĕnî mēʿîr ʿal-Bābel... rûaḥ mašḥît — "See, I am raising up against Babylon... a destructive wind (rûaḥ mašḥît)." The "destructive wind" or "ruining spirit" inverts the creation vocabulary: in Gen 1:2, the divine rûaḥ brings order from chaos; here the divine rûaḥ returns a proud civilization to chaos. Lēb-qāmay (heart of those who rise against me) — a rare Hebrew phrase used only here and 51:41 (Šēšak), suggesting literary intentionality in the OAN's opening.
51.2: wĕšillaḥtî lĕBābel zārîm ûzērûhā — "I will send against Babylon winnowers (zārîm) who will winnow her and strip her land bare." The winnowing image (grain separated from chaff by wind) picks up the wind vocabulary of v.1: YHWH's destructive wind is the winnowing wind that separates Babylon's substance from what remains. ʿAlêhā missābîb bĕyôm rāʿâ — "against her from every side on the day of disaster."
51.3: The call to Babylon's enemies to draw their bows — the military imagery begins the siege oracle. The verse's syntax is difficult (MT has a command that could be read as either addressed to Babylon's defenders or her attackers); the MKT follows the reading that addresses Babylon's enemies: "let the archer draw against him who strings his bow." The command not to spare her youth (ʿōlāmîm) marks the comprehensive judgment.
51.4: The result: slain fall in the land of the Chaldeans (ʾerets Kaśdîm), run through in her streets. The geographic specification "land of the Chaldeans" — Babylon's own territory — is the ironic reversal: the nation that filled other lands with corpses fills its own land with corpses. The MKT renders the two parallel forms: ḥālāl (slain/pierced by sword) and dāqûr (thrust through/run through).
51.5: kî lōʾ-ʾalmān Yiśrāʾēl wîhûdâ mēʾĕlōhāyw — "For Israel and Judah have not been abandoned (lōʾ ʾalmān) by their God." ʾAlmān (widowed/forsaken) — the word for a husband-less widow, applied to the nation. Israel and Judah are not god-less/husband-less despite the exile. The contrast: their land is filled with guilt (māšĕʾû ʾet-ʾarṣām bĕʾāšmāh), but the covenant relationship persists. The MKT renders "abandoned" to convey the abandonment-of-covenant-partner meaning without the gender-specific "widowed."
51.6: nûsû mĕtôk Bābel ûmalĕṭû ʾîš napšô — "Flee from the heart of Babylon! Let each person save his life!" The first exodus-call in the OAN (cf. v.45). Al-tiḏḏammû baʿăwōnāh — "do not be cut off in her punishment." The call to the exiles to flee Babylon before judgment is a pastoral command embedded in an oracle of judgment. The MKT renders "save his life" literally: napšô = his soul/life.
51.7: kôs zāhāb Bābel bĕyad-YHWH — "Babylon was a gold cup in the LORD's hand that made the whole earth drunk." The gold cup image: Babylon was YHWH's instrument for pouring out wrath on the nations (cf. 25:15-17 where Jeremiah gives all nations the cup of wrath). The nations drank from YHWH's cup through Babylon and became mad (hōlĕlû). Now the cup is overturned and Babylon herself drinks. The MKT preserves "gold cup" — the opulence of the instrument matches Babylon's own self-understanding.
51.8: pitta'ôm nāpĕlâ Bābel wattišbar — "Suddenly Babylon has fallen and is shattered!" The word pittaʾôm (suddenly) emphasizes the unexpected speed of the fall. "Wail for her! Bring balm for her wound — perhaps she can be healed." The futile healing attempt (qĕḥû ṣĕrî lĕmaḵʾōbāh ʾûlay tērāpēʾ) deliberately echoes 8:22 (the balm of Gilead) but now applied to Babylon — and the answer is that she cannot be healed.
51.9: rippînû ʾet-Bābel wĕlōʾ nirtāpâ — "We tried to heal Babylon but she could not be healed." The nations who tried to administer balm to Babylon abandon the effort. ʿIzĕbûhā ûnēlēk ʾîš lĕʾarṣô — "Leave her behind — let each of us return to his own land." The scattering of the nations is the reversal of Babylon's gathering of them. The judgment reaches to the heavens (nāgaʿ kî-ʾel-haššāmayim mišpāṭāh) and is lifted up to the skies (niśśāʾ ʿad-šĕḥāqîm).
51.10: hôṣîʾ YHWH ʾet-ṣidqōtênû — "The LORD has established our acquittal (ṣidqōtênû)." The legal vocabulary: ṣedeq (righteousness/vindication) in the plural — multiple acts of covenant-right brought out for display. The exiles' proclamation: "Come, let us announce in Zion what the LORD our God has done." The announcement in Zion means the Zion-community are already envisioning their return. The MKT's "acquittal" captures the forensic dimension of ṣedeq here.
51.11: bārû haḥiṣṣîm malĕʾû haššĕlāṭîm — "Polish the arrows! Fill the quivers!" The battle preparation commands. ʾōrêr YHWH ʾet-rûaḥ malkê māday — "The LORD has roused the resolve (rûaḥ) of the kings of the Medes." YHWH stirs the spirit of the Medes — Media was historically involved in Babylon's fall (though the combined Medo-Persian empire under Cyrus was the historical instrument). The purpose: ʿal-miqdāšô — to take vengeance for his sanctuary/temple. The MKT renders "the resolve of the kings of the Medes" — rûaḥ here is the driving motivation.
51.12: The military preparation verbs in rapid sequence: raise the standard (śĕʾû nēs), reinforce the watch (haḥĕzîqû hammiš mār), post sentries (hāqîmû šōmĕrîm), prepare the ambushes (hākînû hāʾōrĕbîm). Four preparation-commands addressed to Babylon's enemies. The series creates the urgency of imminent military action. The MKT renders all four commands distinctly rather than combining them.
51.13: šōkenât ʿal-mayim rabbîm rabbat ʾôtsĕrôt — "You who live beside great waters and are loaded with treasures — your end has come." Babylon sat beside the Euphrates and the network of Mesopotamian canals — "great waters" was literal. The phrase ammâ bitsṣiʿātēk qēts bitsṣîʿātēk — "the cord of your existence is cut" — bitstsîʿāh (covetousness/unjust gain) — the word translated both as "existence" and "unjust gain" creates an ambiguity: the cord of her life is cut at the point of her covetous accumulation. The MKT follows the contextual reading.
51.14: nišbaʿ YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt bĕnapšô — "The LORD of hosts has sworn by himself." YHWH swears by his own nepeš (himself, his soul/being) — the highest oath form (cf. Amos 6:8; Jer 44:26). Kî ʾim-mālēʾtîkā ʾādām kayyāleq — "I will certainly fill you with men like a locust swarm." The locust swarm (yāleq) is a metaphor for an irresistible overwhelming force. The MKT renders the self-oath: "sworn by himself."
51.15: ʿōśēh ʾerets bĕkōḥô — the Creator-hymn begins, verbatim parallel to 10:12-16. "He made the earth by his power; he established the world by his wisdom and stretched out the heavens by his understanding." Placed here, the hymn of the Creator-God contrasts with Bel/Marduk, the Babylonian creator-god of the Enuma Elish. YHWH's creative power vindicates his power to destroy Babylon. The MKT follows the parallel with ch. 10 exactly.
51.16: The Creator-hymn continued: the storm-God who controls lightning and rain — wĕnōśēʾ nĕśîʾîm (lifting up clouds from the ends of the earth), lightning, wind from storehouses. The cosmic phenomena respond to YHWH's voice (lĕqôl tittô hamôn mayim baššāmayim). The Creator's sovereignty over nature grounds his sovereignty over nations. The MKT renders the parallel with 10:13 consistently.
51.17: The refrain from ch. 10 on idols: nibaʿar kol-ʾādām midaʿat — "Every person is too ignorant to understand this" — too ignorant to see that the Creator-God has power that idols lack. Bōš kol-ṣōrēp mippāsel — every goldsmith shamed by his idol. The "cast image is a fraud" (šeqer ûnepeš ʾên bāhem) — no breath/life in the idol. The MKT follows the 10:14 parallel.
51.18: Hébel hēmmâ maʿăśēh taʿtuʿîm — "They are a sham — the product of deception (taʿtuʿîm)." Taʿtuʿîm (mockery, delusion, deception) — a rare word intensifying the idol's fraudulence. The judgment: "when their day of judgment (ʿēt pĕquddātām) comes, they will be destroyed." The same day that destroys Babylon will destroy the idols Babylon trusted. The MKT renders the parallel with 10:15 exactly.
51.19: lōʾ-kĕʾēlleh ḥēleq Yaʿăqōb — "The Portion of Jacob is not like these." Ḥēleq Yaʿăqōb — the "Portion/Share of Jacob" is YHWH (cf. Ps 16:5). The contrast with the idols: YHWH is the maker of everything; idols are made by human hands. Yiśrāʾēl šēbeṭ naḥălātô — "Israel is the tribe that belongs to him as his inheritance." The reciprocal possession: YHWH is Jacob's portion; Israel is YHWH's portion. The MKT follows the parallel with 10:16 exactly.
51.20: mappēts ʾattâ-lî kĕlê milḥāmâ — "You are my battle-axe and weapon of war." The oracle's addressee has been debated: is it Babylon (YHWH's weapon against the nations, now being turned) or Israel (whom YHWH will use against Babylon)? The MKT follows the MT ambiguity. The anaphoric "with you I break" begins: wĕnippaṣtî bĕkā gôyim... mamlākôt — nations, kingdoms — the comprehensive beginning. The first of seven hammer-blow pairs.
51.21: The second and third pairs of the "I will break" anaphora: horse and rider (sûs wĕrōkĕbô); chariot and driver (rekeb wĕrōkĕbô). The military pairs — horse/chariot — address the cavalry and armored forces. The MKT preserves the exact parallelism; the anaphora's rhythmic accumulation is essential to the oracle's rhetorical force.
51.22: The fourth pair: man and woman (ʾîš wĕʾiššâ); fifth: old and young (zāqēn wĕnaʿar); sixth: young man and young woman (bāḥûr wĕbĕtûlâ). The pairs move from combatants to civilians to demographic groups — the judgment is comprehensive across all social categories. The MKT renders each pair distinctly to preserve the anaphora's comprehensive-catalog function.
51.23: The seventh pair: shepherd and flock (rōʿeh wĕʿedrô); plus the farmer and his team (ʾikkār wĕṣimḥô); and governors and commanders (paḥôt ûsĕgānîm). Three final pairs completing the sevenfold sequence — the economic, agricultural, and political infrastructure all broken. The seven pairs constitute a complete destruction oracle covering military, social, demographic, pastoral, agricultural, and administrative life. The MKT renders all three pairs of v.23 to complete the anaphora.
51.24: wĕšillamtî lĕBābel ûlĕkol yôšĕbê Kaśdîm ʾēt kol-rāʿātām — "I will pay back Babylon and all who live in Chaldea for everything they have done wrong in Zion." The pivot: Babylon was the battle-axe that broke the nations (vv. 20-23), but now Babylon receives payback for what it did in Zion specifically. Lĕʿênêkem — "and you will see it." The exiles will witness the repayment for their suffering. The personal you grounds the oracle in pastoral promise to the exiles.
51.25: hinnî ʾēlêkā har hammašḥît — "I am against you, O mountain of ruin (har hammašḥît), declares the LORD." Mašḥît (the destroyer/ruiner) — the mountain that ruins is named by what it does. YHWH will stretch out his hand (nāṭîtî yādî ʿālêkā) and roll Babylon down from the rocks (wĕgillalltîkā min-haṣṣĕlāʿîm), making her a burnt mountain (har śĕrēpâ). The volcanic/burned-out mountain image for Babylon — the destroyer made desolate.
51.26: wĕlōʾ-yiqqĕḥû mimmĕkā ʾeben lĕpinnâ wĕʾeben lĕmôsādôt — "No stone from you will be used for a cornerstone or a foundation — you will be a ruin forever." A striking archaeological prediction: Babylon's stones are so cursed they will not be repurposed. The verse echoes the reversal of building imagery: Babylon the empire-builder ends as unusable rubble even for basic construction.
51.27: The battle-standard raised in the earth; nations summoned against Babylon: haqdîšû ʿālêhā gôyim hazmîʿû ʿālêhā — consecrate/prepare nations against her. Ararat, Minni, and Ashkenaz — nations around the Armenian highlands, historically associated with the Medes. The listing of specific national confederates gives the oracle a geopolitical specificity. The MKT follows the MT's geographical names without modernizing them.
51.28: The enumeration of Babylon's attackers continues — "the kings of the Medes, with their governors and all their officials, and all the land of their dominion." The exhaustive listing (wĕkōl ʾerets memšaltām) emphasizes the pan-national coalition YHWH will assemble against Babylon. The Medes specifically named three times in vv. 11, 27, 28 suggests historical awareness of their role.
51.29: wattirʿaš hāʾārets wattāḥûl — "The land shakes and quakes." The cosmic trembling at YHWH's plans taking effect. Kî qāmâ ʿal-Bābel maḥšĕbôt YHWH — "for the LORD's plans against Babylon hold firm." Māḥšĕbôt — the same word as 29:11 (YHWH's plans for welfare) — here YHWH's plans are for Babylon's desolation. YHWH's purposeful planning governs both restoration and judgment. The MKT renders "hold firm" to convey the unalterable nature of the divine plans.
51.30: The demoralization of Babylon's army: they stop fighting (ḥādĕlû gibbôrê Bābel lĕhillāḥēm), sit in their strongholds, become women (hāyû lĕnāšîm — the ancient idiom for losing courage). Nišrĕpû miškanotêhā nišbĕrû bĕrîḥêhā — "her dwelling places are set ablaze, her gate bars broken." The internal collapse mirrors the external assault.
51.31: rāts qārēʾ liqrāʾt-rāts — "One runner runs to meet another, one messenger to meet the next." The rapid-fire cascade of bad news reaching the king — runner after runner bringing successive reports of the city's fall. Wayyaggēd lĕmelek Bābel kî nitkĕdâ ʿîrô miqḳāṣeh — "that his city has been captured from end to end." The MKT preserves the doubling of the runner image as the literary marker of panicked report.
51.32: The specific siege details: river crossings seized (hanĕʿābārôt nittpĕśû), reed thickets set on fire (wĕʾet-hāʾăgammîm śārĕpû bāʾēš), soldiers in a panic (wĕʾanšê hammilḥāmâ nibhālû). The burning of the reed thickets — Babylon's reed marshes — eliminates hiding places for refugees. The MKT follows the MT's three-element description of the city's fall.
51.33: bat-Bābel kĕgōren ʿēt hidrikāh — "Daughter Babylon is like a threshing floor at the time of treading." The threshing floor and harvest imagery: Babylon's time of judgment is harvest time, when the grain is threshed. ʿôd meʿaṭ ûbāʾâ ʾet-qatsîr lāh — "in just a little while, her harvest time will come." The temporal marker "just a little while" (meʿaṭ) creates urgency: judgment is imminent.
51.34: ʾăkālānî hĕmāmānî Nĕbûkadreʾṣar — "Nebuchadrezzar the king of Babylon has eaten me up and crushed me." The personified Zion speaks — describing Babylon's consumption of Jerusalem in vivid digestive imagery: eaten (ʾăkālānî), crushed (hĕmāmānî), set me aside as an empty jar (hissîgānî kiklî rîq), swallowed like a monster (bĕlaʿānî kattannîn), filled his belly with my delicacies (millēʾ kĕrēśô mimmĕʿaddannāy). The five verbs of consumption make Babylon the cosmic devourer. The MKT preserves all five verbs of consumption.
51.35: ḥāmāsî ûšĕʾērî ʿal-Bābel tōʾmar yōšebet Ṣiyyôn — "'The violence done to me and my family be upon Babylon,' the people of Zion will say." Zion's imprecation on Babylon: ḥāmāsî (my violence suffered) and šĕʾērî (my flesh/family) — both the abstract injustice and the concrete family losses. The blood-guilt (dāmî) to be laid on the Chaldeans. The MKT preserves "the people of Zion will say" — making this a communal imprecation, not just an individual complaint.
51.36: lākēn kōh-ʾāmar YHWH hinnĕnî-rāb ʾet-rîbēk — "Therefore this is what the LORD says: I will defend your case (rāb ʾet-rîbēk) and take vengeance on your behalf." YHWH as covenant advocate — rîb (legal contest, lawsuit) is Jeremiah's covenant-lawsuit vocabulary. YHWH takes up Zion's cause. Wĕhôbaštî ʾet-yammāh wĕhôbaštî ʾet-māqôrāh — "I will drain her sea and dry up her spring." The sea/spring dried up — Babylon's water-supply severed.
51.37: The desolation vocabulary accumulates: heap of rubble (gal), den for jackals (mĕʿôn tannîm), horror (šammâ), scorn (šĕrēqâ), no one living there (mēʾên yôšēb). The five desolation terms are the comprehensive vocabulary of complete uninhabitability. The MKT renders all five to maintain the comprehensive force of the desolation oracle.
51.38: The lion-roar image: they roar together like lions (yaḥdāw kaʾărāyôt yišʾāgû), snarl like young lions (naʿărû kĕgôrê ʾărāyôt). The image may describe Babylon's soldiers at their feast before their fatal stupor (vv. 39-40), or Babylon's pride before the fall. The lion was Babylon's symbol (cf. the Ishtar Gate's lions). The MKT preserves both lion terms: ʾărāyôt and gôrê ʾărāyôt.
51.39: bĕḥummām ʾāśîm ʾet-mištêhem — "While they are roused and hot, I will set a feast for them and make them drunk so that they celebrate — then sleep a permanent sleep and never wake up." The divine banquet of death: YHWH sets the feast that becomes the soldiers' last meal. The word wĕyāšĕnû šĕnat-ʿôlām wĕlōʾ yāqîṣû (sleep an eternal sleep and not awake) — sleep and death converge. Historical tradition holds that Babylon fell while its soldiers were feasting.
51.40: The execution imagery: brought down like lambs to the slaughter (kĕkārim lĕṭābaḥ), like rams and male goats (kĕʾêlîm ʿim-ʿattûdîm). The sacrificial vocabulary applied to Babylon's warriors — they are sacrificial animals, not combatants. The MKT renders "lambs to the slaughter" — the image will resonate in the NT (Acts 8:32; Isa 53:7 background).
51.41: ʾêk nilĕkĕdâ Šēšak ûtittāpaś tĕhillat kol-hāʾārets — "How Sheshach has been captured! How the celebrated city of all the earth has been seized!" Šēšak is the atbash cipher for Bābel (Babylon) — the Hebrew letter substitution code where bêt=šin, bêt=šin, lamed=kap. This cryptic form appears also in 25:26. Whether it was a literary device, a genuine code for the exiles, or a scribal tradition, the MKT renders it as "Sheshach" with the note that this is Babylon. Tĕhillat kol-hāʾārets — "the celebrated/praised city of all the earth" — Babylon's own self-designation now applied ironically in her fall.
51.42: ʿālâ ʿal-Bābel hayyām bĕhāmôn gallāyw nitkaSSĕtâ — "The sea has surged over Babylon; she is swamped by its roaring waves." The sea of invading armies (hayyām) as a cosmic force overwhelming Babylon. Babylon, which built beside the great waters, is now drowned by them. The MKT renders "roaring waves" for hāmôn gallāyw (the noise/roar of its waves) — both the sound and the force.
51.43: The cities of Babylon become a desolation — hāyû ʿārêhā lĕšammâ ʾerets ṣiyyâ ûmidbar — "a wasteland, a parched and desert land." The urban-to-desert transformation is the anti-Eden: the civilization that built cities and gardens (Nebuchadnezzar's famous gardens) reverts to waterless wilderness. ʾErets ʾăšer lōʾ-yēšeb bāhen kol-ʾîš — "a land where nobody lives, where no one passes through."
51.44: ûpāqadtî ʿal-Bēl bĕBābel — "I will call Bel of Babylon to account (pāqad)." Bel (= Marduk, Babylon's chief god) is subjected to divine prosecution — the same pāqad (punish/attend to) used for human judgment. Wĕhôṣēʾtî ʾet-bĕlîʿô mippîw — "I will make him disgorge what he has swallowed." The nations Babylon swallowed (cf. v.34's digestive imagery) will be expelled from Bel's mouth. Wĕlōʾ-yinnāhărû ʾēlāyw ʿôd gôyim — the nations will no longer stream to him in worship: Marduk's cultic dominance ended.
51.45: ṣĕʾû mĕtôkāh ʿammî — "Come out from her, my people!" The second and climactic exodus-call in the chapter. ʿAmmî — "my people" — the covenant-address identifying the exiles as still YHWH's own even in Babylon. Ûmalĕṭû ʾîš ʾet-napšô mēḥărôn ʾap-YHWH — "let everyone escape to save his life from the LORD's burning anger." The urgency is pastoral: the call is addressed to the exiles who must flee before the judgment overtakes them. The MKT renders "Come out from her, my people" with the possessive "my" to preserve the covenant-identification.
51.46: wĕʾal-yērak lĕbabkem wĕʾal-tîrĕʾû baššĕmûʿâ — "Do not lose heart or be afraid at the rumors that spread through the land." The pastoral word to the exiles: the swirl of conflicting reports in vv. 46b ("when one report comes in one year and another in the next") can generate fear and confusion. The command: do not become soft-hearted (yārēk lēb) — the same hardness-of-heart language in reverse. The MKT renders "lose heart" for yārēk lĕbabkem (your heart becoming soft).
51.47: lākēn hinnēh-yāmîm bāʾîm — "Therefore the days are surely coming when I will judge Babylon's idols." The judgment on the idols (pĕsîlê Bābel) is the theological climax: not just political defeat but the exposure of Babylon's gods as powerless. Wĕkol-ʾarṣāh tēbōš — "and her whole land will be disgraced." The disgrace spreads from the idols to the whole land — the gods' shame is the nation's shame.
51.48: wĕrinnĕnû ʿal-Bābel šāmayim wāʾārets wĕkol ʾăšer bāhem — "Then the heavens and the earth and everything in them will shout for joy over Babylon." The cosmic rejoicing — heaven and earth joining human praise — is the eschatological celebration of YHWH's justice. Kî mitstsāpôn yābōʾ-lāh haššōdĕdîm — "for her destroyers will come from the north." The north has been the source of Babylon's enemies since ch. 1. The circle is complete: the foe from the north (1:14) was Babylon; now the foe from the north will be Babylon's destroyer.
51.49: gam-Bābel linpōl ḥallĕlê Yiśrāʾēl — "Babylon must fall for the sake of Israel's slain." The forensic rationale: blood-justice requires that the slayer of the many be slain. Gam-lĕBābel nāpĕlû ḥallĕlê kol-hāʾārets — "just as at Babylon the slain of all the earth have fallen." The double gam (also/just as) creates the blood-for-blood symmetry. Babylon was the executor of YHWH's judgment on many nations; now Babylon must fall as the accumulated blood-debt is paid.
51.50: pĕlêṭê hereb halĕkû ʾal-taʿamdû — "You who have escaped the sword, go — do not linger!" A third exodus-call, addressed specifically to those who have survived. Zikrû meraḥôq ʾet-YHWH wîrûšālaim tāʿăleh ʿal-lĕbabkem — "Remember the LORD from far off, and keep Jerusalem in your thoughts." The geographic distance (exile in Babylon) does not end the obligation to remember YHWH and Jerusalem. The MKT renders "keep Jerusalem in your thoughts" — tāʿăleh ʿal-lĕbabkem (let it rise on your heart).
51.51: The communal confession of shame: bōšnû kî-šāmaʿnû ḥerpâ — "We are disgraced, for we have heard insults; shame has covered our faces, because foreigners have entered the sacred rooms of the LORD's temple." The violation of the temple — bāʾû zārîm ʿal-miqdĕšê bêt YHWH (foreigners entered the sanctuary of YHWH's house) — is the ultimate covenant humiliation. The shame is communal: "we are disgraced... shame has covered our faces." The MKT renders the communal first-person voice.
51.52: lākēn hinnēh-yāmîm bāʾîm — the "days are coming" formula for a second time in this section (cf. v.47). "When I will call her idols to account, and throughout her whole land the wounded will groan." The repetition of the idol-judgment with the addition of the groaning wounded (ḥālāl) deepens the picture. The MKT preserves the yāmîm bāʾîm formula as a distinct unit from v.47.
51.53: kî taʿăleh Bābel haššāmayim — "Even if Babylon were to climb up to heaven and reinforce her towering heights, destroyers would still come against her." The hyperbolic impossibility: no height is too great for YHWH's judgment. The image may allude to the Babel tower narrative (Gen 11) — the ancient Babylonian ambition to reach heaven. The MKT renders the conditional: "even if... destroyers would still."
51.54: The sound oracle: qôl zĕʿāqâ mibbābel (a cry from Babylon) and šeber gādôl mēʾerets kaśdîm (a sound of great ruin from the land of the Chaldeans). The acoustic announcement of Babylon's fall before the visual description. The MKT renders both sound-words: cry (zĕʿāqâ) and ruin-sound (šeber) — each with its own register.
51.55: kî-šōdēd YHWH ʾet-Bābel wĕʾibbad mimmennāh qôl gādôl — "For the LORD is destroying Babylon and silencing her loud voice." The silencing of the great voice: Babylon's voice (military command, cultural dominance, religious pronouncement) is extinguished by YHWH. The waves of the attackers (galbêhem) roar like many waters while Babylon's voice is silenced. The contrast — the LORD's agents roar; Babylon falls silent.
51.56: kî bāʾ ʿālêhā ʿal-Bābel šōdēd — "For a destroyer has come against her — against Babylon." Warriors taken prisoner, bows broken: kî ʾēl gĕmullôt YHWH šallēm yĕšallēm — "for the LORD is a God of retribution; he will fully repay." ʾĒl gĕmullôt — the God of requital/retribution. The divine name as retributive judge. The MKT renders "a God of retribution" — gĕmullôt (repayments, acts of requital) — and "fully repay" for the infinitive absolute (šallēm yĕšallēm).
51.57: wĕhiššakartî śārêhā waḥăkāmêhā paḥotêhā ûsĕgānêhā wĕgibbôrêhā — "I will make her officials and her wise men drunk — her governors, commanders, and soldiers." The banquet-of-death motif (cf. v.39) — YHWH makes Babylon's leadership drunk, leading to permanent sleep. Wĕyāšĕnû šĕnat-ʿôlām wĕlōʾ yāqîṣû nĕʾum-hammelek YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt šĕmô — sleep an eternal sleep and not awake: the divine oracle sealed with the most complete divine title: "declares the King, whose name is the LORD of hosts."
51.58: kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt ḥômôt Bābel hārābâ ʿarattōr tittĕʿararnā — "Thus says the LORD of hosts: Babylon's wide walls will be demolished to the ground." The famous double walls of Babylon (Herodotus describes them as over 80 feet wide) — now declared worthless. "Her towering gates will be set on fire." The closing line: wĕyiggĕʿû ʿammîm bĕdê-rîq ûlĕʾummîm bĕdê-ʾēš wĕyāʿēpû — "the peoples will labor in vain and nations will exhaust themselves for nothing." The Hab 2:13 parallel: all human empire-building is exhausting futility before YHWH.
51.59: haddābār ʾăšer ṣiwwâ Yirmĕyāhû hannābîʾ ʾet-Śĕrāyāhû ben-Nĕriyyāhû — the Seraiah appendix. Seraiah son of Neriah — the brother of Baruch (both sons of Neriah son of Mahseiah). Jeremiah entrusts an oracle to the prophet's sibling for the same kind of scroll-mission that Baruch performed in Jerusalem. The occasion: Zedekiah's fourth year — the same year as the yoke-oracle of ch. 28 and the letter to the exiles of ch. 29. The MKT preserves śar mĕnûḥâ (quartermaster) as Seraiah's title.
51.60: wayyiktōb Yirmĕyāhû ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ ʾăšer-tābōʾ ʾel-Bābel — "Jeremiah wrote in one scroll all the calamity that would come upon Babylon — all of these words about Babylon." The written oracle against Babylon becomes a physical scroll (sēper) — the same word used for the letter to the exiles (29:1) and for Baruch's scroll (36:4). The writing-down is a prophetic act of preservation and witness: the oracle exists as a document.
51.61: Jeremiah's instruction to Seraiah: ûqĕrāʾtā ʾēt kol-hadd ĕbārîm hāʾēlleh — "when you arrive in Babylon, make sure you read all these words aloud." The public reading of the oracle against Babylon in Babylon itself mirrors Baruch's public reading of the scroll in Jerusalem (36:10). The prophetic word must be proclaimed at its object. The MKT renders "make sure you read" to convey the emphatic instruction.
51.62: wĕʾāmartā YHWH ʾattâ dibbartā ʾel-hammāqôm hazzeh lĕhakrîtô — "Then say: O LORD, you have pronounced judgment on this place, to cut it off so that nothing will live here — neither man nor animal — but that it will be desolate forever." The prayer that Seraiah is to pray in Babylon — addressing YHWH's own spoken word back to him as the ground of the prayer. The MKT renders the prayer-form: "you have pronounced judgment" (dibbartā) — using the divine speech as the foundation of the petition.
51.63: wĕhāyâ kĕkallôtĕkā liqrōʾ ʾet-hassēper hazzeh — "When you have finished reading this scroll, tie a stone to it and throw it into the middle of the Euphrates." The prophetic sign-act: the weighted scroll sinking into the Euphrates enacts the oracle's fulfillment. The physical action of throwing the scroll prefigures the sinking of Babylon. The MKT preserves "the middle of the Euphrates" — the river central to Babylon's identity is the site of the sign-act.
51.64: wĕʾāmartā kākâ tišqaʿ Bābel wĕlōʾ-tāqûm — "And say: So will Babylon sink and not rise again, because of the disaster I am bringing on it." The interpretive word that accompanies the sign-act: the scroll's sinking = Babylon's sinking. Wĕyiʿāpû — "They will become weary" — the last word of Jeremiah's oracles against the nations. ʿAd-hēnnâ dibrê Yirmĕyāhû — "Thus far are the words of Jeremiah" — the colophon closing the original oracle collection before ch. 52's historical appendix.

52.1: The regnal formula for Zedekiah: twenty-one years old when he became king, eleven-year reign, mother Hamutal daughter of Jeremiah of Libnah. The chapter is a near-verbatim parallel of 2 Kgs 24:18-25:30, serving as the historical confirmation that all of Jeremiah's oracles came to pass. Its presence here makes ch. 52 both an appendix and a verification.
52.2: wayyaʿăśeh hārāʿ bĕʿênê YHWH kĕkōl ʾăšer-ʿāśâ Yĕhôyāqîm — "He did what was evil in the sight of the LORD, just as Jehoiakim had done." The evaluative formula aligns Zedekiah with the worst of Judah's kings. The comparison to Jehoiakim (who cut and burned Baruch's scroll, ch. 36) is pointed: Zedekiah, despite consulting Jeremiah repeatedly, ultimately followed Jehoiakim's path of covenantal disobedience.
52.3: kî ʿal-ʾap-YHWH hāyĕtâ bîrûšālaim ûbîhûdâ — "For it was because of the LORD's anger that all this happened to Jerusalem and Judah." The causal statement of the fall: not Babylonian military superiority but YHWH's covenantal anger. ʿAd-hišlîkô ʾōtām mēʿal pānāyw — "until he finally expelled them from his presence." The expulsion-from-presence language echoes Eden — the fall of Jerusalem as a covenant expulsion parallel to the primal expulsion.
52.4: The siege begins: ninth year of Zedekiah, tenth month, tenth day — the precise dating matches Ezek 24:1-2 where YHWH tells Ezekiel the date of the siege's start at the exact moment it begins. The precision is both historical record and theological witness to prophetic foreknowledge.
52.5: The siege lasted until the eleventh year of Zedekiah — a two-year siege. The duration is noted without elaboration; the details of the famine (v.6) and the wall-breach (v.7) fill in the human suffering. The brevity of "the city remained under siege" (wattābōʾ hāʿîr bammātsôr) contrasts with the catastrophic consequences that follow.
52.6: wayĕhî baḥōdeš hārĕbîʿî bĕtiśʿâ laḥōdeš wayĕḥězaq hārāʿāb bāʿîr — "By the ninth day of the fourth month the famine in the city had become so severe that there was no food left for the people of the land." The famine — the covenantal curse of Deut 28:52-57 — reaches its extremity. Lĕḥem lāʿam hāʾārets (bread for the common people) — the hierarchy of suffering is inverted: even the basic food of ordinary people is exhausted.
52.7: The breach in the city wall (nĕbqĕʿâ hāʿîr) and the flight of the soldiers at night. The gate between the two walls (baššaʿar bên haḥōmōtayim) — the soldiers flee through the narrow passage between Zion's double walls. The Chaldeans surrounded the city. The MKT renders "the gate between the two walls" to preserve the archaeological detail of Jerusalem's fortification system.
52.8: wayyirdĕpû ḥêl-Kaśdîm ʾaḥărê hammelek wayyaśśîgû ʾet-Ṣidqiyyāhû — "But the Chaldean army pursued the king and caught up with Zedekiah in the plains of Jericho." The plains of Jericho — the route toward the Jordan and potential escape east — was the flight path. All his troops scattered (wayyāpĕṣû mēʾittô kol-ḥêlô). The king who failed to defend Jerusalem is captured fleeing alone.
52.9: Zedekiah captured, brought to Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah (Riblāh) in Hamath — the Syrian headquarters of Babylonian military command. Sentence passed on him (waydabbēr ʾittô mišpāṭîm). The legal vocabulary: mišpāṭ (judgment, sentence) — the covenant judge judges through the pagan king. Jeremiah had warned Zedekiah explicitly that this was coming if he did not surrender (38:17-18).
52.10: wayyišḥaṭ melek-Bābel ʾet-bĕnê Ṣidqiyyāhû lĕʿênāyw — "The king of Babylon killed Zedekiah's sons before his eyes." The fulfillment of 34:3-4's oracle: Jeremiah had said Zedekiah would see the king of Babylon eye to eye. The last sight Zedekiah has before his blinding is the murder of his sons. The horror is the oracle's precise fulfillment. "Had all the officials of Judah executed at Riblah" — the institutional leadership eliminated.
52.11: wĕʾet-ʿênê Ṣidqiyyāhû ʿiwwēr wayyaʾassrēhû bĕnaḥuštayim — "Then he blinded Zedekiah, bound him in bronze shackles." The blinding and binding fulfills the specific oracle of 32:5: "he will lead Zedekiah to Babylon." Zedekiah dies in prison in Babylon (bêt-happĕquddōt ʿad-yôm mōtô) — the precise fate Jeremiah announced. The MKT renders "bronze shackles" for naḥuštayim — the material (bronze) is specified.
52.12: The fifth month, tenth day, nineteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar — the precise date of the temple's burning. Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard (rab-ṭabbāḥîm, chief of the executioners/bodyguard) as the Babylonian officer carrying out the final destruction. The MKT renders rab-ṭabbāḥîm as "captain of the guard" following standard convention while noting the literal meaning involves slaughtering.
52.13: wayyiśrōp ʾet-bêt-YHWH wĕʾet-bêt hammelek — "He set fire to the house of the LORD, the royal palace, and all the houses of Jerusalem — every important building he burned down." The sentence is stark and comprehensive: temple, palace, all important buildings — the complete annihilation of the Solomonic city. The burning of the temple is the pivotal trauma of the OT — the loss that reshapes all subsequent Judaism and gives Lamentations its occasion.
52.14: The entire Chaldean army tears down all the walls surrounding Jerusalem (kol-ḥômôt Yĕrûšālaim sābîb nātĕṣû). The wall-demolition confirms the siege's completion: no defensive perimeter remains. The MKT renders sābîb (round about, on all sides) to emphasize the comprehensive demolition of Jerusalem's fortifications.
52.15: The deportees: some of the poorest of the people (miḏallôt hāʿām), survivors remaining in the city, those who had gone over to the king of Babylon, and the rest of the artisans (yeter hāʾāmôn). The social mix of the deportees — poor, survivors, defectors, craftsmen — reflects the indiscriminate sweep of the deportation. The MKT follows the list as given.
52.16: Contrast with v.15: Nebuzaradan left behind the very poorest of the land (miḏallôt hāʾārets) to work the vineyards and fields. The agricultural remnant — those too economically marginal to be useful as skilled labor in Babylon — are left on the land. Their agricultural function preserved the land's productivity for Babylonian benefit.
52.17: wĕʾēt ʿammûdê hanneḥōšet ʾăšer bêt-YHWH — the bronze inventory begins. The pillars (Jachin and Boaz of 1 Kgs 7:15-22), the movable stands (mĕkônôt), and the bronze sea (hayyām hanneḥōšet) — the major bronze installations of Solomon's temple — are dismantled and taken. The deliberate cataloguing of the lost objects parallels the cataloguing of the tabernacle components in Exodus: what was carefully listed in construction is now carefully listed in destruction.
52.18: The secondary bronze implements: pots (sîrôt), shovels (yaʿîm), snuffers (mĕzammĕrôt), sprinkling bowls (mizrāqôt), incense dishes (kappôt). All the bronze utensils used in the temple service are taken. The specificity of the list serves theological witness: these are not generic valuables but the instruments of covenant worship, now in Babylonian hands.
52.19: Gold and silver items: small bowls (hassippîm), fire pans (hamaghtôt), basins (mizrāqôt), pots (sîrôt), lampstands (mĕnōrôt), incense dishes (kappôt), and drink offering bowls (qĕśāwôt). The captain of the guard carried away all items — gold ones (gold) and silver ones (silver). The tautology "those made of gold, in gold; those made of silver, in silver" emphasizes the comprehensive precious-metal appropriation.
52.20: The summary of the major bronze pieces: the two pillars, the single sea with its twelve bronze bulls underneath, and the movable stands. "Which King Solomon had made for the LORD's temple — the bronze of all these vessels was beyond weighing." The immeasurability of the bronze underscores Solomon's achievement and the magnitude of the loss. The MKT preserves "beyond weighing" for ʾên mišqāl (no weight/measuring).
52.21: Dimensions of the pillars: eighteen cubits tall (c. 27 feet), twelve cubits in circumference (c. 18 feet), four fingers thick and hollow. The precision of the measurements serves as witness — this is documented loss, not myth. The MKT follows the MT measurements, converting to an approximation: the pillars were substantial structures whose dimensions were worth recording.
52.22: The bronze capital (wĕkōteret) on top of each pillar: five cubits high, decorated with latticework (śĕbākâ) and pomegranates (rimmônîm) all around. The decorative elements — pomegranates and network — were the specific adornment described in 1 Kgs 7:17-20. The MKT follows the detailed description as inventory record.
52.23: The pomegranate count: ninety-six visible on the sides, one hundred total on the surrounding latticework of each pillar. The numerical precision (96 on sides, 100 total) suggests an actual count at removal. This level of exactness — counting pomegranates — is the narrator's way of insisting on the reality of the loss: these are real objects, not symbols, and they were really taken.
52.24: wayyiqqaḥ rab-ṭabbāḥîm ʾet-Śĕrāyāhû kōhēn hārōʾš — "The captain of the guard arrested Seraiah the chief priest, Zephaniah the deputy priest, and the three gatekeepers." The same Seraiah who received Jeremiah's scroll oracle (51:59) is now arrested. The three gatekeepers (šōmĕrê hassap) — the temple's security personnel. The MKT renders kōhēn mišneh as "deputy priest" — the second priest in the temple hierarchy.
52.25: The city officials taken: one military officer, seven royal advisers (šibʿâ ʾănāšîm of the king's council), the secretary who mustered troops (hassōpēr śar haṣṣābāʾ), and sixty citizens of the people (šišším ʾîš). The administrative infrastructure of the fallen city is systematically arrested. The MKT renders the titles distinctly: officer, advisers, secretary, citizens.
52.26: Nebuzaradan takes all of them to Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah. The Babylonian chain of command is preserved: the officer Nebuzaradan executes the arrests; the king Nebuchadnezzar at his headquarters in Hamath pronounces the sentences. Power is concentrated; judgment is formal. The MKT preserves both geographic names (Riblah, Hamath) as markers of Babylonian imperial geography.
52.27: wayyakkēm melek-Bābel waymîtēm bĕRiblâ bĕʾerets Ḥāmāt — "There the king of Babylon had them all struck down and executed at Riblah in the land of Hamath." The execution of the leadership closes the narrative of the fall. Wayyigel Yĕhûdâ mēʿal ʾadmātô — "So Judah was carried away from its land." The concluding statement of exile — the covenantal expulsion formula. The MKT renders gālâ (exile/carry into captivity) as "carried away."
52.28: zeh hāʿām ʾăšer heglâ Nĕbûkadreʾṣar — the deportation census begins. First deportation: seventh year of Nebuchadnezzar, 3,023 Judeans. The number 3,023 is specific enough to suggest official records. This is the deportation after Jehoiachin's surrender (2 Kgs 24:10-16), which the kings account numbers as 10,000 (counting all social classes). The 3,023 may be the number of specifically Judean adult males. The MKT renders the numbers as given in the MT.
52.29: Second deportation: eighteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar, 832 persons. The smaller number (832 vs 3,023) for the main deportation after the fall of Jerusalem is striking — it may count only certain categories of deportees (craftsmen, officials) rather than the total. The discrepancy between the census here and the accounts in 2 Kings has generated extensive discussion; the MKT follows the Jeremiah figure without harmonizing.
52.30: Third deportation: twenty-third year of Nebuchadnezzar, Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard deported 745 Judeans. This deportation is not mentioned in Kings — it may be connected to the events following the assassination of Gedaliah (chs. 40-41). Total: 4,600. The three-wave deportation census is unique to Jeremiah's appendix. The small total (4,600) compared to the larger estimates elsewhere may indicate that only certain categories of deportees are counted in each wave.
52.31: wayhî bišlōšîm wĕšebaʿ šānâ lĕgālût Yĕhôyākîn melek-Yĕhûdâ — "In the thirty-seventh year of Jehoiachin king of Judah's exile, on the twenty-fifth day of the twelfth month, Evil-merodach king of Babylon in the year he became king... lifted up the head of Jehoiachin." The book's final episode: Jehoiachin released and honored under Evil-merodach (Amel-Marduk, 562-560 BC). The phrase nāśāʾ ʾet-rōʾš Yĕhôyākîn (lifted up his head) — nāśāʾ rōʾš means both to lift the head (honor) and appears in Gen 40:13 for Pharaoh's release of the cupbearer. The MKT renders "lifted up the head of Jehoiachin" preserving the idiom.
52.32: waydabbēr ʾittô ṭōbôt — "He treated him with respect (ṭōbôt, good things) and gave him a seat of honor above the other captive kings in Babylon." The elevation above other captive kings shows political rehabilitation — Jehoiachin is treated as a legitimate king in the Babylonian court, not merely a prisoner. This detail was confirmed by the Weidner Tablets (Babylonian ration tablets) discovered in Berlin which list oil rations for "Yaukin king of Judah" and his sons.
52.33: wĕhēlîp ʾēt bigdê kilʾô ûʾākal leḥem lĕpānāyw tāmîd — "So Jehoiachin changed out of his prison clothes and ate regularly at the king's table for the rest of his life." Prison clothes exchanged for palace clothes — the restoration of dignity through clothing. Leḥem... tāmîd (bread/food... regularly) — the covenant-provision formula: sustained at the king's table. The MKT renders "for the rest of his life" (kol-yĕmê ḥayyāyw) to mark the permanence of the rehabilitation.
52.34: wĕʾarḥātô ʾarḥat tāmîd nittĕnâ-lô mēʾēt melek-Bābel dĕbar-yôm bĕyômô ʿad-yôm mōtô — "As for his food allowance, a regular daily portion was granted him by the king of Babylon, every day until his death." The book ends with a daily food allowance for the Davidic heir — a small mercy extended by a pagan king. The ending is deliberately quiet: no restoration of the kingdom, no return to Jerusalem, just daily bread from a foreign king. Yet the Davidic line survives; the covenant is not extinguished. The MKT renders dĕbar-yôm bĕyômô (the matter of a day in its day) as "every day" — the idiom for daily provision. Jeremiah ends where it began: in the shadow of Babylon, with a remnant alive, and the covenant word still in force.

49.1: מִלְכֹּם (Milkom): the national deity of Ammon (cf. 1 Kgs 11:5,7). The oracle's opening is a legal challenge: has Israel no heir to possess Gad's territory? The verb yarash (inherit, dispossess) is the covenant-land term used for Israel's original conquest. Milcom 'possessing' (yarash) Israelite territory is a theological affront — it is YHWH's land, and the Ammonite national god has no legitimate claim.
49.2: תְּרוּעַת מִלְחָמָה (teru'at milchama, war cry): the same term as the sacred shout (teru'ah) at Sinai and the signal for holy war. YHWH announces that he will deploy the war-shout against Rabbah (Rabbat Bene-Ammon, modern Amman) — the Ammonite capital. תֵּהְיֶה לְתֵל שְׁמָמָה (tehyeh letel shemamah, will become a heap of ruin): tel (mound) is the archaeological designation for ancient ruin mounds, still used today.
49.3: הֵילִילִי חֶשְׁבּוֹן (heilili Cheshbon, wail, O Heshbon): Heshbon was previously a Moabite/Reubenite city; the overlap of territories in these oracle poems reflects historical complexity. שַׂקִּים חִגְרוּ (saqim chigru, gird on sackcloth): the mourning garment. מַלְכָּם (Malkam): some manuscripts have melek (their king) and others Milkom (the god) — the ambiguity may be intentional: the god who was their 'king' goes into exile.
49.4: הַבַּת הַשּׁוֹבֵבָה (habbat hashsovevah, the backsliding/faithless daughter): the feminine personification of Ammon as a daughter who has turned away — shavav is the apostasy root. בָּטְחָה בְּאֹוצְרֹתֶיהָ (vatcha be'otzarotehah, trusted in her treasures): trust-vocabulary (batach) applied to material security rather than YHWH — the Ammonite error that mirrors Israel's recurring failure.
49.5: מְבִיא עָלַיִךְ פַּחַד (mevi alayikh pachad, bringing terror upon you): pachad is dread/terror — the covenant curse of Deut 28:66-67. The full title Adonai YHWH Tzvaot (Lord YHWH of Hosts) carries maximum divine-warrior weight — the armies of heaven mobilized against Ammon.
49.6: וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן אָשִׁיב אֶת-שְׁבוּת בְּנֵי עַמּוֹן (ve'acharei-khen ashiv et-shevut benei Ammon, but afterward I will restore the fortunes of the Ammonites): the same restoration formula used for Israel (29:14; 30:3). YHWH's restoration purpose extends even to Ammon — the cup of wrath is followed by the possibility of return, countering the idea that the nations oracles are simply doom.
49.7: חָכְמָה בְּתֵימָן (chokhmah beteman, wisdom in Teman): Edom/Teman had an ancient reputation for wisdom in the ANE — Job's friend Eliphaz is from Teman (Job 2:11). The oracle's opening question — has Edomite wisdom perished? — is rhetorical: Edom's famous wisdom has failed to anticipate the judgment. Obadiah 8 uses the same motif.
49.8: עַמְקוּ לָשֶׁבֶת (amqu lashevet, go deep to dwell): the imperative to Dedan (an Edomite/Arabian tribe) to flee into the deep wilderness. אֵיד עֵשָׂו (eid Esav, the disaster of Esau): Edom consistently identified as Esau's descendants — the fraternal enmity of Jacob and Esau projected onto national history. The eid (disaster) echoes the cup-of-wrath theme (v12).
49.9: בֹּצְרִים (botzrim, grape-gatherers): the harvest analogy — even a grape-harvester leaves gleanings by covenant law (Lev 19:10). What will happen to Edom is worse: like nighttime thieves who take only what they want, but YHWH's stripping (chashafti, I have stripped bare) is total (v10). The legal harvest imagery contrasts the partial taking of human agents with YHWH's comprehensive judgment.
49.10: אֲנִי חָשַׂפְתִּי אֶת-עֵשָׂו (ani chasafti et-Esav, but I myself have stripped Esau bare): emphatic personal pronoun — YHWH himself, not Nebuchadnezzar, is the agent. Chashaf (strip/uncover) is used of removing a garment: Edom's protective covering is removed. גֻּלּוּ זַרְעוֹ (gullu zar'o, his offspring are devastated): the exile vocabulary, galah, applied to Edom.
49.11: עָזְבָה יְתֹמֶיךָ (azvah yetomekha, leave your orphans): YHWH will keep the orphans alive and the widows can trust him. The covenant care for the vulnerable (Deut 10:18; 27:19) extends even in the context of national destruction. YHWH's judgment on Edom does not revoke his care for the weak who survive it.
49.12: כּוֹס (kos, cup): the cup-of-wrath motif from ch25. If those who did not deserve to drink (asher lo mishpat lahem lishtos et-hakos) were made to drink — i.e., Judah — then certainly Edom will not escape. Paul uses similar reasoning in Rom 11:21 (if God did not spare the natural branches, neither will he spare you).
49.13: נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי בִי (nishbati vi, I have sworn by myself): the divine self-oath formula used for the most irrevocable commitments — used for Abraham's blessing (Gen 22:16) and the new covenant (Heb 6:13). Here it guarantees Bozrah's desolation. בָּצְרָה (Botzrah): the principal city of Edom, modern Buseirah in southern Jordan.
49.14: שְׁמוּעָה שָׁמַעְתִּי מֵאֵת יְהוָה (shmu'ah shamati me'et YHWH, I have heard a message from YHWH): the prophetic reception formula — the prophet hears before he speaks. An envoy (tzir) is sent among the nations to form a coalition against Edom. Obadiah 1 parallels this verse almost exactly.
49.15: קָטֹן נְתַתִּיךָ בַּגּוֹיִם (qaton netattikha baggoyim, I have made you small among the nations): the inversion of Edom's pride. בָּזוּי בָּאָדָם (vazui ba'adam, despised among human beings): the trajectory of pride → humiliation is the Proverbs principle of divine governance (Prov 16:18) operating at the national level.
49.16: זְדוֹן לִבֶּךָ (zedon libbkha, the pride of your heart): zadon (pride/insolence) is from the root of Pharaoh's hardening and Babylon's arrogance. שֹׁכְנִי בְּחַגְוֵי הַסֶּלַע (shokhni bechagvei hasela', you who dwell in the clefts of the rock): Petra/Sela, Edom's rock-carved mountain fortress. The security of the rock citadel is exposed as a delusion: YHWH's judgment is not blocked by topography.
49.17: שַׁמָּה תִּהְיֶה אֱדוֹם (shammah tihyeh Edom, Edom will become a horror): the horror-and-hissing formula (shammah + sherikah) is the standard covenant-curse expression for complete desolation, used repeatedly for Jerusalem and now applied to Edom. The rhetorical transfer: what Jerusalem suffered for covenant-breaking, Edom suffers for pride and violence against her brother.
49.18: כְּמַהְפֵּכַת סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה (kemahpekat Sedom va'Amorah, as in the overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah): the Sodom/Gomorrah desolation as the paradigm case of total divine judgment. No one will dwell there: lo yeshev sham ish velo yagur bah ben adam. The same formula appears in 50:40 for Babylon — Edom and Babylon parallel recipients of Sodom-type judgment.
49.19: כַּאֲרִי יַעֲלֶה מִגְּאוֹן הַיַּרְדֵּן (ka'ari ya'aleh migge'on haYarden, like a lion coming up from the thickets of the Jordan): the same lion simile used in 4:7 and 5:6 for the enemy as YHWH's agent. מִי כָּמֹנִי (mi khamoni, who is like me?): YHWH's rhetorical self-assertion — the incomparability formula from Isa 40:25 applied to his judicial authority over Edom.
49.20: יַעֲצַת יְהוָה (ya'atzat YHWH, the counsel/plan of YHWH): Edom's fate is YHWH's deliberate plan, not accident. Etza (counsel/plan) is wisdom vocabulary — YHWH's sovereign governance operates through deliberate design. The sheep of Teman (tzon teman) will be dragged away — the shepherd-flock imagery of judgment.
49.21: מִקּוֹל נִפְלָתָם רָעֲשָׁה הָאָרֶץ (miqqol nifllatam ra'ashah ha'aretz, at the noise of their fall the earth trembles): the cosmic-scale earthquake response to the fall of a nation — similar imagery to Isa 13:13 (Babylon) and Rev 18:19. The eschatological echo: cosmic disturbance marks the fall of the proud.
49.22: כַּנֶּשֶׁר יִדְאֶה וְיִפְרֹשׂ כְּנָפָיו (kannesher yid'eh veyifros kenafav, like an eagle he will soar and spread his wings): the eagle attack imagery — used for Nebuchadnezzar in Ezek 17:3 as the great eagle. Over Bozrah (al-Botzrah) — the eagle spreads its wings over Edom's capital as a bird of prey over its quarry.
49.23: חָמַת וְאַרְפָּד (Chamat ve'Arfad, Hamath and Arpad): Syrian cities — Hamath on the Orontes, Arpad in northwestern Syria. The shemu'ah ra'ah (bad news/evil report) causes dismay. הַיָּם (hayyam, the sea): their anxiety is like the troubled sea — uncontrollable agitation without resolution.
49.24: הֵרְפְּתָה דַמֶּשֶׂק (herpetah Dammesek, Damascus has grown feeble): the great Aramean capital enfeebled — the city that once threatened Israel (Isa 7:1-9) now flees. Chalchalah ve'chivlim (anguish and pangs) — the childbirth imagery for national catastrophe (cf. 4:31; 6:24).
49.25: אֵיךְ לֹא-עֻזְּבָה עִיר תְּהִלָּה (eikh lo-uzzevah ir tehillah, how is the renowned city not abandoned!): the elegiac eikh (how!) introduces a lament over Damascus's fall — the same opening word as Lamentations 1:1. Ir tehillah (city of praise/renown) — Damascus's reputation for civilization is used as a measure of the pathos of its destruction.
49.26: יִשְׁכְּבוּ בַחוּצֹתֶיהָ בַּחוּרֶיהָ (yishkevu bakhutzotyeha bachurehah, her young men will lie in her streets): the inverse of the city's vitality — bachurim (choice young men in their prime) slain in public spaces. The oracle for Damascus echoes the lament over Jerusalem (Lam 2:21).
49.27: וְהִצַּתִּי אֵשׁ בְּחוֹמַת דַּמָּשֶׂק (vehitzatti esh bechomatDammesek, I will set fire to the wall of Damascus): the fire-on-the-walls formula used throughout the nations oracles (Amos 1:4,7,10,12,14). בִּירֹנוֹת בֶּן-הֲדַד (biranot Ben-Hadad, the palaces of Ben-hadad): Ben-hadad was the dynastic name of Aramean kings (cf. 1 Kgs 20; 2 Kgs 6-8).
49.28: The oracle formula identifies the historical event: Nebuchadnezzar struck (hikkah) Kedar and the kingdoms of Hazor. Kedar = the Arab tribes of the northern Arabian Peninsula (Gen 25:13; Isa 21:16-17) — descended from Ishmael; pastoral/semi-nomadic people, famous for their black tents (Song 1:5) and archery (Isa 21:17).
49.29: אָהֳלֵיהֶם וְצֹאנָם (ohaleihem vetzonam, their tents and flocks): the specific material culture of the tent-dwelling Arab tribes — tents, flocks, curtains (yeriot), vessels (kelim), camels. The list of plunder enumerates the pastoral economy's wealth. מָגוֹר מִסָּבִיב (magor missaviv, terror on every side): Jeremiah's own signature phrase (20:3-4,10) now applied to the nations.
49.30: נוּסוּ נֻדוּ מְאֹד (nusu nudu me'od, flee! go far away!): urgent triple imperatives. עָמַק עָצָה (amaq atzah, he has made a deep plan): Nebuchadnezzar's strategic intelligence is noted, but behind it is YHWH's counsel — echoing Isa 29:15's warning against hiding plans from YHWH.
49.31: גּוֹי שַׁלְאֲנָן (goi shala'nan, a nation at ease/living in security): the irony of security as vulnerability — a peaceful nation that has no defensive walls, gates, or bars (lo-delatot velo-beriach) is easy prey. Security built on prosperity rather than YHWH's protection is an illusion.
49.32: גְּמַלֵּיהֶם לָבַז (gemaleihem labaz, their camels for plunder): the camel economy stripped. קֹצְצֵי פֵאָה (qotztzei pe'ah, those who clip the corners of their hair): a distinctive ethnic identifier for certain Arabian tribes (cf. 9:26; 25:23). They will be zarah lekhol-ruach (scattered to every wind) — the diaspora language.
49.33: הָיְתָה חָצוֹר לִמְעוֹן תַּנִּים (hayetah Chatsor lime'on tannim, Hazor will become a haunt of jackals): when human habitation ends, wild animals take over. Tannim (jackals) are the emblematic inhabitants of ruins throughout the prophets (cf. 9:11; 10:22 for Jerusalem). שְׁמָמָה עוֹלָם (shemamah olam, desolation forever): not temporary exile but total abandonment.
49.34: The Elam oracle opens with a precise date: the beginning (bereshit) of the reign of Zedekiah. Elam was an ancient power east of Babylon (modern southwestern Iran). The oracle predicts Elam's defeat before the historical rise of Persian power (Elam was absorbed into the Persian Empire under Cyrus). The date formula grounds this among the earliest of Jeremiah's dated prophecies.
49.35: שֹׁבֵר אֶת-קֶשֶׁת עֵילָם (shover et-qeshet Elam, I will break Elam's bow): Elam was famous for its archers — their bow was their primary military asset and national symbol (Isa 22:6). Breaking the bow = destroying the nation's military power at its source. רֵאשִׁית גְּבוּרָתָם (reshit gevuratam, the foremost of their strength): the bow was Elam's qualitative edge in battle.
49.36: אַרְבַּע רוּחוֹת הַשָּׁמַיִם (arba ruchot hashamayim, the four winds of heaven): cosmic scope — YHWH will bring the four winds against Elam and scatter them in all directions. This imagery recurs in Ezekiel (37:9) and Daniel (7:2; 8:8) and Revelation's four winds (Rev 7:1).
49.37: וְחַתֵּתִי אֶת-עֵילָם (vachatteti et-Elam, I will terrify Elam): chatt (terror/dismay) — the same root for the shattered self-confidence that judgment brings. The covenant curse of Deut 28:25 ('YHWH will cause you to be defeated before your enemies') applied to the nations: YHWH's moral governance is universal.
49.38: וְשַׂמְתִּי כִסְאִי בְּעֵילָם (vesamti kis'i be'Elam, I will place my throne in Elam): YHWH's throne over Elam — the divine king's judicial seat established in judgment over foreign territory. The throne (kisse') is the symbol of legal authority. YHWH as universal sovereign has a throne that moves with his judicial activity — cf. Isa 66:1 and Ezek 1 (the mobile chariot-throne).
49.39: וְהָיָה בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים (vehayah be'acharit hayyamim, but in the latter days): the eschatological phrase. אָשִׁיב אֶת-שְׁבוּת עֵילָם (ashiv et-shevut Elam): the same restoration formula applied to Israel and Ammon (v6) is given to Elam. YHWH's purposes for the nations include their ultimate restoration — anticipating the eschatological vision of nations streaming to Zion (Isa 2:2-4; Rev 21:24-26).

50.1: הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר יְהוָה אֶל-בָּבֶל (haddavar asher-dibber YHWH el-Bavel, the word that YHWH spoke concerning Babylon): the opening of the most extensive nations oracle in the OT — two full chapters (50-51). The title formula is emphatic: not through vision or symbolic act but davar (word) — direct divine speech. The word concerning the instrument of judgment is itself a judgment.
50.2: בֵּל (Bel) and מְרֹדַךְ (Merodakh/Marduk): Bel (='lord') is the title of the chief Babylonian deity Marduk, the patron god of Babylon. הֹבִישׁוּ עֲצַבֶּיהָ (hovish atzabbeha, her idols are put to shame): when Babylon falls, its gods are exposed as powerless. Cf. Isa 46:1-2 (Bel bows; Nebo stoops). The fall of Babylon is simultaneously the defeat of Babylon's gods.
50.3: מִצָּפוֹן עָלָה עָלֶיהָ גּוֹי (mitzafon alah aleha goy, from the north a nation has come up against her): the standard 'enemy from the north' oracle formula — used for Babylon's attack on Judah in chs 1-6, now applied against Babylon itself. The wheel of judgment turns: the instrument becomes the object.
50.4: בָּכוֹ יֵלֵכוּ (bakho yelekhu, they will come weeping): Israel and Judah (Israel uYehudah yachdav) will come together — the reunification of the divided kingdom, a theme from chs 30-33. אֶת-יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיהֶם יְבַקֵּשׁוּ (et-YHWH Eloheihem yevaqqeshu, they will seek YHWH their God): baqesh (seek earnestly) — the return involves not just physical migration but spiritual re-orientation toward YHWH.
50.5: בְּרִית עוֹלָם (berit olam, everlasting covenant): the foundational covenant phrase — cf. Gen 9:16 (with Noah), 17:7 (with Abraham), Isa 55:3 (Davidic covenant). The covenant joined at Zion is eternal — not conditional like the Mosaic covenant that was broken (31:32) but permanent like the new covenant (31:33-34). The return from exile culminates in covenant-renewal, not merely political restoration.
50.6: צֹאן אֹבְדוֹת (tzon ovedot, lost sheep): the lost-sheep metaphor for Israel — used by Jesus directly (Matt 10:6; 15:24; Luke 15:4-6). רֹעֵיהֶם הִתְעוּם (ro'eihem hit'um, their shepherds led them astray): the shepherds (kings and prophets) who led Israel into apostasy. The people have forgotten (shakhechu) their resting place (rivtzam) — the covenant land and its rest.
50.7: כָּל-מְצָאֵיהֶם אֲכָלוּם (kol-metzaehem akhalum, all who found them devoured them): the nations that conquered Israel claim innocence — 'we are not guilty, for they sinned against YHWH the habitation of righteousness.' The enemies invoke Israel's own covenant guilt as moral justification for predation: the conquerors are YHWH's instruments even while acting from their own interests.
50.8: נוּדוּ מִתּוֹךְ בָּבֶל (nudu mitokh Bavel, go out from the midst of Babylon): the call to flee Babylon — the foundational text for the eschatological 'come out of her' in Rev 18:4 and 2 Cor 6:17. כָּאֵילִים לִפְנֵי צֹאן (ka'eilim lifnei tzon, like he-goats leading the flock): the goat leads the flock — those who flee first enable others to follow.
50.9: מֵעַרֵּר גּוֹיִם גְּדֹלִים (me'arrer goyim gedolim, an assembly of great nations from the north): the eschatological coalition against Babylon — historically the Medes (Isa 13:17) and Persians. חִצָּיו כְּגִבּוֹר מַשְׁכִּיל (chitzav kegibor maskil, his arrows like those of a skilled warrior): the arrows return not riqam (empty) — they all find their mark. Total military effectiveness.
50.10: וְהָיְתָה כַשְׂדִּים לְשָׁלָל (vehayetah Khashdim leshalal, Chaldea will be for plunder): the reversal — Chaldea/Babylon, which plundered all the nations, becomes itself plunder. The retributive principle of 51:24 and Rev 18:6 (repay her double).
50.11: שֹׁסֵי נַחֲלָתִי (shosei nachalati, plunderers of my inheritance): Israel as YHWH's nachalah (inheritance/portion) — the attack on Israel is an attack on YHWH's own property. כְּעֶגְלָה דָשָׁה (ke'eglah dashah, like a heifer on grass): the leaping/playful image of Babylon's joy over Israel's destruction — frivolous delight in conquest that YHWH will reverse.
50.12: בֹּשָׁה אִמְּכֶם מְאֹד (boshah imkhem me'od, your mother will be utterly ashamed): Babylon as mother — the city personified as progenitor of its people. אַחֲרִית גּוֹיִם (acharit goyim, the least of nations): the inversion of imperial status — the empire that dominated all nations will be last among them.
50.13: מֵחֲמַת יְהוָה (mechamat YHWH, because of YHWH's wrath): the explicit theological cause of Babylon's desolation — not geopolitical accident but YHWH's directed wrath. שַׁמָּה תִּהְיֶה לְכֹלָה (shammah tihyeh lekhollah, she will be completely desolate): the superlative desolation formula. Historically ironic: Babylon was indeed abandoned, a desolate mound for centuries.
50.14: עִרְכוּ עַל-בָּבֶל מִסָּבִיב (irkhu al-Bavel missaviv, take your positions against Babylon from every side): the battle deployment command. כָּל-דֹּרְכֵי קֶשֶׁת (kol-dorkhei qeshet, all who draw the bow): archers surround the city. אַל-תַּחְמֹלוּ (al-tachmolu, do not spare your arrows): no quarter — the covenant standard of cherem applied to Babylon.
50.15: נָתְנָה יָדָהּ (natneah yadah, she has surrendered): the surrender gesture — offering the hand. נִקְמַת יְהוָה (niqmat YHWH, the vengeance of YHWH): naqam (vengeance/retribution) — YHWH's retributive justice on behalf of his people. The vengeance is not personal spite but covenant-legal satisfaction.
50.16: כִּרְתוּ זוֹרֵעַ מִבָּבֶל (kirtu zore'a mibbavel, cut off the sower from Babylon): the agricultural infrastructure destroyed. The sword drives everyone to their own people (el-ammo yifanu) — the dissolution of Babylon's polyglot population that sustained its imperial economy (cf. Rev 18:17-19 on the merchants fleeing).
50.17: שֶׂה פְזוּרָה יִשְׂרָאֵל (seh pezurah Yisrael, Israel is a scattered sheep): pazar (scatter) is the diaspora verb. Two predators named: the king of Assyria first (rishonah) devoured, and last (ve'acharonah) Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon. The two-stage scattering maps onto Assyrian conquest of the north (722 BCE) and Babylonian conquest of the south (586 BCE).
50.18: לָכֵן כֹּה-אָמַר יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת (lakhen koh-amar YHWH Tzvaot, therefore thus says YHWH of Hosts): the consequential conjunction. YHWH will punish (paqad) the king of Babylon as he punished the king of Assyria. The same judicial standard applies to each imperial power in sequence — precedent binds YHWH's judgment.
50.19: וַהֲשִׁבֹתִי אֶת-יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל-נָוֵהוּ (vahashivoti et-Yisrael el-naveihu, I will restore Israel to his pasture): shuv (return/restore) and naveh (pasture/home) — restoration to covenant land for grazing. כַּרְמֶל וְהַבָּשָׁן (Karmel vehaBashan): the most fertile grazing territories — Carmel and Bashan were proverbial for lush vegetation and well-fed livestock.
50.20: יְבֻקַּשׁ אֶת-עֲוֹן יִשְׂרָאֵל וְאֵינֶנּוּ (yevuqqash et-avon Yisrael ve'einennu, Israel's iniquity will be searched for and not found): the forensic search — baqash (seek/search diligently) — finding nothing. The complete erasure of recorded iniquity: the covenant-accusation against Israel will have no evidence. This is the experiential content of the new covenant's 'I will remember their sin no more' (31:34).
50.21: מְרָתַיִם (Meratayim): a cryptographic name for Babylon (from marah = bitter/rebel; meratayim = double rebellion). פְּקוֹד (Pekod): another Babylonian name with wordplay — paqad means 'visit in judgment.' Both names encode YHWH's accusation: Babylon is doubly rebellious and is being visited with judgment. The wordplay is both political geography and theological verdict.
50.22: קוֹל מִלְחָמָה בָּאָרֶץ (qol milchama ba'aretz, the sound of battle in the land): a terse proclamation — two words frame the announcement. וְשֶׁבֶר גָּדוֹל (veshever gadol, and great destruction): shever (breaking/destruction) is the covenant word for catastrophic collapse (cf. Isa 30:14; Jer 6:14: 'they have healed the wound of my people lightly'). Now there is no light healing.
50.23: פַּטִּישׁ כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (pattish kol-ha'aretz, the hammer of the whole earth): Babylon's imperial self-designation — the hammer that smashes other nations. אֵיךְ נִכְרַת (eikh nikkrat, how it is cut down!): the elegiac eikh as for Damascus (49:25). The great instrument of divine judgment has itself been broken: the hammer becomes the nail.
50.24: יָקַשְׁתִּי לְךָ (yaqashti lekha, I laid a snare for you): YHWH as the hunter who set the trap. וְלֹא יָדָעַתְּ (velo yada'at, and you did not know it): the ignorance of the prey — Babylon did not know when the trap was set. כִּי בַיהוָה הִתְגָּרִית (ki vaYHWH hitgarit, for against YHWH you have contended): the root charge — Babylon's arrogance was ultimately a contest (garah) with YHWH himself.
50.25: פָּתַח יְהוָה אֶת-אוֹצָרוֹ (patach YHWH et-otzaro, YHWH has opened his armory): YHWH's weapons-treasury — the storehouse of covenant judgment. מְלֶאכֶת אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה צְבָאוֹת (melekhet Adonai YHWH Tzvaot, the work of the Lord YHWH of Hosts): Babylon's destruction is described as YHWH's own 'work' — cf. Isa 28:21 where YHWH's 'strange work' of judgment is his own purposeful project.
50.26: כִּי-עֵת הִיא (ki-et hi', for it is the time): the appointed time (et) — the kairos moment of Babylon's judgment. כְּמוֹ עֲרֵמִים הַחֲרִימוּהָ (kemo aremim hacheriumuha, pile her up like heaps): the grain-heap imagery. Hacharim (completely destroy): the covenant cherem term — total dedication to destruction, the same language used for Canaanite cities in the conquest.
50.27: טִבְחוּ כָל-פָּרֶיהָ (tivkhu kol-pareha, slaughter all her bulls): the temple sacrifice vocabulary — the Babylonian military/elite as bulls going to slaughter. כִּי-בָא יוֹמָם עֵת פְּקֻדָּתָם (ki-va yomam et pequddatam, for their day has come, the time of their judgment): the yom paquddah (day of visitation/judgment) — the appointed accountability day has arrived.
50.28: קוֹל נָסִים וּפְלֵטִים (qol nasim ufletim, the voice of those who flee and escape): the news is carried out of Babylon by its survivors. לְהַגִּיד בְּצִיּוֹן אֶת-נִקְמַת יְהוָה (lehagid beTziyyon et-niqmat YHWH, to declare in Zion the vengeance of YHWH): the refugees become heralds in Zion — the crimes committed against YHWH's house are publicly avenged. The proclamation in Zion completes the legal circle.
50.29: שַׁלְּמוּ-לָהּ כְּפָעֳלָהּ (shalemu-lah kefo'alah, repay her according to her work): the shillem (repay/requite) principle of covenant justice — what Babylon did, do to her. כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר עָשְׂתָה עֲשׂוּ-לָהּ (kekhol asher astha asuha, according to all that she did, do to her): the lex talionis at the national level. Rev 18:6 quotes this principle directly for the eschatological Babylon.
50.30: לָכֵן יִפְּלוּ בַחוּרֶיהָ בִּרְחֹבֹתֶיהָ (lakhen yippelu vachehurehah birkhovoteiha, therefore her young men will fall in her streets): the lament-pattern — the city's vitality dies in its own public spaces. The same formula applied to Damascus (49:26) and Jerusalem in Lamentations is now applied to Babylon — all proud cities receive the same covenant reckoning.
50.31: הִנְנִי אֵלֶיךָ זָדוֹן (hineni elekha zadon, behold, I am against you, O arrogance!): the divine hineni elekha formula — used against Tyre, Egypt, and Gog in Ezekiel. Babylon is personified as zadon (pride/arrogance) itself. כִּי בָא יוֹמֶךָ (ki va yomekha, for your day has come): the day of YHWH vocabulary applied to Babylon — cf. Isa 13:6,9.
50.32: וְכָשַׁל זָדוֹן וְנָפַל (vekhushal zadon venafal, arrogance will stumble and fall): the Proverbs principle at the national level (Prov 16:18: pride goes before a fall). וְהִצַּתִּי-אֵשׁ בְּעָרָיו (vehitzatti-esh be'arav, I will set fire to his cities): the fire-judgment formula visited on the hammer that wielded fire against others.
50.33: עֲשׁוּקִים בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל וּבְנֵי-יְהוּדָה יַחְדָּו (ashuqim benei-Yisrael uvenei-Yehudah yachdav, the people of Israel and the people of Judah are oppressed together): the reunification of north and south even in their oppression — yachdav (together) prepares for common restoration. מֵאֲנוּ לְשַׁלְּחָם (me'anu leshallecham, they refused to let them go): the Exodus vocabulary — Pharaoh refused (maen) to let Israel go; now Babylon repeats the pattern and faces the same pattern's judgment.
50.34: גֹּאֲלָם חָזָק (go'alam chazaq, their Redeemer is strong/mighty): the go'el (kinsman-redeemer) theology — the legal-familial concept of the nearest relative with both the obligation and the right to redeem a family member from slavery. YHWH Tzvaot shemo (YHWH of Hosts is his name): the title emphasizes military power — the Redeemer is not only legally entitled but militarily capable. The go'el theme runs from Ruth through Isaiah 40-55 (where 'Redeemer' appears 13 times) to Christ who ransoms.
50.35: חֶרֶב אֶל-כַּשְׂדִּים (cherev el-Kashdim, a sword against the Chaldeans): the extended sword-sequence poem — nine cherev el (sword against) stanzas using anaphoric accumulation. The sword Babylon wielded against others is now turned against every class of Babylonian society: officials, sages, warriors, horses, foreigners, treasures.
50.36: חֶרֶב אֶל-הַבַּדִּים (cherev el-habaddim, a sword against her diviners/false prophets): baddim can mean liars/boasters or diviners who speak falsely — both meanings fit. וְנֹאָלוּ (veno'alu, they will become fools): the reversal of Babylon's claim to wisdom and divinatory expertise.
50.37: חֶרֶב אֶל-הַסּוּסִים (cherev el-hasusim, a sword against her horses): the complete military-economic complex dismantled — horses, chariots (rekev), foreign troops (erev). וְהָיוּ לְנָשִׁים (vehayou lenashim, they will become as women): the covenant-curse reversal of martial valor (Deut 28:25). The treasury (otzarotehah) plundered: economic infrastructure follows the military.
50.38: חֹרֶב אֶל-מֵימֶיהָ (chorev el-meimehah, a drought against her waters): the wordplay shifts from cherev (sword) to chorev (drought/dryness) — nearly identical sounds (ch-r-v). Babylon's hydraulic engineering (the Euphrates, Tigris, irrigation canals) targeted. אֶרֶץ פְּסִילִים הִיא (eretz pesilim hi', for it is a land of idols): Babylon's water-wealth has become spiritually insane (yiththolellu) through idol-madness — cf. Isa 44:9-20 on the idolater's derangement.
50.39: צִיִּים וְאִיִּים (tziyyim ve'iyyim, wild desert creatures and jackals): the desolation fauna who occupy ruin sites. This desolation formula applied to Edom (49:33) anticipates Revelation's 'Babylon ... a haunt of every foul bird and beast' (Rev 18:2). The sequence: Sodom → Edom → Babylon → eschatological Babylon, all described in the same vocabulary.
50.40: כְּמַהְפֵּכַת אֱלֹהִים אֶת-סְדֹם וְאֶת-עֲמֹרָה (kemahpekat Elohim et-Sedom ve'et-Amorah, as when God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah): the identical formula used for Edom (49:18). Edom and Babylon are parallel types of YHWH's total judgment. In the NT, both serve as figures for eschatological judgment (Rev 14:8; 18:2,21).
50.41: הִנֵּה עַם בָּא מִצָּפוֹן (hineh am ba mitzafon, behold, a people is coming from the north): the enemy-from-the-north oracle pattern used against Judah (1:14; 4:6; 6:1,22) reversed and applied to Babylon. גּוֹי גָּדוֹל וּמְלָכִים רַבִּים (goi gadol umelakhim rabbim, a great nation and many kings): the Medo-Persian coalition under Cyrus.
50.42: קֶשֶׁת וְכִידוֹן (qeshet vekidon, bow and spear): the full weapons complement. אַכְזָרִי הוּא וְלֹא יְרַחֵמוּ (akhzari hu velo yerachemu, they are cruel and show no mercy): the same characterization given to Babylon when it attacked Judah (6:23) — now applied to Babylon's attackers. The judgment is symmetrical.
50.43: שָׁמַע מֶלֶךְ-בָּבֶל אֶת-שִׁמְעָם (shama melekh-Bavel et-shim'am, the king of Babylon heard the report of them): the same shama' (hear/listen) that marks Israel's failure throughout Jeremiah — now the king of Babylon hears and his hands go limp (raffu yadav). The word that Babylon refused to hear (YHWH's prophetic warnings through Jeremiah) now comes as military intelligence: the same paralysis that fell on Judah falls on Babylon.
50.44: כַּאֲרִי יַעֲלֶה מִגְּאוֹן הַיַּרְדֵּן (ka'ari ya'aleh migge'on haYarden): the lion-from-Jordan simile applied verbatim to Babylon as Jeremiah applied it to Edom (49:19). The repetition is deliberate — Edom and Babylon are parallel targets, and YHWH's incomparability (mi khamoni) governs both judgments equally.
50.45: שִׁמְעוּ עֲצַת יְהוָה (shim'u atzat YHWH, hear the plan of YHWH): the etzah (plan/counsel) of YHWH against Babylon — parallel to 49:20 for Edom. אֲשֶׁר יָעַץ אֶל-אֶרֶץ כַּשְׂדִּים (asher ya'atz el-eretz Kashdim, which he has devised against the land of the Chaldeans): ya'atz (devise/plan/purpose) — YHWH's purposes for the nations are not spontaneous but deliberately planned in his council.
50.46: מִקּוֹל תִּפְלַשׂ בָּבֶל (miqqol tiplas Bavel, at the sound of Babylon's capture): the earth quakes (ra'ashah ha'aretz) — cosmic-scale seismic response to the fall of the world-empire. בַּגּוֹיִם יִשָּׁמַע זַעֲקָהּ (baggoyim yishama za'aqah, her cry will be heard among the nations): cf. Rev 18:9-10 where the kings of the earth weep at Babylon's destruction from afar. The chapter closes with the full extent of Babylon's significance: its fall reverberates through all the nations, as its rise did.
